The Agentic Payroll Agent

A Naturalist's Account

The Agentic Payroll Agent

Mehran MozaffariJune 2026

01 STP Phase 2 — Disaggregated Reporting and the Payroll Framework

STP2 income type codes, the ATO's data model, PAYG withholding framework, and the determinism-first principle

Single Touch Payroll Phase 2 changed how Australia reports wages. Where Phase 1 reported a single gross figure, Phase 2 requires employers to disaggregate that gross into its components — each with its own ATO classification, each subject to different withholding and contribution rules. This chapter establishes the STP2 reporting framework, explains why determinism must sit at the centre of every payroll agent, and introduces the H1/H2/H3 gate model that governs what an agent can do without human sign-off.

STP Phase 2 — Disaggregated Gross

The ATO's STP Phase 2 employer reporting guidelines require that each pay event report disaggregated income. STP Phase 2 reporting commenced on 1 January 2022, with ATO transition concessions applying through 2022. The single gross earnings figure that satisfied Phase 1 is no longer sufficient.

Disaggregation means every earning type is reported separately. The ATO uses these figures to pre-fill tax returns, calculate Medicare Levy Surcharge, assess health insurance rebates, and detect misclassification of employment income. Getting the disaggregation wrong produces cascading errors: a reporting breach, potential incorrect PAYG withholding for the employee, and incorrect SG calculations for the employer.

Conceptually, Phase 2 takes the single gross figure and splits it into a set of separately coded streams, as Figure 1.1 shows.

The key disaggregation categories under STP2 are:

STP2 Field What it covers Notes
Gross All taxable earnings not separately reported Residual after all itemised amounts are stripped out
Allowances Tool allowances, travel, laundry, overtime meal, etc. Each allowance type has its own ATO code
Salary sacrifice (super) Pre-tax super contributions made by employee Reduces reportable gross; reported on IITR as RESC
Salary sacrifice (other) Pre-tax benefits other than super (e.g. novated lease) Reported separately from super sacrifice
Leave Annual leave, personal/carer's leave, long service leave Leave type affects withholding method
Bonuses and commissions Performance bonuses, sales commissions Different withholding rules apply
Director fees Fees paid to a director of a company Separate income type, specific withholding treatment
Return to work payments Payments to induce an employee to return May have special withholding treatment
ETPs Employment termination payments Two caps apply; detailed in chapter 06

My take: The single biggest source of STP2 non-compliance I see is payroll systems that still report one gross figure and then add allowances as a footnote. The ATO's data model expects itemisation. If your payroll software cannot produce it, you are already behind.

The reporting obligation does not end at lodgement. The STP2 data feeds directly into the ATO's income statement prefill. Employees see their disaggregated income in myGov. An employer who lumps a tool allowance into gross creates a prefill mismatch that the employee must correct — at their expense in time, and at your expense in reputation.

Income Type Codes — SAW, CHP, FEI, and Others

STP2 assigns an income type to each payee. The income type determines which withholding rules apply and how the ATO processes the data. The codes are not optional — an STP submission without a valid income type code will fail validation.

Code Full name When to use
SAW Salary and wages Standard employment — most employees
CHP Closely held payees Employees who are related to the employer entity (e.g. family members in a family company, director/sole shareholder)
FEI Foreign employment income Employees working overseas for an Australian employer; specific withholding and tax treatment applies
WHM Working holiday maker Visa subclass 417 or 462; flat 15% on first $45,000 (verify against ATO — as at June 2026)
SWS Supported wage system Employees with a disability assessed under the supported wage system; rate below award minimum wage permitted
IAA Inbound assignees to Australia Foreign nationals assigned to work in Australia by a foreign employer
OSP Overseas employment Employees working overseas for an Australian employer who elected into the overseas employment rules

The stp-agent is responsible for assigning the correct income type code. It does this by querying the employee record and running a decision tree:

// Income type classification — stp-agent
function classifyIncomeType(employee: Employee): STPIncomeType {
  if (employee.visaSubclass === "417" || employee.visaSubclass === "462") {
    return "WHM";
  }
  if (employee.isInboundAssignee && employee.foreignEmployer) {
    return "IAA";
  }
  if (employee.workLocation === "overseas" && employee.overseasElection) {
    return "OSP";
  }
  if (employee.foreignEmploymentAgreement) {
    return "FEI";
  }
  if (employee.isSWSAssessed) {
    return "SWS";
  }
  if (employee.isCloselyHeld) {
    return "CHP";
  }
  return "SAW"; // default for standard employees
}

The order of the checks matters: they run top to bottom and the first match wins, so the most specific visa and assignment cases are tested before the SAW default (Figure 1.2).

My take: The CHP code catches employers off guard. A family company paying a spouse or adult child must use CHP — not SAW. The withholding rules for CHP payees allow quarterly reporting instead of each pay event, but the employer must still lodge. Missing this is a compliance breach.

WHM classification is particularly important. A working holiday maker misclassified as SAW will have the wrong tax withheld — too much if they are below the $45,000 threshold, and the employer is liable for the difference under a pay-as-you-go withholding variation audit. Always verify the visa subclass before payroll onboarding.

Example 1.1 (assumed-fact illustration): Robert joins Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd on a 417 visa. The onboarding form captures his visa subclass. The stp-agent classifies him as WHM. The payroll engine applies the WHM withholding rate of 15% on earnings up to $45,000 (verify against ATO — as at June 2026). Robert's pay event reports income type WHM. Six months later, Robert's visa expires and he obtains a permanent residency. The HR team updates the employee record. The stp-agent reclassifies him as SAW from the next pay event. The withholding rate reverts to the standard tax table. A payroll note is appended to the audit trail: "Income type changed SAW from pay period 2026-07-01. Prior WHM classification confirmed against visa record."

What this illustrates: Income type is not set-and-forget. The stp-agent must re-evaluate classification on every pay event when employee status can change. The payroll engine adjusts withholding in real time. The audit trail preserves the reason for the change so any ATO review can trace the reclassification without ambiguity.

PAYG Withholding Framework Overview

Pay As You Go withholding is the mechanism by which employers collect income tax on behalf of their employees and remit it to the ATO. The obligation arises under Division 12 of Schedule 1 to the Taxation Administration Act 1953 (verify — as at June 2026). The employer withholds from each payment, lodges an activity statement (either monthly or quarterly depending on withholding volume), and remits the withheld amounts to the ATO. This withhold–report–remit cycle is shown in Figure 1.3.

The ATO publishes the tax withholding tables (NAT 1008 series). The payroll engine looks up the applicable table for each employee based on:

  • Payment frequency (weekly, fortnightly, monthly, quarterly)
  • Gross taxable earnings for the period
  • TFN declaration claims (tax-free threshold, Medicare levy exemption, HECS/HELP debt)
  • Income type (WHM has a separate table)

These four inputs together select a single row in the applicable NAT 1008 table, which the engine resolves into the withholding figure (Figure 1.4).

The PAYG withholding obligation is not optional and not subject to negotiation with the employee. An employer cannot agree to withhold less than the ATO table requires. Any such agreement is void. The employer remains liable for the shortfall.

Every PAYG withholding figure comes from the payroll engine, never from the agent. The LLM agent does not calculate withholding at all — it classifies the employee, checks the TFN declaration, assembles the inputs, and hands them to the engine, which returns the amount for the agent to record. This is a hard architectural rule, not a design preference.

// PAYG withholding — agent calls engine, never calculates itself
async function computePAYGWithholding(
  payEvent: PayEvent,
  tfnDeclaration: TFNDeclaration,
  engine: PayrollEngine
): Promise<WithholdingResult> {
  const inputs: WithholdingInputs = {
    grossTaxable: payEvent.grossTaxable,
    frequency: payEvent.paymentFrequency,
    taxFreeThreshold: tfnDeclaration.claimsTaxFreeThreshold,
    medicareLevyExemption: tfnDeclaration.medicareLevyExemption,
    helpDebt: tfnDeclaration.hasHELPDebt,
    incomeType: payEvent.incomeType,
    asAtDate: payEvent.paymentDate,
  };

  // computed by the engine, not here — never approximate withholding
  const result = await engine.computeWithholding(inputs);

  return {
    withholdingAmount: result.amount,
    tableUsed: result.tableReference,
    asAtDate: result.asAtDate,
    provenance: result.provenance,
  };
}

My take: I have seen payroll agents that interpolate between table values or use a formula approximation because "it is close enough." It is not close enough. The ATO audits withholding against its own tables. An approximation that is $3 short per fortnight becomes a $78 annual shortfall per employee. Multiply by 50 employees and you have a $3,900 withholding shortfall with penalties and general interest charge. Use the table. Leave the arithmetic to the engine.

Determinism-First Principle for Payroll

Determinism-first means every regulated payroll figure — PAYG withholding, SG contribution, OTE, ETP tax-free component, ETP taxable component, leave loading — must be computed by the deterministic payroll engine, not estimated or approximated by an LLM agent. A figure the engine did not produce is a figure the ATO can recompute, find short, and penalise. The path every regulated figure takes is shown in Figure 1.5.

LLMs are probabilistic. They generate outputs that are statistically likely given training data. That property is useful for drafting payslip narratives, classifying allowances, and explaining award conditions. It is incompatible with computing an employee's tax withheld. A figure that is "probably right" is wrong from a compliance standpoint if it differs from the ATO table by even one cent.

The determinism-first principle has three corollaries:

Corollary 1 — Regulated figures belong to the engine. The payroll engine is a deterministic calculation module — a function that given the same inputs always returns the same outputs. Agents call the engine; they never compute regulated figures themselves.

Corollary 2 — Provenance is mandatory. Every figure produced by the engine must be accompanied by a provenance record: the table reference, the as-at date, the version of the engine, and the inputs used. If the ATO queries a withholding amount in three years, the employer can reproduce the calculation exactly.

Corollary 3 — NoOverride invariant. No agent, no matter how confident, may override a figure produced by the engine. If an agent disagrees with the engine output, it must escalate to the H3 gate for practitioner review. It must not substitute its own number.

// Provenance envelope — every engine output carries this
interface ProvenanceEnvelope {
  engineVersion: string;        // e.g. "payroll-engine@2.4.1"
  tableReference: string;       // e.g. "NAT 1008 — Weekly — 2025-26"
  asAtDate: string;             // ISO date the table was current
  inputs: Record<string, unknown>;
  computedAt: string;           // ISO timestamp
  noOverride: true;             // invariant — agents must not mutate
}

// Engine call — deterministic, typed, auditable
const withholdingResult = await engine.computeWithholding({
  grossTaxable: 3_200.00,
  frequency: "fortnightly",
  taxFreeThreshold: true,
  medicareLevyExemption: false,
  helpDebt: true,
  incomeType: "SAW",
  asAtDate: "2026-06-20",
});

// withholdingResult.provenance.noOverride === true
// withholdingResult.provenance.tableReference === "NAT 1008 — Fortnightly — 2025-26"
// DO NOT use withholdingResult.amount as a base for further agent computation

Example 1.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Angela works at Adelaide Retail Services. Her fortnightly gross is $3,200. She claims the tax-free threshold and has a HELP debt. The payg-agent assembles these inputs and calls the payroll engine. The engine consults the NAT 1008 fortnightly table (as at June 2026 — verify against ATO before relying on this figure) and returns a withholding amount with a full provenance envelope. The payg-agent records the provenance in the audit trail. Two years later, Angela disputes a tax assessment. The employer produces the provenance envelope. The ATO can see exactly which table was used, which inputs were provided, and which version of the engine ran. The dispute is resolved without penalty because the provenance is complete.

What this illustrates: The provenance envelope is the employer's primary defence in an ATO audit. The payroll engine owns the figure, and the provenance record proves it was computed correctly. An agent that computed the figure itself, without a table reference, cannot produce this defence.

H1/H2/H3 Gate Model

The H1/H2/H3 gate model defines which payroll decisions require a human — and which specific type of human (Figure 1.6). Agents automate everything they can. At each gate, the agent stops, packages its output, and routes it to the appropriate person. The gate is not optional.

H1 — Registered tax agent or BAS agent (non-delegable)

H1 gates cover actions where the Tax Agent Services Act 2009 (TASA 2009) (verify — as at June 2026) requires a registered practitioner. These actions cannot be delegated to an unregistered person or performed autonomously by an agent:

  • STP finalisation (End of Year finalisations submitted to the ATO)
  • Formal ATO correspondence on behalf of the employer
  • PAYG withholding variation applications
  • Lodgement of activity statements where the employer is a client of a tax agent

H2 — Registered tax agent (Tax Agent Services Act capacity)

H2 gates cover actions that require a registered tax agent specifically — not a BAS agent:

  • ATO audit responses and objection lodgements
  • Formal objections to assessments
  • Reclassification of income with direct tax consequences (e.g. reclassifying a contractor as an employee for prior years)

H3 — Practitioner review before proceeding

H3 gates cover actions where a payroll practitioner, accountant, or HR specialist must review and approve before the agent proceeds. The practitioner need not be registered under TASA 2009, but must be competent:

  • Award compliance issues (incorrect classification, underpayment risk)
  • Contractor multi-factor test results that produce an AMBER verdict
  • ETP classification for unusual termination circumstances
  • Payroll tax jurisdiction questions for multi-state employers
// Gate routing — payroll-orchestrator
type GateLevel = "H1" | "H2" | "H3" | null;

function determineGate(action: PayrollAction, confidence: number): GateLevel {
  if (action.type === "STP_FINALISATION") return "H1";
  if (action.type === "ATO_CORRESPONDENCE") return "H1";
  if (action.type === "ATO_OBJECTION") return "H2";
  if (action.type === "CONTRACTOR_RECLASSIFICATION_PRIOR_YEARS") return "H2";

  if (action.type === "AWARD_COMPLIANCE" && action.status === "AMBER") return "H3";
  if (action.type === "CONTRACTOR_MULTI_FACTOR" && confidence < 0.9) return "H3";
  if (action.type === "ETP_CLASSIFICATION" && action.unusual) return "H3";

  return null; // agent can proceed autonomously
}

Example 1.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Wei works at Blue Mountain Healthcare. At the end of the financial year, the stp-agent prepares the EOFY finalisation file — summarising each employee's YTD gross, tax withheld, allowances, and super contributions. The file is complete and validated. The agent does not lodge it. Instead, it packages the file and routes it to the H1 gate: the business's registered tax agent. The gate notification reads: "STP EOFY finalisation ready for lodgement. H1 gate — registered tax agent or BAS agent sign-off required. No autonomous lodgement permitted." The tax agent reviews, confirms, and lodges. The audit-trail-agent records the lodgement event with the agent's registration number and the lodgement timestamp.

What this illustrates: The H1 gate is non-delegable. The agent can prepare everything. It cannot lodge a finalisation — that requires a registered practitioner. The gate exists to preserve the employer's compliance posture and to ensure the TASA 2009 obligations are met.

payroll-orchestrator Overview

The payroll-orchestrator is the top-level coordinator. It receives a pay event — either from the HR system as a scheduled run or as an ad hoc request — and fans out to the specialist agents. It assembles the results, evaluates the RED/AMBER/GREEN routing, and either produces a finalised pay run or routes to the appropriate gate.

The orchestrator coordinates rather than computes. Every computation is performed by either the payroll engine (regulated figures) or a specialist agent (classification, compliance checking, STP formatting). The orchestrator assembles outputs, checks for conflicts, and routes. Figure 1.7 shows the fan-out and the routing decision that follows.

// payroll-orchestrator — top-level pay run handler
async function runPayroll(
  payRun: PayRun,
  engine: PayrollEngine
): Promise<PayRunResult> {
  // Fan out to specialist agents in parallel where possible
  const [sgResult, paygResult, awardResult] = await Promise.all([
    sgAgent.computeSG(payRun, engine),
    paygAgent.computeWithholding(payRun, engine),
    awardAgent.checkCompliance(payRun),
  ]);

  // Termination agent runs only if termination event present
  const terminationResult = payRun.hasTermination
    ? await terminationAgent.processTermination(payRun, engine)
    : null;

  // Evaluate routing
  const routing = evaluateRouting([sgResult, paygResult, awardResult, terminationResult]);

  if (routing.level === "RED") {
    return routeToGate(routing, payRun);
  }

  if (routing.level === "AMBER") {
    return routeForReview(routing, payRun);
  }

  // GREEN — proceed to STP lodgement preparation
  const stpPayload = await stpAgent.buildPayload(payRun, sgResult, paygResult, terminationResult);
  return { status: "GREEN", stpPayload, provenance: collectProvenance([sgResult, paygResult]) };
}

The four primary specialist agents are:

Agent Responsibility Gate level if fails
sg-agent OTE calculation, SG amount, fund routing, MSCB check H3 (AMBER), H1 (SGC penalty)
payg-agent TFN declaration check, withholding lookup, WHM flag H3 (unusual income type), H1 (no TFN)
award-agent Award classification, minimum rate check, penalty rates, allowances H3 (any non-compliant finding)
termination-agent ETP components, genuine redundancy tax-free amount, unused leave on termination H3 (unusual ETP), H2 (reclassification)

Example 1.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Sasha runs a fortnightly payroll at Westfield Logistics for 23 employees. The payroll-orchestrator initiates the pay run. It fans out to sg-agent, payg-agent, and award-agent in parallel. For 22 employees, all three agents return GREEN. For one employee — a casual driver — the award-agent returns AMBER: the employee's base rate falls below the Transport and Distribution Award minimum for a casual Grade 2 driver (verify against the applicable award — as at June 2026). The orchestrator holds the entire pay run. It packages the AMBER finding: the employee record, the award rate, the employee's current rate, and the shortfall. It routes to the H3 gate — practitioner review. Sasha reviews, confirms the shortfall is real, corrects the rate, and marks the gate cleared. The orchestrator re-runs the award check, receives GREEN, and proceeds to STP payload generation. The audit trail records the AMBER finding, the gate clearance, and the rate correction.

What this illustrates: The AMBER/GREEN routing is a hard gate. The orchestrator does not process an AMBER pay run without human clearance. The award check is mandatory on every pay run, not just at onboarding. Award rates change. Employee classifications change. The award-agent must verify the applicable award on each cycle.

The audit-trail-agent runs throughout the pay cycle. Rather than a step in the pipeline, it is an observer that records every event: agent invocations, engine calls, gate routings, gate clearances, STP lodgement events, and error states. Every record includes a timestamp, the agent that produced it, and the provenance envelope of any computed figure.

// audit-trail-agent — observer pattern
interface AuditEvent {
  eventId: string;
  timestamp: string;           // ISO 8601
  agentId: string;
  eventType: AuditEventType;
  payRunId: string;
  employeeId?: string;
  gateLevel?: "H1" | "H2" | "H3";
  gateStatus?: "PENDING" | "CLEARED" | "REJECTED";
  provenance?: ProvenanceEnvelope;
  notes?: string;
}

type AuditEventType =
  | "PAY_RUN_START"
  | "ENGINE_CALL"
  | "AGENT_RESULT"
  | "ROUTING_DECISION"
  | "GATE_RAISED"
  | "GATE_CLEARED"
  | "STP_PAYLOAD_BUILT"
  | "STP_LODGED"
  | "ERROR";

Next: Chapter 02 covers the payroll-orchestrator fan-out in detail — how each specialist agent receives its inputs, how the payroll engine processes each pay event, and how the RED/AMBER/GREEN routing model handles conflicts across agents.

02 The Payroll Orchestrator and the Calculation Engine

Fan-out from payroll-orchestrator to SG, PAYG, award, termination, and STP agents; the engine as deterministic core

The payroll-orchestrator is the nervous system of an agentic payroll stack. It coordinates rather than computes. Every specialist agent in the fan-out has a precise boundary: what it receives, what it produces, and which gate it escalates to when it cannot produce a GREEN result. This chapter dissects the orchestrator, traces each fan-out path, and establishes the noOverride invariant that ensures the payroll engine's figures are never mutated by downstream agents.

payroll-orchestrator — Inputs and Routing

The payroll-orchestrator receives a pay event payload. A pay event is a structured record that describes a single employee's earnings for a specific pay period. It is produced by the HR system or by a pay run scheduler and passed to the orchestrator at the start of each cycle.

A minimal pay event contains:

// Pay event — minimum viable structure
interface PayEvent {
  payEventId: string;
  employeeId: string;
  employerId: string;
  payPeriodStart: string;     // ISO date
  payPeriodEnd: string;       // ISO date
  paymentDate: string;        // ISO date — actual payment date
  paymentFrequency: "weekly" | "fortnightly" | "monthly" | "quarterly";
  earnings: EarningsLine[];
  allowances: AllowanceLine[];
  deductions: DeductionLine[];
  leaveAccruals: LeaveAccrualLine[];
  hasTermination: boolean;
  terminationDetails?: TerminationDetails;
  asAtDate: string;           // date for rate/threshold lookups
}

The orchestrator's first action is validation. Before it fans out to any specialist agent, it validates that the pay event is structurally complete: all required fields are present, the payment date is within the pay period plus a reasonable extension, the employee record exists, and the employer is registered for STP2 reporting.

// Orchestrator validation — before fan-out
async function validatePayEvent(payEvent: PayEvent): Promise<ValidationResult> {
  const errors: ValidationError[] = [];

  if (!payEvent.paymentDate) errors.push({ field: "paymentDate", code: "MISSING" });
  if (!payEvent.employeeId) errors.push({ field: "employeeId", code: "MISSING" });
  if (payEvent.earnings.length === 0 && !payEvent.hasTermination) {
    errors.push({ field: "earnings", code: "NO_EARNINGS_ON_NON_TERMINATION" });
  }

  const employeeRecord = await employeeRepo.find(payEvent.employeeId);
  if (!employeeRecord) errors.push({ field: "employeeId", code: "EMPLOYEE_NOT_FOUND" });

  const stpRegistration = await stpRepo.findRegistration(payEvent.employerId);
  if (!stpRegistration || stpRegistration.phase !== "STP2") {
    errors.push({ field: "employerId", code: "STP2_NOT_REGISTERED" });
  }

  return { valid: errors.length === 0, errors };
}

A validation failure produces an immediate RED routing. The pay run is held. The error is recorded in the audit trail. The operator is notified. No specialist agent is invoked until the pay event is structurally valid.

Once validated, the orchestrator enriches the pay event with data from the employee record: TFN declaration, income type, award classification, fund details, salary sacrifice agreements, and prior-period figures needed for annualised salary reconciliation. This enrichment step ensures every specialist agent receives a complete input set — it does not have to query the HR system itself.

// Enrichment — orchestrator adds employee context
async function enrichPayEvent(
  payEvent: PayEvent,
  employeeRecord: EmployeeRecord
): Promise<EnrichedPayEvent> {
  return {
    ...payEvent,
    tfnDeclaration: employeeRecord.tfnDeclaration,
    incomeType: employeeRecord.incomeType,           // SAW, WHM, CHP, etc.
    awardCode: employeeRecord.awardCode,             // e.g. "MA000002"
    awardClassification: employeeRecord.awardClassification,
    fundDetails: employeeRecord.superFundDetails,
    salarySacrifice: employeeRecord.salarySacrifice,
    ytdFigures: await ytdRepo.getYTD(payEvent.employeeId, payEvent.payPeriodEnd),
  };
}

My take: The enrichment step is where most payroll systems have hidden errors. The employee record is a snapshot from the HR system at the moment the pay run starts. If an award reclassification, a salary sacrifice change, or a fund change is made in the HR system after the snapshot is taken, it will not be reflected in this pay run. The orchestrator must record the snapshot timestamp and flag any employee records that changed between snapshot and payment date for manual review.

Specialist Fan-Out — SG, PAYG, Award, Termination, STP

After enrichment, the orchestrator fans out. The sg-agent, payg-agent, and award-agent run in parallel — they are independent given the enriched pay event. The termination-agent runs only if hasTermination is true. The stp-agent runs last, assembling the STP2 payload from all prior results.

// Fan-out sequence
async function fanOut(enriched: EnrichedPayEvent, engine: PayrollEngine): Promise<FanOutResult> {
  // Parallel: sg, payg, award
  const [sgResult, paygResult, awardResult] = await Promise.all([
    sgAgent.run(enriched, engine),
    paygAgent.run(enriched, engine),
    awardAgent.run(enriched),
  ]);

  // Sequential: termination only if needed
  const terminationResult = enriched.hasTermination
    ? await terminationAgent.run(enriched, engine)
    : null;

  // Sequential: STP depends on all prior results
  const stpPayload = await stpAgent.buildPayload(
    enriched, sgResult, paygResult, awardResult, terminationResult
  );

  return { sgResult, paygResult, awardResult, terminationResult, stpPayload };
}

Each specialist agent produces a typed result with three mandatory fields: a status (GREEN/AMBER/RED), a data payload, and a provenance record. An agent that cannot produce a GREEN result must include a gate recommendation.

// Standard agent result shape
interface AgentResult<T> {
  agentId: string;
  status: "GREEN" | "AMBER" | "RED";
  data: T;
  gate?: { level: "H1" | "H2" | "H3"; reason: string; instructions: string };
  provenance: ProvenanceEnvelope;
  computedAt: string;
}

Example 2.1 (assumed-fact illustration): Priya is employed at Metro Digital Solutions on a fortnightly cycle. Her pay event shows: base salary component $3,800, performance bonus $500, tool allowance $80 (genuine expense-of-employment allowance). The orchestrator enriches the pay event with her SAW income type, tax-free threshold claim, HECS/HELP debt flag, and her industry super fund details. The sg-agent receives her OTE: $3,800 base plus $500 bonus (commissions are OTE — verify applicable award) but excludes the tool allowance (genuine expense reimbursement — not OTE — verify against ATO SG ruling). The payg-agent receives her total taxable earnings: $4,300 (base plus bonus plus allowance). The award-agent checks her rate against the applicable modern award. All three return GREEN. The stp-agent builds the disaggregated STP2 payload: gross $4,300, allowance subtype "tool" $80, bonus component $500, HELP debt indicator true. The orchestrator routes to the STP lodgement queue.

What this illustrates: The OTE calculation and the PAYG gross calculation start from different bases. The tool allowance is taxable but not OTE. The performance bonus is both taxable and OTE. The fan-out design ensures each agent receives precisely the inputs it needs — sg-agent gets OTE, payg-agent gets gross taxable — without either agent needing to know what the other is computing. The orchestrator holds the complete picture.

The Payroll Engine — Deterministic Core

The payroll engine is a deterministic calculation module. It is not an agent. It has no language model component. It is a typed function library that accepts structured inputs and returns structured outputs. Given identical inputs, it always returns identical outputs. This property is essential: it means an ATO auditor can reproduce any historical calculation by supplying the same inputs to the same engine version.

The engine exposes a defined API to agents:

// Payroll engine public API
interface PayrollEngine {
  computeWithholding(inputs: WithholdingInputs): Promise<WithholdingResult>;
  computeSG(inputs: SGInputs): Promise<SGResult>;
  computeETP(inputs: ETPInputs): Promise<ETPResult>;
  computeUnusedLeaveOnTermination(inputs: LeaveTerminationInputs): Promise<LeaveTerminationResult>;
  computeAnnualisedReconciliation(inputs: AnnualisedReconciliationInputs): Promise<AnnualisedResult>;
}

// Engine does NOT expose:
// - Natural language interpretation
// - Award classification
// - Fund routing logic
// - STP payload formatting
// These belong to agents.

The engine is versioned. Every computation result carries the engine version in its provenance envelope. When the ATO updates withholding tables mid-year, a new engine version is released. Historical pay runs retain a reference to the engine version that computed them. This means a pay run from September 2025 can always be reproduced using engine version that was current in September 2025, even if the current engine is a later version.

// Engine versioning — embedded in every result
interface WithholdingResult {
  amount: number;              // dollars, rounded per ATO rounding rules
  tableReference: string;      // e.g. "NAT 1008 — Fortnightly — 2025-26"
  engineVersion: string;       // e.g. "payroll-engine@2.4.1"
  asAtDate: string;
  rounding: "ATO_ROUNDING";   // ATO-specified rounding rules applied
  noOverride: true;
}

My take: Engine versioning is treated as optional by most payroll implementations until the first ATO audit. Then it becomes critical. Build versioning in from day one. The cost of retrofitting an audit trail that references engine versions is disproportionate compared to building it correctly at the start.

The engine applies ATO rounding rules. For PAYG withholding, the ATO specifies that amounts should be rounded to the nearest dollar. The engine does not round differently based on an agent's preference. The rounding rule is encoded in the engine and is not configurable by agents.

// ATO rounding — encoded in engine, not configurable by agents
function applyATORounding(rawWithholding: number): number {
  // ATO rules: round to nearest dollar
  // Where .50 exactly — round up (banker's rounding does NOT apply here)
  return Math.round(rawWithholding);
}
// Agents cannot change this. noOverride applies to rounding too.

Provenance Envelope — Citations and As-At Dates

Every figure the engine produces is wrapped in a provenance envelope that records what was computed, what inputs were used, which rules applied, and when those rules were current. Those four facts are exactly what an ATO auditor asks you to substantiate after the fact — so the envelope captures them at the moment of calculation, not under reconstruction pressure months later.

// Full provenance envelope — complete example
const withholdingProvenance: ProvenanceEnvelope = {
  engineVersion: "payroll-engine@2.4.1",
  tableReference: "NAT 1008 — Fortnightly — 2025-26",
  asAtDate: "2026-06-20",
  inputs: {
    grossTaxable: 4300.00,
    frequency: "fortnightly",
    taxFreeThreshold: true,
    medicareLevyExemption: false,
    helpDebt: true,
    incomeType: "SAW",
  },
  computedAt: "2026-06-20T10:14:33.211Z",
  noOverride: true,
  citations: [
    "NAT 1008 — Tax withheld for individuals (ATO, updated July 2025)",
    "TAA 1953 Sch 1 Div 12 — Withholding obligations",
  ],
};

The audit-trail-agent stores every provenance envelope. The storage is append-only — a provenance record once written cannot be modified. Any correction to a pay run produces a new provenance record that references the original; the original record itself is never mutated.

// Audit trail — append-only, immutable records
interface AuditTrailEntry {
  entryId: string;
  payRunId: string;
  employeeId: string;
  entryType: "ORIGINAL" | "CORRECTION" | "REVERSAL";
  supersedes?: string;         // entryId of the record this corrects
  provenance: ProvenanceEnvelope;
  authorisedBy?: string;       // gate clearance — H1/H2/H3 actor
  createdAt: string;
}

Example 2.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Marcus at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd receives a pay run correction. In the original run, his overtime was incorrectly included in OTE, inflating the SG contribution. The correction run produces a new SGResult with a revised provenance envelope: entryType: "CORRECTION", supersedes: "orig-sg-2026-05-31-marcus". The engine computes the corrected SG amount. The provenance records both the corrected amount and the original. The audit trail now contains two records: the original (which is wrong) and the correction (which supersedes it). Neither record is deleted. When the ATO audits the employer, both records are visible, showing the error was detected and corrected within the same quarter.

What this illustrates: The audit trail is a ledger of every state the system has passed through, not a record of its current state alone. Corrections append to history; they never delete it. An immutable audit trail is the employer's evidence that errors were self-detected and self-corrected, which the ATO distinguishes from errors discovered by audit. The difference can affect the penalty position.

RED/AMBER/GREEN Routing

The RED/AMBER/GREEN routing system governs what the orchestrator does after it receives all specialist agent results. The routing is evaluated across all results — a single RED anywhere produces a RED outcome for the entire pay run. A single AMBER produces an AMBER outcome. Only all-GREEN produces a GREEN outcome.

Status Meaning Orchestrator action
GREEN All agents confident, no issues found Proceed to STP payload generation and lodgement queue
AMBER One or more agents flagged a review item Hold pay run, route to H3 gate, await clearance
RED One or more agents found a blocking error or structural failure Hold pay run, route to H1 or H2 gate (or operator for structural errors), no payment until resolved 
// Routing evaluation — aggregate across all results
function evaluateRouting(results: AgentResult<unknown>[]): RoutingDecision {
  const statuses = results
    .filter((r) => r !== null)
    .map((r) => r.status);

  if (statuses.includes("RED")) {
    const redResults = results.filter((r) => r?.status === "RED");
    const highestGate = resolveHighestGate(redResults.map((r) => r?.gate?.level));
    return {
      level: "RED",
      gate: highestGate,
      findings: redResults.map((r) => r?.gate?.reason),
    };
  }

  if (statuses.includes("AMBER")) {
    const amberResults = results.filter((r) => r?.status === "AMBER");
    return {
      level: "AMBER",
      gate: "H3",
      findings: amberResults.map((r) => r?.gate?.reason),
    };
  }

  return { level: "GREEN", gate: null, findings: [] };
}

// Gate precedence: H2 > H1 > H3
function resolveHighestGate(gates: (string | undefined)[]): "H1" | "H2" | "H3" {
  if (gates.includes("H2")) return "H2";
  if (gates.includes("H1")) return "H1";
  return "H3";
}

Gate precedence matters. If the termination-agent returns RED with an H2 gate (reclassification with tax consequences) and the award-agent returns AMBER with an H3 gate, the orchestrator routes the entire run to H2. The H2 practitioner can also clear the H3 finding. Do not route to two separate gates simultaneously — the higher gate covers the lower.

My take: AMBER should be treated as seriously as RED in practice. I have seen employers clear AMBER findings without actually reading them, then discover six months later that every pay run had an award underpayment that was flagged but ignored. The H3 gate clearance must be genuine review, not a rubber stamp. Build your gate UI to require the practitioner to read the finding text and confirm understanding before the clearance button is enabled.

Example 2.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Nathan is terminated by Adelaide Retail Services. The termination involves both a genuine redundancy and an unused annual leave payout. The termination-agent processes the pay event. The genuine redundancy tax-free amount depends on years of service and the current indexed threshold (verify against ATO — indexed annually, as at June 2026). The engine computes the tax-free component. The termination-agent classifies the ETP type and the leave payout separately. However, Nathan's termination involves a disputed rostered day off balance. The RDO dispute makes the leave balance uncertain. The termination-agent returns AMBER: "RDO balance disputed. Leave payout amount uncertain. H3 gate — practitioner review required before ETP finalisation." The orchestrator holds the entire termination pay run. It routes to H3 with the ETP breakdown, the disputed RDO balance, and the award reference (verify applicable modern award for RDO provisions). The practitioner resolves the dispute with Nathan and HR, confirms the correct RDO balance, and clears the H3 gate. The orchestrator re-runs the termination calculation with the corrected balance. GREEN result. Payment proceeds.

What this illustrates: AMBER gates are an expected outcome for any termination with a disputed element. The orchestrator's job is to surface the uncertainty early, before payment. From there, each component plays its distinct part: the H3 gate provides the mechanism, the practitioner makes the decision, and the engine runs the calculation once that decision is in.

The noOverride Invariant

Under the noOverride invariant, no agent — not the orchestrator, not the stp-agent, not any downstream consumer — may modify a figure produced by the payroll engine. The moment a downstream agent edits a figure, the number you report to the ATO stops matching the one the engine computed and can defend. So the figure travels through the system with its provenance envelope intact, and an agent that disagrees with it escalates and leaves the figure untouched.

The invariant is enforced at the type level. The engine returns results with noOverride: true as a literal type, not a boolean. TypeScript's type system makes it impossible to assign false to this field. Any code that attempts to modify the figure must first copy it — and copying strips the provenance, which produces a type error at the STP assembly step.

// noOverride as a literal type — not configurable
interface EngineResult {
  readonly noOverride: true;   // Literal type — never false, never absent
  readonly amount: number;
  readonly provenance: ProvenanceEnvelope;
}

// Attempting to override fails at compile time
function badAgentPattern(result: EngineResult): void {
  // result.amount = 500; // TypeScript error: cannot assign to readonly
  // result.noOverride = false; // TypeScript error: type 'false' not assignable to 'true'
}

// Correct pattern: escalate, never override
function correctAgentPattern(result: EngineResult, expected: number): void {
  if (result.amount !== expected) {
    // Do not modify result. Raise gate.
    raiseH3Gate({
      reason: "Engine figure does not match expected amount",
      engineFigure: result.amount,
      agentExpected: expected,
      provenance: result.provenance,
    });
  }
}

The invariant extends beyond the calculation. It applies to rounding, to sign (positive vs negative), and to the as-at date. An agent cannot reinterpret an engine figure with a different as-at date. If the pay event uses the June 2026 tables and the agent believes the July 2025 tables should apply, the agent raises an H3 gate and leaves the figure as computed. Reprocessing it under a different date is forbidden.

// Date mismatch — never recompute, always escalate
async function checkAsAtDateConsistency(
  payEvent: EnrichedPayEvent,
  engineResult: WithholdingResult
): Promise<void> {
  if (engineResult.provenance.asAtDate !== payEvent.asAtDate) {
    // This should never happen — but if it does, do NOT recompute
    await raiseH3Gate({
      reason: "Engine as-at date does not match pay event as-at date",
      engineAsAt: engineResult.provenance.asAtDate,
      payEventAsAt: payEvent.asAtDate,
      instruction: "Verify correct table version is loaded. Do not recompute.",
    });
    throw new Error("INVARIANT_VIOLATED: as-at date mismatch — pay run halted");
  }
}
Scenario Agent response (correct) Agent response (prohibited)
Engine figure differs from agent's expected amount Raise H3 gate, halt run, provide both figures in gate payload Substitute agent's figure, proceed silently
As-at date mismatch between engine and pay event Raise H3 gate, halt run, log invariant violation Recompute with "correct" date
Rounding produces unexpected result Verify rounding rule matches ATO specification, raise H3 if inconsistent Apply different rounding rule to adjust output
Engine version is older than expected Check if engine update required, raise operational alert Override result with agent's preferred calculation

Example 2.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Chloe works at Blue Mountain Healthcare. During a fortnightly pay run, the payg-agent receives the engine's withholding result. The agent has an internal consistency check: it estimates withholding using an approximate formula (not the engine) and compares. For Chloe's pay, the engine returns a figure and the agent's estimate is slightly different — a discrepancy explained by the HECS/HELP debt component that the agent's formula does not precisely model. The agent detects the discrepancy. It does not use the estimate. It raises an H3 gate with the explanation: "PAYG withholding consistency check flagged discrepancy between engine output and agent estimate. Engine figure is authoritative. H3 gate raised for practitioner awareness — no recomputation required unless payroll officer confirms engine inputs are incorrect." The practitioner reviews, confirms Chloe's HECS/HELP flag is correct and active, and clears the gate: "Engine inputs verified. Engine figure accepted. HECS/HELP adjustment is correct — agent estimate did not model HECS component." The gate is cleared. The engine figure proceeds unchanged. The agent estimate is discarded.

What this illustrates: The noOverride invariant means the agent's own consistency check cannot override the engine. The check is useful for catching input errors, but when the engine and the estimate differ, the agent escalates and lets the engine's figure stand.

Next: Chapter 03 covers the Superannuation Guarantee — the 12% rate effective 1 July 2025, ordinary time earnings, the maximum super contributions base, quarterly due dates, the SGC penalty, and how the sg-agent computes and validates each SG obligation.

03 Superannuation Guarantee — 12%, the SGC, and Quarterly Due Dates

SG rate from 1 July 2025, the SGC penalty, ordinary time earnings, quarterly due dates, and the sg-agent

The Superannuation Guarantee is the employer's most consequential recurring payroll obligation after PAYG withholding. The rate is 12% from 1 July 2025. The SGC penalty for late payment is non-deductible, includes a nominal interest component, and carries an administration fee of $20 per employee per quarter. The sg-agent owns the OTE calculation, the SG amount, and the fund routing — but every dollar figure is computed by the engine, not the agent. This chapter covers the SG framework completely, from the rate change through to the choice of fund rules for new employees.

SG Rate — 12% from 1 July 2025

The SG rate rose to 12% on 1 July 2025 under the staged increase schedule in s 19 of the Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act 1992 (0.5% annual increments from 10% in FY2021-22 to the 12% ceiling in FY2025-26 — verify legislative reference — as at June 2026). The prior rate of 11.5% applied in FY2024-25. Never state 11.5% as the current rate. Never apply 11.5% to payments made on or after 1 July 2025.

The rate applies to ordinary time earnings, not to gross pay. The distinction matters. An employee earning significant overtime can have a gross pay substantially higher than the OTE base on which SG is calculated. Applying 12% to gross rather than OTE overstates the SG obligation and can — perversely — produce employer overpayments that are difficult to recover from super funds.

// SG rate — never hardcode, always source from rate schedule
interface SGRateSchedule {
  effectiveFrom: string;  // ISO date
  effectiveTo: string | null;  // null means current
  rate: number;           // decimal fraction, not percentage
}

const SG_RATE_SCHEDULE: SGRateSchedule[] = [
  { effectiveFrom: "2024-07-01", effectiveTo: "2025-06-30", rate: 0.115 },
  { effectiveFrom: "2025-07-01", effectiveTo: null, rate: 0.12 },
  // Add future rates here as legislated
];

function getSGRate(asAtDate: string): number {
  const entry = SG_RATE_SCHEDULE.find(
    (r) => asAtDate >= r.effectiveFrom && (r.effectiveTo === null || asAtDate <= r.effectiveTo)
  );
  if (!entry) throw new Error(`No SG rate found for date: ${asAtDate}`);
  return entry.rate;
}

// As at June 2026 — verify against ATO before relying on this rate schedule

The rate schedule must be sourced from the ATO, not hardcoded from memory. The sg-agent calls the engine with the payment date. The engine applies the rate in effect on that date. A payment made on 30 June 2025 uses 11.5%. A payment made on 1 July 2025 uses 12%. A payroll system that uses a calendar-year lookup rather than a financial-year lookup will misapply rates at every year boundary.

My take: The 12% rate is simple. The failure mode is rarely a misunderstanding of the rate. It is applying the wrong year's rate because the system uses a hardcoded constant that was last updated in 2023. Architect the rate as a date-keyed lookup from a source-of-truth table, and update that table whenever the ATO announces a change.

Employer contributions made before the quarterly due date satisfy the SG obligation for that quarter. If an employer pays fortnightly, the contributions accumulate. As long as the total contributions for the quarter are paid by the due date, there is no SGC liability. The sg-agent tracks the contribution position across the quarter and flags any shortfall risk.

// Quarterly contribution tracker — sg-agent
async function checkQuarterlyPosition(
  employeeId: string,
  quarter: SGQuarter,
  currentSGAmount: number,
  engine: PayrollEngine
): Promise<QuarterlyPositionResult> {
  const ytdContributions = await sgRepo.getContributionsForQuarter(employeeId, quarter);
  const projectedOTE = await sgRepo.getProjectedOTEForQuarter(employeeId, quarter);
  const rate = getSGRate(quarter.endDate);
  const requiredSG = engine.computeSG({ ote: projectedOTE, rate, asAtDate: quarter.endDate });

  const remainingRequired = requiredSG.amount - ytdContributions.total;

  return {
    quarter,
    ytdContributions: ytdContributions.total,
    projectedRequired: requiredSG.amount,
    shortfallRisk: remainingRequired > 0,
    remainingRequired: Math.max(0, remainingRequired),
    dueDate: quarter.dueDate,
  };
}

Ordinary Time Earnings — What Is and Is Not Included

Ordinary time earnings (OTE) is the base on which SG is calculated. It sits below gross pay and below total remuneration, and conflating the three is the most common cause of SG underpayment audits.

OTE applies to periods up to 30 June 2026. From 1 July 2026, qualifying earnings (QE) replace OTE as the SG contribution base under Payday Super — see §03.7. The OTE rules in this chapter apply to periods up to 30 June 2026; QE rules apply for FY2026-27 — verify ATO payday-super guidance.

OTE is defined by section 6(1) of the Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act 1992 (verify — as at June 2026) and interpreted through ATO guidance (SGR 2009/2 — verify currency). The core definition: earnings received for ordinary hours of work, calculated before income tax is deducted.

Payment type OTE? Notes
Base salary / wages for ordinary hours Yes Core OTE
Casual loading Yes Casual loading is paid in lieu of leave entitlements — it is OTE
Annual leave payments (ordinary time component) Yes Leave taken during employment — OTE
Commissions Yes Unless commission replaces the ordinary time component entirely — verify applicable award and ATO ruling
Shift allowances (ordinary shift) Yes If the employee's ordinary hours include shift work, the shift allowance is OTE
Overtime payments No Overtime is excluded from OTE — a hard exclusion
Overtime meal allowances No Reimbursement of an expense incurred in relation to overtime — not OTE
Expense reimbursements (genuine) No Tool allowances, travel reimbursements at cost — not OTE if genuinely expense-of-employment
Unused annual leave on termination No Leave not taken — lump sum on termination — not OTE
Workers' compensation payments (total incapacity) No Payments made while employee cannot work — verify against state legislation
Director fees Yes — if director is also an employee Director fees paid to a working director who is also an employee — OTE; verify against ATO SG ruling

The overtime exclusion is the most commonly misapplied. An employer who includes overtime in OTE will overcalculate SG. This is not a minor issue — it creates an overpayment to super funds that cannot be easily recovered, and it cannot be set off against future SG obligations without an ATO-approved calculation adjustment.

My take: The casual loading question catches employers regularly. A casual employee's 25% loading (verify applicable award and NES — as at June 2026) is paid in lieu of annual leave, personal leave, and other entitlements. It is ordinary time earnings because it is paid for ordinary hours. Excluding it from OTE understates the SG obligation. Verify the award for the specific loading percentage applicable to your employees.

// OTE calculation — sg-agent logic
function computeOTE(earnings: EarningsLine[], allowances: AllowanceLine[]): OTEResult {
  let ote = 0;

  for (const line of earnings) {
    switch (line.type) {
      case "BASE_SALARY":
      case "CASUAL_LOADING":
      case "SHIFT_ALLOWANCE_ORDINARY":
      case "ANNUAL_LEAVE_TAKEN":
      case "COMMISSION":
        ote += line.amount;
        break;
      case "OVERTIME":
      case "ANNUAL_LEAVE_ON_TERMINATION":
      case "UNUSED_LONG_SERVICE_LEAVE_TERMINATION":
        // Explicitly excluded — do not add to OTE
        break;
      default:
        // Unknown type — flag for H3 review
        return {
          ote: 0,
          status: "AMBER",
          flagged: [line],
          reason: `Unknown earnings type '${line.type}' — cannot determine OTE inclusion without practitioner review`,
        };
    }
  }

  for (const allowance of allowances) {
    if (allowance.isGenuineExpenseReimbursement) continue; // Not OTE
    if (allowance.relatesOnlyToOvertime) continue;        // Not OTE
    ote += allowance.amount; // Shift allowances, other ordinary-time allowances
  }

  return { ote, status: "GREEN" };
}

Maximum Super Contributions Base

The maximum super contributions base (MSCB) is the cap on OTE above which an employer is not required to pay SG. Contributions on OTE above the MSCB are voluntary. The MSCB is indexed annually by AWOTE (average weekly ordinary time earnings). For FY2025-26 the quarterly MSCB is $62,500 (as at June 2026 — decreased from $65,070 in FY2024-25). Always verify the current figure against the ATO — do not rely on a figure in this book.

The MSCB applies per quarter, not per year. An employer who pays an employee a $500,000 annual salary must check the MSCB quarterly. If the quarterly salary component exceeds the quarterly MSCB, SG is calculated only up to the MSCB for that quarter.

// MSCB check — sg-agent
interface MSCBSchedule {
  financialYear: string;  // e.g. "2025-26"
  quarterlyMSCB: number;  // verify against ATO — indexed annually
}

// DO NOT use hardcoded amounts — always verify against ATO
// The table below is a STRUCTURAL EXAMPLE ONLY
const MSCB_SCHEDULE_EXAMPLE: MSCBSchedule[] = [
  { financialYear: "2025-26", quarterlyMSCB: 62500 }, // Verified FY2025-26 figure — as at June 2026; re-verify against ATO annually
];

function applySGWithMSCB(
  ote: number,
  quarterlyMSCB: number,
  rate: number
): number {
  const cappedOTE = Math.min(ote, quarterlyMSCB);
  return cappedOTE * rate;
}
// VERIFY quarterly MSCB against ATO before applying — indexed annually

My take: The MSCB is indexed. A figure that was correct in July 2025 is not necessarily correct in July 2026. Build the MSCB as a date-keyed lookup, just as you do for the SG rate. If you hardcode the MSCB, you will underpay or overcalculate SG on high-income employees every time the ATO adjusts the figure. The cost of fixing this is always greater than the cost of building it correctly. Verify against ATO — as at June 2026.

Example 3.1 (assumed-fact illustration): James is a senior engineer at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd. His quarterly salary component is above the MSCB (verify against ATO — as at June 2026). The sg-agent receives his OTE for the quarter: base salary plus ordinary-time shift allowance, no overtime. The OTE exceeds the quarterly MSCB. The engine computes SG on the capped amount: MSCB * 0.12. The provenance envelope records: OTE computed, MSCB applied, rate 12%, amount computed on capped base. The agent records the uncapped OTE and the capped OTE in the audit trail so the employer can see both figures. A note is appended: "SG calculated on quarterly MSCB — verify current MSCB against ATO. Employer not required to contribute on OTE above MSCB." The employer may elect to contribute above the MSCB voluntarily — but the SG obligation is satisfied at the MSCB cap.

What this illustrates: The MSCB cap is per-quarter, per-employee. High-income employees can have their SG obligation satisfied at a lower contribution than 12% of total annual salary. The sg-agent must check the cap on every quarter's calculation and document both the uncapped and capped figures in the audit trail.

Quarterly Due Dates — 28 October, 28 January, 28 April, 28 July

SG contributions for each quarter must be received by the super fund by the due date. Not sent — received. If you initiate a payment on 27 April and the fund receives it on 29 April, you have missed the due date. The SGC applies immediately.

Quarter Period Due date ATO SGC Form due
Q1 1 July – 30 September 28 October 28 November (if SGC arises)
Q2 1 October – 31 December 28 January 28 February (if SGC arises)
Q3 1 January – 31 March 28 April 28 May (if SGC arises)
Q4 1 April – 30 June 28 July 28 August (if SGC arises)

Verify due dates against the ATO — if a due date falls on a weekend or public holiday, the ATO typically allows the next business day, but always confirm (as at June 2026).

The sg-agent tracks due dates as hard deadlines. It generates an alert 10 business days before each due date showing: the total contributions lodged to date for the quarter, the projected total required, and the shortfall. The alert is an AMBER output that requires H3 acknowledgement — it does not hold the current pay run, but it flags the upcoming obligation.

// Due date alert — sg-agent proactive monitoring
async function generateDueDateAlert(
  employerId: string,
  quarter: SGQuarter
): Promise<DueDateAlert | null> {
  const tenBusinessDaysBeforeDue = subtractBusinessDays(quarter.dueDate, 10);
  const today = new Date().toISOString().split("T")[0];

  if (today < tenBusinessDaysBeforeDue) return null; // Not yet in alert window

  const allEmployees = await employeeRepo.getActiveForQuarter(employerId, quarter);
  const alerts: EmployeeSGAlert[] = [];

  for (const employee of allEmployees) {
    const position = await checkQuarterlyPosition(employee.id, quarter, 0, engine);
    if (position.shortfallRisk) {
      alerts.push({
        employeeId: employee.id,
        shortfall: position.remainingRequired,
        dueDate: quarter.dueDate,
      });
    }
  }

  if (alerts.length === 0) return null;

  return {
    status: "AMBER",
    gate: "H3",
    reason: `${alerts.length} employee(s) may have SG shortfall before ${quarter.dueDate}`,
    employees: alerts,
    instruction: "Review and ensure contributions reach fund by due date to avoid SGC",
  };
}

SGC — What Happens When SG is Late

If SG contributions are not received by the fund by the quarterly due date, the Superannuation Guarantee Charge (SGC) applies automatically. The SGC is not a penalty that can be negotiated away — it is a statutory obligation that arises by operation of law under the Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act 1992 (verify — as at June 2026).

The SGC has three components:

  • SG shortfall amount: The amount of SG that should have been paid but was not — calculated for SGC purposes on total salary and wages for the quarter, a broader base than the OTE base used for ordinary contributions (SGAA 1992 ss 17–19).
  • Nominal interest: Interest on the shortfall at 10% per annum from the start of the relevant quarter (SGAA 1992 s 49 — verify current ATO position as at June 2026).
  • Administration fee: $20 per employee per quarter (verify against ATO — as at June 2026).

The SGC is non-deductible. A corporation tax deduction is available for SG contributions made on time to complying super funds. The SGC — the charge for late payment — is not deductible. This makes the effective cost of late SG substantially higher than the face amount of the shortfall.

// SGC components — illustrative structure
// VERIFY all figures against ATO before using in production
interface SGCLiability {
  shortfallAmount: number;       // SG not paid on time
  nominalInterest: number;       // ATO-specified rate on shortfall from quarter start
  administrationFee: number;     // $20 per employee per quarter (verify ATO)
  total: number;                 // sum of above
  isDeductible: false;           // SGC is NEVER deductible
  mustBeLodged: true;            // SGC statement required even if paying
}

function computeSGCComponents(
  shortfall: number,
  nominalInterestRate: number,  // verify against ATO — current quarter rate
  employeeCount: number,
  adminFeePerEmployee: number   // verify $20 per ATO — as at June 2026
): SGCLiability {
  const nominalInterest = shortfall * nominalInterestRate;
  const administrationFee = employeeCount * adminFeePerEmployee;
  return {
    shortfallAmount: shortfall,
    nominalInterest,
    administrationFee,
    total: shortfall + nominalInterest + administrationFee,
    isDeductible: false,
    mustBeLodged: true,
  };
}

My take: Employers consistently underestimate the real cost of the SGC because they focus on the shortfall amount and ignore the non-deductibility. If a company in the 30% corporate tax bracket underpays $10,000 in SG, the after-tax cost of the SG if paid on time is $7,000 (the tax deduction saves $3,000). The SGC is $10,000 plus interest plus $20 per employee — with no tax deduction. The effective additional cost of being late goes well beyond the interest and admin fee: the lost deduction applies to the entire shortfall. This should be in every payroll officer's briefing. Verify figures against ATO before presenting to clients.

An SGC statement must be lodged with the ATO even if the employer pays the shortfall before the SGC due date. The statement is separate from the payment. Missing the statement lodgement triggers additional penalties (verify against ATO). The sg-agent flags any SGC situation immediately as a RED with an H1 gate — the SGC statement is formal ATO correspondence.

// SGC detection — immediate RED, H1 gate
async function detectSGCRisk(
  quarter: SGQuarter,
  contributions: QuarterlyContributions
): Promise<AgentResult<SGCAlert>> {
  const dueDate = quarter.dueDate;
  const today = new Date().toISOString().split("T")[0];

  // Missed due date?
  if (today > dueDate && contributions.total < contributions.required) {
    return {
      agentId: "sg-agent",
      status: "RED",
      data: {
        quarter,
        shortfall: contributions.required - contributions.total,
        daysMissed: daysBetween(dueDate, today),
        action: "LODGE_SGC_STATEMENT",
      },
      gate: {
        level: "H1",
        reason: "SG contributions not received by fund by quarterly due date — SGC applies",
        instructions:
          "Registered tax agent must lodge SGC statement with ATO and arrange payment. " +
          "SGC is non-deductible. Do not delay — nominal interest accrues from quarter start.",
      },
      provenance: buildProvenance("sg-agent", quarter),
      computedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
    };
  }

  return { agentId: "sg-agent", status: "GREEN", data: {}, provenance: buildProvenance("sg-agent", quarter), computedAt: new Date().toISOString() };
}

Example 3.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Westfield Logistics has a payroll administrator who goes on sick leave in late January. The Q2 SG contributions (1 October – 31 December) are due 28 January. The payments are not initiated until 30 January. The funds receive the contributions on 31 January. The sg-agent runs its daily SGC detection job. It identifies that contributions were received after 28 January. It immediately returns RED for all 34 employees in that quarter: shortfall amount, day count (3 days past due), and a flag: "H1 gate — SGC liability has arisen. Registered tax agent must lodge SGC statement with ATO. SGC is non-deductible. Nominal interest accrues from 1 October 2025." The business's tax agent is notified. The agent reviews the shortfall, prepares the SGC statement, and lodges it with the ATO. The SGC is paid. The audit trail records the missed due date, the detection, the H1 gate, the tax agent's clearance, and the lodgement reference number.

What this illustrates: The sg-agent does not wait for a human to notice the missed due date. It monitors daily. When the due date passes with contributions unfunded, it raises the RED gate immediately. The SGC amount itself is the engine's to compute. The H1 gate ensures a registered practitioner handles the ATO communication. Speed of detection limits the nominal interest accrual and demonstrates active compliance management.

sg-agent — OTE Calculation and SG Lodgement Check

The sg-agent orchestrates the SG workflow for each pay event and across each quarter. Its responsibilities span two timescales: per-pay-event OTE calculation and SG amount computation, and per-quarter contribution tracking and due date management.

Per pay event, the sg-agent:

  1. Receives the enriched pay event from the orchestrator
  2. Computes OTE using the OTE classification logic
  3. Checks the OTE against the quarterly MSCB (pro-rated if mid-quarter commencement)
  4. Calls the payroll engine with the capped OTE and the as-at-date rate
  5. Records the SG obligation and instructs the fund routing module
  6. Updates the quarterly position tracker
// sg-agent — per-pay-event handler
async function handlePayEvent(
  enriched: EnrichedPayEvent,
  engine: PayrollEngine
): Promise<AgentResult<SGPayEventResult>> {
  // Step 1: compute OTE
  const oteResult = computeOTE(enriched.earnings, enriched.allowances);
  if (oteResult.status === "AMBER") {
    return buildAmberResult("sg-agent", oteResult.reason, "H3");
  }

  // Step 2: check MSCB
  const quarterlyMSCB = await mscbRepo.getMSCB(enriched.asAtDate); // verify against ATO
  const proRatedMSCB = proRateMSCB(quarterlyMSCB, enriched.payPeriodStart, enriched.payPeriodEnd);
  const cappedOTE = Math.min(oteResult.ote, proRatedMSCB);

  // Step 3: call engine — the SG amount is computed here, not in the agent
  const sgRate = getSGRate(enriched.asAtDate); // 0.12 from 1 July 2025
  const sgResult = await engine.computeSG({
    ote: cappedOTE,
    rate: sgRate,
    asAtDate: enriched.asAtDate,
  });

  // Step 4: fund routing
  const fundInstruction = buildFundInstruction(enriched.fundDetails, sgResult.amount);

  // Step 5: update quarterly tracker
  await quarterlyTracker.record(enriched.employeeId, enriched.quarter, sgResult.amount);

  return {
    agentId: "sg-agent",
    status: "GREEN",
    data: {
      ote: oteResult.ote,
      cappedOTE,
      mscp: proRatedMSCB,
      sgAmount: sgResult.amount,
      fundInstruction,
    },
    provenance: sgResult.provenance,
    computedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
  };
}

Choice of fund obligations apply to new employees and require separate handling. An employer must offer eligible employees a choice of super fund. For new employees who do not make a choice, the employer must contact the ATO to identify the employee's stapled fund. If no stapled fund exists, contributions go to the employer's default fund.

// Stapled fund check — new employee onboarding flow
async function resolveSuperfund(
  employee: Employee,
  employerDefaultFund: string
): Promise<FundResolution> {
  if (employee.hasChosen && employee.chosenFund) {
    return { fund: employee.chosenFund, basis: "EMPLOYEE_CHOICE" };
  }

  // No choice made — check stapled fund via ATO
  // ATO stapled fund lookup via online services for employers or API
  const stapledFund = await atoService.queryStapledFund(employee.tfn, employee.employerABN);

  if (stapledFund) {
    return { fund: stapledFund.usi, basis: "STAPLED_FUND", atoReference: stapledFund.reference };
  }

  // No stapled fund — use default
  return { fund: employerDefaultFund, basis: "EMPLOYER_DEFAULT" };
}
// ATO stapled fund query requires employer access to ATO online services
// Verify current ATO process for stapled fund requests — as at June 2026
Fund resolution basis When it applies Employer obligation
Employee choice Employee completes choice form and nominates a fund Contribute to the chosen fund from next available pay cycle
Stapled fund No choice made; ATO stapled fund query returns a result Query ATO at or before the time first SG contribution is due; contribute to stapled fund
Employer default fund No choice made; ATO stapled fund query returns no result Contribute to the employer's default MySuper authorised fund

Example 3.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Mei joins Metro Digital Solutions on 15 June 2026. She does not complete a super choice form within the first pay cycle. On her first pay event, the sg-agent detects no fund choice on record. It initiates a stapled fund query with the ATO (via the employer's ATO online services access — verify current ATO process as at June 2026). The ATO returns a USI for Mei's existing industry fund from her prior employment. The sg-agent records: "Fund resolved via ATO stapled fund query. Basis: STAPLED_FUND. ATO reference: [reference number]. USI: [fund USI]." Mei's SG contribution for the period is directed to her stapled fund. The audit trail records the ATO query date, reference, and the fund USI used. On the next pay cycle, Mei submits a choice form nominating a different fund. From the next pay event, the fund resolution changes to EMPLOYEE_CHOICE and contributions redirect to her nominated fund.

What this illustrates: The stapled fund rules require an active ATO query. The employer must run that query before defaulting a new employee who has not made a choice into the default fund. The sg-agent must initiate this query automatically. The audit trail must record the query and result. A failure to query the ATO and an incorrect default fund contribution is a breach of the SG choice-of-fund obligations.

Example 3.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Adelaide Retail Services runs a monthly pay cycle for its salaried employees. For the Q1 quarter (July–September), the sg-agent computes SG on three monthly pay events: July, August, and September. Each month's OTE is computed separately. The July and August contributions are made to the fund immediately after each monthly payroll. The September contribution is due by 28 October. On 20 October, the sg-agent generates its due date alert: "Q1 SG due 28 October. July and August contributions confirmed received by fund. September contribution not yet lodged. Shortfall: [amount]. H3 alert — action required by 28 October." The payroll officer reviews the alert, initiates the September payment on 21 October, and the fund confirms receipt on 23 October — within the due date. The sg-agent records the fund confirmation. No SGC arises. The audit trail shows: three pay events, three SG computations, three fund lodgements, all received before 28 October.

What this illustrates: For monthly pay cycles, Q1 contains three separate pay events. The sg-agent must track contributions across all three and alert on the cumulative shortfall before the quarterly due date. For monthly payers, SG is a quarterly aggregation of per-pay-event contributions that must collectively arrive at the fund before the due date, not a single payment per quarter.

Payday Super from 1 July 2026 — the End of the Quarterly Rhythm

Everything in this chapter so far describes the law as it applies to quarters ending on or before 30 June 2026. From 1 July 2026, the Treasury Laws Amendment (Payday Superannuation) Act 2025 — passed November 2025, in force from 1 July 2026 — replaces the quarterly rhythm with a per-payday obligation. This book's as-at date is 24 June 2026, eight days before commencement. The quarterly framework above remains the operative law for every quarter up to and including Q4 2025-26, whose contributions are still due by 28 July 2026.

  • Per-payday obligation: from 1 July 2026, SG must be paid on each payday and received by the employee's fund within 7 business days of the payday (verify ATO payday-super guidance — as at June 2026).
  • Qualifying earnings (QE) replace OTE: the contribution base changes from ordinary time earnings to qualifying earnings. Every OTE passage in this chapter applies to periods up to 30 June 2026; QE rules apply for FY2026-27 — verify ATO payday-super guidance.
  • Annual maximum contribution base: the maximum contribution base moves from a quarterly cap to an annual cap from 1 July 2026. Secondary sources quote $250,000–$270,830 for 2026-27 — verify against ATO before relying on any figure.
  • SGC redesigned: the SGC is restructured around per-payday shortfalls rather than quarterly shortfalls — verify ATO guidance on the redesigned charge.
  • Small Business Superannuation Clearing House closes: the ATO's Small Business Super Clearing House closes on 30 June 2026. Employers using it must move to an alternative clearing channel before the first FY2026-27 payday.

The sg-agent design changes accordingly. The quarterly tracker coded in this chapter — checkQuarterlyPosition and generateDueDateAlert — becomes the per-payday equivalent: a per-pay-event due-date tracker that computes each payday's SG obligation, counts forward 7 business days, and alerts AMBER with an H3 gate when a fund receipt confirmation is outstanding inside that window. The engine still owns every regulated figure; the agent still routes, tracks and drafts. What changes is the cadence — due-date tracking moves from four quarterly deadlines to one deadline per pay event.

Transition rule: the quarterly due dates in §03.4 apply to quarters ending on or before 30 June 2026. Q4 2025-26 contributions are still due by 28 July 2026 under the old rules. The first per-payday obligations arise on the first payday on or after 1 July 2026.

Next: Chapter 04 covers PAYG withholding — the ATO tax tables (NAT 1008 series), TFN declarations, the no-TFN top-marginal-rate withholding obligation, and how the payg-agent validates each employee's declaration before calling the payroll engine.

04 PAYG Withholding — Tax Tables, TFN Declarations, and the Calculation Engine

ATO tax tables (NAT 1008), weekly vs fortnightly vs monthly, TFN declarations, no-TFN withholding rate, and the payg-agent

PAYG withholding is the mechanism by which employers collect income tax in real time. Every payment to an employee triggers a withholding obligation. The ATO publishes the tables. The payroll engine looks them up. The payg-agent assembles the inputs, validates the TFN declaration, and hands everything to the engine. It never computes the figure itself, and never bypasses the table. That number comes from the engine, not from the agent.

PAYG Withholding Framework — The Employer's Obligation

An employer's PAYG withholding obligation arises under Division 12 of Schedule 1 to the Taxation Administration Act 1953 (verify — as at June 2026). The obligation is strict: withhold the correct amount, remit it to the ATO on time, and report it via STP2 on each pay event. Failure to withhold the correct amount makes the employer primarily liable — the employee's tax liability is secondary.

The obligation attaches to the payment, not to the employment relationship. A payment to a director who is not technically an employee may still attract withholding. A payment to a contractor who is misclassified as a contractor when they are actually an employee triggers withholding obligations from the date of misclassification. The worker classification question is addressed in chapter 09 — but the payg-agent must flag any payment where the worker classification has not been confirmed as correct (H3 gate — contractor multi-factor test result required).

Withholding amounts are remitted to the ATO via the Business Activity Statement (BAS). The remittance frequency depends on the employer's total annual withholding:

Annual withholding Reporting and remittance frequency Notes
Up to $25,000 Quarterly Small withholder — verify ATO threshold as at June 2026
$25,001 to $1,000,000 Monthly Medium withholder — verify ATO threshold
Over $1,000,000 Paid electronically within 6–8 days of each withholding event Large withholder — verify ATO thresholds

Verify all thresholds against the ATO — as at June 2026. Thresholds can change. The payg-agent does not determine the remittance frequency; that is set at employer registration level. The agent's job is to compute the correct withholding for each pay event. The aggregation and remittance against the BAS is a separate process handled by the accounting function.

STP2 reporting does not replace the BAS. The STP2 report tells the ATO what was withheld on each pay day. The BAS and remittance tells the ATO what was paid to the ATO. The two must reconcile. A mismatch between STP2 year-to-date withholding and BAS remittances is an ATO risk flag that can trigger a withholding audit.

// PAYG withholding reconciliation check — end of BAS period
async function reconcilePAYGWithholding(
  employerId: string,
  bassPeriod: BASPeriod,
  stpData: STPYTDSummary,
  basRemittance: number
): Promise<ReconciliationResult> {
  const difference = stpData.totalWithheld - basRemittance;

  if (Math.abs(difference) > 0.01) {
    return {
      reconciled: false,
      stpYTD: stpData.totalWithheld,
      basRemitted: basRemittance,
      discrepancy: difference,
      status: "AMBER",
      gate: "H3",
      reason: `PAYG withholding reconciliation discrepancy: STP reports $${stpData.totalWithheld.toFixed(2)}, BAS remitted $${basRemittance.toFixed(2)}`,
      instruction: "Investigate before next BAS lodgement. Discrepancy may indicate missed pay events in STP or remittance shortfall.",
    };
  }

  return { reconciled: true, difference: 0, status: "GREEN" };
}

My take: The STP-BAS reconciliation is a blind spot in most payroll implementations. The STP data is handled by payroll. The BAS is handled by accounting. No one checks that they agree until an ATO letter arrives. Build the reconciliation check into the payg-agent's BAS-period closing process. Run it automatically before the BAS is lodged. A discrepancy caught before lodgement costs minutes. A discrepancy caught by the ATO costs weeks and potentially triggers a wider audit.

ATO Tax Tables — NAT 1008 Series

The ATO publishes PAYG withholding tax tables under the NAT 1008 series. Each table covers a specific payment frequency: weekly (NAT 1008), fortnightly (NAT 1008), monthly (NAT 1008). The tables are published for each financial year and updated when the government changes tax rates or thresholds.

The payroll engine maintains a structured representation of the current tables. The engine applies the exact step function defined by the table rather than a formula approximation. The ATO's tables are step functions, not smooth curves. An interpolation between two table rows will produce an incorrect answer.

// Tax table structure — payroll engine internal representation
// This is a structural example — actual table values must come from ATO NAT 1008
interface TaxTableEntry {
  weeklyIncomeFrom: number;    // Weekly gross for which this row applies
  weeklyIncomeTo: number;
  taxFreeThresholdYes: number; // Withholding when TFT claimed
  taxFreeThresholdNo: number;  // Withholding when TFT not claimed
  // Note: this is illustrative structure only
  // Actual table has additional adjustments for HELP, SFSS, Medicare, etc.
}

// Engine table lookup — exact match, never interpolate
function lookupWeeklyWithholding(
  weeklyGross: number,
  table: TaxTableEntry[],
  claimsTFT: boolean
): number {
  // Find the exact row
  const row = table.find(
    (entry) => weeklyGross >= entry.weeklyIncomeFrom && weeklyGross <= entry.weeklyIncomeTo
  );

  if (!row) {
    // Above the table — apply top marginal rate logic
    throw new Error(`Weekly gross $${weeklyGross} exceeds table range — apply top marginal rate rules`);
  }

  return claimsTFT ? row.taxFreeThresholdYes : row.taxFreeThresholdNo;
}
// NEVER interpolate between rows. The table is a step function.
// Always use ATO NAT 1008 as source of truth — verify table year matches payment year.
