The tables are annual. The 2025-26 tables apply to payments made in the 2025-26 financial year (1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026). The 2026-27 tables apply from 1 July 2026. The payroll engine must use the table that corresponds to the financial year of the payment date — not the financial year of the pay period start date. A pay period that spans 30 June / 1 July requires care: the payment date determines which table applies.

// Table year selection — based on payment date
function selectTable(paymentDate: string, tables: TaxTablesByYear): TaxTable {
  const financialYear = getFinancialYear(paymentDate);
  const table = tables[financialYear];
  if (!table) {
    throw new Error(`No tax table loaded for financial year ${financialYear}. Verify ATO NAT 1008 table is loaded.`);
  }
  return table;
}

function getFinancialYear(isoDate: string): string {
  const date = new Date(isoDate);
  const year = date.getFullYear();
  const month = date.getMonth() + 1; // 1-indexed
  return month >= 7 ? `${year}-${year + 1}` : `${year - 1}-${year}`;
}
// Payment on 2026-07-01 → financial year "2026-27" → use 2026-27 table
// Payment on 2026-06-30 → financial year "2025-26" → use 2025-26 table

The tables include adjustments for HECS/HELP debt, Student Financial Supplement Scheme (SFSS), Student Start-up Loan (SSL), and Trade Support Loan (TSL). These adjustments are additive — the base withholding from the frequency table is increased by the applicable debt repayment amount. The payg-agent must pass all active debt flags to the engine.

// HELP/HECS adjustment — additive on top of base withholding
interface HELPAdjustment {
  weeklyGrossFrom: number;
  weeklyGrossTo: number;
  annualRepaymentRate: number; // ATO-specified rate — verify against ATO
  weeklyAdjustment: number;   // Pre-computed weekly addition
}

// Engine computes: baseWithholding + helpAdjustment + sfssAdjustment
// Both are sourced from ATO tables, not derived from formula
// Verify ATO NAT 2185 (HELP/HECS adjustment) — as at June 2026

Example 4.1 (assumed-fact illustration): Robert is now classified as SAW (his WHM visa expired and he obtained permanent residency — see chapter 01). His fortnightly gross is $3,200. He claims the tax-free threshold. He has a HECS/HELP debt. His TFN declaration is on file. The payg-agent assembles the inputs: gross $3,200, frequency fortnightly, TFT claimed, HELP debt flagged. It calls the payroll engine. The engine converts to weekly equivalent ($1,600), looks up the fortnightly table directly (some engines use fortnightly tables directly — the key point is the table is not interpolated), applies the TFT column, then adds the HELP/HECS adjustment for the weekly gross band. It returns the total fortnightly withholding. The provenance records: table reference NAT 1008 Fortnightly 2025-26, as-at date 20 June 2026, inputs used, engine version. The payg-agent records the withholding in the pay event. The STP2 payload includes the withholding amount and the HELP debt indicator.

What this illustrates: The HELP/HECS adjustment is not discretionary. If the TFN declaration shows a HELP debt, the engine must apply the adjustment table. The payg-agent is responsible for reading the TFN declaration correctly and passing all flags. An engine call that omits the HELP flag will under-withhold by the adjustment amount on every pay period — creating a year-end shortfall for the employee that they must cover in their tax return.

TFN Declarations — Tax-Free Threshold, Medicare Levy Exemption, HECS/HELP

A Tax File Number declaration (TFN declaration) is a form the employee completes at the start of employment (or when their circumstances change). The declaration is the employer's authority to apply the correct withholding treatment. Without a TFN declaration, the no-TFN withholding rate applies.

The TFN declaration covers several elections that affect withholding:

Declaration field What it does Withholding effect
TFN provided (or ATO exemption) Identifies the taxpayer to the ATO Normal rates apply; no-TFN rate does not apply
Tax-free threshold claim Australian tax residents can claim a tax-free threshold of $18,200 (verify ATO — as at June 2026) Lower withholding; employee can only claim with one employer at a time
No tax-free threshold claim Resident who does not claim (e.g. second job, or income above threshold) Higher withholding rate — employee must claim the threshold with their primary employer only
Non-resident Foreign residents for tax purposes Non-resident withholding rates apply; no tax-free threshold; verify ATO non-resident tables
Medicare levy exemption Certain visa holders, non-residents, and people who qualify may claim full or half exemption Medicare levy component (currently 2% — verify ATO) is reduced or removed from withholding
HECS/HELP debt Employee has an outstanding HELP, HECS, VET FEE-HELP, or similar debt Additional withholding applied from ATO adjustment table — additive on base withholding
Student Financial Supplement Scheme (SFSS) Employee has an outstanding SFSS debt (legacy scheme) Additional withholding from SFSS adjustment table — additive on base withholding

The employer is entitled to rely on the TFN declaration as long as it appears genuine and the employer has no reason to doubt it. If a new employee claims the tax-free threshold but has already claimed it with another employer in the same financial year, the withholding calculated from the declaration will under-represent their actual tax liability — but the employer is not liable for the shortfall if they relied in good faith on the declaration.

// TFN declaration — typed structure for payg-agent
interface TFNDeclaration {
  tfn: string | null;                    // null if not provided
  isATOExemptFromTFN: boolean;           // e.g. under 18 earning under threshold
  residencyStatus: "RESIDENT" | "NON_RESIDENT" | "WORKING_HOLIDAY_MAKER";
  claimsTaxFreeThreshold: boolean;
  medicareLevyExemption: "NONE" | "HALF" | "FULL";
  hasHELPDebt: boolean;
  hasSFSSDebt: boolean;
  hasSSLDebt: boolean;                   // Student Start-up Loan
  hasTSLDebt: boolean;                   // Trade Support Loan
  declarationSignedDate: string;         // ISO date
  employerReceivedDate: string;          // ISO date
}

// Validation — payg-agent checks declaration before calling engine
function validateTFNDeclaration(decl: TFNDeclaration): ValidationResult {
  const errors: string[] = [];

  if (!decl.tfn && !decl.isATOExemptFromTFN) {
    errors.push("NO_TFN: no TFN provided and no ATO exemption — top marginal rate withholding applies");
  }

  if (decl.residencyStatus === "WORKING_HOLIDAY_MAKER" && decl.claimsTaxFreeThreshold) {
    errors.push("WHM_TFT_INVALID: working holiday makers cannot claim tax-free threshold");
  }

  if (decl.residencyStatus === "NON_RESIDENT" && decl.claimsTaxFreeThreshold) {
    errors.push("NON_RESIDENT_TFT_INVALID: non-residents cannot claim tax-free threshold");
  }

  return { valid: errors.length === 0, errors };
}

My take: The tax-free threshold rule is misunderstood by employees more than by employers. An employee with two jobs who claims the threshold with both employers will under-withhold across the year and face a tax bill. The employer's obligation is to withhold correctly based on the declaration; advising the employee on which job to claim the threshold with is outside that obligation. A payg-agent that detects a possible double-claim (same employee, same ABN claiming period overlap) should flag it as an AMBER for H3 review — not to override the withholding, but to prompt a conversation with the employee before the year-end surprise.

Payment Frequency — Weekly, Fortnightly, Monthly, Quarterly

The ATO publishes separate tax tables for each payment frequency. The engine selects the table based on the payment frequency declared in the pay event. An employer who moves an employee from weekly to fortnightly must update the payment frequency in the HR system before the next pay event. The engine uses whatever frequency is in the pay event — if the frequency is wrong, the withholding will be wrong.

The tables are not identical scaled versions of each other. A fortnightly pay of $3,200 does not yield exactly twice the withholding of a weekly pay of $1,600 — because the step function boundaries in the tables are set independently for each frequency. Always use the table for the actual payment frequency.

// Frequency table selection — payroll engine
type PaymentFrequency = "weekly" | "fortnightly" | "monthly" | "quarterly";

interface FrequencyTables {
  weekly: TaxTable;
  fortnightly: TaxTable;
  monthly: TaxTable;
  quarterly: TaxTable;
}

function selectFrequencyTable(
  frequency: PaymentFrequency,
  tables: FrequencyTables
): TaxTable {
  return tables[frequency];
  // Do NOT scale. Do NOT approximate. Use the table for the actual frequency.
}

Quarterly withholding applies to directors or closely held payees (CHP) who are paid quarterly rather than per pay event. The quarterly table is not the same as four times the monthly table. Quarterly withholding tables are published separately by the ATO — verify NAT 1008 for quarterly variant, as at June 2026.

Mid-cycle frequency changes require care. If an employee moves from weekly to fortnightly mid-quarter, the year-to-date withholding position is relevant. The engine computes each pay event independently using the current frequency. The employee's year-end position depends on the total withholding across all periods. A mid-cycle frequency change simply changes which table is used from the change date forward, leaving prior periods untouched.

Example 4.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Angela at Adelaide Retail Services has been paid weekly. Her employer restructures the pay cycle to fortnightly from the first pay period of July 2026. The HR system is updated. The payg-agent receives Angela's first fortnightly pay event: gross $3,200, frequency now fortnightly, TFT claimed, HELP debt. The engine selects the fortnightly table (NAT 1008 Fortnightly 2026-27 — verify against ATO). It applies the TFT column and adds the HELP adjustment. The withholding result is produced. It is not equal to two times Angela's prior weekly withholding — the fortnightly table has different step boundaries. The payg-agent records both the previous frequency (weekly) and the new frequency (fortnightly) in the audit trail: "Pay frequency changed from weekly to fortnightly effective 2026-07-01. Fortnightly table NAT 1008 applied from this period."

What this illustrates: Frequency changes must be recorded in the audit trail because they change the withholding calculation. If an ATO auditor sees different withholding amounts across periods without an explanation, they will assume an error. The audit trail's frequency change record is the explanation. Because the engine alone produces each amount, the number will differ across frequencies, and the provenance documents why.

No TFN — Top Marginal Rate Withholding

An employee who does not provide a TFN within 28 days of starting employment must have withholding applied at the top marginal rate, including the Medicare levy. The combined rate is 47% — verify against ATO as at June 2026. The 47% combined rate applies to residents; a non-resident who does not provide a TFN is withheld at 45% (no Medicare levy) — verify against ATO. This is a withholding default that ensures tax is collected in the absence of identification, rather than a penalty on the employee. The employee receives a credit for all amounts withheld when they lodge their return.

The 47% rate (verify) applies to the full taxable payment — there is no tax-free threshold, no partial rate, and no negotiation. An employer who withholds less because the employee is unhappy with the rate is breaching the withholding obligation. The employee's remedy is to provide a TFN — not to negotiate the rate.

// No-TFN withholding — engine applies top marginal rate
interface NoTFNWithholdingInputs {
  grossTaxable: number;
  hasNoTFN: true;
  asAtDate: string;
}

interface NoTFNWithholdingResult {
  amount: number;
  effectiveRate: number;      // Should be top marginal + Medicare (47% — verify ATO)
  noOverride: true;
  provenance: ProvenanceEnvelope;
}

// Engine applies top marginal rate — no lookup needed, rate is fixed at top
// VERIFY: 47% = 45% top marginal + 2% Medicare levy (verify ATO — as at June 2026)
// Rate may be higher if temporary budget repair levy applies — always verify ATO
async function computeNoTFNWithholding(
  inputs: NoTFNWithholdingInputs,
  engine: PayrollEngine
): Promise<NoTFNWithholdingResult> {
  return engine.computeNoTFNWithholding(inputs);
  // computed by the engine; the payg-agent never derives this rate itself.
}

The no-TFN obligation ends when the employee provides a valid TFN. From the next pay event after a valid TFN is received, the payg-agent switches to the standard table lookup. The switch is not retrospective — prior pay events withheld at the no-TFN rate remain at that rate. The employee recovers the excess withholding through their tax return.

There are limited ATO exemptions from the TFN requirement — for example, an employee under 18 earning below the threshold may be exempt (verify ATO — as at June 2026). The payg-agent must check for these exemptions before applying the no-TFN rate. An incorrectly applied no-TFN rate over-withholds and can damage the employer's relationship with the employee.

// TFN status check — payg-agent pre-engine validation
async function determineTFNStatus(
  employee: EmployeeRecord,
  declaration: TFNDeclaration | null
): Promise<"VALID_TFN" | "NO_TFN" | "ATO_EXEMPT"> {
  if (declaration?.isATOExemptFromTFN) {
    // Verify exemption conditions: under 18 and below threshold, or ATO-specified exemption
    const exemptionValid = await atoExemptionService.verify(employee, declaration);
    return exemptionValid ? "ATO_EXEMPT" : "NO_TFN";
  }

  if (!declaration?.tfn) {
    // Check if 28-day grace period has elapsed
    const daysSinceStart = daysBetween(employee.startDate, new Date().toISOString());
    if (daysSinceStart <= 28) {
      // Within grace period — still need to withhold correctly
      // ATO allows using TFT rate during grace period if declaration lodged — verify ATO
      return "NO_TFN"; // conservative: apply no-TFN rate; consult ATO for grace period treatment
    }
    return "NO_TFN";
  }

  return "VALID_TFN";
}
// Verify ATO treatment during 28-day grace period — as at June 2026

Example 4.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Wei starts employment at Blue Mountain Healthcare on 1 June 2026. He does not provide a TFN declaration. His first pay is on 15 June. The payg-agent checks: no TFN, no ATO exemption, 14 days since start (within the 28-day window — verify ATO grace period treatment). The agent applies conservative treatment: no-TFN rate. The engine computes withholding at 47% (verify against ATO — as at June 2026) on his fortnightly taxable earnings. The audit trail records: "No TFN declaration on file. Within 28-day grace period. Conservative treatment applied: no-TFN rate. H3 advisory: follow up TFN declaration with employee." An H3 advisory (not a gate hold) is raised for the payroll officer. On 28 June — day 27 — Wei provides a TFN declaration, claiming the tax-free threshold and a HECS/HELP debt. The payg-agent records the declaration receipt. From the next pay event (1 July), the standard fortnightly table applies with TFT and HELP. Prior withholding at the no-TFN rate is not revised. Wei's excess withholding is recovered in his tax return.

What this illustrates: The no-TFN rate is a default that the payg-agent applies mechanically when required. It also tracks the 28-day window and alerts the payroll officer so the TFN is followed up before the grace period expires. The H3 advisory is not a gate hold — the pay run proceeds, because the engine has a valid answer. The advisory is a reminder to resolve the missing TFN before the next period.

payg-agent — TFN Validation and Engine Routing

The payg-agent is the coordinator for PAYG withholding. It sits between the payroll-orchestrator and the payroll engine. Its job is to validate all inputs and assemble the correct engine call — not to compute anything itself.

The payg-agent pipeline for a standard pay event:

  1. Receive the enriched pay event from the orchestrator
  2. Retrieve the TFN declaration for the employee
  3. Validate the TFN declaration (valid TFN, correct residency, valid elections)
  4. Determine TFN status (VALID_TFN, NO_TFN, ATO_EXEMPT)
  5. Classify the income type (SAW, WHM, CHP, etc.)
  6. Compute taxable earnings for PAYG purposes (gross less pre-tax salary sacrifice)
  7. Assemble the engine inputs
  8. Call the payroll engine — receive the withholding result
  9. Verify the provenance envelope (as-at date, table reference, noOverride flag)
  10. Return the AgentResult with GREEN/AMBER/RED status
// payg-agent — full pipeline
async function handlePayEvent(
  enriched: EnrichedPayEvent,
  engine: PayrollEngine
): Promise<AgentResult<PAYGResult>> {
  // Step 1-2: retrieve and validate TFN declaration
  const declaration = await tfnRepo.get(enriched.employeeId);
  const validation = validateTFNDeclaration(declaration);

  if (!validation.valid && validation.errors.includes("NO_TFN: no TFN...")) {
    // No TFN — proceed with top marginal rate, add H3 advisory
    const noTFNResult = await computeNoTFNWithholding(
      { grossTaxable: enriched.grossTaxable, hasNoTFN: true, asAtDate: enriched.asAtDate },
      engine
    );
    return buildNoTFNResult(noTFNResult, enriched);
  }

  if (!validation.valid) {
    // Invalid declaration (not just no-TFN) — RED, H3 gate
    return {
      agentId: "payg-agent",
      status: "RED",
      data: { validationErrors: validation.errors },
      gate: { level: "H3", reason: "TFN declaration invalid", instructions: validation.errors.join("; ") },
      provenance: buildAgentProvenance("payg-agent", enriched.asAtDate),
      computedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
    };
  }

  // Step 3-6: classify and compute taxable earnings
  const tfnStatus = await determineTFNStatus(enriched.employeeRecord, declaration);
  const taxableEarnings = computeTaxableEarnings(enriched.earnings, enriched.salarySacrifice);

  // Step 7: assemble engine inputs
  const engineInputs: WithholdingInputs = {
    grossTaxable: taxableEarnings,
    frequency: enriched.paymentFrequency,
    taxFreeThreshold: declaration.claimsTaxFreeThreshold,
    medicareLevyExemption: declaration.medicareLevyExemption !== "NONE",
    medicareLevyExemptionType: declaration.medicareLevyExemption,
    helpDebt: declaration.hasHELPDebt,
    sfssDebt: declaration.hasSFSSDebt,
    incomeType: enriched.incomeType,
    residencyStatus: declaration.residencyStatus,
    asAtDate: enriched.asAtDate,
  };

  // Step 8: call engine — let the engine produce the number
  const withholdingResult = await engine.computeWithholding(engineInputs);

  // Step 9: verify provenance
  if (!withholdingResult.provenance.noOverride) {
    throw new Error("INVARIANT_VIOLATED: engine result missing noOverride flag");
  }

  // Step 10: return result
  return {
    agentId: "payg-agent",
    status: "GREEN",
    data: {
      withholdingAmount: withholdingResult.amount,
      taxableEarnings,
      tableReference: withholdingResult.tableReference,
    },
    provenance: withholdingResult.provenance,
    computedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
  };
}

The payg-agent has specific handling for WHM employees. Working holiday makers (visa 417 or 462) are subject to a flat rate on the first $45,000 of income per financial year (verify against ATO — as at June 2026). Above $45,000, resident rates apply. The engine tracks the WHM income band across the year and switches rates when the threshold is crossed.

// WHM withholding — rate band management
interface WHMBandTracker {
  employeeId: string;
  financialYear: string;
  ytdWHMIncome: number;
  bandThreshold: number;  // verify against ATO — $45,000 as at June 2026
  belowThresholdRate: number;  // verify against ATO — 15% as at June 2026
}

async function computeWHMWithholding(
  enriched: EnrichedPayEvent,
  engine: PayrollEngine,
  tracker: WHMBandTracker
): Promise<WithholdingResult> {
  const remainingBelowThreshold = Math.max(0, tracker.bandThreshold - tracker.ytdWHMIncome);
  const thisPaymentBelowThreshold = Math.min(enriched.grossTaxable, remainingBelowThreshold);
  const thisPaymentAboveThreshold = enriched.grossTaxable - thisPaymentBelowThreshold;

  // Engine computes each component separately
  const belowResult = thisPaymentBelowThreshold > 0
    ? await engine.computeWHMBelowThreshold({ amount: thisPaymentBelowThreshold, asAtDate: enriched.asAtDate })
    : null;

  const aboveResult = thisPaymentAboveThreshold > 0
    ? await engine.computeWithholding({
        grossTaxable: thisPaymentAboveThreshold,
        frequency: enriched.paymentFrequency,
        taxFreeThreshold: false, // WHM cannot claim TFT
        residencyStatus: "WORKING_HOLIDAY_MAKER",
        asAtDate: enriched.asAtDate,
      })
    : null;

  const totalWithholding = (belowResult?.amount ?? 0) + (aboveResult?.amount ?? 0);

  // Update tracker
  await whmTracker.update(enriched.employeeId, enriched.financialYear, enriched.grossTaxable);

  return { amount: totalWithholding, provenance: buildProvenanceCombined(belowResult, aboveResult), noOverride: true };
}
// VERIFY: $45,000 threshold and 15% rate against ATO — as at June 2026. Indexed annually.
Withholding scenario payg-agent action Engine table used Gate
Standard resident, TFT claimed, no debts Validate declaration, call engine with TFT=true NAT 1008 — applicable frequency — TFT column None (GREEN)
Standard resident, no TFT, HELP debt Validate declaration, call engine with TFT=false + HELP flag NAT 1008 — no TFT column + HELP adjustment table None (GREEN)
Working holiday maker (WHM) Check income band tracker, split payment if crossing threshold WHM rate (15% below threshold — verify ATO); NAT 1008 above None if band tracked correctly (GREEN)
No TFN provided Apply no-TFN rate; add H3 advisory for TFN follow-up Top marginal rate table — 47% (verify ATO) H3 advisory (not a hold)
Invalid declaration (non-resident claiming TFT) Return RED result with validation errors None — engine not called H3 gate — hold pay run
CHP payee — quarterly payment Use quarterly frequency table; confirm CHP income type NAT 1008 quarterly variant (verify ATO) H3 review if quarterly amount is unusual

My take: The WHM band tracker is the most complex part of the payg-agent. WHMs cross the $45,000 threshold during the year — sometimes in the middle of a pay period. The engine must handle split payments. I have seen implementations that apply the 15% rate to the entire year's income without tracking the band crossing, producing a significant under-withholding above $45,000. The tracker is mandatory. Verify the $45,000 threshold and the 15% rate against the ATO before implementing — both are subject to change.

Example 4.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Sasha's employer, Westfield Logistics, has a new employee — James — who is a non-resident for tax purposes. He completes a TFN declaration: non-resident status, no TFT (non-residents cannot claim), no HELP debt, Medicare levy exemption claimed (full, as a non-resident — verify ATO). The payg-agent validates the declaration. Non-resident status is consistent with full Medicare levy exemption claim. TFT not claimed — correct for non-residents. The engine is called with non-resident inputs: it applies the non-resident withholding table (higher than resident rates for the same income — no tax-free threshold built into the rate structure; verify ATO NAT 1008 non-resident variant). The Medicare levy is removed from the withholding (full exemption claimed and valid). The withholding result is produced. Provenance records: NAT 1008 non-resident variant, full Medicare exemption applied, no TFT, as-at date 20 June 2026. GREEN result. The STP2 payload flags the employee as non-resident and includes the Medicare exemption indicator.

What this illustrates: Non-resident withholding requires its own table, not the resident table read at a different row. The Medicare levy exemption for non-residents is structurally different from the resident exemption (non-residents do not pay Medicare levy at all). The payg-agent's validation must correctly parse the residency status and route to the correct engine inputs. The STP2 payload must carry the non-resident and Medicare exemption indicators so the ATO's pre-fill is accurate. Validating the TFN declaration directly determines which withholding table the engine applies.

Next: Chapter 05 covers award compliance — modern awards, the National Minimum Wage, penalty rates, annualised salary reconciliation obligations, and how the award-agent checks every pay event against the applicable award minimum.

05 Award Compliance and the Underpayment Risk

Modern awards, the National Minimum Wage, annualised salary provisions, and the award-agent's compliance check

Every payslip your payroll engine produces sits inside a legal minimum envelope set by the applicable modern award, the National Employment Standards, and — at the floor — the National Minimum Wage. The award-agent checks every payslip against that envelope before it leaves the system. When it finds a gap, it flags it, calculates the shortfall, and routes it to a human gate. The payroll engine owns the figure. The award-agent owns the classification and the flag. A practitioner owns the decision to correct and pay.

Modern Awards — the Source of Minimum Entitlements

There are more than 120 modern awards in Australia as at June 2026. Each governs a specific industry or occupation. Awards set:

  • Minimum hourly rates (by classification level and age)
  • Penalty rates (evenings, weekends, public holidays)
  • Overtime rates (after daily or weekly ordinary hours thresholds)
  • Allowances (tools, uniforms, travel, meals, heat, danger)
  • Leave entitlements (annual leave loading, personal/carer's leave, compassionate leave)
  • Annualised salary arrangement provisions
  • Rostering and engagement rules (minimum shift lengths, rest breaks)

The Award is a legal instrument published by the Fair Work Commission. It is not a guideline. It is not a default. It is the floor below which you cannot pay, regardless of what any employment contract says.

My take: I have seen businesses running for years on a payroll setup that was correct when the award rate was set in 2019 and never updated. The award rate changes every 1 July when the Fair Work Commission hands down its Annual Wage Review. An award rate lookup that was accurate on 30 June is wrong on 1 July. The award-agent must pull the current rate on the date of each pay run — not the rate it cached at system setup.

The first problem the award-agent solves is identification. Which award applies? An employee at a cafe might be covered by the Restaurant Industry Award, the Hospitality Industry Award, or in some cases no award at all if an enterprise agreement has replaced it. The award-agent accepts the employer's registered award mapping and verifies that the classification code on the employee record matches the current award schedule.

Award identification is an H3 gate. The award-agent can narrow the field and flag conflicts, but a practitioner must review before the mapping is locked to a new employee's record.

National Minimum Wage — Changes 1 July Each Year

The National Minimum Wage (NMW) is the absolute floor for adult employees not covered by a modern award or enterprise agreement. It is set by the Fair Work Commission each year and takes effect on 1 July.

Award minimum rates are always at or above the NMW. The NMW is relevant as a cross-check — if an award's minimum rate for a particular classification somehow fell below it (which should not happen in practice), the NMW applies. Always verify the current NMW against the Fair Work Commission's website before relying on any figure. As at the date of publication of this book (June 2026), the Fair Work Commission's 2026 Annual Wage Review determination applies from 1 July 2026 — verify the announced rate before it takes effect.

National Minimum Wage — Key Facts (current as at June 2026 — verify against Fair Work Commission before relying)
Item Detail Verification source
Current rate $24.95/hour ($948.00/week) for FY2025-26 — as at June 2026. From the first full pay period on or after 1 July 2026: $26.44/hour ($1,004.90/week), per the Fair Work Commission 2026 Annual Wage Review decision of 2 June 2026 — verify against the FWC decision text fairwork.gov.au
Effective date 1 July each year (Annual Wage Review determination) Fair Work Commission
Who it covers Adult employees not covered by a modern award or enterprise agreement Fair Work Act 2009
Casual loading The NMW applies to the base rate. A casual employee's minimum includes the casual loading under their award (typically 25% — verify in the applicable award). Applicable modern award
Junior rates Awards set junior rates as a percentage of the adult rate — verify in the applicable award schedule Applicable modern award

The payroll engine receives the NMW and award minimum rates as configuration inputs — verified and version-stamped by the award-agent on 1 July each year. The engine does not infer or estimate rates. If a rate is not in the configuration, the run fails with a MISSING_RATE error.

// award-agent: rate refresh on 1 July
{
  "task": "refresh_award_rates",
  "trigger": "financial_year_start",
  "effective_date": "2026-07-01",
  "awards": [
    {
      "award_code": "MA000009",
      "award_name": "Hospitality Industry (General) Award 2020",
      "source_url": "https://www.fwc.gov.au/documents/awardsandorders/html/ma000009.htm",
      "classification_rates": [
        { "level": "Level 1", "rate_hourly": "verify-against-FWC", "as_at": "2026-07-01" },
        { "level": "Level 2", "rate_hourly": "verify-against-FWC", "as_at": "2026-07-01" }
      ],
      "status": "PENDING_VERIFICATION",
      "gate": "H3 — practitioner must approve rate schedule before use"
    }
  ]
}

The H3 gate fires on every annual rate refresh. No award-rate configuration goes into production without practitioner sign-off, every time.

Penalty Rates and Allowances

Penalty rates are where most underpayments hide. An employee who works a Saturday shift at 150% of their ordinary rate costs 50% more per hour than a weekday shift. If the payroll system applies the ordinary rate and the employee gets paid for 8 hours at the base rate, the underpayment is 4 ordinary-rate hours per Saturday shift — and it compounds over every pay period the error runs.

The award-agent checks penalty rates by reading the shift type from the timesheet record and applying the applicable multiplier from the award schedule. The payroll engine computes the dollar amount. The award-agent never computes the dollar amount — that is the engine's job.

// award-agent: penalty rate lookup
{
  "function": "lookup_penalty_rate",
  "employee_id": "EMP-2241",
  "award_code": "MA000009",
  "shift_date": "2026-07-05",
  "shift_day_type": "SUNDAY",
  "classification": "Level 2",
  "result": {
    "penalty_multiplier": 2.00,
    "rate_source": "MA000009 cl.31.2 — Sunday penalty rate, Level 2",
    "as_at": "2026-07-01",
    "verify": "Confirm against current award clause before relying"
  }
}
// payroll-engine: compute penalty pay component
{
  "function": "compute_pay_component",
  "employee_id": "EMP-2241",
  "component": "PENALTY_SUNDAY",
  "ordinary_rate": "[engine reads from employee rate card]",
  "penalty_multiplier": 2.00,
  "hours": 8.0,
  "result": {
    "gross_penalty_amount": "[engine computed — do not override]",
    "stp2_income_type": "SAW",
    "stp2_disaggregation": "ALLOWANCE_OR_PENALTY",
    "engine_ref": "PE-2241-20260705-SUN",
    "noOverride": true
  }
}

Allowances follow the same pattern. The award specifies when an allowance is payable, the amount (or the method for calculating it), and whether it is included in ordinary time earnings for SG purposes. The award-agent reads the allowance trigger from the timesheet — tool use, laundry, travel — and emits a structured allowance record to the payroll engine.

Common Award Allowance Types — Payroll Engine Treatment (verify against applicable award)
Allowance type Typical trigger OTE for SG? STP2 disaggregation Verification note
Tool allowance Employee required to supply own tools Generally no — confirm in award ALLOWANCE_TD (task/role-based) Verify in applicable award clause
Meal allowance Overtime shift exceeding threshold hours Generally no ALLOWANCE_LD (living-away or domestic) ATO FBT rules may apply if above exempt threshold
Uniform/laundry Required to wear uniform and launder it Generally no ALLOWANCE_TD Verify award amount; ATO exempt threshold applies
Travel allowance Required to travel for work Generally no — confirm nature ALLOWANCE_TD or reimbursement Distinction between allowance and reimbursement is critical — verify
Heat/danger allowance Working in extreme conditions Generally yes — confirm in award SAW (embedded in ordinary pay) Confirm OTE inclusion in award definition

My take: The OTE classification of allowances is the most technically contested corner of SG compliance. If an allowance is OTE, 12% SG applies to it. If it is not OTE, it does not. An award may define OTE differently from the SGAA definition. Never assume — the payroll engine must carry explicit OTE flags per allowance, verified against both the applicable award and the SGAA. When the award and the SGAA conflict, the SGAA governs. This is H3 territory: if you are unsure, route to a registered tax agent before the next super quarter.

Annualised Salary Provisions — the Reconciliation Obligation

Some awards allow an employer to pay employees an annualised salary instead of computing penalty rates, overtime, and allowances pay-period by pay-period. The annualised salary is structured to absorb the expected entitlements. The employer pays a fixed salary that is at least equal to what the employee would have earned under the award rates if all the entitlements were computed individually.

The catch — and it is significant — is the reconciliation obligation. Awards that allow annualised salaries (the Clerks Award, the Professional Employees Award, some others — verify the applicable award) require the employer to reconcile annually: calculate what the employee would have earned under the award and compare it to what they were actually paid. If the actual pay was less, the employer must top up the difference within a set timeframe. That top-up is itself an employment obligation with a due date.

The award-agent manages annualised salary arrangements by maintaining a running accumulator per employee. Every pay period, it records:

  • Hours worked, by day type (ordinary, evening, Saturday, Sunday, public holiday)
  • Overtime hours worked
  • Award entitlement computed by the payroll engine for that period
  • Annualised salary paid that period
  • Running surplus or deficit
// award-agent: annualised salary accumulator update (per pay period)
{
  "function": "update_annualised_accumulator",
  "employee_id": "EMP-3311",
  "employee_name": "Angela Kowalski",
  "employer": "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
  "award_code": "MA000002",
  "pay_period_end": "2026-08-15",
  "period_summary": {
    "ordinary_hours": 76,
    "saturday_hours": 8,
    "sunday_hours": 0,
    "public_holiday_hours": 0,
    "overtime_hours": 4
  },
  "payroll_engine_award_entitlement": {
    "ordinary_pay": "[engine computed]",
    "saturday_penalty": "[engine computed — 150% multiplier, cl.29.3 MA000002]",
    "overtime_pay": "[engine computed — 150% first 2h, 200% thereafter]",
    "total_award_entitlement": "[engine computed]",
    "engine_ref": "PE-ANN-3311-20260815",
    "noOverride": true
  },
  "annualised_salary_paid": "[from payroll run — fortnightly component]",
  "period_surplus_or_deficit": "[engine computed: paid minus entitlement]",
  "ytd_surplus_or_deficit": "[engine running total]",
  "flag": "GREEN — salary is absorbing entitlements this period"
}

Every figure in that accumulator is the engine's to produce. The award-agent reads the structured output and updates the flag. When the YTD deficit passes a threshold the employer configures (for example, more than two periods of expected shortfall), the award-agent escalates to AMBER and the flag routes to H3.

Annualised Salary Reconciliation Failure — Worked Example

Assumed-fact illustration. All figures are illustrative. Nothing here is employment law or tax advice.

Blue Mountain Healthcare employs Priya Sharma as a Level 4 registered nurse under the Nurses Award 2020 (MA000034 — verify applicable award for your situation). Priya is on an annualised salary arrangement approved under cl.20 of the award. Her annualised salary is set at the start of the financial year based on an expected pattern of shifts including some weekend and evening work.

By November 2026, Priya has worked significantly more weekend shifts than the pattern the salary was designed to absorb. The award-agent's accumulator shows a YTD deficit: the award entitlement computed by the payroll engine (weekend penalties at 150% for Saturday, 175% for Sunday — verify current award rates) exceeds the annualised salary paid by a material amount.

// award-agent: annualised salary reconciliation alert
{
  "function": "annualised_reconciliation_alert",
  "employee_id": "EMP-4402",
  "employee_name": "Priya Sharma",
  "employer": "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
  "award_code": "MA000034",
  "ytd_as_at": "2026-11-30",
  "annualised_salary_paid_ytd": "[engine total — all pay periods]",
  "award_entitlement_ytd": "[engine total — all periods, all shift types]",
  "ytd_deficit": "[engine computed: entitlement minus paid]",
  "status": "RED",
  "alert": "YTD deficit exceeds threshold. Annualised salary is not absorbing award entitlements for actual shifts worked.",
  "recommended_action": "Immediate review. If deficit confirmed, top-up payment required. Timeline: verify deadline in applicable award clause.",
  "gate": "H3 — practitioner review before any top-up payment or salary renegotiation"
}

The payroll-orchestrator receives the RED alert and creates a task assigned to the payroll manager with a deadline linked to the award's reconciliation window. No top-up payment is made without a practitioner sign-off that the computed shortfall is correct and the top-up amount has been verified against the payroll engine's output.

What this illustrates: Annualised salary arrangements do not eliminate the award compliance obligation — they defer part of the computation to the annual reconciliation. The payroll engine must run the full award entitlement calculation every pay period and accumulate the deficit. A salary that made sense at the start of the year can become non-compliant mid-year if the employee's actual shift pattern diverges from the assumed pattern.

Underpayment Risk — the Employer's Liability

Underpayment of wages is a serious legal and reputational risk. Under the Fair Work Act, underpayment can result in back-payment orders, civil penalties, and — since 2025 amendments — in serious cases, criminal liability for intentional wage theft. Verify the current state of the law and applicable penalties before acting.

When the payroll engine detects an underpayment in a previous period's run (triggered either by an award rate correction or by an audit of historical payslips), the award-agent computes the gross shortfall per period, per employee. That figure goes into a structured underpayment workpaper.

// award-agent: underpayment detection and workpaper
{
  "function": "compute_underpayment",
  "employee_id": "EMP-1187",
  "employee_name": "Marcus Johnson",
  "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
  "award_code": "MA000004",
  "detection_trigger": "AWARD_RATE_CORRECTION — Level 3 rate incorrectly configured for FY2024-25",
  "periods_affected": ["2024-07-01 to 2025-06-30"],
  "periods_count": 26,
  "payroll_engine_recomputation": {
    "original_gross_paid": "[engine historical recomputation]",
    "corrected_gross_entitlement": "[engine recomputation at correct rates]",
    "gross_shortfall": "[engine computed: corrected minus original]",
    "estimated_interest": "[engine computed — interest rate per Fair Work Act, verify current rate]",
    "engine_ref": "PE-UNDERPAY-1187-AUDIT-20260624",
    "noOverride": true
  },
  "gate": "H3 — practitioner review of recomputation before any payment or ATO/FWC notification",
  "workpaper_status": "DRAFT — not final until H3 sign-off"
}

Interest applies to underpayments. The Fair Work Act provides for an interest component — verify the applicable rate and method with a registered tax agent or employment law adviser. The payroll engine estimates interest but the H3 gate requires a practitioner to verify the interest calculation before the underpayment notice is issued.

Underpayment Liability Framework — Australia (as at June 2026 — verify against current legislation)
Liability type Basis Who is liable Verification source
Back-payment of underpaid wages Fair Work Act — underpayment is a contravention Employer Fair Work Act 2009 — verify current provisions
Civil penalty (employer) Per contravention — serious and repeated underpayment Employer (body corporate) Fair Work Act — verify current penalty units
Civil penalty (individual) Directors, managers who were involved Individual officers Fair Work Act — accessory liability provisions
Wage theft (criminal — from 1 Jan 2025) Intentional underpayment of wage or entitlement Employer; responsible individual Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes) Acts — verify
Interest on underpayment Fair Work Act Employer Verify rate with practitioner

The phrase intentional underpayment in the criminal provisions matters. If an employer is told by the award-agent that an underpayment exists and fails to correct it, that failure has a very different character than an underpayment caused by a misconfigured rate. Keep the audit trail. The workpaper generated by the award-agent and signed off at H3 is evidence that you acted on the flag.

My take: Every employer should run a backward-looking award compliance check over the past three financial years at minimum. Not because they think they have underpaid — because the payroll system's award rate history is almost always less accurate than anyone believes. One incorrect classification, one rate that was not updated on 1 July, one allowance that was mislabelled as non-OTE — these compound silently. The award-agent's retroactive audit tool is the most valuable thing in this chapter.

The Award-Agent — Compliance Check Workflow

The award-agent sits inside the payroll-orchestrator pipeline as a mandatory pre-approval step for every pay run. It does not run as an optional check. If the award-agent is not GREEN, the pay run does not proceed to STP2 submission.

// payroll-orchestrator: award compliance gate in pay run pipeline
{
  "pipeline": "pay_run",
  "employer": "Westfield Logistics",
  "pay_period": "2026-07-01 to 2026-07-14",
  "steps": [
    {
      "step": 1,
      "agent": "timesheet-ingestion-agent",
      "action": "ingest_and_validate_timesheets",
      "status": "COMPLETE"
    },
    {
      "step": 2,
      "agent": "award-agent",
      "action": "check_award_compliance",
      "gate_required": true,
      "gate_level": "H3",
      "status": "IN_PROGRESS",
      "outputs": [
        "per_employee_award_compliance_report",
        "penalty_rate_schedule_applied",
        "allowances_verified",
        "annualised_accumulator_updated",
        "flags: [GREEN, GREEN, AMBER, GREEN, GREEN, GREEN, GREEN, GREEN]"
      ],
      "amber_count": 1,
      "amber_employees": ["EMP-2887 — shift classification ambiguous: ordinary vs overtime boundary unclear"],
      "escalation": "Route EMP-2887 to H3 before proceeding"
    },
    {
      "step": 3,
      "agent": "payroll-engine",
      "action": "compute_gross_pay_all_employees",
      "prerequisite": "award-agent GREEN or H3 cleared",
      "status": "WAITING"
    },
    {
      "step": 4,
      "agent": "payg-agent",
      "action": "apply_withholding_from_tax_tables",
      "status": "WAITING"
    },
    {
      "step": 5,
      "agent": "sg-agent",
      "action": "compute_sg_contributions",
      "status": "WAITING"
    },
    {
      "step": 6,
      "agent": "stp2-agent",
      "action": "generate_stp2_event",
      "status": "WAITING",
      "gate": "H1 — BAS agent or registered tax agent lodges"
    }
  ]
}

The AMBER flag on EMP-2887 shows the award-agent at work. It cannot determine from the timesheet data alone whether the hours after 7.5 ordinary hours are overtime or a different engagement type. Rather than guessing, it flags AMBER and routes to the practitioner with the exact question: is this employee on a set ordinary hours engagement where the additional hours are overtime, or are they on a flexible engagement pattern? The answer changes the award rate applied — and the payroll engine cannot compute the right figure until the classification is confirmed.

Worked Example — New Employee, Wrong Award Level

Assumed-fact illustration. All figures are illustrative. Nothing here is employment law or tax advice.

Nathan Briggs is onboarded at Metro Digital Solutions as a Level 2 employee under the Clerks — Private Sector Award 2020 (MA000002 — verify applicable award). The payroll team enters his classification as Level 2 based on his employment contract, but does not verify the award classification criteria against his actual duties.

At onboarding, the award-agent runs a classification pre-check and returns AMBER:

// award-agent: classification pre-check at onboarding
{
  "function": "classification_precheck",
  "employee_id": "EMP-5012",
  "employee_name": "Nathan Briggs",
  "employer": "Metro Digital Solutions",
  "proposed_classification": {
    "award_code": "MA000002",
    "level": "Level 2"
  },
  "check_result": {
    "status": "AMBER",
    "reason": "Employee role description includes duties consistent with both Level 2 and Level 3 under the award schedule. Specific duties involving [task category] may attract the Level 3 rate.",
    "risk": "Underpayment if Level 2 rate is applied when Level 3 is correct. Overpayment risk is lower but misclassification has compliance implications.",
    "recommended_action": "Practitioner review of role duties against award classification criteria before payroll is run.",
    "gate": "H3 — practitioner review of classification before first pay"
  }
}

The H3 gate fires. The payroll manager reviews Nathan's position description against the award classification criteria and determines Level 3 applies. The payroll engine is updated with Level 3 rates before the first pay run. Nathan is paid correctly from day one.

What this illustrates: The award-agent catches classification errors at the point of hire — the cheapest moment to fix them. An error that runs for two years and is caught in a Fair Work audit costs multiples of an error caught on day one. The pre-check gate exists precisely to interrupt the classification before the first payslip is produced.

Worked Example — Saturday Penalty Rate Not Applied

Assumed-fact illustration. All figures are illustrative. Nothing here is employment law or tax advice.

Chloe Wang works at Adelaide Retail Services as a Level 1 retail worker under the General Retail Industry Award 2020 (MA000004 — verify applicable award). She works every Saturday. For six months, the payroll system has been paying her at the ordinary weekday rate because the timesheet integration was not passing the day-type field to the award-agent correctly. The Saturday penalty rate (150% under MA000004 — verify in current award) was never applied.

// award-agent: retrospective audit — penalty rate gap
{
  "function": "retrospective_penalty_audit",
  "employee_id": "EMP-0884",
  "employee_name": "Chloe Wang",
  "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
  "award_code": "MA000004",
  "audit_period": "2026-01-01 to 2026-06-30",
  "finding": {
    "issue": "Saturday shifts paid at ordinary rate. Day-type field not passed to award-agent — penalty multiplier not applied.",
    "saturday_shifts_affected": 26,
    "hours_per_saturday": 8.0,
    "total_saturday_hours_affected": 208.0,
    "ordinary_rate_applied": "[engine historical rate — from employee rate card]",
    "penalty_multiplier_should_have_applied": 1.50,
    "shortfall_per_hour": "[engine computed: 0.50 x ordinary rate]",
    "total_gross_shortfall": "[engine computed: 208 x shortfall per hour]",
    "estimated_super_shortfall": "[engine computed: 12% x shortfall OTE component — verify OTE inclusion]",
    "engine_ref": "PE-RETRO-0884-PENALTYAUDIT-20260624",
    "noOverride": true
  },
  "gate": "H3 — practitioner review of shortfall before back-payment and SGC assessment"
}

The payroll engine computes the gross shortfall. It also computes the estimated SG shortfall — because if the Saturday penalty is OTE (which it is under MA000004 — verify in the current award), the employer also owes the super on the underpaid component. If the SG shortfall was not paid by the quarterly due date, a Superannuation Guarantee Charge (SGC) lodgement may be required. The H3 gate connects to H2 if the SGC has tax consequences requiring a registered tax agent's involvement.

What this illustrates: A timesheet integration bug that fails to pass day-type is a payroll compliance failure in its own right, beyond being a data quality issue. The award-agent cannot check penalty rates it is not told about. The data pipeline into the award-agent is itself a compliance control — it must be tested, monitored, and alarmed.

Worked Example — Annualised Salary Reconciliation Failure

Assumed-fact illustration. All figures are illustrative. Nothing here is employment law or tax advice.

James Okafor is a Senior Accountant at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd on an annualised salary under the Professional Employees Award 2020 (MA000065 — verify applicable award). His annualised salary was set to absorb up to 10 hours overtime per week on average. In the second half of the financial year, a major project requires James to work consistently above that level — 15 to 18 hours overtime per week for 16 weeks.

The award-agent's accumulator runs the deficit calculation each fortnight. By June, the YTD deficit is significant. The annual reconciliation — due within 14 days after the anniversary date of the annualised salary arrangement under the award (verify timing in the applicable award) — shows James was underpaid.

// award-agent: annual reconciliation report
{
  "function": "annual_reconciliation",
  "employee_id": "EMP-7731",
  "employee_name": "James Okafor",
  "employer": "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
  "award_code": "MA000065",
  "arrangement_anniversary": "2026-06-30",
  "reconciliation_period": "2025-07-01 to 2026-06-30",
  "annualised_salary_paid": "[engine total]",
  "award_entitlement_computed": {
    "ordinary_pay": "[engine total — 52 weeks]",
    "overtime_pay": "[engine total — all overtime hours at award rates]",
    "penalty_pay": "[engine total — any applicable evening/weekend penalties]",
    "allowances": "[engine total — applicable award allowances]",
    "total_entitlement": "[engine computed]",
    "engine_ref": "PE-ANNREC-7731-20260630",
    "noOverride": true
  },
  "deficit": "[engine computed: entitlement minus salary paid]",
  "top_up_required": true,
  "top_up_deadline": "verify against award clause — typically 14 days from anniversary",
  "status": "RED",
  "gate": "H3 — practitioner sign-off on reconciliation figures before top-up payment; H2 if SG shortfall attracts SGC"
}

The payroll-orchestrator creates an urgent task. James receives the top-up payment on the next pay run after H3 sign-off. The SG agent re-runs on the top-up amount — 12% SG applies to the top-up to the extent the underpaid overtime was OTE. If the SG quarterly deadline has passed, the employer must lodge an SGC statement — H2 gate.

What this illustrates: The annualised salary arrangement does not cap the employer's liability at the annual salary amount. When actual work exceeds the absorbed pattern, the employer owes the difference. The reconciliation is mandatory. The award-agent's accumulator turns what would otherwise be a year-end surprise into a visible running deficit that management can act on mid-year — either by adjusting the salary upward or by managing overtime levels.

Award-Agent Configuration — Employer Setup

Before the award-agent can run, it needs a correctly configured employer award mapping. Every employee record must carry:

  • The applicable modern award code (Fair Work Commission MA-number)
  • The employee's classification level under that award
  • Whether an annualised salary arrangement is in place, and if so, the assumed hours pattern
  • Any applicable enterprise agreement that overrides the award (if an enterprise agreement is registered, verify whether it passes the Better Off Overall Test — employment law advice recommended)
  • The engagement type (full-time, part-time, casual)
  • Junior employee status (if applicable — award junior rates apply by age)
// award-agent: employee award mapping record
{
  "employee_id": "EMP-3311",
  "employee_name": "Wei Chen",
  "employer": "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
  "effective_date": "2026-07-01",
  "award_mapping": {
    "award_code": "MA000034",
    "award_name": "Nurses Award 2020",
    "classification": "Registered Nurse Grade 3, Year 2",
    "engagement_type": "FULL_TIME",
    "ordinary_hours_per_week": 38,
    "annualised_salary_arrangement": false,
    "enterprise_agreement_override": false
  },
  "junior_rate_applies": false,
  "rate_card_version": "2026-07-01",
  "rate_card_verified_by": "practitioner sign-off — H3 complete",
  "next_review_date": "2027-07-01"
}

The rate card version date matters. When the Fair Work Commission's Annual Wage Review takes effect on 1 July, every employee's rate card must roll to the new rates. The award-agent manages this as a batch operation — but it does not go live without a practitioner running the H3 gate on the new rate schedule.

// payroll-orchestrator: 1 July rate roll workflow
{
  "workflow": "annual_award_rate_roll",
  "trigger_date": "2026-07-01",
  "steps": [
    {
      "step": 1,
      "agent": "award-agent",
      "action": "load_new_award_rates_from_FWC",
      "source": "Fair Work Commission — Annual Wage Review 2026",
      "status": "PENDING"
    },
    {
      "step": 2,
      "agent": "award-agent",
      "action": "generate_rate_comparison_report",
      "output": "New rates vs previous rates — all award codes in use",
      "status": "PENDING"
    },
    {
      "step": 3,
      "action": "practitioner_review",
      "gate": "H3 — payroll manager reviews rate comparison report and approves",
      "status": "PENDING"
    },
    {
      "step": 4,
      "agent": "payroll-engine",
      "action": "update_all_employee_rate_cards",
      "prerequisite": "H3 approval",
      "effective_date": "2026-07-01",
      "status": "PENDING"
    },
    {
      "step": 5,
      "agent": "award-agent",
      "action": "verify_first_pay_run_post_roll",
      "check": "Confirm new rates applied in first pay run after 1 July",
      "status": "PENDING"
    }
  ]
}

What the Award-Agent Cannot Do

The award-agent is powerful, but its scope is bounded. Know what sits outside it.

The award-agent cannot determine the applicable modern award from first principles if the employer has not configured the mapping. It can flag that no award is mapped and route to H3, but it cannot read a position description and reliably identify the correct award without practitioner input. Modern award coverage is a legal question. Award-agent helps; a practitioner decides.

The award-agent cannot assess whether an enterprise agreement passes the Better Off Overall Test (BOOT). That is H2 territory — a registered tax agent and an employment lawyer are the right resources.

The award-agent cannot determine whether a worker classified as a contractor is actually an employee. The multi-factor test is a legal analysis. The award-agent can flag that a worker's engagement pattern has characteristics consistent with employment (fixed hours, regular pattern, single principal), but the classification itself is H3 at minimum — and H2 if the reclassification has tax consequences including PAYG withholding and SG obligations that were not met.

My take: The award-agent's value lies in making award knowledge systematic rather than in replacing it. Without the agent, award compliance lives in a payroll officer's memory and is as good as their last training. With the agent, award compliance is a documented, version-controlled check that runs on every payslip, every pay cycle, without exception. That reduces underpayment risk materially; it does not eliminate it. The human gates remain because award law is not deterministic. The combinations of award, enterprise agreement, individual flexibility arrangement, and actual hours worked require human judgement at the edges. The agent handles the centre; practitioners handle the edges.

Compliance Check — Putting It Together

Here is the full award compliance check as the award-agent runs it for a single employee in a single pay period:

// award-agent: full compliance check — single employee, single period
{
  "function": "per_employee_compliance_check",
  "employee_id": "EMP-1809",
  "employee_name": "Sasha Petrov",
  "employer": "Westfield Logistics",
  "award_code": "MA000039",
  "pay_period": "2026-07-01 to 2026-07-14",
  "checks": [
    {
      "check": "MINIMUM_RATE",
      "result": "PASS",
      "detail": "Hourly rate on payslip exceeds award minimum for Level 3 — verified against rate card version 2026-07-01"
    },
    {
      "check": "PENALTY_RATES",
      "result": "PASS",
      "detail": "Saturday shift on 2026-07-05 — 150% applied — correct per award clause 28.2"
    },
    {
      "check": "OVERTIME",
      "result": "PASS",
      "detail": "3.5 hours overtime on 2026-07-09 — 150% applied first 2 hours, 200% thereafter — correct"
    },
    {
      "check": "ALLOWANCES",
      "result": "PASS",
      "detail": "Vehicle allowance on 2026-07-11 — amount matches ATO exempt rate for the quarter — non-OTE correctly flagged"
    },
    {
      "check": "CASUAL_LOADING",
      "result": "N/A",
      "detail": "Employee is full-time — casual loading check not applicable"
    },
    {
      "check": "ANNUALISED_SALARY",
      "result": "N/A",
      "detail": "No annualised salary arrangement in place"
    },
    {
      "check": "NMW_FLOOR",
      "result": "PASS",
      "detail": "Award minimum rate exceeds NMW — floor check satisfied"
    }
  ],
  "overall_status": "GREEN",
  "engine_ref": "PE-AWARDCHECK-1809-20260714",
  "workpaper": {
    "generated": true,
    "archived": "audit-trail/2026-07/EMP-1809-award-check-20260714.json",
    "noOverride": true
  }
}

The workpaper is archived. Every GREEN check for every employee for every pay period becomes part of the audit trail. If a Fair Work Inspector asks for evidence of award compliance for the last 12 months, the answer is not "we think we were compliant" — it is a structured JSON archive of every compliance check the award-agent ran, with the engine references attached.

That archive is the difference between a payroll compliance posture and a payroll compliance system.

Next: Chapter 06 covers termination payments — ETPs, genuine redundancy, leave on termination, and the termination-agent's classification workflow. Termination is the highest-risk payroll event: get the ETP classification wrong and the tax on a departing employee's final payment is wrong. The engine computes; the practitioner classifies; the BAS agent lodges.

06 Termination Payments — ETPs, Genuine Redundancy, and Leave Accruals

ETP tax-free components, genuine redundancy tax-free amount, leave on termination, and the termination-agent

Termination is the most complex payroll event an employer will process. Multiple components with different tax treatments arrive simultaneously: accrued annual leave, long service leave, employment termination payments, and potentially a genuine redundancy tax-free amount — each flowing through a separate withholding rule, each reported differently under STP2. The termination-agent classifies; the payroll engine computes; no amount ships without a H3 practitioner gate on ETP classification.

Employment Termination Payments — The Two Types

An employment termination payment (ETP) is a payment made in consequence of the termination of employment. The tax treatment depends on whether the payment is a life benefit ETP or a death benefit ETP. Getting this classification wrong produces incorrect withholding and incorrect STP2 reporting — both of which require amended submissions and potential ATO interaction.

The definitions are in Division 82 of the Income Tax Assessment Act 1997 (ITAA 1997). Before I discuss the engine, understand what the law puts in each bucket.

Life Benefit ETP

A life benefit ETP is paid to a living employee in consequence of termination. It includes:

Payment type Typical scenario Notes
Golden handshake / ex-gratia payment Negotiated exit package above entitlements May be ETP or ordinary income depending on characterisation — H3 gate required
Payment in lieu of notice Employer elects to pay out notice period rather than work it ETP if not contractually required; ordinary income if a contractual entitlement
Compensation for loss of employment Wrongful dismissal settlement, redundancy top-up above statutory minimum Generally ETP — verify with H3 before processing
Genuine redundancy — amount above tax-free threshold Redundancy payment that exceeds the genuine redundancy tax-free amount Excess is ETP, not ordinary income — see section 06.3
Invalidity payment Payment for permanent incapacity before retirement age A tax-free component applies; invalidity segment under s 82-150 ITAA 1997 — verify

My take: Payment in lieu of notice is the most mis-classified ETP I see in practice. Whether it is an ETP or ordinary income turns on whether the contract gives the employer the right to elect payment in lieu. If the contract is silent, a common law analysis applies. That is a legal question. The payroll engine cannot resolve it from a contract field alone. The H3 gate is not optional here.

Death Benefit ETP

A death benefit ETP is a payment made in consequence of a deceased employee's termination. The recipient is a death benefit dependant or non-dependant rather than the employee. The tax treatment differs from life benefit ETPs, and the ETP cap rules differ. The payroll engine distinguishes death benefit ETPs by recipient type and applies the correct withholding rate accordingly.

Death benefit ETPs are relatively rare in a standard payroll run. The termination-agent flags them immediately and routes to H1 — a registered tax agent — before any amount is computed, because the interplay between the death benefit tax-free component, the dependant/non-dependant status, and superannuation death benefit payments can only be safely resolved with full knowledge of the deceased's affairs.

Rule: Life benefit ETP → H3 gate on classification, payroll engine computes. Death benefit ETP → H1 gate before the engine touches it. Once classification is confirmed, the engine computes the amount. It does not own the classification.

ETP Tax-Free Component and the ETP Cap

Life benefit ETPs can have a tax-free component. The component arises from the invalidity segment or the pre-July 1983 segment of a payment. Most modern terminations will not involve a pre-July 1983 segment — that requires an employee whose service began before 1 July 1983. The invalidity segment arises from permanent incapacity payments under s 82-150 ITAA 1997; the pre-July 1983 segment under s 82-155.

For the majority of life benefit ETPs, there is no tax-free component in the Division 82 sense. The tax concession for ETPs comes instead from a concessional rate applied to the taxable component, up to the ETP cap.

The ETP cap is an annually indexed amount — $260,000 for FY2025-26 (as at June 2026). Verify the current cap from the ATO directly — it is indexed under Subdiv 960-M of the ITAA 1997 (CPI-based indexation) and changes on 1 July each year. The payroll engine must be configured with the correct indexed amount for the relevant financial year.

My take: I have seen payroll software that hard-codes the ETP cap and is never updated. An employee terminated in September who receives an ETP processed against last year's cap produces incorrect withholding. Always verify the ETP cap against the ATO's current schedule at ato.gov.au before processing any termination in a new financial year. The arithmetic belongs to the engine — but only if it is seeded with correct inputs.

The taxable component of a life benefit ETP is taxed at concessional rates up to the cap, and at the top marginal rate (plus Medicare levy — verify current rates) above it. The payroll engine applies the ATO withholding rates from the published tax tables for ETPs. You do not manually compute the withholding amount — you direct the engine to apply the correct schedule.

There is a second cap that applies to ETPs: the whole-of-income cap. The whole-of-income cap limits the total of all income in the income year (other income plus ETP) that qualifies for the concessional rate. Once the employee's other income plus the ETP exceeds the whole-of-income cap, any ETP amount above that combined threshold is taxed at the top marginal rate. The whole-of-income cap is fixed at $180,000 (not indexed — s 82-10 ITAA 1997; verify current ATO position).

ETP taxable component amount Withholding rate Note
Up to ETP cap (age-tested) Concessional rate (verify ATO schedule) Lower rate for those at preservation age or older
Above ETP cap Top marginal rate + Medicare levy No concession applies
Whole-of-income cap breach Top marginal rate + Medicare levy Applied to the excess above the combined income threshold

The preservation age test matters for ETPs. An employee at or above their preservation age (currently 60, verify against ATO for future years) receives a different concessional rate than one below it. The payroll engine must hold the employee's date of birth and compute age at the date of payment — not the date of notice or the date the termination was decided.

Worked Example — ETP Cap Application

(Assumed-fact illustration — rates and caps are placeholder; verify all indexed amounts against ATO before processing)

Marcus works at Westfield Logistics and is terminated by agreement. His payment in lieu of notice is correctly classified as an ETP (H3 gate completed; classification confirmed). His other taxable income for the year is $85,000. The termination-agent passes the following payload to the payroll engine:

{
  "employee_id": "WL-0412",
  "employee_name": "Marcus Chen",
  "date_of_birth": "1978-04-14",
  "termination_date": "2026-06-18",
  "financial_year": "2025-26",
  "etp_classification": "LIFE_BENEFIT",
  "etp_type_code": "O",
  "etp_taxable_component": 42000,
  "etp_tax_free_component": 0,
  "other_income_ytd": 85000,
  "etp_cap_fy2026": "verify_ato",
  "whole_of_income_cap_fy2026": "verify_ato",
  "preservation_age_reached": false,
  "h3_gate": {
    "completed": true,
    "practitioner": "James Hooper CA",
    "date": "2026-06-16",
    "classification_confirmed": "PILON_ETP"
  }
}

The payroll engine resolves the age test (Marcus is 48, below preservation age), checks the ETP taxable component against the indexed ETP cap for FY2026, applies the below-cap concessional rate to the component up to the cap, and applies top marginal rate to any excess. It also checks whether Marcus's other income plus the ETP exceeds the whole-of-income cap and adjusts withholding accordingly.

What this illustrates: Once it receives a validated, H3-confirmed classification payload, the engine performs every arithmetic step. The practitioner signs off on the type of payment, and the engine computes the amount. Each step depends on the other.

Genuine Redundancy — The Separate Tax-Free Amount

Genuine redundancy is defined in s83-175 ITAA 1997. A payment is a genuine redundancy payment if the employee is dismissed because the job — not the person — is genuinely redundant. The conditions require: the dismissal is not voluntary, the job genuinely no longer exists, the payment is no more than what an arm's length employer would pay, and the employee is under age 65 at the time (verify current age limit against ATO).

Genuine redundancy produces a separate tax-free amount that is distinct from the ETP tax-free component discussed in section 06.2. The genuine redundancy tax-free amount is calculated as: a base amount plus an amount per completed year of service. Both amounts are indexed annually. Verify the current figures from the ATO — they change on 1 July each year. Do not carry forward prior year amounts.

The structure of a genuine redundancy payment is:

Payment component Tax treatment STP2 reporting
Genuine redundancy amount — up to the tax-free threshold Tax-free; no withholding Reported as lump sum D
Genuine redundancy amount — excess above tax-free threshold Life benefit ETP; subject to ETP withholding rules Reported as ETP
Accrued annual leave paid on redundancy Ordinary income; marginal rate withholding Reported as lump sum A (if unused leave) or in gross
Long service leave component Depends on state and accrual period — see section 06.4 Lump sum B or ordinary income depending on circumstances
Notice (worked or paid in lieu) Ordinary income if contractual; ETP if not — H3 gate Depends on classification

My take: Genuine redundancy is where small payroll teams make costly mistakes. They see "redundancy payment" and assume the whole amount is tax-free. Only the amount up to the indexed genuine redundancy tax-free threshold is tax-free. The excess is an ETP. Annual leave paid on termination sits outside both buckets — it is ordinary income at marginal rates. These three buckets must never be merged.

Worked Example — Genuine Redundancy Calculation Split

(Assumed-fact illustration — all thresholds are placeholders; verify all indexed amounts against ATO before processing)

Angela works at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd. She is made redundant after 9 completed years of service. She is 52. The payroll team asks the termination-agent to classify and split the payment.

{
  "employee_id": "HE-0204",
  "employee_name": "Angela Lowe",
  "date_of_birth": "1974-01-22",
  "termination_date": "2026-06-15",
  "termination_type": "GENUINE_REDUNDANCY",
  "completed_years_of_service": 9,
  "total_redundancy_payment": 75000,
  "annual_leave_accrued_days": 18,
  "annual_leave_daily_rate": 280,
  "long_service_leave_accrued_weeks": 3.4,
  "lsl_weekly_rate": 1350,
  "genuine_redundancy_tax_free_base": "verify_ato_fy2026",
  "genuine_redundancy_tax_free_per_year": "verify_ato_fy2026"
}

The termination-agent computes: tax-free threshold = base + (per_year * 9). It compares this to $75,000. If $75,000 exceeds the threshold, the excess is classified as an ETP and routed through the ETP computation path. If $75,000 is below the threshold, the entire redundancy payment is tax-free.

Annual leave ($280 * 18 = $5,040) is withheld at marginal rates and reported as lump sum A in STP2. Long service leave is processed separately — see section 06.4.

What this illustrates: A single termination event produces multiple payment streams with different tax treatments. The termination-agent must split them before the payroll engine can compute withholding. Once the split is confirmed, the engine calculates each component.

Early Retirement Scheme vs Genuine Redundancy

An early retirement scheme (ERS) is a separate concept to genuine redundancy. Payments under an approved ERS also attract a tax-free amount (same formula as genuine redundancy — verify against ATO). The ATO must approve the scheme. If the employer has not obtained ATO approval, the payment is not an ERS payment regardless of what the termination agreement calls it. The termination-agent checks for an ATO scheme approval reference number before routing to the ERS path. No reference number, no ERS treatment.

Leave on Termination — Annual Leave, LSL, and the Marginal Rate Rule

Leave accruals paid on termination are not ETPs. This is one of the most persistent misunderstandings in Australian payroll. Annual leave and long service leave paid on termination are included in assessable income at the employee's marginal rate. They are not concessionally taxed. They do not go through the ETP cap.

Annual Leave on Termination

Annual leave paid on termination is subject to PAYG withholding at marginal rates. The ATO publishes withholding schedules that apply to unused annual leave paid on termination. These differ from the withholding that applies to ordinary annual leave taken during employment.

Withholding on unused annual leave paid on termination is based on the employee's marginal rate. You must use the ATO's tax tables for unused leave payments — do not apply the ordinary weekly tax table. Verify the correct schedule at ato.gov.au/tax-tables before processing.

Under STP2, unused annual leave paid on termination is reported as lump sum A — type R for genuine redundancy, invalidity or an approved early retirement scheme, type T for other terminations (ATO STP2 employer reporting guide — verify). Long service leave reporting differs — see below.

Long Service Leave on Termination

Long service leave (LSL) is a creature of state and territory legislation. There is no federal LSL statute. Each jurisdiction has its own minimum entitlement, accrual rate, and tax treatment. The tax treatment of LSL paid on termination varies materially by state, and that variation drives real withholding differences.

The general principle under Commonwealth income tax law: LSL taken as leave during employment is ordinary income at marginal rates. LSL paid as a lump sum on termination has special treatment under s83-75 to s83-80 ITAA 1997 for the pre-16 August 1978 period (the 5% rule), with the post-1978 portion taxed at marginal rates. For the vast majority of employees in 2026, the pre-August 1978 period is irrelevant — they had not yet started employment. But verify the employee's commencement date before assuming no concessional treatment applies.

My take: Every state has its own LSL Act: Victoria's Long Service Leave Act 2018, NSW Long Service Leave Act 1955, Queensland Industrial Relations Act 2016, and so on. The accrual rate, portability rules, and payment on termination rules differ. The payroll engine must be state-aware. A payroll system that applies a single national LSL rule is wrong by design. Always verify the applicable state legislation. The engine, not the agent, calculates this — once the applicable state rules are correctly configured.

Leave type on termination Tax treatment STP2 field
Unused annual leave Marginal rate — ATO unused leave withholding table Lump sum A
Long service leave (post-Aug 1978) Marginal rate — same table as annual leave unless pre-1978 service exists Lump sum B (if accrued before 16 Aug 1978) or lump sum A
Personal/carer's leave Not payable on termination under most awards — verify applicable award N/A if not paid; ordinary income if paid
Annual leave loading Included in the unused leave amount; same withholding treatment Included in lump sum A total

Award-governs: the applicable modern award (or enterprise agreement) governs whether annual leave loading is payable on termination and whether specific leave types accrue. The payroll engine must be configured with the correct award. Verify the applicable award using Fair Work Commission resources before finalising any termination payment. The engine does not self-select the award.

Worked Example — Leave Computation at Termination

(Assumed-fact illustration — verify all award entitlements and withholding rates before processing)

Priya is a full-time employee of Blue Mountain Healthcare. She resigns with 4 weeks notice, which she works. At termination she has 22 days of accrued annual leave and 6 weeks of LSL accrued. Her ordinary daily rate is $320 and weekly rate is $1,600. She is employed under the Health Professionals and Support Services Award 2020 (verify applicable award).

{
  "employee_id": "BMH-0317",
  "employee_name": "Priya Nair",
  "termination_type": "RESIGNATION",
  "applicable_award": "HEALTH_PROFESSIONALS_SUPPORT_SERVICES_AWARD_2020",
  "state": "NSW",
  "commencement_date": "2017-03-01",
  "termination_date": "2026-06-20",
  "annual_leave_accrued_days": 22,
  "annual_leave_daily_rate": 320,
  "annual_leave_loading_rate": 0.175,
  "lsl_accrued_weeks": 6,
  "lsl_weekly_rate": 1600,
  "lsl_pre_aug78_weeks": 0,
  "ytd_other_income": 68000
}
// Termination-agent computed split
{
  "annual_leave_gross": 7040,  // 22 * 320
  "annual_leave_loading": 1232,  // 7040 * 0.175 — verify award entitlement to loading on termination
  "annual_leave_total": 8272,
  "lsl_gross": 9600,  // 6 * 1600
  "lsl_pre_aug78_amount": 0,
  "lsl_taxable_at_marginal": 9600,
  "withholding_schedule": "ATO_UNUSED_LEAVE_TABLE",
  "stp2_annual_leave_field": "LUMP_SUM_A",
  "stp2_lsl_field": "LUMP_SUM_A",
  "etp_components": [],
  "note": "Resignation — no ETP. Leave paid at marginal rates. Verify award entitlement to loading on termination before processing."
}

What this illustrates: A straightforward resignation still produces multiple withholding calculations. The termination-agent separates them and flags the award entitlement question for loading before the engine computes final figures. The arithmetic is the engine's to produce; the award governs whether loading is included.

Golden Handshakes and Ex-Gratia Payments

A golden handshake is an ex-gratia payment made to an employee on termination — typically above their contractual entitlements. The tax treatment of a golden handshake depends on its character.

If the payment is made in consequence of termination, it is generally a life benefit ETP. But a payment can be ordinary income if it is characterised as a payment for services rendered (a bonus or commission for past work) rather than compensation for loss of employment. The distinction requires analysis of the facts — the payment amount, the circumstances, the wording of any agreement, and the purpose the employer had in mind.

This is a H3 gate. No golden handshake payment should be processed until a practitioner has confirmed the characterisation. A payroll officer cannot resolve this from the face of a termination agreement. Putting an ordinary income payment through the ETP path produces incorrect withholding and incorrect STP2 reporting. Putting an ETP through the ordinary income path has the same effect.

// termination-agent golden handshake flag
{
  "employee_id": "ARS-0119",
  "employee_name": "Robert Henley",
  "payment_description": "ex-gratia payment per termination agreement clause 7",
  "amount": 55000,
  "classification": "UNRESOLVED",
  "flags": [
    "PAYMENT_IN_EXCESS_OF_STATUTORY_ENTITLEMENTS",
    "NO_CONTRACTUAL_BASIS_IDENTIFIED",
    "EX_GRATIA_LANGUAGE_IN_AGREEMENT"
  ],
  "routing": "H3_GATE_REQUIRED",
