  "h3_reason": "Payment characterisation — ETP vs ordinary income cannot be determined from payroll data alone. Refer to practitioner before processing.",
  "blocked": true
}

The termination-agent blocks the payment and routes to H3. The practitioner reviews the agreement, considers the case law on employment termination payment characterisation [case citation — verify before use], and confirms whether the payment is an ETP or ordinary income. Only after confirmation does the payroll engine process withholding.

STP2 Reporting of Termination Payments

Termination payments under STP2 require precise field mapping. The ATO's STP2 employer reporting guide sets out which payment type maps to which income stream and field. Getting the mapping wrong means incorrect data in the employee's income statement — which affects their tax return prefill, their Medicare Levy Surcharge assessment, and potentially their government benefit eligibility.

The key STP2 fields for termination payments are:

Payment STP2 field Code / type Notes
Life benefit ETP — taxable component ETP taxable component ETP type O or R (verify ATO guide) O = non-excluded ETP; R = excluded ETP — type determines which cap applies
Life benefit ETP — tax-free component ETP tax-free component Paired with type code Zero for most modern terminations absent invalidity or pre-83 service
Genuine redundancy — tax-free amount Lump sum D LUMP_SUM_D Only the amount up to the genuine redundancy threshold
Unused annual leave on termination Lump sum A Type R (redundancy/invalidity/approved ERS) or type T (other terminations) — verify ATO STP2 guide Includes leave loading if award entitlement confirmed
Long service leave (post-Aug 1978) Lump sum A Verify ATO STP2 guide for code Pre-Aug 1978 portion is lump sum B — rare in 2026
Death benefit ETP ETP — death benefit fields Dependant / non-dependant split H1 gate required before any computation

The STP2 submission for a termination event must also include: a cessation date, a cessation type code (V = voluntary cessation, I = ill health, D = deceased, R = redundancy, F = employer initiated dismissal, etc. — verify current codes in ATO STP2 guide), and the final pay period end date. An STP submission missing a cessation type code will fail.

ETP Type Codes — O vs R

Life benefit ETPs are reported with a type code that determines which cap applies:

Type O (non-excluded ETP): the standard ETP cap applies. Type R (excluded ETP): a separate cap rule applies. Excluded ETPs arise from compensation for unfair dismissal, personal injury, harassment, discrimination, or loss of a contingent right. Because an excluded ETP has a different cap, mis-coding O as R (or vice versa) produces incorrect withholding for the entire ETP amount. This is a H3 gate check — the practitioner confirms the type before the engine applies any cap.

// STP2 termination event payload — termination-agent output
{
  "event_type": "TERMINATION_STP2",
  "employer_abn": "51 824 753 556",
  "payee_id": "HE-0204",
  "period_start": "2026-06-15",
  "period_end": "2026-06-15",
  "cessation_date": "2026-06-15",
  "cessation_type": "R",
  "income_streams": [
    {
      "income_type": "SAW",
      "gross": 0,
      "tax_withheld": 0
    }
  ],
  "lump_sum_d": 42500,
  "etp": {
    "type_code": "O",
    "taxable_component": 8500,
    "tax_free_component": 0,
    "withholding": "COMPUTE_VIA_ENGINE",
    "h3_confirmed": true
  },
  "lump_sum_a": {
    "amount": 8272,
    "code": "R",
    "withholding": "COMPUTE_VIA_ENGINE"
  },
  "lump_sum_b": null,
  "super_sg": {
    "applicable": false,
    "reason": "Termination payments (ETP, leave) do not attract SG — verify applicable award for notice period"
  },
  "notes": "Genuine redundancy. Tax-free amount confirmed at $42,500 (verify ATO indexed figure). ETP excess $8,500 type O. Annual leave $8,272. All figures H3-confirmed."
}

Worked Example — Full STP2 Termination Payload

(Assumed-fact illustration — all dollar amounts, thresholds, and rates are placeholders; verify against ATO and applicable award before processing)

Nathan works at Metro Digital Solutions. He is made genuinely redundant after 11 years. His package comprises: a genuine redundancy payment of $68,000, 25 days accrued annual leave at $350/day, 8 weeks LSL at $1,750/week, and a negotiated ex-gratia payment of $20,000 (characterised as ETP — H3 confirmed). The termination-agent constructs the following full payload:

{
  "employee_id": "MDS-0508",
  "employee_name": "Nathan Okafor",
  "termination_date": "2026-06-30",
  "cessation_type": "R",
  "completed_years_of_service": 11,
  "applicable_award": "PROFESSIONAL_EMPLOYEES_AWARD_2020",
  "state": "VIC",

  "genuine_redundancy_payment": 68000,
  "genuine_redundancy_tax_free_threshold": "verify_ato_fy2026",
  "genuine_redundancy_tax_free_amount": "computed_by_engine",
  "genuine_redundancy_etp_excess": "computed_by_engine",

  "annual_leave_days": 25,
  "annual_leave_daily_rate": 350,
  "annual_leave_loading_applicable": true,
  "annual_leave_loading_rate": 0.175,
  "annual_leave_total": "computed_by_engine",

  "lsl_weeks": 8,
  "lsl_weekly_rate": 1750,
  "lsl_total": 14000,
  "lsl_pre_aug78_weeks": 0,

  "ex_gratia_payment": 20000,
  "ex_gratia_classification": "LIFE_BENEFIT_ETP_TYPE_O",
  "ex_gratia_h3_confirmed": true,
  "ex_gratia_practitioner": "James Hooper CA",

  "ytd_other_income": 72000,
  "date_of_birth": "1981-09-03",
  "preservation_age_reached": false,

  "etp_cap_fy2026": "verify_ato",
  "whole_of_income_cap_fy2026": "verify_ato",

  "sg_on_termination_payments": false,
  "sg_on_notice_worked": "N/A_no_notice_period",

  "h3_gate": {
    "completed": true,
    "date": "2026-06-28",
    "practitioner": "James Hooper CA",
    "confirmed": [
      "Genuine redundancy conditions met",
      "Ex-gratia payment characterised as life benefit ETP type O",
      "LSL entitlement confirmed under Vic LSL Act 2018",
      "Annual leave loading payable on termination per award clause 35.5"
    ]
  }
}

What this illustrates: A complete termination event requires classification of four separate payment streams, two H3-confirmed decisions (redundancy genuineness and ex-gratia characterisation), state-law verification for LSL, and award verification for leave loading. The payroll engine computes every withholding figure once those inputs are confirmed. The practitioner owns the classification; the engine produces the arithmetic; each is wholly dependent on the other.

Superannuation on Termination Payments

A common payroll error is computing superannuation guarantee contributions on termination payments. The rules are specific:

ETPs — life benefit or death benefit — do not attract SG. Annual leave paid on termination does not attract SG. Long service leave paid on termination does not attract SG. A payment in lieu of notice worked does not attract SG. A payment for a notice period that is worked does attract SG — because it is ordinary time earnings for that period.

Verify SG obligations for any notice period payment against the ATO's guidance on ordinary time earnings. The payroll engine must correctly exclude termination payment components from the OTE base. An engine that includes ETP amounts in the SG calculation overpays super — and if the error is discovered in an ATO audit, it creates reporting complexity around excess contributions.

My take: SG on termination payments is a binary rule, not a judgement call. ETPs do not attract SG. Leave paid on termination does not attract SG. Worked notice does. The payroll engine should enforce this automatically — if you are manually computing SG exclusions for termination events, you have a gap in your engine configuration.

The Termination-Agent — Classification and Engine Routing

The termination-agent is the classification layer that sits between the raw termination event (an HR system record, a letter of termination, a separation agreement) and the payroll engine. Its job is to read the facts of the termination, identify every payment component, apply classification rules, trigger the appropriate human gate, and produce a structured payload for the engine.

The termination-agent classifies and routes; computing withholding and lodging with the ATO are outside its remit. The payroll engine computes. The payroll-orchestrator coordinates both and manages the STP2 submission.

Termination-Agent Configuration

// termination-agent system configuration
{
  "agent_id": "termination-agent-v2",
  "role": "CLASSIFICATION_AND_ROUTING",
  "inputs_required": [
    "termination_type",
    "termination_date",
    "payment_components",
    "employee_profile",
    "applicable_award",
    "state_jurisdiction",
    "employment_agreement",
    "commencement_date",
    "ytd_income"
  ],
  "classification_rules": {
    "payment_in_lieu_of_notice": {
      "contractual_election_clause": "ORDINARY_INCOME",
      "no_contractual_basis": "PROBABLE_ETP",
      "uncertain": "H3_GATE"
    },
    "ex_gratia_payment": {
      "any_amount": "H3_GATE",
      "reason": "Character of payment requires practitioner determination"
    },
    "genuine_redundancy": {
      "conditions_check": [
        "job_genuinely_redundant",
        "employee_under_65",
        "payment_arm_length",
        "not_voluntary"
      ],
      "all_conditions_met": "GENUINE_REDUNDANCY_PATH",
      "conditions_uncertain": "H3_GATE",
      "conditions_not_met": "ORDINARY_TERMINATION_PATH"
    },
    "death_benefit_etp": {
      "any_case": "H1_GATE",
      "reason": "Non-delegable — registered tax agent required"
    }
  },
  "gates": {
    "H1": "REGISTERED_TAX_AGENT — death benefit ETP, formal ATO correspondence",
    "H3": "PRACTITIONER_REVIEW — ETP classification, redundancy genuineness, ex-gratia character"
  },
  "engine_handoff": {
    "condition": "classification_confirmed AND relevant_gate_cleared",
    "payload_schema": "termination_engine_payload_v3",
    "blocking": true
  },
  "stp2_output": {
    "produces": "TERMINATION_STP2_EVENT",
    "cessation_type_required": true,
    "lump_sum_fields": ["A", "B", "D"],
    "etp_fields": ["type_code", "taxable_component", "tax_free_component", "withholding"]
  }
}

Classification Decision Tree

termination_event
  │
  ├─ DEATH → H1 gate → registered tax agent → then engine
  │
  ├─ GENUINE_REDUNDANCY
  │    ├─ conditions_met → split into:
  │    │    ├─ tax_free_amount → lump sum D (no withholding)
  │    │    ├─ etp_excess → ETP type O path → H3 confirm → engine
  │    │    ├─ annual_leave → lump sum A → engine
  │    │    └─ lsl → state_rules → engine
  │    └─ conditions_uncertain → H3 gate
  │
  ├─ ORDINARY_TERMINATION (resignation / performance / fixed term end)
  │    ├─ annual_leave → lump sum A → engine
  │    ├─ lsl → state_rules → engine
  │    ├─ pilon → contractual? → ordinary income : H3 gate
  │    └─ ex_gratia → H3 gate (always)
  │
  └─ EARLY_RETIREMENT_SCHEME
       ├─ ato_approval_number present → ERS path → split same as redundancy
       └─ no_approval_number → H3 gate

Payroll-Orchestrator Coordination

The payroll-orchestrator manages the sequence. It does not allow the engine to produce an STP2 submission for a termination event unless all required gates are cleared and the termination-agent has produced a confirmed classification payload.

// payroll-orchestrator termination sequence
async function processTermination(employeeId, terminationData) {
  // Step 1: termination-agent classifies
  const classification = await terminationAgent.classify({
    ...terminationData,
    employeeProfile: await payrollDb.getEmployee(employeeId)
  });

  // Step 2: check for blocking gates
  if (classification.blocked) {
    await notifications.sendToQueue({
      queue: classification.routing,  // H1 or H3
      payload: classification,
      urgent: classification.routing === "H1_GATE"
    });
    return { status: "PENDING_GATE", gate: classification.routing };
  }

  // Step 3: once gate cleared (callback or manual confirmation)
  const confirmedClassification = await gates.awaitConfirmation(
    classification.id,
    { timeout_hours: 48 }
  );

  if (!confirmedClassification.approved) {
    throw new Error(`Gate rejected: ${confirmedClassification.rejection_reason}`);
  }

  // Step 4: payroll engine computes all withholding components
  const engineResult = await payrollEngine.computeTermination({
    classification: confirmedClassification,
    financialYear: "2025-26",
    atoCaps: await atoRates.getTerminationCaps("2025-26"),
    taxTables: await atoRates.getUnusedLeaveTable("2025-26"),
    stateConfig: await stateRules.getLSLConfig(terminationData.state)
  });

  // Step 5: produce STP2 event
  const stp2Event = await stp2Builder.buildTerminationEvent({
    engine: engineResult,
    cessationDate: terminationData.terminationDate,
    cessationType: confirmedClassification.cessationTypeCode
  });

  // Step 6: lodge
  await stpLodger.lodge(stp2Event);

  return { status: "COMPLETE", stp2EventId: stp2Event.id };
}

The engine produces the number at Step 4. Steps 1 through 3 are classification and governance. Steps 5 and 6 are reporting and lodgement. The orchestrator enforces that no step can skip over the gate check.

Worked Example — Termination-Agent ETP Classification Block

(Assumed-fact illustration — demonstrates agent blocking behaviour, not specific tax outcomes)

Chloe is dismissed from Adelaide Retail Services. Her termination letter includes a payment described as "consideration for signing a deed of release." The HR system records it as "ex-gratia — $35,000." The payroll team submits the termination event to the payroll-orchestrator.

{
  "employee_id": "ARS-0891",
  "employee_name": "Chloe Renard",
  "termination_date": "2026-06-10",
  "termination_type": "EMPLOYER_INITIATED",
  "payments": [
    { "description": "Unused annual leave", "amount": 4200 },
    { "description": "Consideration for deed of release", "amount": 35000 }
  ],
  "agreement_text": "In consideration of the Employee executing the Deed of Release..."
}

The termination-agent processes this payload. It identifies "consideration for deed of release" as a flag pattern — this language may indicate a payment for personal injury, harassment, or discrimination under s83-170 ITAA 1997 (which would make it an excluded ETP, type R) or it may indicate an ex-gratia payment (type O ETP) or conceivably ordinary income. The agent cannot resolve the character from the description string alone.

// termination-agent response
{
  "classification": "BLOCKED",
  "blocked_components": [
    {
      "description": "Consideration for deed of release",
      "amount": 35000,
      "flags": [
        "DEED_OF_RELEASE_LANGUAGE",
        "POSSIBLE_TYPE_R_ETP_IF_PERSONAL_INJURY_CLAIM",
        "POSSIBLE_TYPE_O_ETP_IF_PURE_EX_GRATIA",
        "CHARACTER_UNRESOLVABLE_FROM_PAYROLL_DATA"
      ],
      "routing": "H3_GATE",
      "reason": "Deed of release consideration: practitioner must determine whether claim involves personal injury, harassment, or discrimination. ETP type O vs R turns on this. Wrong type code produces incorrect cap and incorrect withholding."
    }
  ],
  "cleared_components": [
    {
      "description": "Unused annual leave",
      "amount": 4200,
      "classification": "UNUSED_LEAVE_LUMP_SUM_A",
      "withholding": "ATO_UNUSED_LEAVE_TABLE",
      "cleared": true
    }
  ],
  "stp2_status": "PARTIAL_HOLD",
  "note": "Annual leave can be processed. Deed of release payment held pending H3 gate."
}

What this illustrates: The termination-agent does not guess. When the character of a payment is ambiguous, it blocks and routes. Annual leave — an unambiguous component — is cleared for processing. The uncertain component waits. The payroll engine will not receive the blocked component until the gate is cleared.

Deceased Employee Termination — H1 Gate

When an employee dies while employed, the termination is a special case. Final salary, accrued leave, and death benefit ETPs must be handled carefully. The interplay with superannuation death benefits and the estate means that a payroll team should not process these payments without H1 involvement — a registered tax agent.

The termination-agent immediately blocks and routes to H1 when it detects a cessation type of D (deceased). It classifies nothing, computes nothing, lodges a notification, and waits.

// termination-agent response — deceased employee
{
  "classification": "H1_MANDATORY_HOLD",
  "cessation_type": "D",
  "reason": "Employee deceased — all termination payments require registered tax agent review before processing. Death benefit ETP rules, dependant vs non-dependant characterisation, and superannuation death benefit interactions cannot be resolved at payroll level.",
  "action": "NOTIFY_H1",
  "blocked": true,
  "engine_locked": true
}

This is non-delegable. A payroll officer cannot decide that the estate is simple enough to skip H1. The registered tax agent reviews the position, obtains legal confirmation of beneficiary status where needed, and provides the termination-agent with confirmed classification inputs before the engine is unlocked.

Common Errors and Their Consequences

Termination payment errors are among the most expensive in Australian payroll. Each error has a specific consequence chain:

Error Consequence Remediation path
Treating annual leave on termination as an ETP Wrong STP2 field; wrong withholding rate; employee's tax return prefill incorrect Amended STP2 event; refund or collect difference; H3 review
Not splitting genuine redundancy into tax-free and ETP excess Withholding on tax-free amount (over-withheld); employee must claim refund via tax return Amended STP2; amended payment summary; H3 review
Using prior year ETP cap Wrong concessional rate threshold; possible under-withholding; ATO reconciliation issue Verify cap, recompute, amended event
ETP type O coded as type R (or vice versa) Wrong cap applied; incorrect withholding; ATO may assess penalties for incorrect reporting H3 review, corrected classification, amended STP2
Including ETP amounts in SG calculation Excess SG contribution; possible breach of employee's concessional contribution cap Consult superannuation fund; adjust future contributions; document
Applying single national LSL rule across all states Wrong accrual, wrong entitlement, wrong withholding in some states State-by-state audit; correct LSL configuration in payroll engine
Processing death benefit ETP without H1 gate Potential incorrect characterisation of dependant status; serious tax consequences for beneficiary Registered tax agent review; amended processing; possible estate involvement

My take: The cost of getting termination payments right is a 30-minute H3 practitioner review on any case with uncertainty. The cost of getting them wrong is an amended STP2 event, an employee who has to deal with an incorrect tax return, a possible ATO audit inquiry, and the reputational damage of a mistake on someone's last day of employment. The termination-agent's job is to make the 30-minute review inevitable for uncertain cases — not optional.

Configuring the Payroll Engine for Termination — Rate and Threshold Management

The payroll engine requires correct configuration for each financial year before it can process terminations. Configuration is a recurring obligation. Every 1 July, the following must be verified and updated:

ETP cap (life benefit) — indexed annually, verify at ato.gov.au. Genuine redundancy tax-free base and per-year amounts — indexed annually, verify at ato.gov.au. Whole-of-income cap — indexed annually, verify at ato.gov.au. ATO unused leave withholding tables — published with annual tax tables update. STP2 cessation type codes and ETP type codes — verify against current ATO STP2 employer reporting guide after any ATO system update.

// Annual rate configuration update — payroll engine
{
  "financial_year": "2025-26",
  "effective_date": "2025-07-01",
  "termination_config": {
    "etp_cap_life_benefit": {
      "value": "VERIFY_ATO_FY2026",
      "source": "ato.gov.au/super/key-superannuation-rates-and-thresholds",
      "last_verified": "2025-07-01",
      "next_review": "2026-07-01"
    },
    "genuine_redundancy_tax_free": {
      "base_amount": "VERIFY_ATO_FY2026",
      "per_year_service": "VERIFY_ATO_FY2026",
      "source": "ato.gov.au/employment-termination-payments",
      "last_verified": "2025-07-01"
    },
    "whole_of_income_cap": {
      "value": "VERIFY_ATO_FY2026",
      "source": "ato.gov.au/employment-termination-payments",
      "last_verified": "2025-07-01"
    },
    "preservation_age": {
      "value": 60,
      "source": "s995-1 ITAA 1997 and SIS Regs",
      "note": "Verify — transitional rules applied for those born before 1 July 1964"
    },
    "concessional_withholding_rates": {
      "below_preservation_age": "VERIFY_ATO_ETP_TAX_TABLE",
      "at_or_above_preservation_age": "VERIFY_ATO_ETP_TAX_TABLE",
      "above_cap": "TOP_MARGINAL_RATE_PLUS_MEDICARE"
    }
  }
}

The "VERIFY_ATO" placeholder is intentional in my examples. I deliberately do not publish specific dollar figures for indexed thresholds in this book — they will be out of date by the time you read this. Go to ato.gov.au. Check the current financial year's schedule. Seed the engine with the correct figures. Document the date you verified them. Do this on 1 July of every year.

Worked Example — Engine Rate Validation Check

(Assumed-fact illustration — demonstrates rate validation pattern, not specific rates)

Mei is the payroll manager at Westfield Logistics. On 2 July 2025 she runs the payroll engine's annual rate validation check:

// Payroll engine rate validation — FY2026
{
  "validation_run": "2025-07-02T09:00:00+10:00",
  "operator": "Mei Zhang",
  "checks": [
    {
      "item": "ETP cap — life benefit",
      "configured_value": 245000,
      "ato_published_value": 260000,
      "status": "REQUIRES_VERIFICATION",
      "action": "Mei to confirm against ATO FY2026 schedule and update"
    },
    {
      "item": "Genuine redundancy base amount",
      "configured_value": 12524,
      "ato_published_value": 13100,
      "status": "REQUIRES_VERIFICATION",
      "action": "Mei to confirm and update before first FY2026 termination"
    },
    {
      "item": "Genuine redundancy per year of service",
      "configured_value": 6264,
      "ato_published_value": 6552,
      "status": "REQUIRES_VERIFICATION",
      "action": "Mei to confirm and update"
    },
    {
      "item": "SG rate",
      "configured_value": 0.12,
      "ato_published_value": 0.12,
      "status": "VERIFIED",
      "note": "12% from 1 July 2025 — confirmed"
    }
  ],
  "termination_processing_locked": true,
  "unlock_condition": "All REQUIRES_VERIFICATION items confirmed by operator"
}

What this illustrates: The engine locks termination processing until the operator confirms that indexed thresholds have been verified against the ATO's current schedule. It does not carry forward last year's values silently. Silence is not confirmation. The engine produces the figure — but only once Mei confirms the inputs.

Next: Chapter 07 covers salary packaging — salary sacrifice to super, reportable employer super contributions, FBT interaction, and the salary-packaging-agent.

07 Salary Packaging and Reportable Super Contributions

Pre-tax salary sacrifice, FBT interaction, reportable employer super contributions, and the salary-packaging-agent

Salary packaging sits at the intersection of income tax, superannuation, and fringe benefits tax — three separate legislative regimes that must be reconciled inside a single pay period. Get any one of them wrong and you corrupt the employee's income statement, misstate their adjusted taxable income, and potentially expose the employer to FBT liability they did not price in. The salary-packaging-agent exists to route every sacrifice dollar to the correct STP2 field and to make the downstream consequences computable. Every dollar amount, though, is the engine's to produce — never the agent's.

Salary Sacrifice Mechanics — Pre-Tax Reduction

Salary sacrifice is a contractual arrangement between employer and employee. The employee agrees to forgo a portion of their future entitlement to salary or wages in exchange for the employer providing something of equivalent value — typically superannuation contributions or non-cash benefits. The arrangement must be in place before the entitlement arises. You cannot retrospectively salary sacrifice money the employee has already earned.

The tax consequence of a valid salary sacrifice arrangement is that the sacrificed amount is not included in the employee's assessable income. The employer pays the agreed benefit from pre-tax dollars. For super, those dollars then enter the super fund as concessional contributions and are taxed at 15% inside the fund rather than at the employee's marginal rate. For employees in the 30% or higher brackets, that differential is the core of the value proposition.

Three conditions must hold for the arrangement to be valid:

  1. The arrangement is in writing, executed before the relevant pay period commences.
  2. The amount foregone is a genuine reduction in the employee's ordinary time earnings or other remuneration entitlement — not a reclassification of an existing obligation.
  3. The benefit provided is something the employer has legal authority to provide and is not already owed as cash wages.

Award-governs: Some awards and enterprise agreements restrict salary sacrifice arrangements or impose conditions on how the base rate is maintained. Always verify the applicable modern award or enterprise agreement before implementing a salary sacrifice arrangement for an award-covered employee. The payroll-packaging-agent flags this check — it does not resolve it.

In the payroll system, salary sacrifice is modelled as a pre-tax deduction that reduces the gross taxable income used for PAYG withholding calculation. The SG base calculation is separate — and this is a point where errors are common.

SG Base and Salary Sacrifice — the Legislative Change

From 1 January 2020, amendments to the Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act 1992 removed the ability for employers to count salary-sacrificed super contributions toward their SG obligation. The employer's 12% SG obligation (as at 1 July 2025 — verify against ATO) is calculated on the ordinary time earnings (OTE) base before the salary sacrifice reduction. This is called the "salary sacrifice base" requirement.

My take: I have seen implementations that reduce the OTE base by the salary sacrifice amount before applying the SG rate. That was the old world. In the current world, if an employee earns $8,000/month in OTE and sacrifices $1,000/month to super, the employer's SG obligation is 12% of $8,000 ($960) — not 12% of $7,000. The salary-packaging-agent enforces this. If your payroll system still uses the pre-2020 logic, you have an underpaid SG liability accumulating every pay cycle.

The sg-agent receives the OTE figure from the payroll engine before any salary sacrifice reduction is applied. It then calculates SG independently. The salary-packaging-agent receives both the raw OTE and the salary sacrifice election and computes the net taxable income for PAYG purposes. These two agents operate on different inputs for good reason.

Every salary sacrifice arrangement is captured as a structured document before it takes effect. The arrangement document is the authoritative record — it drives the agent's computation for the life of the arrangement.

// Salary sacrifice arrangement document — super sacrifice
// Written before the first pay period to which it applies
{
  "arrangementRef": "SSA-BM-0041-2026",
  "employeeId": "EMP-0041",
  "employer": "Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd",
  "effectiveDate": "2026-07-01",
  "expiryDate": null,
  "type": "super_sacrifice",
  "amount": {
    "value": 100000,
    "unit": "cents",
    "frequency": "monthly"
  },
  "benefitDetail": {
    "category": "super",
    "fundUSI": "STA0100AU",
    "memberNumber": "BM-04192"
  },
  "stpFieldAssignment": "SalarySacrificeType.S",
  "sgBaseProtected": true,
  "reviewRequired": {
    "awardCheck": true,
    "awardCode": "MA000001",
    "h3Cleared": false,
    "h3ClearedBy": null,
    "h3ClearedDate": null
  },
  "createdAt": "2026-06-18T09:14:00+10:00",
  "createdBy": "payroll-orchestrator",
  "engineNote": "Every figure from this arrangement is computed by the engine; the agent only routes."
}

Salary Sacrifice to Super — Concessional Contributions

Salary-sacrificed amounts directed to superannuation are classified as concessional contributions — contributions made from pre-tax dollars for which a tax deduction has been claimed (by the employer) or which are excluded from the employee's assessable income. The concessional contributions cap limits how much can be contributed in this tax-favoured way each financial year. Verify the current cap against ATO — it is indexed annually: it was $30,000 for 2024-25, remains $30,000 for 2025-26, and rises to $32,500 from 1 July 2026.

Concessional contributions include:

  • Compulsory employer SG contributions (12% as at 1 July 2025 — verify against ATO)
  • Salary-sacrificed amounts directed to super
  • Personal contributions for which the individual has lodged a notice of intent to claim a deduction

The payroll engine tracks only the first two. The salary-packaging-agent is responsible for calculating how much of the salary sacrifice election fits within the cap given the SG contributions already projected for the year. It does not guarantee cap compliance — that is the employee's responsibility — but it surfaces a warning when the combined figure approaches or exceeds the threshold.

Worked Example A — Super Sacrifice within Cap

Assumed-fact illustration. Angela works at Blue Mountain Healthcare. Her annual base salary is $110,000. She has elected to sacrifice $12,000/year ($1,000/month) to super. SG rate is 12%. Her monthly OTE is $9,166.67.

SG contribution (monthly): 12% × $9,166.67 = $1,100. Annualised SG: $13,200. Salary sacrifice super: $12,000. Total projected concessional contributions: $25,200. The current cap is $30,000 (verify against ATO — indexed annually). Angela is within the cap by $4,800. No warning is generated.

For PAYG purposes, Angela's monthly taxable income is $9,166.67 − $1,000 = $8,166.67. Withholding is calculated on $8,166.67 using the ATO tax tables — not on $9,166.67. This is the pre-tax reduction that delivers Angela's marginal rate saving.

What this illustrates: The SG base ($9,166.67) is independent of the salary sacrifice reduction. Withholding is computed on the reduced figure. The engine computes each figure independently; the agent computes neither.

STP2 Classification — Salary Sacrifice Super

Under STP Phase 2, salary sacrifice to super is reported in a dedicated field: SalarySacrificeType.S (type "S" = superannuation). This amount is reported gross: the full dollar amount sacrificed, before any netting. The gross income fields in STP2 reflect the pre-sacrifice amount; the salary sacrifice field records the reduction. The ATO reconciles these two figures to arrive at the employee's assessable income.

The salary-packaging-agent populates SalarySacrificeType.S for each pay event. If the agent routes a sacrifice to the wrong STP2 field — for example, to SalarySacrificeType.O (other benefits) — the ATO's income statement will show an incorrect figure and the employee's tax return will be wrong. Correct field routing is the agent's responsibility to get right, not the employee's problem to discover later.

{
  "stpPayEvent": {
    "employeeId": "EMP-0041",
    "payPeriod": "2026-06",
    "grossIncome": 916667,
    "salarySacrifice": [
      {
        "type": "S",
        "amount": 100000,
        "description": "Pre-tax super sacrifice — Blue Mountain Healthcare SSA-0041"
      }
    ],
    "taxableIncome": 816667,
    "withholdingAmount": "ATO tax tables applied — see withholding ledger",
    "sgContribution": {
      "base": 916667,
      "rate": 0.12,
      "amount": 110000,
      "fund": "AustralianSuper",
      "fundUSI": "STA0100AU"
    },
    "reportableEmployerSuperContributions": 0
  }
}

Note: amounts are in cents. The reportableEmployerSuperContributions field is zero here because Angela's employer contributes only the mandated SG — no additional voluntary contributions above SG made at Angela's direction. RESC is addressed in the next section.

Reportable Employer Super Contributions (RESC)

RESC is one of the most frequently misunderstood concepts in payroll. Many practitioners conflate it with salary sacrifice super — the two overlap but are not identical. The statutory definition matters: RESC is an employer contribution to a superannuation fund that is made under a salary sacrifice arrangement, or that the employee has otherwise directed, and that exceeds what the employer would otherwise be required to contribute under the law or a binding industrial instrument.

The operative question for the payroll engine: would the employer have made this contribution absent any action by the employee? If yes — it is not RESC. If no, it arose from the employee's election — it is RESC.

What is RESC and What is Not

Contribution type RESC? Reason
Mandatory 12% SG on OTE No Required by law — employer has no discretion
Award or EBA mandated employer super above SG No Required by binding industrial instrument — not at employee's direction
Salary-sacrificed super elected by employee Yes Arises from employee election — employer would not contribute absent that election
Employer "matching" contributions triggered by employee salary sacrifice Depends If the employer match is contractually required when the employee sacrifices, it may still be RESC — requires H3 review of the arrangement
Employer voluntary super above SG at employer's discretion No Not made at the employee's direction — employer chose to contribute unilaterally

My take: The "employer discretion vs employee direction" test is clean in theory and murky in practice. Where an employer has a formal matching program — "we match your sacrifice dollar for dollar up to 5%" — the matching component is arguably at the employee's direction because the employee triggered it by electing to sacrifice. I treat those matching contributions as RESC unless there is a clear legal basis to do otherwise. H3 review before finalising the arrangement.

RESC in STP2

In STP Phase 2, RESC is reported in the ReportableEmployerSuperContributions field of the income statement. This figure flows to the employee's income statement and appears on their tax return as "reportable employer super contributions." The ATO uses this figure — among others — to calculate the employee's adjusted taxable income (ATI). ATI is a broader measure than taxable income.

// salary-packaging-agent: RESC classification logic
function classifyEmployerSuperContributions(arrangement, payPeriod) {
  const sgObligation = computeSGObligation(arrangement.oteBase, SG_RATE);

  const industrialMinimum = lookupIndustrialMinimum(
    arrangement.employeeId,
    arrangement.awardCode,
    arrangement.ebaRef
  );

  // Employer's non-discretionary floor
  const nonRescFloor = Math.max(sgObligation, industrialMinimum);

  const totalEmployerContributions =
    arrangement.sgContribution +
    arrangement.salarySacrificeSuper +
    arrangement.employerVoluntary;

  // Only the employee-directed portion above the floor is RESC
  const resc = Math.max(
    0,
    arrangement.salarySacrificeSuper - Math.max(0, industrialMinimum - sgObligation)
  );

  return {
    sgContribution: sgObligation,
    resc: resc,
    nonRescEmployerVoluntary: arrangement.employerVoluntary,
    stpField: "ReportableEmployerSuperContributions",
    rescAmount: resc,
    requiresH3Review: arrangement.hasMatchingProgram
  };
}

Worked Example B — RESC Calculation

Assumed-fact illustration. Wei works at Westfield Logistics. Monthly OTE: $7,500. SG obligation: 12% × $7,500 = $900. His enterprise agreement requires 13% super on OTE: $975. Wei has also elected to salary sacrifice a further $500/month to super. Total employer super contributions per month: $975 (EBA) + $500 (sacrifice) = $1,475.

The SG obligation ($900) is subsumed in the EBA minimum ($975). The EBA minimum is required by a binding industrial instrument — not RESC. The $500 sacrifice is at Wei's direction — RESC. Monthly RESC: $500. Annual RESC: $6,000.

The payroll engine reports: sgContribution = $975, ReportableEmployerSuperContributions = $500. Wei's income statement will show $6,000 RESC for the year. This flows into his ATI calculation.

What this illustrates: The EBA minimum absorbs the SG — no double-counting. The salary sacrifice above the industrial floor is RESC. Both numbers come from the engine, not from the agent.

RESC in Adjusted Taxable Income — Downstream Effects

RESC appears in the ATI formula. ATI is the income measure used for a range of means-tested government benefits and tax concessions. Understanding what RESC does to ATI is essential for employees making salary sacrifice decisions — and it is a point that payroll practitioners often fail to communicate.

ATI Components

Component Included in ATI? Note
Taxable income Yes Base component — after salary sacrifice reduction
Reportable fringe benefits (total grossed-up taxable value) Yes From FBT — discussed below
Reportable employer super contributions (RESC) Yes Added back to taxable income for ATI purposes
Total net investment losses Yes Rental losses, financial investment losses
Personal super deductions Yes — partially Added back if claimed as deduction

The consequence: salary sacrificing to super reduces taxable income but does not reduce ATI by the same amount. RESC is added back. For means-tested entitlements calculated on ATI, the benefit of salary sacrifice is partially or fully neutralised.

ATI-Sensitive Tests Affected by RESC

  • Government co-contribution: Eligibility requires ATI below the higher income threshold (verify against ATO — indexed annually). RESC increases ATI, potentially reducing or eliminating co-contribution eligibility.
  • Spouse contribution tax offset: The offset is available when the receiving spouse's income is below a threshold (verify against ATO). RESC on the contributing spouse's income statement does not affect this test directly — but RESC on the receiving spouse's income does.
  • Child support assessments: Child support is calculated on each parent's income for child support purposes, which is derived from ATI. RESC increases the payer's income for assessment purposes.
  • Division 293 tax: An additional 15% tax on concessional contributions is imposed when an individual's income — which includes RESC in the Div 293 income measure — exceeds the $250,000 Division 293 threshold (fixed, not indexed — verify against ATO). RESC can push an individual over or further over this threshold.
  • Medicare levy surcharge and private health insurance rebate: These tests also use an income measure that includes RESC.

My take: The ATI add-back of RESC is the most under-explained aspect of salary sacrifice advice. An employee earning $240,000 taxable who sacrifices $15,000 to super reduces their taxable income to $225,000 and thinks they have avoided Div 293. They have not. Their Div 293 income includes the $15,000 RESC, putting them back over the threshold. The salary-packaging-agent should surface this calculation at arrangement setup — not as advice, but as a disclosure of the engine's projection. H1 gate if the employee is asking the practitioner to confirm the tax outcome.

Worked Example C — Div 293 with RESC

Assumed-fact illustration. Sasha works at Metro Digital Solutions. Annual salary $265,000. She salary sacrifices $20,000/year to super. Taxable income: $265,000 − $20,000 = $245,000. SG: 12% × $265,000 = $31,800. RESC: $20,000.

Division 293 income = taxable income + low tax contributions. Low tax contributions = SG contributions + RESC = $31,800 + $20,000 = $51,800. Div 293 income = $245,000 + $51,800 = $296,800. Threshold: $250,000 (verify against ATO). Div 293 tax = 15% × min($51,800, $296,800 − $250,000) = 15% × $46,800 = $7,020. Verify against ATO — this is an assumed-fact illustration.

The payroll engine does not pay Div 293 — the ATO assesses it after the employee lodges their return. But the salary-packaging-agent projects this figure at arrangement setup so the employee understands the full cost.

What this illustrates: RESC feeds Div 293 income. Salary sacrifice does not escape Div 293 — it shifts where the contribution sits, not whether it is caught by the threshold test. The engine produces the RESC figure; the Div 293 assessment belongs to the ATO.

The salary-packaging-agent emits a Div 293 projection at arrangement setup for high-income employees. This projection is informational. It discloses the engine's forward calculation given current inputs, and must not be presented to the employee as a tax assessment or advice.

// salary-packaging-agent: Div 293 projection (informational)
// Assumed-fact illustration — verify all thresholds against ATO
function projectDiv293(arrangement, annualInputs) {
  const { grossSalary, salarySacrificeSuper, sgRate, div293Threshold } = annualInputs;

  const taxableIncome = grossSalary - salarySacrificeSuper;
  const sgContribution = grossSalary * sgRate;           // pre-sacrifice base
  const resc = salarySacrificeSuper;                     // employee-directed
  const lowTaxContributions = sgContribution + resc;

  const div293Income = taxableIncome + lowTaxContributions;
  const excessOverThreshold = Math.max(0, div293Income - div293Threshold);
  const div293Taxable = Math.min(lowTaxContributions, excessOverThreshold);
  const div293Tax = div293Taxable * 0.15;

  return {
    taxableIncome,
    sgContribution,
    resc,
    lowTaxContributions,
    div293Income,
    div293Threshold,
    div293Taxable,
    div293Tax,
    projection: "informational only — not a tax assessment",
    verifyAgainst: "ATO — Div 293 threshold fixed at $250,000, not indexed",
    h1GateNote: "If employee requests confirmation of tax outcome, H1 gate applies"
  };
}

// Example call — Sasha, Metro Digital Solutions
const projection = projectDiv293(
  { ref: "SSA-MD-SASHA-2026" },
  {
    grossSalary: 26500000,      // cents: $265,000
    salarySacrificeSuper: 2000000,   // cents: $20,000
    sgRate: 0.12,
    div293Threshold: 25000000   // cents: $250,000 — verify against ATO
  }
);
// projection.div293Tax = 702000 cents = $7,020 — matches Worked Example C above

FBT Interaction — Employer's Liability

When salary sacrifice involves non-cash benefits — a car, a laptop, meal entertainment, expense payments — fringe benefits tax enters the picture. FBT is the employer's tax liability, not the employee's. The FBT year runs from 1 April to 31 March — a different year from the income tax year. This timing difference creates reconciliation complexity when benefits straddle years.

FBT Fundamentals for Payroll

The employer provides a fringe benefit to the employee as part of a salary sacrifice arrangement. The taxable value of that benefit is determined by one of several valuation methods — statutory formula method for cars, actual cost method for expenses, grossed-up market value for others. The FBT liability = taxable value × gross-up rate × FBT rate. The rates and gross-up factors are set annually — verify against ATO for the current FBT year.

FBT is the employer's cost. It is not withheld from the employee's wages. The employer pays it. The commercial question — who bears the FBT in a salary sacrifice arrangement — is a matter of contract, not law. Many employers structure salary sacrifice arrangements so the employee's sacrifice amount is set at a level that covers the employer's FBT liability. Others absorb FBT as a cost of providing the benefit. Both are legally valid.

Award-governs: Salary sacrifice arrangements involving FBT-liable benefits must be modelled against the employee's base rate of pay. Some awards prohibit salary sacrifice below the base rate. If the arrangement reduces the employee's cash pay below the award floor, it is unlawful regardless of whether the benefit has commercial value. Verify applicable award before implementing.

Reportable Fringe Benefits Amount (RFBA)

Employees whose total taxable value of fringe benefits provided in the FBT year exceeds $2,000 (FBTAA 1986 s 135P — verify against ATO) have a reportable fringe benefits amount (RFBA) shown on their income statement. The RFBA is the grossed-up taxable value of the benefits. It is not additional income — the employee does not pay tax on it — but it is included in ATI.

Under STP2, the RFBA is reported in the ReportableFringeBenefits field. The salary-packaging-agent receives the FBT data from the benefits ledger and routes it to this field. The payroll engine does not calculate FBT — FBT is calculated by a separate FBT computation process (or the employer's tax advisers). The salary-packaging-agent consumes the RFBA figure as an input.

Benefit type FBT exposure RFBA reported? Exempt?
Car benefit (employer-owned, salary sacrifice) Yes — statutory formula Yes, if total taxable value of benefits > $2,000 in the FBT year (verify) No standard exemption
Laptop, portable device (primary use work) Exempt (one per employee per year) No Yes — exempt benefit
Meal entertainment (FBT concessional employers) May be concessional or capped Depends on employer type and cap Partial — health/charity employers have concessions
Portable electronic device (multiple, not work-primary) Yes Yes, if total taxable value of benefits > $2,000 in the FBT year (verify) No
Expense payment — work-related expenses May be exempt if otherwise deductible to employee No, if exempt Otherwise deductible exemption may apply

Blue Mountain Healthcare is a public hospital that qualifies as an FBT-exempt employer for the purposes of certain fringe benefits. These concessional employer rules are complex and outside the payroll engine's computation scope — they require H3 review before any packaging arrangement is implemented.

Worked Example D — FBT and RFBA for a Car Benefit

Assumed-fact illustration. James works at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd. He salary sacrifices into a novated lease for a car. The car's cost price is $45,000. The statutory fraction under the FBT statutory formula method is 20% (verify against ATO — the fraction is a fixed 20% regardless of kilometres under current rules). Taxable value = $45,000 × 20% = $9,000. Assuming the arrangement runs the full FBT year (1 April 2025 to 31 March 2026) and there is no employee contribution, the FBT taxable value is $9,000.

The FBT liability = $9,000 × type 1 gross-up rate × FBT rate (verify rates against ATO for the current FBT year). The employer bears this liability. The RFBA on James's income statement = $9,000 × type 2 gross-up rate (verify). James's ATI includes this grossed-up RFBA. The RFBA carries no additional income tax for James; it affects ATI-sensitive tests only.

Under STP2, the salary-packaging-agent reports:

{
  "stpIncomeStatement": {
    "employeeId": "EMP-0088",
    "financialYear": "2025-26",
    "grossIncome": 9500000,
    "salarySacrifice": [
      {
        "type": "O",
        "amount": 800000,
        "description": "Novated lease pre-tax contribution — HAR-NL-0088"
      }
    ],
    "reportableFringeBenefits": {
      "exempt": false,
      "grossedUpTaxableValue": 1698120,
      "fbtYearEnd": "2026-03-31",
      "stpField": "ReportableFringeBenefits"
    },
    "reportableEmployerSuperContributions": 0,
    "note": "FBT taxable value and gross-up rate: verify against ATO — rates change annually"
  }
}

What this illustrates: Salary sacrifice for a car benefit uses SalarySacrificeType.O (other benefits) in STP2 — not type "S" (super). The RFBA is a separate field. Both must be routed correctly by the salary-packaging-agent. Routing is the agent's job; the FBT calculation itself arrives as an external input, and the deterministic layer reconciles it.

FBT-Exempt Employers — Health and Charity Concessions

Public hospitals, public ambulance services, and certain not-for-profit organisations are FBT-exempt or FBT-rebatable employers. These employers can offer salary packaging up to a capped threshold without the employer incurring FBT. The cap amounts and eligibility conditions are in the Fringe Benefits Tax Assessment Act 1986 — verify against current ATO guidance.

For these employers, the salary-packaging-agent must apply different RFBA reporting logic. Benefits within the capped threshold may not generate an RFBA — they are not reportable fringe benefits within the meaning of the legislation. This is an H3 review point: the classification of an employer's FBT status is not something the payroll engine can self-determine from a configuration flag. It requires practitioner sign-off before the arrangement is activated.

My take: FBT-concessional employer status is conditional and time-limited, and it must be confirmed each year. I have seen payroll systems carry forward a "FBT exempt = true" configuration flag that was set correctly in year one and never reviewed. When the employer's circumstances change — a hospital corporatises, a charity changes its activities — the flag stays true and every packaged benefit is mis-reported. The salary-packaging-agent should require an annual attestation before processing FBT-concessional arrangements.

The salary-packaging-agent — STP2 Field Routing

The salary-packaging-agent is the orchestration layer that converts a salary sacrifice arrangement into correctly routed STP2 fields. Its scope stops at execution: it runs the arrangement as configured and routes each component to the correct ATO-specified field, leaving policy and employee advice to humans.

Agent Configuration Schema

// salary-packaging-agent configuration schema
{
  "$schema": "https://payroll.internal/schemas/packaging-agent/v2.json",
  "agentId": "salary-packaging-agent",
  "version": "2.4.0",
  "dependsOn": ["sg-agent", "payroll-engine", "payroll-orchestrator"],
  "inputs": {
    "employeeId": "string",
    "arrangementRef": "string",
    "arrangements": [
      {
        "type": "super_sacrifice | other_benefit",
        "amount": "number (cents, per-period)",
        "frequency": "weekly | fortnightly | monthly | annual_prorate",
        "benefitDetail": {
          "category": "super | car | laptop | meal | expense | other",
          "fbtExempt": "boolean",
          "fbtConcessionalEmployer": "boolean",
          "rfbaGrossedUpValue": "number | null (cents, annual)"
        },
        "stpField": "SalarySacrificeType.S | SalarySacrificeType.O | ReportableFringeBenefits",
        "employerFBTLiability": "number | null (cents, annual)"
      }
    ],
    "sgBaseOTE": "number (cents, per-period — pre-sacrifice)",
    "awardCode": "string | null",
    "ebaRef": "string | null",
    "industrialMinimumSuperRate": "number | null",
    "fbtConcessionalStatus": {
      "confirmed": "boolean",
      "attestedDate": "ISO8601 date",
      "attestedBy": "string (practitioner ID)"
    }
  },
  "outputs": {
    "grossIncome": "number (cents, pre-sacrifice)",
    "salarySacrificeSuper": "number (cents)",
    "salarySacrificeOther": "number (cents)",
    "taxableIncome": "number (cents, post-sacrifice)",
    "resc": "number (cents)",
    "rfba": "number (cents, grossed-up, annual)",
    "sgBase": "number (cents — same as sgBaseOTE, passed to sg-agent)",
    "stpPayload": "object (STP2 pay event fields)",
    "warnings": "array of string",
    "h3ReviewRequired": "boolean",
    "h1GateRequired": "boolean"
  }
}

STP2 Field Routing Table

Arrangement type STP2 field Reduces taxable income? In ATI?
Salary sacrifice to super SalarySacrificeType.S Yes Yes — via RESC add-back
Salary sacrifice to other benefits (car, meals, expenses) SalarySacrificeType.O Yes Via RFBA if FBT liability > threshold
Reportable fringe benefits amount ReportableFringeBenefits No — not deducted from pay Yes — grossed-up value added to ATI
Reportable employer super contributions ReportableEmployerSuperContributions No — not deducted from pay Yes — added to ATI directly
Non-reportable FBT benefits (exempt or below threshold) Not reported Yes (if sacrificed) No

Agent Routing Logic

// salary-packaging-agent: main routing function
async function routeSalaryPackaging(config, payPeriod) {
  const warnings = [];
  let h3ReviewRequired = false;

  // 1. Validate arrangement is pre-period (not retrospective)
  const arrangementStart = parseDate(config.arrangementRef.effectiveDate);
  const periodStart = parseDate(payPeriod.startDate);
  if (arrangementStart >= periodStart) {
    throw new PackagingError(
      "RETROSPECTIVE_ARRANGEMENT",
      `Arrangement ${config.arrangementRef} cannot be applied to period ${payPeriod.startDate} — effective date must precede period start`
    );
  }

  // 2. Validate FBT concessional status if applicable
  const hasFBTConcessional = config.arrangements.some(
    a => a.benefitDetail.fbtConcessionalEmployer
  );
  if (hasFBTConcessional) {
    const daysSinceAttestation = daysBetween(
      config.fbtConcessionalStatus.attestedDate,
      payPeriod.startDate
    );
    if (!config.fbtConcessionalStatus.confirmed || daysSinceAttestation > 365) {
      warnings.push("FBT_CONCESSIONAL_STATUS_UNCONFIRMED: annual attestation required — H3 review");
      h3ReviewRequired = true;
    }
  }

  // 3. Compute sacrifice amounts per arrangement
  let salarySacrificeSuper = 0;
  let salarySacrificeOther = 0;
  let rfba = 0;

  for (const arrangement of config.arrangements) {
    const periodAmount = prorateToPeriod(arrangement.amount, arrangement.frequency, payPeriod);

    if (arrangement.type === "super_sacrifice") {
      salarySacrificeSuper += periodAmount;
    } else if (arrangement.type === "other_benefit") {
      salarySacrificeOther += periodAmount;
      if (arrangement.benefitDetail.rfbaGrossedUpValue !== null) {
        rfba += arrangement.benefitDetail.rfbaGrossedUpValue;
      }
    }
  }

  // 4. Compute RESC
  const rescResult = classifyEmployerSuperContributions({
    oteBase: config.sgBaseOTE,
    salarySacrificeSuper,
    awardCode: config.awardCode,
    ebaRef: config.ebaRef,
    industrialMinimumSuperRate: config.industrialMinimumSuperRate,
    employerVoluntary: 0,
    hasMatchingProgram: false
  });

  if (rescResult.requiresH3Review) {
    h3ReviewRequired = true;
    warnings.push("MATCHING_PROGRAM_DETECTED: RESC classification requires H3 review");
  }

  // 5. Concessional cap proximity check
  const annualisedSG = config.sgBaseOTE * payPeriod.periodsPerYear * 0.12;
  const annualisedSacrificeSuper = salarySacrificeSuper * payPeriod.periodsPerYear;
  const projectedConcessionalContributions = annualisedSG + annualisedSacrificeSuper;
  const concessionalCap = await getAtoThreshold("CONCESSIONAL_CAP", payPeriod.financialYear);

  if (projectedConcessionalContributions > concessionalCap * 0.9) {
    warnings.push(
      `CONCESSIONAL_CAP_PROXIMITY: projected annual concessional contributions ` +
      `${formatCents(projectedConcessionalContributions)} approaching cap ` +
      `${formatCents(concessionalCap)} — verify against ATO`
    );
  }

  // 6. Build STP2 payload
  const taxableIncome = config.grossIncome - salarySacrificeSuper - salarySacrificeOther;

  const stpPayload = {
    grossIncome: config.grossIncome,
    salarySacrifice: [
      salarySacrificeSuper > 0 ? { type: "S", amount: salarySacrificeSuper } : null,
      salarySacrificeOther > 0 ? { type: "O", amount: salarySacrificeOther } : null
    ].filter(Boolean),
    taxableIncome,
    reportableEmployerSuperContributions: rescResult.resc,
    reportableFringeBenefits: rfba > 0 ? rfba : undefined
  };

  // 7. H1 gate for STP finalisation
  const h1GateRequired = payPeriod.isFinalisation === true;

  return {
    grossIncome: config.grossIncome,
    salarySacrificeSuper,
    salarySacrificeOther,
    taxableIncome,
    resc: rescResult.resc,
    rfba,
    sgBase: config.sgBaseOTE,
    stpPayload,
    warnings,
    h3ReviewRequired,
    h1GateRequired,
    engineNote: "Computed by the engine; not derived by the agent."
  };
}

Orchestrator Integration

The payroll-orchestrator calls the salary-packaging-agent before calling the sg-agent and before PAYG withholding is computed. The sequence is enforced — not advisory.

// payroll-orchestrator: per-employee pay period sequence
async function processEmployee(employeeId, payPeriod) {
  // Step 1: Load base entitlements
  const entitlements = await payroll-engine.computeEntitlements(employeeId, payPeriod);

  // Step 2: Apply salary sacrifice arrangements
  const packagingResult = await salary-packaging-agent.routeSalaryPackaging({
    ...entitlements,
    grossIncome: entitlements.grossOTE
  }, payPeriod);

  // Halt if H3 review required and not yet cleared
  if (packagingResult.h3ReviewRequired && !payPeriod.h3Cleared) {
    return {
      status: "HELD_FOR_H3_REVIEW",
      employeeId,
      payPeriod: payPeriod.ref,
      warnings: packagingResult.warnings,
      gate: "H3 — practitioner review required before pay event submission"
    };
  }

  // Step 3: SG calculation on pre-sacrifice OTE base
  const sgResult = await sg-agent.computeSG({
    employeeId,
    oteBase: packagingResult.sgBase,   // pre-sacrifice OTE — not taxableIncome
    payPeriod
  });

  // Step 4: PAYG withholding on post-sacrifice taxable income
  const withholdingResult = await payg-withholding-agent.compute({
    taxableIncome: packagingResult.taxableIncome,  // post-sacrifice
    taxFileNumber: entitlements.tfn,
    residencyStatus: entitlements.residencyStatus,
    payPeriod,
    atoTableRef: "NAT1008 — verify current edition"
  });

  // Step 5: Build STP2 pay event
  const stpEvent = buildSTPPayEvent({
    ...packagingResult.stpPayload,
    sgContribution: sgResult.contribution,
    withholdingAmount: withholdingResult.amount
  });

  return {
    status: "READY",
    employeeId,
    stpEvent,
    h1GateRequired: packagingResult.h1GateRequired,
    warnings: packagingResult.warnings
  };
}

Gate Points in the Salary-Packaging-Agent

The salary-packaging-agent enforces three gate points:

H3 — Practitioner review before proceeding is triggered when: (1) the arrangement involves an FBT-concessional employer and the annual attestation is missing or stale; (2) a matching contribution program is detected and RESC classification is ambiguous; (3) the concessional cap proximity warning fires and the arrangement was set up more than twelve months ago without review; (4) the arrangement involves meal entertainment, which has complex FBT treatment depending on employer type.

H2 — Registered tax agent (formal reclassification with tax consequences) is triggered when: (1) the employer requests reclassification of an existing employer obligation as RESC; (2) a prior year RESC figure needs amendment and the amendment has tax consequences for the employee.

H1 — Registered tax agent or BAS agent (non-delegable) is triggered when: (1) STP income statement finalisation is requested; (2) an amended STP finalisation is requested. Finalisation is the formal closure of the employee's income statement for the financial year. This is a tax agent function. The salary-packaging-agent never auto-finalises. It queues the finalisation event with status AWAITING_H1_AUTHORISATION.

Worked Example E — Multiple Arrangements, Mixed STP Fields

Assumed-fact illustration. Priya works at Adelaide Retail Services. Monthly gross: $10,000. She has two active salary sacrifice arrangements: (1) $800/month to super (type "S"), and (2) $500/month toward a novated car lease (type "O"). The novated lease generates an RFBA of $9,500 (annualised, grossed-up — verify against ATO FBT rates).

The salary-packaging-agent processes in sequence. Gross income: $10,000. Post-sacrifice taxable income: $10,000 − $800 − $500 = $8,700. SG base: $10,000 (pre-sacrifice OTE — unchanged). RESC: $800 (the super sacrifice). RFBA: $9,500 annual (reported at finalisation, not per pay period in STP2 — the finalisation event carries the annual RFBA).

STP2 pay event payload for this month:

{
  "payEvent": {
    "employeeId": "EMP-0022",
    "payPeriod": "2026-06",
    "grossIncome": 1000000,
    "salarySacrifice": [
      { "type": "S", "amount": 80000 },
      { "type": "O", "amount": 50000 }
    ],
    "taxableIncome": 870000,
    "withholdingAmount": "ATO tax tables applied — see withholding ledger",
    "sgBase": 1000000,
    "sgContribution": 120000,
    "reportableEmployerSuperContributions": 80000,
    "engineNote": "Calculated by the engine, not by agent code."
  },
  "annualIncomeStatement": {
    "reportableFringeBenefits": 950000,
    "reportedAtFinalisation": true,
    "h1GateRequired": true,
    "status": "AWAITING_H1_AUTHORISATION"
  }
}

What this illustrates: Two sacrifice types produce two distinct STP2 salary sacrifice entries. RESC is reported per pay event. RFBA is annual and goes to the income statement at finalisation — which requires H1 authorisation. Every number in the payload is the engine's to produce. Priya's taxable income is $8,700, not $10,000. Her SG is computed on $10,000.

Common Errors and How the Agent Prevents Them

The salary-packaging-agent is designed around a known error taxonomy. Understanding what it guards against explains why the validation rules exist.

Error 1 — Retrospective Arrangement Application

The arrangement effective date is set after the pay period start. The agent refuses to apply it, raising RETROSPECTIVE_ARRANGEMENT. The employer must establish a new arrangement for the next period. H3 review is flagged — the employee may have received a benefit they believed was packaged but was not.

Error 2 — SG Base Reduction

The SG base is incorrectly set to the post-sacrifice taxable income. The agent computes SG on the pre-sacrifice OTE passed from the payroll engine. It does not accept a SG base that is lower than the OTE base without explicit employer super floor configuration that has itself been H3-reviewed. This error, if undetected, underestimates the SG obligation every pay period.

Error 3 — Super Sacrifice Routed to Type "O"

A super sacrifice arrangement is misconfigured with SalarySacrificeType.O instead of SalarySacrificeType.S. The ATO's STP2 reconciliation will not identify the amount as concessional super. The employee's income statement will misstate their RESC. The agent validates the type field against the benefit category and throws STP_FIELD_MISMATCH before submitting the pay event.

Error 4 — RFBA Not Reported at Finalisation

The novated lease RFBA is calculated at the FBT year end (31 March) but the payroll team forgets to load it into the income statement before STP finalisation (end of financial year, 14 July deadline for most employers — verify ATO lodgment due dates). The agent tracks active car benefit arrangements and raises a pre-finalisation warning: RFBA_NOT_LOADED. Finalisation is blocked until the RFBA is provided or explicitly waived with a practitioner attestation.

Error 5 — FBT-Concessional Status Not Re-Attested

The employer's FBT-concessional status flag was set at system setup and has not been re-confirmed. Benefits are processed as exempt when they may not be. The agent checks attestation currency on every pay period and escalates to H3 after 365 days without re-attestation.

Error 6 — Concessional Cap Breach

An employee's total concessional contributions — SG plus salary sacrifice super — exceed the annual cap. The agent projects this at arrangement setup and flags CONCESSIONAL_CAP_PROXIMITY when within 10% of the threshold. Cap breach itself is the employee's ATO problem (excess concessional contributions are included in assessable income with a 15% offset), but the employer's payroll system should surface the risk. The agent warns rather than blocking the contribution.

// salary-packaging-agent: pre-finalisation validation
async function validateForFinalisation(employeeId, financialYear) {
  const active = await loadActiveArrangements(employeeId, financialYear);
  const errors = [];
  const warnings = [];

  // Check RFBA loaded for all car/non-exempt benefit arrangements
  for (const arrangement of active.filter(a => a.benefitDetail.category === "car")) {
    if (!arrangement.rfbaLoaded) {
      errors.push({
        code: "RFBA_NOT_LOADED",
        message: `Car benefit arrangement ${arrangement.ref} has no RFBA for ${financialYear}`,
        resolution: "Load RFBA from FBT calculation before proceeding",
        gate: "H3 — practitioner review of FBT calculation required"
      });
    }
  }

  // Check FBT concessional attestation
  if (active.some(a => a.benefitDetail.fbtConcessionalEmployer)) {
    const attestation = await loadFBTAttestation(employeeId, financialYear);
    if (!attestation || !attestation.confirmed) {
      errors.push({
        code: "FBT_CONCESSIONAL_NOT_ATTESTED",
        message: "FBT-concessional employer status unconfirmed for finalisation",
        gate: "H3 — practitioner attestation required"
      });
    }
  }

  // Block finalisation pending H1
  if (errors.length > 0) {
    return {
      status: "BLOCKED",
      errors,
      warnings,
      gate: "H1 — registered tax agent or BAS agent must clear errors before finalisation"
    };
  }

  return {
    status: "READY_FOR_H1_AUTHORISATION",
    warnings,
    gate: "H1 — registered tax agent or BAS agent must authorise finalisation"
  };
}

Worked Example F — Finalisation Blocked, H1 Required

Assumed-fact illustration. Marcus works at Metro Digital Solutions. He has a novated lease arrangement active for the full 2025-26 financial year. The payroll team initiates STP finalisation on 8 July 2026. The salary-packaging-agent runs validateForFinalisation. It finds: (1) the RFBA for the car benefit has not been loaded — the FBT return for the year ended 31 March 2026 was lodged by the tax agent on 28 June 2026 but the RFBA figure was not uploaded into the payroll system; (2) the FBT-concessional status is not applicable (Westfield Logistics is not a concessional employer).

Status: BLOCKED. Error: RFBA_NOT_LOADED. The payroll team uploads the RFBA figure from the FBT return. The agent re-validates. Status: READY_FOR_H1_AUTHORISATION. A registered BAS agent or tax agent reviews the income statement, confirms the figures, and authorises finalisation. The STP event is submitted with the RFBA. Marcus's income statement is correct.

What this illustrates: The salary-packaging-agent enforces the H1 gate on finalisation. It does not allow auto-finalisation. The FBT return data must flow back into the payroll system before finalisation can proceed. H1 owns the authorisation.

Annual Review Cycle for Salary Sacrifice Arrangements

Salary sacrifice arrangements do not self-maintain. Several events require review or renegotiation:

  • SG rate change: The SG rate reached its legislated 12% ceiling on 1 July 2025 — no further increase is legislated, and future changes would require new legislation. Any rate change affects the SG contribution, the total concessional contributions calculation, and potentially the Div 293 threshold proximity. The salary-packaging-agent reads the current SG rate from the payroll engine's rate table — it does not hardcode it. Verify against ATO.
  • Concessional cap indexation: The cap is indexed annually. An arrangement that was within the cap last year may be over it this year if the salary increased. The agent re-runs the cap proximity check on the first pay period of each new financial year.
  • FBT year-end (31 March): Car benefit RFBA must be calculated and loaded before STP finalisation in July. The agent tracks this dependency.
  • Employee's circumstances change: New child support assessment, change in family situation, change in income that affects Div 293 exposure. The payroll engine cannot know this — the employee must notify payroll. The agent surfaces a recommendation for annual arrangement review in its finalisation report.
  • Award or EBA renegotiation: If the industrial minimum super rate changes, the RESC classification may change. H3 review required.

My take: The annual review cycle is where most salary packaging programs break down. The arrangement is set up correctly in year one. In year two, the SG rate ticked up, the concessional cap moved, and the employee got a salary increase. Nobody updated the arrangement. The salary sacrifice amount is now producing a concessional contributions breach. The payroll engine detects this only if the cap proximity check is re-run with current-year inputs. Schedule the re-run. Do not assume last year's arrangement is still optimal.

Determinism-First: Salary Packaging Edition

Every figure in a salary sacrifice arrangement is computable. The gross income is a fact. The sacrifice election is a documented amount. The SG rate is a legislated rate. The STP2 field assignment is a rule lookup. The RESC classification is a logical test against the industrial floor. The RFBA is an external input from the FBT calculation — the one figure the payroll engine does not self-generate — but once loaded, it is deterministic.

The salary-packaging-agent makes this computation explicit, documented, and auditable. Every pay event output carries: the arrangement reference, the sacrifice amounts, the STP2 field assignments, the RESC figure, the SG base used, the warnings generated, and the gate status. If the ATO queries a salary packaging arrangement two years later, the audit trail is in the pay event log. Every figure that appears in it was computed by the engine, never derived by the agent.

Human judgement enters at three points and only those three points: H3 for arrangement setup review and FBT concessional attestation; H2 for formal reclassification or amendment with tax consequences; H1 for STP finalisation. Everything between those gates runs without human intervention. That is the point.

Next: Chapter 08 covers state and territory payroll tax — each state's separate regime, grouping rules, contractor provisions, and the payroll-tax-agent that maps wages and deemed wages across jurisdictions.

08 State and Territory Payroll Tax

Each state's threshold and rate, the grouping rules, contractor provisions, and the payroll-tax-agent

There is no federal payroll tax in Australia. Each state and territory runs its own payroll tax regime — its own act, its own rate, its own threshold, its own grouping rules, and its own contractor provisions. That means eight different sets of rules, and a single employer operating across jurisdictions can be liable in multiple states simultaneously. The payroll-tax-agent exists to classify employers, apply grouping logic, monitor threshold proximity, and surface lodgement obligations — but every figure it produces must be verified against the relevant state revenue office before you rely on it.

State and Territory Payroll Tax — No Federal Equivalent

This is the first thing practitioners get wrong: they assume payroll tax is a Commonwealth obligation, like PAYG withholding or SG. It is not. Payroll tax is a state and territory tax, collected and audited by the state revenue offices. The ATO has no role in it.

Each jurisdiction has its own enabling legislation. NSW operates under the Payroll Tax Act 2007 (NSW). Victoria operates under the Payroll Tax Act 2007 (Vic). Queensland operates under the Payroll Tax Act 1971 (Qld). The names look similar. The rules are not.

The eight jurisdictions are:

  • New South Wales (NSW) — Revenue NSW
  • Victoria (VIC) — State Revenue Office Victoria
  • Queensland (QLD) — Queensland Revenue Office
  • South Australia (SA) — RevenueSA
  • Western Australia (WA) — RevenueWA
  • Tasmania (TAS) — State Revenue Office Tasmania
  • Northern Territory (NT) — Territory Revenue Office
  • Australian Capital Territory (ACT) — ACT Revenue Office

I will refer to these bodies collectively as "the SRO" (state revenue office), with the jurisdiction qualifier where it matters. Every rate, threshold, and provision in this chapter carries a mandatory verification note. Payroll tax parameters change annually — sometimes mid-year. Do not rely on any figure in this book without checking the relevant SRO.

The key structural features common to most jurisdictions:

  • Payroll tax applies to "taxable wages" above a threshold.
  • The threshold is the total wages below which no payroll tax is payable.
  • Above the threshold, tax applies to the excess (or in some jurisdictions, to total wages above a phase-out point).
  • Monthly returns are required in most jurisdictions once registered.
  • An annual reconciliation is required after the financial year ends.
  • Related employers may be grouped — the group shares one threshold.
  • Contractor provisions may bring labour hire and service arrangements into scope.

My take: The threshold is the most misunderstood feature. Employers assume that if they are under the threshold, payroll tax is not their problem. That is only true if they are not part of a group. A small employer with $700,000 in wages may think they are safe under every jurisdiction's threshold. If they are related to an entity with $3 million in wages, the group is well over threshold, and both entities may share the liability. Run the grouping check first.

The payroll-tax-agent is responsible for classifying each employer, identifying related entities, computing group wages, and comparing against jurisdictional thresholds. The liability itself is computed by the engine, never by agent code. Neither agent substitutes for verification with the relevant SRO.

/payroll-tax-agent classify --employer "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd" --jurisdiction NSW
payroll_tax_agent.classify_employer({
  employer: "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
  jurisdiction: "NSW",
  relatedEntities: [
    "Harbour Engineering Holdings Pty Ltd",
    "Harbour Civil Works Pty Ltd"
  ],
  annualWagesEstimate: 4200000
})

The agent returns a classification result: whether the employer is a single entity or part of a group, the jurisdictions in which wages are paid, the estimated group wages, and a threshold proximity flag. Computing the final liability requires a human review step by design.

Rates and Thresholds by State — Verify With Each SRO

Rates and thresholds are published by each SRO and are updated — usually annually, often as part of the state budget. The figures below are indicative as at June 2026 only. Do not use them for lodgement without verifying against the relevant SRO website.

Current as at June 2026 — verify against each state revenue authority before relying on these figures. They change annually and vary by jurisdiction.

Jurisdiction Rate (indicative — verify) Annual threshold (indicative — verify) Lodgement frequency Revenue authority
NSW 5.45% (general) — verify with Revenue NSW ~$1.2m — verify with Revenue NSW Monthly + annual reconciliation Revenue NSW (revenue.nsw.gov.au)
VIC 4.85% (general); 1.2125% regional; +0.5% COVID-19 debt levy for national wages >$10m (+1% >$100m) — verify with SRO Vic $1.0m from 1 July 2025; threshold phases out between $3m–$5m of national wages — verify with SRO Vic Monthly + annual reconciliation State Revenue Office Vic (sro.vic.gov.au)
QLD 4.75%–4.95% (tiered); +0.25% mental health levy for national wages >$10m (+0.5% >$100m) — verify with QRO ~$1.3m — verify with QRO Monthly + annual reconciliation Queensland Revenue Office (qro.qld.gov.au)
SA 4.95% — verify with RevenueSA ~$1.5m — verify with RevenueSA Monthly + annual reconciliation RevenueSA (revenuesa.sa.gov.au)
WA 5.5% (tiered above threshold) — verify with RevenueWA ~$1.0m — verify with RevenueWA Monthly + annual reconciliation RevenueWA (wa.gov.au/revenueWA)
TAS 4% ($1.25m–$2m of wages); 6.1% above $2m — two-tier — verify with SRO Tas $1.25m — verify with SRO Tas Monthly + annual reconciliation State Revenue Office Tas (sro.tas.gov.au)
NT 5.5% — verify with TRO $2.5m from 1 July 2025; deduction diminishes $1 per $2 above the threshold (nil deduction at $7.5m) — verify with TRO Monthly + annual reconciliation Territory Revenue Office (treasury.nt.gov.au)
ACT 6.85% — verify with ACT Revenue ~$2.0m — verify with ACT Revenue Monthly + annual reconciliation ACT Revenue Office (revenue.act.gov.au)

Forward-dated changes already announced: from 1 July 2026, the NT adds a 6.5% tier for national wages of $100m or more, and the ACT cuts its threshold to $1.75m and its general rate to 6.75% for wages up to $20m — verify both against the relevant SRO.

Several points about these figures demand emphasis.

First: some jurisdictions use a diminishing threshold or phase-out range. Victoria, for example, currently phases out the tax-free threshold for national wages between $3m and $5m — verify the current range with SRO Vic. The rate that applies to the excess is not always the same as the marginal rate above the phase-out. These rules change. Do not apply last year's formula to this year's wages.

Second: some states offer concessions for regional employers, for small businesses during transition, or for specific industries. Do not assume the general rate is the applicable rate without checking concession eligibility.

Third: the SG rate is 12% from 1 July 2025. That increase lifts employer costs across the board — and it lifts payroll tax too, because employer superannuation contributions — SG, salary-sacrificed super and any SGC — are included in taxable wages in every jurisdiction. Treat super as inside the base; verify the jurisdiction's definition of wages for edge cases with the relevant SRO.

My take: Practitioners who operate nationally tend to develop a mental model based on the jurisdiction where they do most of their work. That model is wrong everywhere else. NSW and Victoria alone have different rate structures, different thresholds, different grouping rules, and different contractor provisions. Build jurisdiction-specific logic, not a generic approximation.

Example 8.1 (assumed-fact illustration): Angela is the payroll manager at Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd, which employs staff in both NSW and VIC. Annual wages in NSW are $1,800,000. Annual wages in VIC are $850,000. Blue Mountain Healthcare has no related entities.

Angela runs the threshold check for each jurisdiction separately. The payroll-tax-agent is invoked with both jurisdictions:

Task("payroll-tax-agent", {
  action: "threshold-check",
  employer: "Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd",
  jurisdictions: ["NSW", "VIC"],
  annualWages: {
    NSW: 1800000,
    VIC: 850000
  },
  groupedEmployers: [],
  monthsElapsed: 12
})

The agent returns: NSW — wages exceed indicative threshold, likely registered and liable (verify with Revenue NSW); VIC — wages below indicative threshold as at June 2026, but verify current threshold with SRO Vic before relying on this. Angela confirms with each SRO directly. NSW registration is confirmed. VIC requires no current registration but Angela sets a threshold proximity alert at 80% — approximately $800,000 against the $1.0m VIC threshold — so the system flags if VIC wages approach registration territory.

What this illustrates: A single employer can be liable in one jurisdiction and exempt in another based on jurisdiction-specific thresholds. The payroll-tax-agent handles the multi-jurisdiction check, but the employer must verify each result against the relevant SRO. The threshold proximity alert prevents a surprise registration obligation mid-year.

Monthly Lodgement and Annual Reconciliation
