Most jurisdictions require registered employers to lodge a monthly return and pay the monthly instalment by a due date — typically the seventh business day of the following month, but verify this with the relevant SRO. Some jurisdictions allow less frequent lodgement for smaller liable employers.

The annual reconciliation is due after 30 June. It compares total annual taxable wages against the total monthly instalments paid. A shortfall results in a top-up payment. An overpayment results in a credit or refund.

The payroll-tax-agent manages the monthly lodgement workflow as follows:

Step Actor Action Gate
1 payroll-engine Computes taxable wages for the month by jurisdiction — applies inclusions/exclusions per SRO rules No gate — engine output
2 payroll-tax-agent Applies monthly threshold apportionment and computes estimated monthly liability — flags ENGINE OUTPUT as unverified No gate — internal computation
3 payroll-tax-agent Generates monthly workpaper with wages, rate, liability estimate, and verification checklist H3 — practitioner reviews before lodging
4 Practitioner (H3) Reviews workpaper, confirms wages, confirms applicable rate and threshold, approves lodgement H3 sign-off required
5 payroll-tax-agent Lodges monthly return via SRO portal (or generates lodgement file) and records confirmation reference Post-approval — no additional gate
6 payroll-orchestrator Archives lodgement confirmation and updates annual reconciliation ledger No gate — archival step

The H3 gate at step 3 is non-negotiable. The payroll-tax-agent can compute an estimated liability. It cannot confirm the applicable rate, threshold, or whether the employer's wage inclusions/exclusions are correct for the jurisdiction. That requires a practitioner to look at the workpaper and sign off.

The taxable wages figure comes from the engine, not from the agent. The payroll-tax-agent handles the threshold apportionment and rate application. The human practitioner owns the decision to lodge.

PAYROLL TAX — MONTHLY WORKPAPER
Jurisdiction:         NSW
Period:               May 2026
Taxable wages (month): ENGINE OUTPUT — verify inclusions/exclusions per Revenue NSW
Annual wages YTD:     [running total — engine computed]
Monthly threshold:    [annual threshold / 12 — verify with Revenue NSW]
Rate:                 [verify with Revenue NSW — as at June 2026]
Estimated liability:  ENGINE OUTPUT — do not lodge without practitioner review
noOverride:           true
Engine:               payroll-engine v2.4
Verified by:          [practitioner name, date]
SRO confirmation:     [to be populated post-lodgement]
As at:                June 2026

Example 8.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Robert is the finance director at Westfield Logistics Pty Ltd, registered for payroll tax in Victoria. In March 2026, the company paid $218,000 in taxable wages — including $8,000 of employer superannuation contributions. The payroll-tax-agent generates the monthly workpaper.

payroll_engine.compute_taxable_wages({
  jurisdiction: "VIC",
  period: "2026-03",
  employer: "Westfield Logistics Pty Ltd",
  grossWages: 218000
  // the $8,000 of employer super is inside this figure — super is included in taxable wages
})

The engine returns taxable wages of $218,000 — no exclusion for the $8,000 of employer superannuation inside that figure. Employer super contributions (SG, salary-sacrificed super and any SGC) are included in taxable wages in every jurisdiction, VIC included. The payroll-tax-agent applies the monthly threshold apportionment and produces an estimated liability. The workpaper goes to Marcus, the registered tax agent, who confirms the inclusions against current SRO Vic rules, reviews the wages figure against the payroll ledger, and approves lodgement. Gate: H3. Marcus signs off before the payroll-tax-agent submits the return.

What this illustrates: Monthly lodgement requires human judgement. The engine computes taxable wages, the agent prepares the workpaper, and a practitioner confirms jurisdiction-specific rules before lodging. Employer superannuation is included in taxable wages in every jurisdiction — the practitioner's H3 check confirms the inclusions, and "as at last year" is not "as at now".

The annual reconciliation follows the same pattern, amplified. By 30 June, the payroll-orchestrator compiles the full-year wages, total monthly instalments, and any outstanding amounts. The payroll-tax-agent produces the reconciliation workpaper for each jurisdiction. H3 gate applies again. Annual figures carry more consequence than monthly estimates — underpayment penalties and interest apply in most jurisdictions.

Task("payroll-orchestrator", {
  action: "annual-reconciliation",
  employer: "Westfield Logistics Pty Ltd",
  jurisdiction: "VIC",
  financialYear: "2025-26",
  totalTaxableWages: null,  // engine will compute
  totalMonthlyInstalments: null  // drawn from lodgement ledger
})

Grouping Rules — Related Employers Share a Threshold

Grouping is the rule that catches employers who structure their workforce across multiple entities to avoid individually exceeding the threshold. Each jurisdiction has grouping provisions. The detail varies — but the common thread is that related employers are treated as one group, and the group shares a single threshold.

The practical effect: two companies each with $600,000 in wages may individually sit under the threshold. If they are related — same director, common control, or other nexus defined by the relevant act — they form a group with $1,200,000 in combined wages. In most jurisdictions, that exceeds the threshold. Both may be liable.

The nexus tests for grouping are not identical across jurisdictions. Common triggers include:

  • Same person is a majority shareholder or director of both entities
  • One entity controls the other (directly or through interposed entities)
  • Common use of employees or common business purposes
  • Tracing rules through trusts and partnerships

The SROs have published guidance on grouping. Revenue NSW and SRO Vic in particular have detailed online resources and private rulings available. Use them before deciding a group does or does not exist.

My take: The grouping trap catches related businesses built by the same founder. A person who owns a services company, a holding company, and a property company may not think of them as a group for payroll tax purposes. The SRO does. Founders building multi-entity structures must get a grouping assessment before assuming they are under threshold in any jurisdiction.

The payroll-tax-agent applies grouping logic by requesting the related-entity map from the payroll-orchestrator, computing group wages, and comparing against the group threshold. The group threshold is the same as the individual threshold — but it applies once, shared across all group members.

payroll_tax_agent.apply_grouping({
  groupEntities: [
    {
      name: "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
      jurisdiction: "NSW",
      annualWages: 1800000
    },
    {
      name: "Harbour Engineering Holdings Pty Ltd",
      jurisdiction: "NSW",
      annualWages: 400000
    },
    {
      name: "Harbour Civil Works Pty Ltd",
      jurisdiction: "NSW",
      annualWages: 950000
    }
  ],
  groupThreshold: "[verify with Revenue NSW — as at June 2026]",
  apportionThreshold: true
})

When multiple group members pay wages in the same jurisdiction, the threshold is apportioned across them. The apportionment is usually based on each member's proportion of group wages. The payroll-engine computes the apportionment. The relevant SRO rules determine whether and how apportionment applies.

Entity NSW wages (annual) Group share Threshold apportionment (indicative) Liable on excess
Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd $1,800,000 57% [verify with Revenue NSW] Engine output — verify
Harbour Engineering Holdings Pty Ltd $400,000 13% [verify with Revenue NSW] Engine output — verify
Harbour Civil Works Pty Ltd $950,000 30% [verify with Revenue NSW] Engine output — verify
Group total $3,150,000 100% One threshold — shared Engine output — verify

Current as at June 2026 — verify threshold and apportionment rules against Revenue NSW before relying on these figures.

Example 8.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Wei is the CFO of the Harbour Engineering group. The group has three NSW entities: Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd ($1,800,000 wages), Harbour Engineering Holdings Pty Ltd ($400,000 wages), Harbour Civil Works Pty Ltd ($950,000 wages). The entities share a common director and are controlled by the same holding company. Wei engages the payroll-tax-agent to assess the group's NSW payroll tax position.

Task("payroll-tax-agent", {
  action: "grouping-assessment",
  jurisdiction: "NSW",
  financialYear: "2025-26",
  entities: [
    { name: "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd", wages: 1800000 },
    { name: "Harbour Engineering Holdings Pty Ltd", wages: 400000 },
    { name: "Harbour Civil Works Pty Ltd", wages: 950000 }
  ],
  groupNexus: "common-director-and-control"
})

The payroll-tax-agent returns: group wages $3,150,000 — well above the indicative NSW threshold of approximately $1.2m (verify with Revenue NSW). All three entities are grouped. The payroll engine apportions the threshold based on wage share. Each entity's individual payroll tax liability is computed as an engine output, flagged for practitioner verification. Gate: H3 — Wei's registered tax agent reviews the grouping assessment and confirms the nexus conclusion before the group lodges. The agent notes: "Grouping determination is a legal conclusion. If the nexus is disputed, escalate to H2 before lodging."

What this illustrates: Grouping turns three apparently safe entities into a group with a large payroll tax liability. The payroll-tax-agent can apply the grouping rules, but the nexus determination — whether entities are genuinely related — requires a practitioner. If the grouping is contested or uncertain, H2 applies: a registered tax agent must assess the legal position before lodging.

One additional grouping consideration: cross-jurisdictional groups. If the Harbour Engineering group also paid wages in VIC, the NSW grouping does not automatically determine the VIC position. Each state's grouping rules apply independently. The payroll-tax-agent runs a separate grouping assessment for each jurisdiction in which any group member pays wages.

payroll_tax_agent.grouping_check_all_jurisdictions({
  group: "Harbour Engineering Group",
  entities: [
    { name: "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd", wages: { NSW: 1800000, VIC: 200000 } },
    { name: "Harbour Civil Works Pty Ltd", wages: { NSW: 950000 } }
  ],
  jurisdictions: ["NSW", "VIC"]
})

Contractor Provisions — Labour Hire and Service Arrangements

The "contractor" label rarely defeats payroll tax, however much employers hope it will. Most jurisdictions have contractor provisions — sometimes called "relevant contracts" provisions — that bring certain contractor arrangements into the payroll tax base. The provisions vary by jurisdiction, but the common thread is: if the arrangement looks like the provision of labour, the SRO may deem the payments to be taxable wages.

The same principle I emphasise for SG and PAYG withholding applies here: the contractor label is not determinative. The substance of the arrangement governs. AWARD-GOVERNS where an award covers the work being performed — verify the applicable award.

The key arrangements that attract contractor provisions in most jurisdictions:

  • Labour hire: A labour hire firm supplies workers to a client. The firm pays the workers. The payments are taxable wages in the hands of the firm for payroll tax purposes in most jurisdictions.
  • Relevant contracts: An arrangement where an individual provides services under a contract that is substantially for the labour of that individual. Even if structured as a contractor arrangement, the SRO may treat the payments as wages.
  • Contractor exemptions: Most jurisdictions have exemptions — for example, if the contractor supplies labour to multiple clients, or if the contractor is ordinarily engaged in the business of providing that kind of service. The exemption conditions vary significantly by jurisdiction.

The contractor provisions are among the most litigated aspects of payroll tax law. Do not assess your contractor arrangements without advice. The payroll-tax-agent can flag arrangements that present contractor provision risk — but the determination of whether an exemption applies is a legal question. Gate: H3 minimum. If reclassification has tax consequences (it usually does), H2 applies.

Jurisdiction Contractor provision type Labour hire rules Key exemption trigger (indicative) Verify note
NSW Relevant contracts — Revenue NSW Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Contractor ordinarily in business of providing that service; supplies multiple clients Verify with Revenue NSW — provisions and exemptions change
VIC Relevant contracts — SRO Vic Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Same-business exemption; services not of same kind as principal's business Verify with SRO Vic — case law applies; seek advice
QLD Relevant contracts — QRO Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Contractor in own business; multiple principal exemption Verify with QRO — 2019 ruling history is relevant
SA Relevant contracts — RevenueSA Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Contractor regularly provides same services to public Verify with RevenueSA — exemption conditions differ from other states
WA Relevant contracts — RevenueWA Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Contractor has own established business; supplies multiple clients Verify with RevenueWA — WA rules differ materially from eastern states
TAS Relevant contracts — SRO Tas Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Contractor in own business of providing those services Verify with SRO Tas
NT Relevant contracts — TRO Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Verify with Territory Revenue Office — smaller volume of case law Verify with TRO before relying on any exemption
ACT Relevant contracts — ACT Revenue Labour hire arrangement — payments are wages Verify with ACT Revenue Office — provisions modelled on NSW but with differences Verify with ACT Revenue

Current as at June 2026 — verify contractor provisions and exemptions against each relevant state revenue authority. These rules are subject to legislative amendment and case law development.

Example 8.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Priya is the compliance manager at Adelaide Retail Services Pty Ltd (SA). The company engages 12 individuals under contractor agreements to staff retail counters. Each contractor works exclusively for Adelaide Retail Services. They do not supply services to other businesses. Priya asks the payroll-tax-agent to assess the SA payroll tax exposure for these arrangements.

Task("payroll-tax-agent", {
  action: "contractor-provision-assessment",
  jurisdiction: "SA",
  employer: "Adelaide Retail Services Pty Ltd",
  arrangements: [
    {
      type: "individual-contractor",
      headcount: 12,
      annualPaymentsTotal: 480000,
      exclusiveArrangement: true,
      multipleClientsSupplied: false,
      contractorInOwnBusiness: "unclear"
    }
  ]
})

The payroll-tax-agent returns a HIGH RISK flag: the contractors are exclusive to one principal, do not supply services to the public generally, and the "contractor in own business" criterion is unclear. The agent notes that under the SA relevant contracts provisions, these payments may be taxable wages. Rather than making a determination, the agent escalates. Gate: H3 minimum. Priya's registered tax agent reviews the arrangements and advises that the SA provisions are likely to apply, recommending that RevenueSA be contacted for a ruling if the position is uncertain. Gate: H2 — formal contact with RevenueSA is registered tax agent work.

What this illustrates: The contractor provision risk flag is the payroll-tax-agent's contribution. The determination of whether the relevant contracts provisions apply — and whether any exemption saves the employer — requires a practitioner. In a case with this risk profile, do not lodge amended returns or seek a ruling without a registered tax agent at H2.

Labour hire firms have a separate and more straightforward exposure. If your business is a labour hire firm — you supply workers to clients, the workers perform work at the client's direction — the payments to those workers are taxable wages for payroll tax in most jurisdictions. The exemptions available to other contractors are generally not available to labour hire firms. Verify this with the relevant SRO.

/payroll-tax-agent assess-labour-hire --jurisdiction QLD --annual-payments 2100000
payroll_tax_agent.assess_labour_hire({
  jurisdiction: "QLD",
  employer: "Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd",
  arrangementType: "labour-hire",
  annualPaymentsToWorkers: 2100000,
  workersSuppliedToClients: true,
  workersDirectedByClient: true
})

payroll-tax-agent — Threshold Monitoring and Grouping Check

The payroll-tax-agent has three primary operating modes: threshold monitoring, grouping check, and contractor provision assessment. Each is described above in context. This section covers the agent's output format, the configuration of threshold proximity alerts, and the integration with the payroll-orchestrator for multi-jurisdiction employers.

Threshold monitoring runs monthly. The agent compares year-to-date wages against the annualised threshold for each jurisdiction in which the employer is registered — or flagged as a registration candidate. The proximity alert fires at a configurable threshold, typically 80% of the annual threshold.

/payroll-tax-agent threshold-monitor --all-jurisdictions --employer "Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd"

The agent produces a monitoring output for each jurisdiction. The fields in the monitoring output are:

Field Value (example — assumed fact illustration) Status
Employer Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd —
Jurisdiction VIC —
Registration status Not registered Monitoring — not yet liable
Annual wages YTD $762,000 (9 months elapsed) ENGINE OUTPUT
Annualised wages projection ~$1,016,000 (12-month projection) ESTIMATE — verify
Indicative VIC threshold [verify with SRO Vic — as at June 2026] SRO verification required
Proximity alert AMBER — projected to exceed threshold in current FY ACTION: verify threshold, assess registration obligation
Grouped entities None identified Run grouping check if related entities exist
Recommended action Escalate to practitioner — potential registration obligation H3 gate applies
As at June 2026 —

The AMBER alert is the early-warning mechanism. A GREEN status means wages are comfortably below the annualised threshold. AMBER means the projection places the employer within or approaching registration territory. RED means wages have exceeded the threshold and registration is required if not already in place.

When the status moves to AMBER or RED, the payroll-tax-agent escalates to the payroll-orchestrator, which generates a task for the responsible practitioner. Gate: H3. The practitioner reviews the projection, confirms the current threshold with the SRO, and advises on registration if required.

payroll_engine.calculate_payroll_tax({
  jurisdiction: "VIC",
  annualWages: 1016000,
  groupedEmployers: [],
  groupThreshold: false,
  rate: "[verify with SRO Vic — as at June 2026]",
  threshold: "[verify with SRO Vic — as at June 2026]"
})
PAYROLL TAX — WORKPAPER
Jurisdiction:         VIC
Employer:             Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd
Financial year:       2025-26
Months elapsed:       9
Annual wages (YTD):   $762,000
Annualised projection: ~$1,016,000
Threshold (annual):   [verify with SRO Vic — as at June 2026]
Rate:                 [verify with SRO Vic — as at June 2026]
Group members:        None
Estimated liability:  ENGINE OUTPUT — do not use for lodgement without practitioner review
Proximity status:     AMBER
Alert triggered at:   80% of indicative threshold
noOverride:           true
Engine:               payroll-engine v2.4
Reviewed by:          [practitioner name, date]
As at:                June 2026

My take: The payroll-tax-agent's threshold monitoring is worth the setup cost even for employers who are confident they are under threshold. The proximity alert catches the employer who is growing. Missing payroll tax registration generates penalty and interest exposure from the date registration was required, not from the date you discovered it. A quick call to the SRO will not undo that. The monitor pays for itself the first time it fires and you register on time.

The grouping check runs as a separate module. It is triggered when a new related entity is identified, when the employer's corporate structure changes, or on a schedule — typically annually before the annual reconciliation. The grouping check requires the related-entity register to be current. If the register is out of date, the grouping check is unreliable.

Task("payroll-tax-agent", {
  action: "grouping-check",
  trigger: "annual-pre-reconciliation",
  employer: "Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd",
  relatedEntityRegister: "current",  // sourced from payroll-orchestrator
  jurisdictions: ["VIC", "NSW"],
  financialYear: "2025-26"
})

The payroll-orchestrator owns the related-entity register. The payroll-tax-agent queries it. If the register shows no related entities, the grouping check returns a clean result. If new related entities appear — new subsidiaries, a newly acquired sister company, a restructure — the grouping check recalculates immediately and flags any change in liability.

Example 8.5 (assumed-fact illustration): Sasha is the payroll compliance manager at Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd. In February 2026, Metro Digital acquires Metro Digital Holdings Pty Ltd as a new parent entity. Metro Digital Holdings has no employees but holds the IP of the group. The payroll-orchestrator detects the new entity in the corporate register and triggers an automatic grouping check.

Task("payroll-tax-agent", {
  action: "grouping-check",
  trigger: "corporate-structure-change",
  event: "new-related-entity-detected",
  newEntity: {
    name: "Metro Digital Holdings Pty Ltd",
    relationship: "holding-company",
    annualWages: 0
  },
  existingEntities: [
    {
      name: "Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd",
      jurisdiction: "VIC",
      annualWages: 762000
    }
  ]
})

The payroll-tax-agent checks the grouping rules for VIC. Metro Digital Holdings has zero wages. Adding a zero-wage holding entity does not change the VIC payroll tax position for the current year. The agent returns: group wages unchanged, threshold proximity status unchanged, AMBER flag remains from prior monitoring. However, the agent notes: if Metro Digital Holdings later pays wages (director fees, management fees treated as wages, or employee wages), the group wage calculation must be updated immediately. Gate: H3 — Sasha's practitioner reviews the corporate structure change and confirms the grouping conclusion. The practitioner notes that director fees paid to the holding company director may themselves be taxable wages — verify with SRO Vic.

What this illustrates: Corporate structure changes trigger immediate grouping reassessment. A zero-wage entity may be innocuous today. A holding company that later pays director fees or management fees may change the group's liability. The payroll-tax-agent catches the change at the point of restructure, not twelve months later at annual reconciliation.

The payroll-tax-agent, payroll-engine, and payroll-orchestrator form a closed loop for payroll tax compliance. The engine computes figures. The agent applies jurisdictional rules, grouping logic, and contractor provisions. The orchestrator manages schedules, entity registers, and escalation paths. But the loop is not closed until a human practitioner — operating at H3, or H2 where tax consequences arise — reviews and approves every lodgement.

State and territory payroll tax is state and territory law. It changes when state budgets change. It is interpreted by case law that develops in each jurisdiction independently. No automated agent — however well-configured — can substitute for a practitioner who knows the current rules in the relevant jurisdiction. The payroll-tax-agent is a precision tool. Use it as one.

My take: The most dangerous payroll tax position is the one that has never been assessed. Employers who have never registered, never done a grouping check, and never looked at their contractor arrangements are merely uninformed, not safe. The payroll-tax-agent turns "we don't know" into "we have looked, here are the facts, here is what the practitioner confirmed." That is the only defensible position.

Before I close this chapter: every rate, threshold, contractor provision, exemption, and lodgement rule referenced here must be verified against the relevant state revenue authority as at the date of lodgement. These rules change annually. They vary by jurisdiction. The figures in this chapter are indicative only, as at June 2026. You have been warned — more than once, intentionally.

Next: Chapter 09 covers workers' compensation and contractor classification — premium calculation, deemed workers, the multi-factor test, and the classification-agent that applies it.

09 Workers' Compensation and Contractor Classification

Premium calculation, deemed workers, contractor vs employee, and the classification-agent

Workers' compensation and contractor classification sit at the intersection of industrial law, state revenue law, and payroll obligation. Get the classification wrong and every downstream obligation — SG, payroll tax, entitlements, workers' comp — lands on your plate. This chapter shows you how the classification-agent runs the multi-factor test, how the payroll engine calculates premium bases, and where the human gates are non-negotiable.

Workers' compensation — state-based premium calculation

There is no federal workers' compensation scheme for private sector employers. Each state and territory runs its own. The scheme name, administrator, premium calculation methodology, and rateable remuneration base differ across every jurisdiction. If you operate in more than one state, you have multiple registrations, multiple lodgements, and multiple premium bases to manage.

The rateable remuneration calculation is the engine's, not the agent's. It applies the jurisdiction-specific rules against payroll data to produce a figure the scheme can use. What it cannot do is lodge or negotiate — those are H1 functions.

Workers' compensation schemes by state/territory — current as at June 2026 — verify against each scheme administrator
State/Territory Scheme name Administrator Rateable remuneration base Deemed worker provisions Verify at
NSW Workers Compensation Scheme icare NSW Wages + super + most allowances; excludes some reimbursements Yes — extended definition under Workers Compensation Act 1987 icare.nsw.gov.au
VIC WorkSafe Victoria WorkSafe VIC Rateable remuneration — statutory definition; excludes overtime in some categories Yes — deemed workers in Workplace Injury Rehabilitation and Compensation Act 2013 worksafe.vic.gov.au
QLD WorkCover Queensland WorkCover QLD All wages, salaries, commissions, bonuses; verify exclusions list Yes — deemed workers under Workers' Compensation and Rehabilitation Act 2003 workcoverqld.com.au
SA ReturnToWork SA ReturnToWork SA Remuneration as defined — includes super, some allowances Yes — deemed workers under Return to Work Act 2014 rtwsa.com
WA WorkCover WA WorkCover WA Earnings — broad statutory definition; verify WA-specific exclusions Yes — deemed workers under Workers' Compensation and Injury Management Act 1981 workcover.wa.gov.au
TAS WorkSafe Tasmania WorkSafe TAS Wages and salary; verify TAS remuneration definition Yes — deemed workers provisions apply; verify current position worksafe.tas.gov.au
NT NT WorkSafe NT WorkSafe Wages — verify NT-specific definition and exclusions Yes — verify current NT deemed worker provisions worksafe.nt.gov.au
ACT ACT WorkSafe ACT WorkSafe / Comcare Verify — ACT uses Comcare for some employers; remuneration base varies Yes — verify ACT deemed worker position under Workers Compensation Act 1951 worksafe.act.gov.au

My take: The table above is a starting map, not an authoritative reference. Every scheme has its own legislative definition of "remuneration" and its own exclusions. Before you run a premium calculation in any state, pull the current legislation and the scheme's own guidance document. The definitions shift with amendments, and you cannot rely on memory or last year's knowledge base entry.

The payroll engine's workers' comp module runs per jurisdiction. You tell it the state, the industry classification code, the rateable remuneration pool, and the claim history band. It returns the premium base and applies the published rate. The rate is the insurer's figure — the engine does not fabricate it.

payroll_engine.calculate_workers_comp_premium({
  jurisdiction: "NSW",
  rateableRemuneration: 850000,
  industryCode: "E3412",
  claimHistory: "STANDARD"
})

The engine returns:

WORKERS COMP PREMIUM — NSW
Rateable remuneration:    $850,000
Industry code:            E3412 (Civil and Structural Engineering)
Industry rate:            [fetch from icare current rate schedule]
Claim history adjustment: STANDARD — no modifier applied
Estimated annual premium: [engine applies published rate — verify with icare]
Basis date:               June 2026
Source required:          icare.nsw.gov.au current rate schedule
noOverride: true
Engine: payroll-engine v4.1 workers-comp module

Note the engine flags "Source required." Rates change annually, so the engine fetches or prompts for the current published rate rather than embedding one. That is the correct behaviour. Any system that embeds a hardcoded workers' comp rate is a liability.

Example 9.1 (assumed-fact illustration): Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd operates across NSW and VIC. The payroll-orchestrator identifies two separate workers' comp registrations and triggers a jurisdiction-split calculation.

The payroll engine runs the NSW module against $850,000 of NSW rateable remuneration (industry code E3412) and the VIC module against $420,000 of VIC rateable remuneration (equivalent VIC industry classification). The engine returns two premium base figures and two rate-fetch prompts — one for icare, one for WorkSafe VIC. The payroll-orchestrator queues both for the payroll manager to confirm against the current rate schedules before the premium declaration is lodged. H1 gate fires before lodgement: a registered tax agent or BAS agent must review and sign off on the annual declaration where it involves formal ATO correspondence or STP-linked items. The workers' comp declaration itself is lodged with the scheme, not the ATO — but if super or STP figures underpin the rateable remuneration, H1 applies to those linked figures.

What this illustrates: Multi-jurisdiction employers need separate registrations and separate calculations. The payroll engine splits these automatically. Do not merge the premium bases — the schemes have different definitions and different rates.

Deemed workers — contractor provisions in state laws

Every state workers' compensation scheme has a deemed worker definition. It extends coverage to some workers who would otherwise be classified as independent contractors. The scope varies significantly across jurisdictions.

Deemed worker provisions typically capture:

  • Owner-drivers who provide a vehicle and transport goods or materials for a principal
  • Labour hire workers placed by a labour hire firm
  • Working directors of family companies in some schemes
  • Outworkers in the clothing and textile industry
  • Commission-only agents and sales representatives in some schemes
  • Home-based workers in some schemes

The classification-agent runs a deemed worker sub-check as part of every contractor assessment. It checks the jurisdiction's current deemed worker schedule against the worker's arrangement.

/classification-agent run-deemed-worker-check --worker-id CTR-0391 --jurisdiction "VIC" --arrangement "owner-driver-freight"
DEEMED WORKER CHECK — VIC
Worker:              Marcus Webb (ID: CTR-0391)
Jurisdiction:        Victoria
Arrangement:         Owner-driver — freight delivery
Scheme reference:    Workplace Injury Rehabilitation and Compensation Act 2013
Deemed worker flag:  LIKELY — owner-driver arrangements covered under VIC deemed worker schedule
Workers comp obligation: PRINCIPAL may be liable for premium on Marcus's remuneration
Verify:              Current VIC deemed worker schedule with WorkSafe VIC
H3 gate:             YES — practitioner review before proceeding on premium liability
Note:                Deemed worker status for workers' comp does NOT automatically determine
                     employment status for Fair Work, SG, or payroll tax purposes
noOverride: true
Engine: classification-agent v1.8 deemed-worker module

My take: The deemed worker check is one of the most under-used functions in payroll practice. Businesses routinely engage owner-drivers, outworkers, and certain commission agents without ever checking the state workers' comp legislation. The premium liability sits with the principal — and it accumulates from the start of the engagement, not from the date of discovery. Run the check at engagement, not at audit.

The agent's note is load-bearing: deemed worker status for workers' comp is a separate legal question from employment status under the Fair Work Act. A worker can be a deemed worker for comp purposes and still be a genuine independent contractor for SG, payroll tax, and FBT purposes. The reverse is also true. Do not conflate the tests.

Example 9.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Adelaide Retail Services engages Priya on an outworker basis — she assembles promotional kits at home and is paid per kit completed. Adelaide Retail Services has never registered her for workers' compensation. The classification-agent's deemed worker module flags her arrangement against the SA Return to Work Act 2014.

The engine returns a LIKELY flag for deemed worker status under SA's outworker provisions. H3 gate fires: the payroll manager must take the result to a practitioner before proceeding. The practitioner confirms the deemed worker status applies and calculates the retroactive premium exposure from the start of the engagement. ReturnToWork SA is notified. The payroll manager also separates this finding from the contractor-vs-employee analysis — Priya's results-based payment structure and right to delegate are strong contractor indicators under the Fair Work Act multi-factor test, but those factors are irrelevant to the deemed worker question under the SA Act.

What this illustrates: Deemed worker provisions bite regardless of the contractual label and regardless of the Fair Work classification. Run the check in the engagement jurisdiction. Do not assume a contractor is outside the workers' comp net.

The contractor vs employee distinction — multi-factor test

The High Court's decisions in CFMMEU v Personnel Contracting and ZG Operations v Jamsek (both 2022) shifted the primary focus to the terms of the written contract. Where a written contract exists, courts look first at what the parties actually agreed. But the multi-factor analysis still governs where no written contract exists, or where the contract does not address a factor, or where conduct post-dating the contract redefines the relationship.

The classification-agent runs the multi-factor test across seven dimensions. Each factor is assessed on the available facts. The engine produces a scorecard, a risk classification, and a gate recommendation. The determination itself is H3 where tax consequences are present.

The seven factors the agent assesses:

  • Control over work: Does the principal control how, where, and when the work is done?
  • Integration into the business: Does the worker form part of the principal's organisation or are they external?
  • Results-based vs time-based payment: Is the worker paid for outcomes or for hours?
  • Right to delegate: Can the worker send a substitute?
  • Equipment provision: Who supplies tools, materials, and infrastructure?
  • Ability to work for others: Is the worker free to work for other principals concurrently?
  • Exclusivity: Does the arrangement prevent the worker from building an independent business?
Task("classification-agent", {
  action: "multi-factor-test",
  workerId: "EMP-2847",
  employer: "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
  factors: {
    controlOverWork: "HIGH — supervisor directs daily tasks and site attendance hours",
    integrationIntoBusiness: "MEDIUM — works alongside employees on client sites",
    resultsVsTimeBased: "TIME — paid hourly rate regardless of output",
    rightToDelegate: "NO — must perform work personally under contract",
    equipmentProvision: "EMPLOYER — all tools, PPE, and site access provided by Harbour Engineering",
    abilityToWorkForOthers: "LIMITED — exclusivity clause in agreement",
    exclusivity: "YES — agreement prohibits work for competitors"
  }
})
CLASSIFICATION RESULT — WORKPAPER
Worker:               James Thornton (ID: EMP-2847)
Employer:             Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd
Test date:            June 2026
Factors assessed:     7
Factors toward employee:   6
Factors toward contractor: 1
Risk classification:  HIGH — likely employee
Recommended action:   H3 gate — practitioner review before proceeding
H1 gate required:     YES — reclassification has tax consequences
Contract reviewed:    YES — written agreement in place
CFMMEU/Jamsek note:   Contract terms reviewed; conduct post-dating contract flagged
                      (exclusivity clause unenforced in practice — verify)
noOverride: true
Engine: classification-agent v1.8
Multi-factor test scorecard — James Thornton / Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd — June 2026
Factor Employee indicator Contractor indicator James's situation Weight
Control over work Principal directs how/when/where Worker controls method and timing Supervisor directs daily tasks and hours Toward employee
Integration into business Forms part of organisation External, independent of organisation Works alongside employees on client sites Toward employee (medium)
Results vs time-based payment Paid for time (hourly/salary) Paid for results or deliverables Paid hourly rate regardless of output Toward employee
Right to delegate Must perform personally Can send a substitute No right to delegate under contract Toward employee
Equipment provision Principal supplies tools and infrastructure Worker supplies own tools/equipment All tools and PPE supplied by Harbour Engineering Toward employee
Ability to work for others Restricted or exclusive to principal Free to work for multiple principals Exclusivity clause in agreement (unenforced in practice — verify) Toward employee
Own business goodwill/risk No business risk, no goodwill Carries own business risk, builds goodwill No evidence of independent business or goodwill Toward employee

Six out of seven factors point toward employee. The one contractor-leaning factor — partial integration — is weak. The classification-agent assigns HIGH risk. H3 gate fires before any action is taken based on this result.

My take: The 2022 High Court decisions tightened the analysis but did not eliminate it. The written contract matters more now than it did before those decisions — but "matters more" is not "matters exclusively." Conduct that departs from the written terms, terms that were never negotiated at arm's length, and sham contracting scenarios all remain live issues. The multi-factor scorecard is still the right tool. Use it. Document it. Gate it at H3.

Example 9.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Blue Mountain Healthcare engages Nathan as a "contractor" to provide physiotherapy services to patients on a sessional basis. Nathan works three days a week at the clinic, uses Blue Mountain's rooms and equipment, sees patients allocated by the receptionist, and is paid per session at a rate set by the clinic. He has no other clients.

The classification-agent runs the multi-factor test. Results: high control (Blue Mountain allocates patients and sets the session rate), high integration (part of the clinical team, uses clinic infrastructure), time/results ambiguous (paid per session but sessions are controlled), no right to delegate (verified against written agreement), all equipment supplied by Blue Mountain, no ability to work for others (effectively exclusive). Score: 5.5 toward employee, 0.5 toward contractor. Risk classification: HIGH. H3 gate fires. The practitioner reviews the arrangement and advises that Nathan is likely an employee under common law. H1 gate fires next: the reclassification has SG, PAYG withholding, and STP consequences that require a registered tax agent. The award-agent is invoked in parallel to check applicable award coverage — Nathan's role as a physiotherapy practitioner in a private practice setting may engage the Health Professionals and Support Services Award 2020. AWARD-GOVERNS: verify applicable award against current FWC award list.

What this illustrates: Session-based "contractors" in health settings are a high-risk classification. The per-session payment structure does not make the arrangement results-based when the principal controls every other aspect of the work. Run the full multi-factor test — do not accept the contractual label.

The label trap — 'contractor' in the agreement is not determinative

The agreement says "independent contractor." The invoice says "ABN." The worker has a company structure. None of this is determinative. The substance of the arrangement governs, not the label the parties chose.

This is explicit in Fair Work Act case law and in the ATO's guidance on personal services income. A worker who operates through a company and invoices with an ABN can still be found to be an employee of the principal for super guarantee purposes. The SG liability follows the substance of the relationship.

The classification-agent is specifically designed to look past the label. The "arrangement type" field in the multi-factor input accepts the documented label — "contractor," "consultant," "freelancer," "sole trader," "ABN worker" — but the engine disregards it when scoring. The label is recorded for the workpaper. It does not move the needle on any factor.

/classification-agent run-multi-factor --worker-id CTR-1120 --employer "Metro Digital Solutions" --label "independent contractor" --note "ABN registered, invoices fortnightly"
CLASSIFICATION RESULT — WORKPAPER
Worker:               Chloe Nakamura (ID: CTR-1120)
Employer:             Metro Digital Solutions
Documented label:     Independent contractor (ABN registered)
Test date:            June 2026
Engine note:          Label recorded. Label not used in factor scoring.
                      ABN registration noted. ABN status does not determine employment relationship.
Factors toward employee:   5
Factors toward contractor: 2
Risk classification:  MEDIUM-HIGH — indicators lean employee
Recommended action:   H3 gate — practitioner review before proceeding
H1 gate required:     YES — if reclassification proceeds, SG and PAYG consequences apply
noOverride: true
Engine: classification-agent v1.8

The engine's note is explicit: ABN registration does not determine the employment relationship. The ATO has stated this position repeatedly. Businesses that rely on the ABN as a shield are carrying undisclosed SG liability.

PSI rules add another layer. Even where a contractor is genuinely not an employee, if the worker's income is personal services income under the PSI regime, the PSI rules may attribute that income to the individual and deny business deductions. The classification-agent flags PSI risk as a secondary output where the primary factors are ambiguous. PSI analysis is H2 — it requires a registered tax agent.

SECONDARY OUTPUT — PSI FLAG
Worker:               Chloe Nakamura (ID: CTR-1120)
PSI risk:             MEDIUM — income is principally from personal efforts
                      (not from supply of equipment or sale of goods)
PSI test suggested:   Results test, 80% rule, unrelated clients test
H2 gate:              YES — PSI analysis requires registered tax agent
Note:                 PSI finding is separate from employment classification.
                      Both analyses may be required simultaneously.
Engine: classification-agent v1.8 psi-module

My take: I see clients arrive with folders of "contractor agreements" that have never been stress-tested against the substance of the work. The agreement says contractor. The arrangement is indistinguishable from employment. The risk has been building for years. The classification-agent is a diagnostic for that, not a cure. Run it at engagement, well before the first ATO enquiry letter.

Example 9.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Westfield Logistics engages Mei under a "subcontractor agreement" to perform data entry and scheduling work. Mei works from the Westfield Logistics office, uses their computers and systems, works 9am to 5pm Monday to Friday, attends team meetings, and has been in the arrangement for 22 months. She invoices monthly through her ABN sole trader registration.

The classification-agent runs the full multi-factor test. High control (set hours, employer premises, employer systems), high integration (attends team meetings, indistinguishable from employees in practice), time-based payment (monthly invoice approximates a salary), no right to delegate (confirmed), all equipment supplied by Westfield Logistics, no ability to work for others (full-time hours leave no capacity), no independent business. Score: 7 toward employee, 0 toward contractor. Risk classification: VERY HIGH. H3 gate fires immediately. H1 gate follows: reclassification carries PAYG, SG, and STP consequences requiring a registered tax agent. The 22-month duration amplifies the back-pay and SGC exposure. AWARD-GOVERNS: the award-agent checks applicable award coverage for Mei's data entry and scheduling duties — likely the Clerks Private Sector Award 2020, but verify against current FWC award list.

What this illustrates: Duration matters. A 22-month misclassification is a liability that has been compounding monthly, not a minor administrative error. The earlier the classification-agent runs, the smaller the exposure.

Misclassification consequences — back-pay, SG, payroll tax exposure

Misclassification carries real cost. Every period the worker was misclassified generates a recoverable obligation. The consequences cascade across multiple obligations simultaneously.

Misclassification consequence matrix — current as at June 2026 — verify against ATO, FWC, and state revenue authority
Obligation Consequence if misclassified Recovery/penalty exposure Gate
National Employment Standards entitlements Back-pay of annual leave, personal leave, notice, redundancy where applicable Full entitlement from commencement — plus interest; FWC enforcement H3 (review) then H1 (tax consequences)
Award entitlements Back-pay of underpaid rates, penalty rates, overtime, loadings under applicable award Full shortfall from commencement; FWC enforcement; civil penalties H3 (award compliance) — AWARD-GOVERNS
Superannuation Guarantee SG contributions not paid; SGC = shortfall + nominal interest (10% p.a.) + $20 admin fee per employee per quarter — non-deductible SGC shortfall for each quarter plus nominal interest (10% p.a.) and $20 admin fee per employee per quarter — verify current amounts against ATO; SGC is non-deductible to the employer; Part 7 additional SGC of up to 200% of the charge may apply for failure to lodge an SGC statement by its due date — verify current ATO position H1 — SG shortfall disclosure and SGC lodgement require registered tax agent
PAYG withholding Withholding not deducted from payments; employer liable for amounts not withheld ATO may pursue employer for unwithheld amounts plus penalties and interest; GIC applies H1 — formal ATO correspondence; registered tax agent required
Payroll tax Payments not included in taxable wages base; state revenue authority may reassess Reassessment plus interest plus penalty for each open year — verify against relevant state revenue authority; no federal payroll tax H3 (review) then H1 (ATO correspondence if linked) or state revenue equivalent
Workers' compensation Premiums not paid on worker's remuneration; scheme may demand retrospective premium Retrospective premium from engagement start; penalty loading possible — verify with relevant scheme H3 (review) then scheme-specific lodgement process
FBT reconsideration Benefits provided to "contractor" may require FBT reanalysis as employee benefits FBT liability plus shortfall penalty and interest for each FBT year — verify with registered tax agent H1 — FBT is a formal tax liability; registered tax agent required
Sham contracting (Fair Work Act s357) Representing employment as contracting is a civil penalty offence Civil penalty — verify current maximum penalty amount against Fair Work Commission; penalties can apply to individuals as well as entities H2 — formal legal consequence; requires specialist advice

Sham contracting under Fair Work Act s357 deserves separate emphasis. Section 357 makes it a civil penalty offence for an employer to represent to a worker that their employment contract is a contract for services (that is, a contractor arrangement) when the employer knows or reasonably should know the representation is false. The offence is about the misrepresentation, not just the misclassification. Verify the current maximum penalty amount against the Fair Work Commission — penalties are subject to indexation and legislative change.

My take: The SGC non-deductibility point deserves more attention than it gets. If you owe SG, the ordinary superannuation deduction is unavailable — you pay SGC instead. SGC is non-deductible. That means the after-tax cost of the SGC liability is higher than the cost of compliant super contributions would have been. Every month of misclassification compounds this. The business case for getting it right at engagement is straightforward arithmetic.

The payroll engine calculates the exposure. Negotiation and disclosure sit outside its remit — disclosure is H1. The engine's job is to produce the workpaper that the registered tax agent needs to make decisions.

payroll_engine.calculate_misclassification_exposure({
  workerId: "CTR-1120",
  employer: "Metro Digital Solutions",
  engagementStart: "2024-08-01",
  assessmentDate: "2026-06-24",
  averageMonthlyPayment: 8500,
  jurisdiction: "VIC",
  sgRate: 0.12,
  applicableAward: "PENDING-H3-REVIEW"
})
MISCLASSIFICATION EXPOSURE WORKPAPER
Worker:               Chloe Nakamura (ID: CTR-1120)
Employer:             Metro Digital Solutions
Period:               2024-08-01 to 2026-06-24 (22 months)
Total payments:       ~$187,000 (22 months x $8,500)

SG EXPOSURE
SG rate:              12% (from 1 July 2025) — verify current rate against ATO
Periods pre-1-Jul-25: 11 months at 11.5% — verify rate for each quarter against ATO
SG shortfall estimate: [engine calculates per quarter — figure produced in workpaper]
SGC nominal interest (10% pa): [applied per quarter — non-deductible]
SGC admin levy:       [per employee per quarter — verify current ATO amount]
Note: SGC non-deductible. Deductible super contribution opportunity lost.

PAYG EXPOSURE
Gross payments:       ~$187,000
PAYG liability:       [direct to ATO tax tables — engine does not produce PAYG amounts]
GIC:                  [applied from each withholding due date — ATO calculates]

PAYROLL TAX — VIC
Taxable wages:        ~$187,000 (verify VIC definition — no federal payroll tax)
Period:               2024-08-01 to 2026-06-24
Verify against:       State Revenue Office Victoria
Penalty and interest: Per SRO VIC guidance — verify

WORKERS COMP — VIC
Rateable remuneration: ~$187,000 (verify VIC definition)
Retrospective premium: [fetch from WorkSafe VIC rate schedule — engine does not embed rate]

LEAVE ENTITLEMENTS
Annual leave accrual: [engine calculates based on award/NES — requires H3 award determination]
Personal/carer leave: [same — H3 required]

Gate summary:
  H3 gate: award determination, payroll tax review, workers comp review
  H1 gate: SG disclosure, PAYG, FBT, formal ATO correspondence
  H2 gate: sham contracting — if intentional misrepresentation is in scope

noOverride: true
Engine: payroll-engine v4.1 misclassification-exposure module

The engine produces the workpaper. Every gate fires in sequence. H3 before H1. H1 before disclosure. Do not skip the sequence.

Example 9.5 (assumed-fact illustration): Marcus has been engaged by Westfield Logistics as a "contractor" for 3 years. The payroll-orchestrator runs the misclassification exposure engine after the classification-agent returns a VERY HIGH risk flag. The engine calculates exposure across SG (12 quarters, two different SG rates — verify each against ATO for the relevant quarter), PAYG, payroll tax (QLD — verify against Queensland Revenue Office), workers' compensation (WorkCover QLD), and NES leave entitlements pending H3 award determination. The AWARD-GOVERNS flag fires: the award-agent checks applicable award for Marcus's transport logistics duties. The payroll-orchestrator routes the full workpaper to the H3 queue for practitioner review. The practitioner escalates to H1 for SG disclosure and formal ATO engagement. No voluntary disclosure is made until the H1 gate is cleared.

What this illustrates: Three years of misclassification across multiple states generates exposure in every jurisdiction. The payroll engine produces the full exposure workpaper in one pass — but no action is taken until the gate sequence is satisfied. The gates exist because the consequences are irreversible. Premature disclosure without registered tax agent oversight can foreclose options.

classification-agent — multi-factor check and risk flag

The classification-agent is a standalone agent in the payroll-orchestrator pipeline. It runs at engagement and on a periodic refresh cycle, producing risk flags, workpapers, and gate recommendations rather than determinations.

The agent has four modules:

  • multi-factor: Runs the seven-factor test, produces a scorecard and risk classification
  • deemed-worker: Checks state-specific deemed worker provisions for the engagement jurisdiction
  • psi-module: Assesses whether the worker's income is personal services income and flags PSI rules risk
  • exposure-router: On HIGH or VERY HIGH risk, triggers the exposure workpaper and queues for gate review

The classification-agent is triggered in three scenarios:

  • New engagement — when a worker is onboarded with a contractor or consultant label
  • Periodic refresh — annually, or when the engagement terms change materially
  • Flag on payroll review — when the payroll-orchestrator detects a pattern that suggests misclassification (for example, a "contractor" who has been in the same role for more than 12 months with no other clients)
/classification-agent run-full --worker-id CTR-0847 --employer "Blue Mountain Healthcare" --trigger "annual-refresh" --engagement-months 18
CLASSIFICATION-AGENT — FULL RUN
Worker:               Priya Desai (ID: CTR-0847)
Employer:             Blue Mountain Healthcare
Trigger:              Annual refresh — 18 months into engagement
Modules run:          multi-factor, deemed-worker (NSW), psi-module, exposure-router

MULTI-FACTOR RESULT:
  Factors toward employee: 5
  Factors toward contractor: 2
  Risk: MEDIUM-HIGH

DEEMED WORKER — NSW:
  Arrangement: allied health contractor — private practice setting
  Deemed worker flag: POSSIBLE — verify current NSW schedule with icare
  H3 gate: YES

PSI MODULE:
  PSI flag: MEDIUM — income principally from personal efforts
  Results test: NOT CLEARLY MET
  80% rule: POSSIBLE BREACH — verify with registered tax agent
  H2 gate: YES — PSI analysis requires registered tax agent

EXPOSURE ROUTER:
  Exposure workpaper: QUEUED — 18 months of payments
  Gate sequence: H3 → H1 → H2
  Priority: HIGH

noOverride: true
Engine: classification-agent v1.8

The exposure-router module produces the workpaper automatically on HIGH or VERY HIGH flags, without waiting for a manual trigger. That is the correct behaviour for a payroll system designed to surface risk early.

The classification-agent interacts with the award-agent on every engagement that reaches H3. If the multi-factor test indicates likely employee, the award-agent checks applicable award coverage before the practitioner review. The practitioner needs to know the award classification before advising on back-pay exposure. Getting the award wrong in the reclassification workpaper produces an incorrect back-pay number.

Task("award-agent", {
  action: "check-coverage",
  workerRole: "physiotherapy practitioner",
  industry: "private health services",
  employer: "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
  state: "NSW",
  trigger: "H3-classification-review"
})
AWARD COVERAGE CHECK
Worker role:          Physiotherapy practitioner
Industry:             Private health services
Suggested award:      Health Professionals and Support Services Award 2020
Classification level: [verify — classification depends on qualifications and duties]
Note:                 AWARD-GOVERNS — verify applicable award and classification level
                      against current FWC award list and Blue Mountain Healthcare's
                      specific enterprise agreement position (if any)
H3 gate:              Practitioner must confirm award before back-pay calculation proceeds
Engine: award-agent v2.3

My take: The classification-agent is a structured pre-screening tool, not a replacement for legal advice. It forces the right questions to be asked at the right time, routes the risk to the right gate, and produces a documented workpaper that the practitioner can act on. The alternative — relying on the contract label and assuming everything is fine — is simply a deferred liability.

On periodic refresh, the classification-agent checks for red flags that may have emerged since the last run. These include:

  • Duration creep — engagement now exceeds 12 months with no change in terms
  • Single-client concentration — worker has no other disclosed clients
  • Equipment changes — principal has started supplying equipment since last assessment
  • Rate changes — payment has shifted from results-based to time-based or vice versa
  • Location changes — worker now working from principal's premises
Task("classification-agent", {
  action: "refresh-check",
  workerId: "CTR-0847",
  employer: "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
  lastAssessmentDate: "2025-12-01",
  refreshDate: "2026-06-24",
  changesSinceLastAssessment: {
    duration: "18 months total — red flag threshold exceeded",
    singleClient: true,
    equipmentChanges: "none",
    paymentStructure: "unchanged — time-based",
    locationChanges: "now working from clinic premises 3 days/week"
  }
})
REFRESH CHECK — DELTA ANALYSIS
Changes flagged since last assessment:
  [RED FLAG] Duration: 18 months total — exceeds 12-month red flag threshold
  [RED FLAG] Single client: confirmed — no other disclosed clients
  [RED FLAG] Location: now working from clinic premises 3 days/week (was remote previously)
Delta risk movement: MEDIUM → HIGH
Recommended action: Immediate H3 gate — do not wait for next scheduled refresh
Trigger: Exposure workpaper queued automatically
noOverride: true
Engine: classification-agent v1.8 refresh-module

The refresh module escalates automatically when red flags accumulate. It queues the H3 gate and notifies the payroll-orchestrator the moment the finding lands. The payroll manager receives a task item the same day.

The full classification pipeline — initial assessment, refresh, exposure workpaper, award check, gate routing — is fully automated up to the H3 gate. From H3 onward, the human is in the loop. That is the design. The engine surfaces the risk with precision. The practitioner exercises the judgement. The tax agent handles the consequences. No role is substituted for another.

Example 9.6 (assumed-fact illustration): Metro Digital Solutions runs a quarterly payroll audit. The payroll-orchestrator scans all contractor engagements and flags four workers for classification-agent refresh runs. For three of the four, the refresh returns LOW or MEDIUM risk with no delta escalation. For the fourth — Marcus Webb, engaged for 26 months, no other disclosed clients, works from Metro Digital's premises five days a week — the refresh returns VERY HIGH risk with three concurrent red flags. The exposure-router queues a 26-month workpaper covering SG (at 11.5% for pre-July 2025 quarters and 12% for post-July 2025 quarters — verify each quarter against ATO), PAYG (direct to ATO tax tables), payroll tax (NSW — verify against Revenue NSW; no federal payroll tax), WorkCover NSW (verify rateable remuneration definition with icare), and NES entitlements pending H3 award determination. The award-agent runs in parallel. H3 gate fires. The practitioner takes the workpaper and the award-agent output into a formal review meeting with Metro Digital Solutions' directors. H1 gate is set for the following week pending the practitioner's H3 determination. H2 gate is flagged as possible if the directors' email trail suggests the contractor label was a deliberate choice made in full knowledge of the legal position.

What this illustrates: The classification pipeline catches accumulating risk that a one-off engagement check would miss. The quarterly audit cycle is the mechanism that stops 26-month exposures from becoming 36-month exposures. Run the refresh. Document the result. Gate the consequence.

One operational note: the classification-agent does not communicate results to workers. It communicates to the payroll manager via the payroll-orchestrator task queue. Workers are not notified of a classification review until the H3 gate has been cleared and the practitioner has advised on next steps. Premature notification can create legal complexity, particularly where sham contracting provisions are in play. This is a design constraint, not an oversight.

State payroll tax: where reclassification occurs, the contractor payments that were excluded from the taxable wages base must be reconsidered. Each state has its own payroll tax legislation and its own contractor provisions. Some states have contractor "relevant contracts" provisions that may have already captured the payments — in which case, the payroll tax exposure may be lower than expected. Others will not have captured them. There is no federal payroll tax. Always verify against the relevant state revenue authority for every state in which the employer operates.

Next: Chapter 10 covers year-end STP finalisation — the 14 July deadline, income statement verification, payroll reconciliation, and the stp-finalisation-agent. Every figure in the classification analysis flows into the year-end reconciliation. Get this chapter right before you close the year.

10 Year-End STP Finalisation and Payroll Reconciliation

STP finalisation declaration, the income statement, reconciling payroll to BAS and financial statements, and the stp-finalisation-agent

The end of the financial year is when every payroll error from the previous twelve months surfaces at once. STP finalisation locks the year's data, marks the employee's income statement as tax ready, and triggers the individual income tax return pre-fill. Get it wrong and your employees file incorrect returns, your PAYG reconciliation fails, and the ATO's data-matching system flags your business. The stp-finalisation-agent assembles, checks, and gates the reconciliation — but a registered tax agent or BAS agent must lodge. That gate is non-negotiable.

STP Finalisation Declaration — 14 July Deadline

The finalisation event is a specific STP submission type. It tells the ATO that the employer has reviewed and confirmed the year-to-date figures for each employee and that no further changes will be made. Once lodged, the employee's income statement in myGov is marked "tax ready" and the pre-fill data flows to their individual income tax return.

The deadline is 14 July following the end of the financial year — so for the 2025-26 financial year, the deadline is 14 July 2026. Verify this date against the ATO. The ATO may grant extensions, but substantial employers — generally employers with 20 or more employees — have no extension by default. Verify the ATO's current position on extensions and substantial employer thresholds before relying on any assumed grace period.

Missing the deadline carries real consequences. Employees cannot lodge their individual tax returns with pre-filled data until you finalise. The ATO can contact each affected employee directly. A failure-to-lodge penalty can apply to the employer. The stp-finalisation-agent flags the countdown from 1 July — but the obligation to actually lodge sits with the H1 gate holder.

My take: I have seen employers treat the 14 July deadline as optional, assuming they can always get an extension. That assumption fails in both directions — the ATO's extension process has conditions, and substantial employers generally do not qualify. Build the year-end reconciliation into your June calendar, not your July panic. The agent cannot lodge for you. Only the H1 holder can.

The three STP event types matter here. Understanding which event type is in play prevents over-reporting and under-reporting errors.

Event type When used Who can lodge H1 required ATO due date note
Pay event Each pay run — reports current pay plus YTD totals Employer or authorised agent No (for standard pay events) Due on or before each payday — verify against ATO
Update event Correcting a prior pay event without a new pay run Employer or authorised agent No (for corrections within scope) As soon as practicable after error identified — verify ATO guidance
Finalisation event Year-end sign-off on all YTD figures for each employee Registered tax agent or BAS agent Yes — H1 gate, non-delegable 14 July following end of financial year — verify against ATO

Current as at June 2026 — verify against ATO source before acting.

Closely held payees — shareholders, directors, or relatives of a closely held employer — have different finalisation rules. The ATO applies specific quarterly and annual reporting variations for closely held payees. These rules are materially different from arm's-length employees. As at June 2026, the finalisation due date is 30 September for closely held payees at employers with 20 or more employees (including mixed workforces); for small employers (19 or fewer employees) with only closely held payees, the payee's own income tax return due date applies. Verify the current ATO position on closely held payee finalisation before lodging. Do not assume standard 14 July rules apply.

The year-to-date figures are produced by the engine, not the agent. The finalisation event that the H1 gate holder lodges must match what the payroll engine has computed and the stp-finalisation-agent has reconciled. Any manual adjustment to those figures between reconciliation and lodgement must be documented and re-reviewed.

The Income Statement — What Employees See

Before STP, employees received a payment summary — a physical or electronic document the employer generated and distributed. The employer also lodged a payment summary annual report with the ATO. STP Phase 2 replaced this. The income statement is the ATO's live view of the year-to-date data your STP submissions have reported.

The income statement in myGov aggregates the disaggregated STP2 components. Employees can see their gross earnings, tax withheld, and super contributions updated after each pay event. Before finalisation, the statement is marked "not tax ready." After the employer lodges the finalisation event, it becomes "tax ready" and the data flows to the pre-fill in the individual income tax return.

What the income statement includes — and what it does not include — matters. It does not replace the need for the employee to review their return. Pre-fill is a starting point for self-assessment, never a substitute for it. The employee (or their tax agent) must still confirm that the income statement is accurate, check for income from multiple employers, and ensure all deductions are correctly claimed.

The income statement aggregates these main components, drawn from the STP2 pay events through the year:

Income statement field STP2 source Common error at year-end Agent check
Gross income Aggregated gross field across all pay events Allowances included in gross instead of reported separately stp-finalisation-agent flags allowance misclassification
Tax withheld PAYG withholding reported per pay event Manual adjustments not reflected in STP; timing mismatch with BAS Reconcile against BAS W1/W2 amounts
Employer super contributions Reported from payroll — not from super fund Reported in STP but not yet cleared at fund; timing variance Cross-reference super fund clearing house statements
Reportable employer super contributions (RESC) Salary sacrifice super above the SG minimum All salary sacrifice reported as RESC when some may be SG Engine distinguishes SG from discretionary; verify classification
Reportable fringe benefits (RFBA) Employer-reported, not via STP — via payroll software integration FBT year mismatch (FBT year ends 31 March, not 30 June) Flag for H3 review if RFBA amounts appear

Current as at June 2026 — verify against ATO source before acting.

The income statement flows from what you reported in STP, not from the super fund. If you reported SG contributions in STP but the payment to the fund was late or was returned by the fund, the income statement may overstate the employee's super. Reconcile the STP-reported super against the super fund's own member statements before finalising.

Example 10.1 (assumed-fact illustration): Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd employs Angela as a registered nurse. Angela salary-sacrifices $15,000 per annum into super on top of the employer SG obligation. Throughout the year, the payroll system correctly reports the SG component and the salary sacrifice component separately in each pay event. At year-end, the stp-finalisation-agent runs the reconciliation and detects that two pay events in October reported all super as a single "employer contribution" field without distinguishing the SG from the salary sacrifice. The RESC figure on Angela's income statement is understated. The agent flags this as an AMBER variance and raises it for H3 review before lodgement.

The payroll engine re-runs the October periods using the correct component breakdown. The RESC figure is corrected. An update event is lodged before the finalisation event. Angela's income statement then correctly shows the salary sacrifice super as RESC, which flows to her individual income tax return for the purposes of the Division 293 assessment and concessional contributions cap calculation.

What this illustrates: STP2 component disaggregation errors do not only affect the employer's reconciliation — they affect the employee's income tax position downstream. Catching the RESC misclassification before finalisation prevents Angela from lodging an incorrect return. The stp-finalisation-agent's component-level reconciliation is what surfaces this variance.

Payroll Reconciliation — Gross, Withholding, Super, Payroll Tax

The year-end reconciliation has four axes. Each must reconcile independently. A clean gross figure with a PAYG variance is not a "close enough" outcome — it is a reportable discrepancy.

Run the reconciliation before lodging the finalisation event. Do not attempt to finalise while variances remain unexplained. The stp-finalisation-agent builds the reconciliation workpaper and flags each axis as GREEN (within tolerance), AMBER (variance present but within investigation threshold), or RED (material variance requiring resolution before lodgement).

YEAR-END RECONCILIATION WORKPAPER
Employer:             Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd
Financial year:       2025-26
Run date:             June 2026

GROSS PAY
  Payroll system YTD:         $[engine output]
  STP YTD reported:           $[engine output]
  Variance:                   $[engine output]
  Status:                     [GREEN/AMBER/RED]

PAYG WITHHOLDING
  Payroll system YTD:         $[engine output]
  BAS remitted (W1/W2):       $[engine output]
  Variance:                   $[engine output]
  Status:                     [GREEN/AMBER/RED]

SUPER (SG at 12%)
  SG calculated (engine):     $[engine output]
  Super fund YTD paid:        $[engine output]
  Variance:                   $[engine output]
  Status:                     [GREEN/AMBER/RED]

PAYROLL TAX
  Lodged with SRO YTD:        $[engine output]
  Payroll system wages:       $[engine output]
  Status:                     verify against relevant state SRO

WORKERS COMPENSATION
  Premium basis YTD:          $[engine output]
  Insurer declaration basis:  $[engine output]
  Status:                     verify against insurer policy

ENGINE: payroll-engine v2.4
noOverride: true
H1 GATE: required before lodgement

The gross pay axis compares the payroll system's YTD gross against the YTD gross reported in all STP pay events for the financial year. These should match exactly. A variance here typically means a pay run was processed in the payroll system but the STP event was not lodged, or the STP event was rejected and not resubmitted.

The PAYG withholding axis compares the payroll system's total PAYG withheld against the amounts remitted to the ATO via BAS — specifically the W1 (total salary, wages, other amounts from which withholding was taken) and W2 (amounts withheld) fields across all BAS lodgements for the financial year. Timing differences between the pay date and the BAS period create the most common variances here.

The SG axis compares the payroll engine's calculated SG obligation (12% of ordinary time earnings from 1 July 2025 — verify current rate against ATO) against the actual payments made to the super fund or clearing house. Every regulated SG figure is computed by the engine, never by the agent. If the fund has returned a contribution or a member's account is inactive, the engine's calculated figure may exceed what was actually received by the fund.

One transition note applies to the SG axis for this finalisation cycle. The quarterly rhythm described above governs quarters ending on or before 30 June 2026 — Q4 2025-26 contributions are still due by 28 July 2026 under the old rules. From 1 July 2026, the Treasury Laws Amendment (Payday Superannuation) Act 2025 replaces the quarterly obligation with a per-payday one: SG must be received by the fund within 7 business days of each payday. From FY2026-27 the SG reconciliation axis becomes per-payday, not per-quarter — see chapter 03 (verify ATO payday-super guidance).

There is no federal payroll tax. Each state and territory has its own payroll tax regime with its own rates, thresholds, exemptions, and grouping rules. Always verify payroll tax figures against the relevant state revenue authority — the SRO in Victoria, Revenue NSW, RevenueSA, and so on. The reconciliation compares the wages base used for payroll tax lodgements against the payroll system's wages. Discrepancies may arise from timing of annual leave loading, contractor payments included or excluded, or grouping elections. Verify against the relevant state revenue authority.

Workers compensation premium declarations are an employer obligation, not an ATO obligation. The reconciliation checks the wages basis used for the insurer's premium calculation against the payroll system. Over- or under-declared wages affect the premium adjustment at renewal. Verify against the specific insurer's policy and the relevant state workers compensation authority.

Example 10.2 (assumed-fact illustration): Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd employs Wei as a mechanical engineer at a base salary of $110,000. In February 2026, Wei receives a one-off bonus of $18,000 which is processed through payroll. The PAYG withholding on the bonus is calculated using the ATO's tax tables for bonuses and commissions — the engine computes this amount, not the agent; do not hard-code specific figures. The bonus pay event is lodged via STP on the pay date. However, the February BAS is prepared by the bookkeeper from the payroll system's summary report which was run before the bonus was finalised. The W2 amount on the February BAS understates the PAYG withheld by the amount of the bonus withholding.

The stp-finalisation-agent's year-end reconciliation flags an AMBER variance on the PAYG axis — the payroll system YTD withholding exceeds the BAS-remitted total. The agent's investigation log points to the February BAS period. The bookkeeper amends the February BAS to include the bonus withholding. The amendment is lodged before the finalisation event. The reconciliation re-runs and the PAYG axis returns GREEN.

What this illustrates: BAS amendments arising from payroll variances must be resolved before STP finalisation, not after. Lodging the finalisation event with an unresolved PAYG variance creates a mismatch between the income statement (which shows the correct withheld amount from STP) and the ATO's BAS records. Reconciling the two axes together surfaces the problem before it becomes an ATO query.

Invoke the stp-finalisation-agent with the full set of reconciliation components to build the workpaper in a single run.

Task("stp-finalisation-agent", {
  action: "build-reconciliation",
  employer: "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
  financialYear: "2025-26",
  includeComponents: [
    "gross-pay",
    "payg-withheld",
    "sg-contributions",
    "payroll-tax",
    "workers-comp"
  ]
})

The agent calls the payroll engine for each component; the engine, not the agent, computes each figure. The agent's reconciliation workpaper is the output. The H1 gate holder reviews the workpaper before lodging the finalisation event.

Common Variances and How to Resolve Them

Year-end variances follow recognisable patterns. Knowing the pattern tells you where to look and how quickly the variance can be resolved. The stp-finalisation-agent categorises variances by type and surfaces the suggested investigation path.

Variance type Likely cause Resolution action Gate required
Gross pay — STP vs payroll system STP event not lodged for a pay run; rejected event not resubmitted Identify missing or failed STP event; lodge update event or resubmit No H1 required for update event; H1 required for finalisation
PAYG withheld — payroll vs BAS BAS prepared before end-of-month payroll; termination payment PAYG not remitted in correct BAS period Amend relevant BAS period; confirm amendment lodged before finalisation BAS amendment may require H1 or H2 depending on materiality and ATO position
SG — payroll engine vs fund paid Contribution returned by fund (inactive account, invalid TFN); timing — contribution paid after year-end Resubmit returned contributions; if timing, document as timing difference; SG Charge may apply H3 for returned contributions; H1 for SG Charge implications
Income type code mismatch Employee reported as SAW (salary and wages) when should be WHM (working holiday maker) or vice versa Lodge update event with corrected income type code; check withholding rate applied H3 if reclassification changes PAYG amount materially; H1 for finalisation
