Allowance misclassification Tool allowance, travel allowance, or overtime meal allowance included in gross rather than as a discrete allowance code Lodge update event with correct allowance disaggregation; check withholding treatment of each allowance type H3 review for allowances affecting PAYG; H1 for finalisation
Payroll tax — wages base discrepancy Contractor payments included or excluded in error; annual leave loading treatment differs between payroll and SRO return Review contractor classification using multi-factor test; verify award or contract terms; amend SRO return if required H3 for contractor multi-factor analysis; verify against relevant state revenue authority

Current as at June 2026 — verify all gate decisions and resolution actions against ATO and relevant state authority guidance.

Invoke the variance investigation action when the reconciliation returns an AMBER or RED status on any axis.

Task("stp-finalisation-agent", {
  action: "investigate-variance",
  component: "payg-withheld",
  variance: "AMBER",
  varianceDetail: "BAS remittance $4,200 below payroll system YTD",
  suggestedActions: [
    "Check October BAS amendment",
    "Review termination payments — ETP coding",
    "Verify WHM income type code"
  ]
})

The agent surfaces each variance, categorises it, identifies the most likely cause based on the payroll data, and proposes a resolution path. Resolving it autonomously is beyond its scope. Execution of that resolution path — amending a BAS, lodging an update event, resubmitting a super contribution — is a human action, gated at the appropriate level.

My take: The income type code variance is the one that consistently surprises employers at year-end. A working holiday maker on a WHM visa who was reported as SAW for the whole year has been withheld at the wrong rate. The ATO's data matching will find this. The update event corrects the STP record, but you also need to confirm whether the actual withholding remitted was at the correct rate. If not, there may be a PAYG withholding liability for the employer. That is an H1 issue, not a bookkeeper issue. Catch it before the ATO does.

Termination payments create a specific variance cluster. An employment termination payment (ETP) that was coded incorrectly at the time of termination — or where the cap was not applied correctly — will surface in the year-end reconciliation as a combination of a gross variance, a PAYG variance, and possibly a super variance (ETPs are generally not subject to SG, but the engine must confirm the nature of the payment). ETP caps and tax-free components of genuine redundancy payments are indexed annually. Always verify against the ATO — the engine computes the ETP, but only from correct inputs.

Example 10.3 (assumed-fact illustration): Adelaide Retail Services employs Sasha as a store manager. Sasha's employment is terminated in April 2026 by way of genuine redundancy. The payroll engine calculates the tax-free redundancy amount based on the ATO's indexed figures (verify against ATO for the relevant financial year), the taxable ETP component, and the PAYG withholding on each component using the ATO's ETP withholding rates (refer to ATO tax tables — do not use specific amounts). At year-end, the stp-finalisation-agent's reconciliation flags a RED status on the PAYG axis. Investigation reveals the payroll operator coded the redundancy payment as an ordinary termination rather than a genuine redundancy. The tax-free component was not applied. PAYG was over-withheld from Sasha.

The payroll engine is re-run with the correct ETP type classification. The correct PAYG figure is calculated. An update event is lodged correcting the ETP income type and the PAYG withheld. The BAS for the April period is reviewed — a BAS amendment is required to reflect the corrected PAYG remittance. The stp-finalisation-agent escalates to H1 given the BAS amendment and the ETP reclassification with tax consequences. The H1 gate holder (registered tax agent) reviews, amends the April BAS, and lodges the corrected STP update event before lodging the finalisation event.

What this illustrates: ETP classification errors compound. A wrong ETP type at the time of payment causes a PAYG error, a BAS error, and an income statement error. None of these can be resolved at finalisation without first correcting the underlying classification. The H1 gate fires because the BAS amendment has tax consequences. The ETP calculation is the engine's to perform — but only once it receives the correct classification input.

The contractor classification issue deserves specific attention at year-end. The label "contractor" carries no weight on its own. If a worker treated as a contractor throughout the year meets the ATO's multi-factor employee test, the employer may have a PAYG withholding liability, an SG liability, and a payroll tax liability for the full year. The stp-finalisation-agent flags workers reported under the contractor income type where the payment patterns suggest possible employee characteristics. This is an H3 flag — a practitioner must assess the classification before the employer takes any position. Do not assume the contractor label is correct without review. The multi-factor test governs. Award coverage may also apply — verify applicable award where relevant.

H1 Gate — Registered Tax Agent or BAS Agent Lodgement

The H1 gate is non-delegable for STP finalisation. A registered tax agent or BAS agent must lodge the finalisation event. The employer cannot self-lodge the finalisation event without being themselves a registered agent. The stp-finalisation-agent builds the reconciliation, assembles the workpaper, and flags every unresolved item — but it does not lodge. The H1 holder reviews the completed workpaper, confirms all variances are resolved or documented, and then lodges.

The H1 gate definition for this chapter: a registered tax agent (Tax Practitioners Board registration) or a registered BAS agent (Tax Practitioners Board registration) with appropriate authorisation to lodge STP events on behalf of the employer. Sole trader employers who are themselves registered tax agents or BAS agents satisfy H1 personally. All other employers must engage a registered agent.

The STP finalisation event declaration in the ATO's STP reporting framework requires the lodging agent to declare that the information is true and correct. That declaration has legal weight. The stp-finalisation-agent produces the data. The H1 gate holder makes the declaration.

STP_FINALISATION_EVENT {
  employer:          Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd
  ABN:               [redacted]
  financialYear:     2025-26
  eventType:         FINALISATION
  employeeCount:     47
  submittedBy:       [H1 — registered BAS agent]
  lodgementDeadline: 14 July 2026 (verify against ATO)
  status:            PENDING_H1_APPROVAL
  noOverride:        true
}

The noOverride flag has teeth. The stp-finalisation-agent refuses to produce a lodgement-ready package until the H1 gate holder has explicitly confirmed the reconciliation. There is no bypass. There is no override. If the H1 gate holder is unavailable before 14 July, that is a scheduling problem for the employer to solve — not a reason to relax the gate.

Before the H1 gate holder reviews the workpaper, the stp-finalisation-agent runs its pre-review checklist. The checklist is a precondition to presenting the workpaper for review, and it stands in for none of the professional judgement that review demands.

/stp-finalisation-agent pre-review-checklist --employer "Blue Mountain Healthcare" --fy 2026

Checklist output:

  [PASS] All pay events for 2025-26 lodged and accepted by ATO
  [PASS] No rejected STP events pending resubmission
  [PASS] Gross pay axis: GREEN
  [PASS] PAYG axis: GREEN (BAS amendment lodged 18 June 2026)
  [PASS] SG axis: GREEN (clearing house statement reconciled)
  [WARN] Payroll tax: verify against relevant SRO — no automated reconciliation available
  [WARN] Workers comp: verify against insurer — no automated reconciliation available
  [INFO] Closely held payees: 2 identified — verify CHP finalisation rules against ATO
  [INFO] Employees with WHM income type: 3 — tax rate and income type confirmed correct
  [INFO] Finalisation deadline: 14 July 2026 (verify against ATO)
  [INFO] H1 gate: REQUIRED — present workpaper to registered tax agent or BAS agent

STATUS: READY FOR H1 REVIEW
noOverride: true

The two WARN items — payroll tax and workers comp — reflect the fact that these obligations do not sit with the ATO. The agent cannot automatically reconcile them because they require data from the relevant state revenue authority and the workers compensation insurer. The H1 gate holder must confirm these items are resolved separately.

The two closely held payees flagged in the checklist require specific attention. Closely held payee rules apply different STP reporting and finalisation requirements. The ATO's guidance on closely held payees must be checked before lodging. Do not apply standard employee finalisation logic to closely held payees. Verify the current ATO position.

My take: The H1 gate is the one gate in this book that I will never advocate weakening. Payroll errors have compounding consequences — they flow into employee income tax returns, into the ATO's data-matching system, and into Fair Work compliance positions. The stp-finalisation-agent does the heavy lifting on the reconciliation. But the declaration that the data is true and correct is a human declaration, made by a registered professional who has legal accountability for it. That accountability structure exists for a reason. The agent supports the H1 holder; it does not replace them.

stp-finalisation-agent — Reconciliation Assembly and Flagging

The stp-finalisation-agent is a purpose-built orchestration agent for year-end STP reconciliation. It coordinates the payroll-engine, payroll-orchestrator, and audit-trail-agent to produce a complete, documented reconciliation workpaper. Lodging, overriding, and relaxing gates all sit outside its remit.

The agent's core capability is multi-axis reconciliation. It pulls the YTD payroll figures from the payroll engine, the STP-reported figures from the ATO submission log, the BAS-remitted amounts from the BAS reconciliation module, and the super fund data from the clearing house integration. It assembles these into a workpaper with variance calculations and status flags for each axis.

/stp-finalisation-agent run-reconciliation --employer "Blue Mountain Healthcare" --fy 2026

This slash command invokes the full reconciliation pipeline. The pipeline runs in this sequence:

PIPELINE: stp-finalisation-agent reconciliation
Step 1: payroll-engine — compute YTD gross, PAYG, SG for all employees
Step 2: stp-event-log — retrieve all STP pay events, update events for FY
Step 3: bas-reconciliation-module — retrieve W1/W2 totals across all BAS periods
Step 4: super-fund-integration — retrieve clearing house YTD paid totals
Step 5: variance-engine — calculate delta for each axis; flag GREEN/AMBER/RED
Step 6: audit-trail-agent — log all inputs, outputs, variance flags, timestamps
Step 7: workpaper-builder — assemble reconciliation workpaper for H1 review
Step 8: H1 gate check — block lodgement until H1 approval recorded

noOverride: true
ENGINE: payroll-engine v2.4

The audit-trail-agent records every step. Every input value, every computed variance, every flag status, and every timestamp is logged. If the ATO subsequently queries a finalisation figure, the audit trail is the employer's first line of evidence. The log is immutable — the agent cannot delete or modify an audit entry after it is written.

The agent distinguishes between categories of variance to prioritise the H1 gate holder's review time. RED variances are material and require resolution before any finalisation event is produced. AMBER variances require explanation — the H1 gate holder may accept a documented timing difference as adequate explanation, but must explicitly confirm this. GREEN means the axis reconciles within tolerance. The tolerance threshold itself is a firm-level policy decision that the H1 gate holder must set before the agent runs.

Task("stp-finalisation-agent", {
  action: "build-reconciliation",
  employer: "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
  financialYear: "2025-26",
  includeComponents: [
    "gross-pay",
    "payg-withheld",
    "sg-contributions",
    "payroll-tax",
    "workers-comp"
  ]
})

For each flagged AMBER variance, the agent produces an investigation log. The log identifies the pay period most likely responsible for the variance, the likely cause category, and the suggested resolution action. The H1 gate holder reviews the investigation log as part of the workpaper review.

Task("stp-finalisation-agent", {
  action: "investigate-variance",
  component: "sg-contributions",
  variance: "AMBER",
  varianceDetail: "Super fund YTD paid $3,800 below engine-calculated SG",
  suggestedActions: [
    "Check clearing house for returned contributions — inactive member accounts",
    "Verify Q4 contribution payment date — timing difference if paid after 30 June",
    "Confirm no employee with invalid TFN — fund may have rejected contribution"
  ]
})

The SG variance is particularly important for the payroll tax reconciliation axis in some states — states that exclude exempt super contributions from the payroll tax wages base. If a contribution was returned and not resubmitted, the payroll tax wages base calculation may also be affected. Verify the treatment of returned contributions with the relevant state revenue authority.

Example 10.4 (assumed-fact illustration): Metro Digital Solutions Pty Ltd employs 23 employees including Robert, a software engineer, and Priya, an engagement manager. Metro is a substantial employer (20 or more employees) and has no default extension available for the STP finalisation deadline. The payroll manager runs stp-finalisation-agent on 25 June 2026 as part of the June payroll close. The reconciliation returns GREEN on gross pay, AMBER on PAYG, and RED on SG. The SG variance of $7,200 is traced by the investigation module to three employees whose Q3 contributions were rejected by the super fund because the employees had closed their accounts and nominated new funds without updating the employer's records. The PAYG AMBER variance is traced to a November BAS period where a termination payment for a departed employee was not captured in the BAS remittance.

The payroll manager resubmits the three rejected super contributions to the correct funds using the updated member details. The super fund clears the contributions by 2 July. The payroll manager lodges a BAS amendment for the November period, correcting the W2 remittance amount. The stp-finalisation-agent re-runs on 5 July. All axes return GREEN. The pre-review checklist is satisfied. The workpaper is presented to Metro's registered BAS agent on 6 July. The BAS agent reviews, confirms the reconciliation, and lodges the finalisation event on 8 July — within the 14 July deadline (verify against ATO). The audit trail records the complete sequence.

What this illustrates: Running the reconciliation before the last week of June gives the employer time to resolve RED variances before the deadline. A 25 June run date, 5 July rerun, and 8 July lodgement sequence is achievable for a 23-employee business. A 12 July run date is not. The payroll manager's role is to resolve variances. The H1 gate holder's role is to declare and lodge. The agent's role is to produce the workpaper and enforce the gate. The ATO's role is to mark the income statements as tax ready after lodgement.

The stp-finalisation-agent also handles the multi-employer scenario. An employee who worked for two related entities during the year may have STP data under two different ABNs. The agent consolidates the view at the employer level — it does not produce a combined view across employers. Each employer's finalisation event is separate. The employee's income statement will aggregate data from all employers after each lodges its own finalisation event.

After the finalisation event is lodged, the agent records the lodgement confirmation from the ATO's STP channel. The confirmation reference number is added to the audit trail. The employees' income statements are updated by the ATO — the timing of the "tax ready" status change is controlled by the ATO's systems, not the employer's. The employer's obligation is complete at lodgement.

FINALISATION LODGEMENT CONFIRMATION
Employer:           Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd
ABN:                [redacted]
Financial year:     2025-26
Lodged by:          [H1 — registered BAS agent]
Lodgement date:     [date recorded]
ATO reference:      [reference number recorded]
Employee count:     47
Status:             ACCEPTED
Income statements:  Marked "tax ready" by ATO — timing per ATO systems

AUDIT TRAIL: stp-finalisation-agent run ID [recorded]
noOverride: true

There is one post-lodgement scenario that requires attention. If an error is discovered after the finalisation event is lodged, an amended finalisation event must be lodged. The stp-finalisation-agent can prepare the amendment, but the H1 gate applies again. An amended finalisation event is a substantive ATO submission that cannot be delegated below H1.

The payroll-orchestrator coordinates the finalisation workflow across multiple employers or entities managed by a single payroll practice. It queues the stp-finalisation-agent runs for each entity, tracks completion status, and surfaces the H1 gate confirmation queue for the registered agent to work through in priority order — RED variances first, then AMBER, then final review and lodgement. For practices managing many clients approaching a common 14 July deadline, the orchestrator's queue management is what makes the workflow tractable.

/payroll-orchestrator finalisation-queue --fy 2026 --deadline "2026-07-14"

Queue output (example structure):

  Entity                        Status              H1 Action
  ----------------------------  ------------------  ----------------------------
  Blue Mountain Healthcare      READY FOR H1        Review workpaper — lodge
  Metro Digital Solutions       READY FOR H1        Review workpaper — lodge
  Adelaide Retail Services      AMBER — PAYG        Resolve BAS amendment first
  Harbour Engineering           RED — SG            Returned contributions pending
  Westfield Logistics           NOT STARTED         Run reconciliation required

noOverride: true
H1 GATE: required for all finalisation lodgements

The queue is sorted by urgency. Entities ready for H1 review come first. Entities with unresolved RED variances come last — they cannot proceed to H1 review until the variance is resolved. Entities not yet started are flagged so the payroll practice can prioritise their reconciliation runs to meet the deadline.

My take: The reconciliation workpaper is the most important document the payroll function produces each year. It is the evidence that the employer got the year right. An ATO audit of payroll commences with the reconciliation workpaper — or its absence. The stp-finalisation-agent produces a complete, timestamped, audit-trailed workpaper. Print it, sign it, store it. Not because the ATO will necessarily ask for it, but because having it means the answer to every payroll audit question is already documented. Payroll audits are won or lost on documentation, not on memory.

A note on the STP finalisation agent's scope boundaries. The agent reconciles payroll data. It does not review employment contracts. It does not assess award compliance. It does not determine whether the employer's OTE definition is correct for SG purposes. It does not assess whether a genuine redundancy payment qualifies as genuine for tax purposes. These are practitioner questions — they sit at H2 or H3 depending on the specific issue. The agent's GREEN status on the SG axis means the engine's calculated SG matches the fund's receipts. It does not mean the engine's OTE definition is correct. Award compliance and OTE definition must be verified separately — flag need to verify applicable award where relevant.

The SG rate from 1 July 2025 is 12%. The payroll engine uses 12% as its SG calculation rate for all pay events in the 2025-26 financial year and forward. Verify the current rate and any scheduled increases against the ATO. The engine computes the amount, but the rate it applies must be correct and current.

payroll-engine.sgCalculation({
  employee: "Robert Chen",
  ordinaryTimeEarnings: [engine computes from pay components],
  sgRate: 0.12,  // 12% from 1 July 2025 — verify against ATO
  period: "FY2025-26",
  noOverride: true
})

// Returns:
{
  oteDetermined: [engine output],
  sgObligationFY: [engine output],
  verificationNote: "SG rate 12% applied from 1 July 2025. Verify current rate against ATO.",
  engineVersion: "payroll-engine v2.4"
}

The payroll engine's OTE determination is a separate computation from the gross pay figure. Not all gross earnings are ordinary time earnings for SG purposes. Overtime earnings, bonuses classified as discretionary, and some allowances may be excluded from OTE under the Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act 1992. The engine must apply the correct OTE definition — and that definition should be verified against the applicable award or employment contract. Flag need to verify applicable award where relevant. If the OTE definition is incorrect, the SG calculation is incorrect, and the year-end reconciliation will not catch it — because the variance would appear in the engine's calculation, not between the engine and the fund.

Next: Chapter 11 covers payroll audit risk — ATO data matching programs, the Superannuation Guarantee Charge audit process, Fair Work Act inspection powers, state payroll tax audits, and how the audit-risk-agent maintains a live audit-readiness score across all payroll compliance axes.

11 Payroll Audit Risk Management

ATO data matching, the SG Audit program, Fair Work inspections, state payroll tax audits, and the audit-risk-agent

Payroll audit risk sits at the intersection of four different regulators. The ATO runs SG compliance audits and uses STP data to identify discrepancies automatically. Fair Work inspectors check record-keeping, minimum entitlements, and underpayments. State revenue offices audit payroll tax grouping arrangements. Knowing how each audit pathway is triggered — and how the audit-risk-agent scores and surfaces your exposure — is the difference between a clean letter and a six-year review.

ATO Data Matching — STP vs Super Fund Data

The ATO's data-matching capability runs continuously. Every STP pay event you lodge sits alongside the contribution data reported by superannuation funds. The ATO cross-references them automatically. When the numbers diverge — when your STP report says a particular employee earned $X of ordinary time earnings and the fund says contributions came in as if OTE were $Y — the ATO system flags the discrepancy.

The match runs on three primary dimensions. First, identity: the ATO matches by TFN. An employee whose TFN in your payroll system is incorrect will immediately generate a mismatch — your STP lodgement goes to one taxpayer file, the fund's contribution report goes to another. Second, quantum: the ATO compares the SG liability implied by your STP ordinary time earnings against the contributions the fund received in the relevant quarter. Third, timing: late contributions are visible. A contribution that arrives after the quarterly due date appears in the fund's reporting with a different date and can trigger an SGC liability even if the amount was correct.

The audit-risk-agent ingests both streams — STP lodgement data and super fund contribution reporting — and computes a reconciliation score for each employee for each quarter. Where a gap exists, it flags the gap with a severity classification and surfaces it for human review before the ATO gets there first.

// audit-risk-agent: STP vs fund data reconciliation
const reconciliation = await auditRiskAgent.reconcile({
  employerId: "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
  auditType: "STP_FUND_MATCH",
  period: {
    fromQuarter: "2025-Q1",
    toQuarter: "2025-Q4"
  },
  dataStreams: {
    stpLodgements: await payrollOrchestrator.getSTPData({
      employerId: "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
      financialYear: "2024-25"
    }),
    fundContributions: await superFundConnector.getContributions({
      employerId: "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
      financialYear: "2024-25"
    })
  }
});

// Every figure is engine-computed — no manual overrides
console.log(reconciliation.gapReport);
Data Source What the ATO Compares Risk Trigger
STP Phase 2 pay events Ordinary time earnings per employee per quarter OTE reported in STP exceeds fund contribution base; SG shortfall implied
Super fund member statements Employer contributions received per fund per quarter Contribution amount inconsistent with 12% of STP OTE (as at June 2026 — verify)
STP income type codes Classification of earnings as OTE vs non-OTE Allowances or bonuses coded as non-OTE when they are OTE under the SGAA
TFN declarations and withholding PAYG withheld vs ATO tax table output for declared income Consistent over- or under-withholding relative to tax table expectations
Fund contribution dates Date contributions credited vs quarterly due date Contribution arrives after the 28th of the month following quarter end

The key risk in ATO data matching is misclassification of ordinary time earnings. If your payroll classifies a regular allowance as non-OTE when it is in fact OTE under section 6 of the Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act 1992, the SG calculation on that component is zero. The fund receives less than it should. The ATO matches fund data against STP and identifies the shortfall. The SGC charge that follows includes the unpaid SG, a nominal interest charge, and an administration fee — none of which are tax-deductible. AWARD-GOVERNS: always verify the applicable modern award to determine whether specific allowances are OTE or non-OTE before coding them in the payroll engine.

Rather than guessing at classification, the audit-risk-agent flags every allowance type against a maintained classification matrix and surfaces AMBER or RED flags for any allowance where the OTE status is ambiguous, contested, or award-dependent. The number itself comes from the engine, not from the agent. The agent owns the risk flag.

// STP vs fund gap output — all amounts computed deterministically
{
  "employeeId": "EMP-0042",
  "employeeName": "Robert Chen",
  "quarter": "2025-Q3",
  "stpOTE": "[engine-computed]",
  "fundContributionReceived": "[from-fund-api]",
  "impliedSGRequirement": "[engine-computed at 12%]",
  "gap": "[engine-computed]",
  "gapStatus": "RED",
  "flagReason": "Contribution shortfall — verify OTE classification of site allowance",
  "gateRequired": "H2"
}

My take: You cannot outrun the ATO's data-matching program. It is automated, it is continuous, and it processes scale that no manual review team could match. The only rational position is to run your own reconciliation before the ATO runs it for you. The audit-risk-agent exists precisely for this: surface the gap internally so you address it voluntarily, not under audit compulsion. Voluntary disclosure significantly changes the trajectory of an SG audit.

SG Audit Program — No Statutory Time Limit on SGC

The ATO's Superannuation Guarantee Compliance audit program is the mechanism through which the ATO formally investigates SG non-compliance. It can be triggered by a number of inputs: an employee complaint lodged via the ATO's online tip-off service, an ATO data-matching flag, a referral from another regulator, or a random industry audit targeting sectors with known non-compliance patterns.

There is no statutory time limit on SGC recovery. The four-year amendment period in s 37 of the SGAA 1992 runs only after an SGC assessment has been made; where no assessment exists, the ATO can raise SGC for any historical quarter. In practice the ATO commonly works to a five-year review horizon — but five years is the practical minimum, not the legal maximum. Employers need to maintain records that can withstand an unlimited-horizon retrospective review.

When an SG audit commences, the ATO will typically request payroll records for the entire review period. These include payslips, timesheets, leave records, super contribution receipts, and any documentation of worker classification decisions. The audit examines whether each worker was correctly classified, whether OTE was correctly calculated, whether contributions were made on time, and whether the contribution base was correct.

// SGC workpaper structure — no figures; engine computes all
const sgcWorkpaper = await auditRiskAgent.buildSGCWorkpaper({
  employerId: "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
  reviewPeriod: {
    from: "2020-07-01",
    to: "2025-06-30"
  },
  sections: [
    "workerClassification",
    "oteComponentAnalysis",
    "contributionTimingAnalysis",
    "maximumSuperContributableBase",
    "voluntaryDisclosureSummary"
  ],
  // Every computed figure in this workpaper is the engine's
  engineRef: "PAYROLL-ENGINE-v4.2"
});

The SGC workpaper produced by the audit-risk-agent does not contain actual dollar amounts from user input. The payroll engine computes every regulated figure. The workpaper references the engine's output for each line item and flags any quarter where the engine's computation implies a shortfall. This creates an auditable trail: the workpaper shows what the engine computed, what was remitted, and where gaps exist.

The Maximum Super Contribution Base (MSCB) is relevant to SG audits. Employers are not required to pay SG on earnings above the MSCB. If the payroll system has been applying SG on earnings above the MSCB unnecessarily, the workpaper will surface that too. VERIFY: the MSCB is indexed annually — always confirm the current-year figure against ATO published rates. As at June 2026, verify the current MSCB before use.

Part 7 of the SGAA imposes additional SGC of up to 200% of the charge for failing to lodge an SGC statement by its due date. The Commissioner has discretion to remit that additional charge in whole or in part (PS LA 2011/28), and voluntary disclosure — lodging an SGC statement before the ATO commences an audit — is the strongest remission factor. Where the audit-risk-agent identifies a gap, the correct sequence is: (1) engine computes the SGC liability, (2) H2 gate fires — a registered tax agent reviews the SGC statement before lodgement, (3) the SGC statement is lodged, (4) payment is made. Do not bypass the H2 gate on an SGC statement. The tax agent's review is the control.

// payroll-orchestrator delegating to audit-risk-agent for SG audit prep
const auditPrep = await payrollOrchestrator.delegate({
  task: "SG_AUDIT_PREPARATION",
  agent: "audit-risk-agent",
  params: {
    employerId: "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
    auditNoticeDate: "2026-05-15",
    atoReferenceNumber: "AUD-2026-XXXXXX",
    reviewPeriodRequested: "2020-2025"
  },
  gates: {
    sgcStatementLodgement: "H2",
    atoCorrespondenceResponse: "H2",
    formalObjection: "H2"
  }
});

My take: Five years of payroll records is not a small ask. I have seen employers genuinely unable to reconstruct their OTE calculations from four years ago because the payroll system was upgraded and old data was not migrated cleanly. The audit-risk-agent running a quarterly reconciliation and archiving its output to the audit trail gives you the retrospective record the ATO will ask for. Build the record forward; do not try to reconstruct it backward under audit pressure.

Record-Keeping Obligations for Payroll

Record-keeping obligations for payroll come from multiple sources simultaneously. The Fair Work Act 2009 imposes obligations on the employer to keep certain payroll records for seven years. The SGAA imposes separate obligations in relation to superannuation. The income tax legislation imposes obligations on withholding. State payroll tax legislation imposes its own. These obligations overlap but are not identical.

The audit-trail-agent exists to make compliance with these obligations systematic. Rather than relying on individual staff to maintain records manually, the audit-trail-agent creates a timestamped, immutable record of every payroll computation, every STP lodgement, every super contribution, and every agent decision. Each record includes the engine version, the inputs used, the output produced, and the gate status at the time.

Document Type Retention Period Responsible Party
Pay records (employee name, employment basis, TFN, rate of pay, gross pay, deductions, net pay) 7 years — Fair Work Act Employer
Time and wages records (hours worked, penalty rates, overtime) 7 years — Fair Work Act Employer
Leave records (leave accrued, leave taken, leave balances) 7 years — Fair Work Act Employer
Super contribution receipts and fund acknowledgements 5 years — SGAA (recommended 7 years in practice) Employer
STP lodgement records (pay event ID, lodgement timestamp, event data) 5 years minimum — income tax Employer / STP provider
TFN declarations and withholding variation notices 5 years from last pay event referencing the declaration Employer
Worker classification records (contractor agreements, ABN declarations, multi-factor assessments) 7 years — Fair Work; 5 years — SGAA; retain the longer Employer
Payroll tax returns and supporting schedules Varies by state — typically 5 years; confirm with your state revenue office Employer
SGC statements (if lodged) 5 years from lodgement Employer

The audit-trail-agent writes a record at every payroll event. The record is structured, queryable, and contains sufficient information to reconstruct the calculation independently. This is not a log for debugging — it is a legal record. Treat it as such.

// audit-trail-agent: producing a record for a pay event
const trailRecord = await auditTrailAgent.record({
  eventType: "PAY_EVENT",
  employerId: "ADELAIDE-RETAIL-001",
  eventId: "PAY-EVENT-20260620-0049",
  engineVersion: "PAYROLL-ENGINE-v4.2",
  engineOutputRef: "[engine-signed-hash]",
  stpLodgementId: "STP-20260620-XXXXXX",
  gateStatus: {
    h1Required: false,
    h2Required: false,
    h3Required: false,
    allClear: true
  },
  retentionPolicy: {
    minimumRetainUntil: "2033-06-20",  // 7 years
    basis: "FAIR_WORK_ACT_S535"
  },
  timestamp: "2026-06-20T14:32:17.000Z"
});

Fair Work inspectors have the power to enter premises and request records on the spot. If you cannot produce payroll records during an inspection, the absence itself is a record-keeping breach. The penalty for a record-keeping breach under the Fair Work Act is civil. For a serious contravention, it is significantly higher. The audit-trail-agent provides the on-demand retrieval that an inspection requires.

// Retrieve full record for a specific employee — inspection scenario
const employeeRecord = await auditTrailAgent.retrieve({
  employerId: "ADELAIDE-RETAIL-001",
  employeeId: "EMP-0091",
  fromDate: "2019-07-01",
  toDate: "2026-06-20",
  recordTypes: [
    "PAY_RECORDS",
    "LEAVE_RECORDS",
    "TIME_AND_WAGES",
    "TFN_DECLARATION",
    "SUPER_CONTRIBUTIONS"
  ],
  format: "FAIR_WORK_INSPECTION_BUNDLE"
});

Fair Work Inspections — What They Check

Fair Work inspectors are authorised officers of the Fair Work Ombudsman. They have broad powers: to enter premises during working hours, to require production of records, to interview employees and employers, and to issue infringement notices. An inspection can be triggered by an employee complaint, by a tip-off, or proactively in a targeted industry campaign. The FWO runs regular campaigns targeting specific sectors — hospitality, retail, cleaning, and labour hire have all been subject to focused investigation programs.

An inspector will typically check six things. First, whether the employer holds the relevant payroll records at all. Second, whether those records match what has actually been paid. Third, whether the rates paid satisfy the applicable modern award or enterprise agreement minimum. Fourth, whether penalty rates, loadings, and allowances have been paid correctly. Fifth, whether overtime calculations are correct. Sixth, whether leave has accrued and been paid at the correct rate.

AWARD-GOVERNS: every minimum rate, penalty rate, loading, and allowance check by an inspector is against the applicable modern award or enterprise agreement. The payroll engine must be configured with the correct award coverage for each employee. If the award coverage in the payroll system is wrong, every rate derived from it is potentially wrong.

// Fair Work record-keeping check — agent call
const fwInspectionCheck = await auditRiskAgent.runCheck({
  checkType: "FAIR_WORK_RECORD_KEEPING",
  employerId: "ADELAIDE-RETAIL-001",
  scope: {
    employeeIds: "ALL",
    periodFrom: "2023-07-01",
    periodTo: "2026-06-30"
  },
  checkPoints: [
    "payRecordCompleteness",
    "rateCompliance",
    "penaltyRateApplication",
    "leaveAccrualAccuracy",
    "overtimeCalculation",
    "awardCoverageMapping"
  ]
});

Underpayment identified during a Fair Work inspection generates a compliance notice requiring back-payment, with interest at the rate specified in the Act. If the underpayment is found to be deliberate, the matter can be referred for prosecution and civil penalty proceedings. There is no proportionality between the magnitude of the underpayment and the maximum penalty — a $20 underpayment committed deliberately can attract the same maximum penalty as a larger one.

The concept of "wage theft" criminalisation is relevant in some states as at June 2026. Verify the current legislative position in each state where you have employees, as the criminal law landscape for deliberate wage theft has been evolving.

Where an inspection surfaces a gap, the gate structure applies immediately. A record-keeping gap that requires legal advice about the employer's obligations goes to H3 — practitioner review before proceeding. An underpayment that requires a formal back-payment agreement or a compliance notice response goes to H2 — registered tax agent where there are withholding implications, or to an employment law practitioner for the Fair Work elements. An STP amendment arising from a back-payment goes to H1 — registered tax agent or BAS agent.

State Payroll Tax Audits — Grouping and Contractor Provisions

There is no federal payroll tax. Each state and territory has its own payroll tax legislation, its own rate, its own threshold, and its own administrative agency. The Northern Territory, Australian Capital Territory, New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, South Australia, Western Australia, Tasmania — each operates independently. The rates and thresholds vary. VERIFY: always confirm the current rate and threshold for each state in which you have liable wages, against the relevant state revenue office's published figures, as at June 2026.

Payroll tax audits are conducted by state revenue offices, not the ATO. The key risk areas in a state payroll tax audit are three: grouping, contractor provisions, and threshold misapplication.

Grouping provisions deem multiple entities to be a single employer for payroll tax purposes when they meet the relevant statutory tests — typically, common ownership or control. A group of related companies cannot each claim the full payroll tax threshold independently. The combined wages of the group count together, and only one threshold is available to the group. An employer who does not appreciate that its related entities form a payroll tax group will under-report its payroll tax liability.

Contractor provisions in state payroll tax legislation deem certain contractor payments to be wages for payroll tax purposes. The deemed wages provisions operate independently of the Fair Work Act's worker classification framework and independently of the ATO's superannuation guarantee worker classification tests. A worker can be correctly classified as an independent contractor for income tax and SG purposes while simultaneously having their payments treated as wages for payroll tax purposes under the relevant state's contractor provisions.

// Payroll tax grouping check
const groupingCheck = await auditRiskAgent.runCheck({
  checkType: "PAYROLL_TAX_GROUPING",
  primaryEmployerId: "BLUE-MTN-HEALTHCARE-001",
  relatedEntities: [
    { entityId: "BMH-STAFFING-PTY", abn: "XX XXX XXX XXX" },
    { entityId: "BMH-SERVICES-TRUST", abn: "XX XXX XXX XXX" }
  ],
  jurisdiction: "NSW",
  auditYear: "2025-26",
  checkPoints: [
    "commonOwnershipTest",
    "controlTest",
    "commonEmployerTest",
    "contractorDeemedWages"
  ]
  // All wage figures computed by payroll engine
});

Threshold misapplication occurs when an employer with employees in multiple states allocates wages to the wrong state for payroll tax purposes. The allocation rules under the Model Payroll Tax legislation (adopted with variations by most states) allocate wages based on where services are performed. An employee who works partly in NSW and partly in Victoria generates wages allocated to each state based on the services performed there. Getting the allocation wrong affects both states' payroll tax liability.

H1 gate: payroll tax registration, lodgement of returns, and formal correspondence with state revenue offices can be handled by a registered tax agent or BAS agent (depending on the state's requirements). Verify what level of registration is required in each relevant state before proceeding.

H2 gate: a formal objection or dispute with a state revenue office in relation to a payroll tax assessment, or a grouping dispute with significant tax consequences, requires a registered tax agent.

H3 gate: a contractor classification decision under the contractor provisions — before the payroll tax position is adopted — should go to practitioner review. The payroll tax contractor provisions are not identical to the Fair Work or SG worker classification tests, and a practitioner familiar with the relevant state's legislation should assess the position before it is adopted in the system.

// Contractor deemed wages assessment — H3 gate fires before adoption
const contractorAssessment = await auditRiskAgent.assessContractor({
  employerId: "BLUE-MTN-HEALTHCARE-001",
  workerId: "WKR-0034",
  jurisdiction: "NSW",
  assessmentInputs: {
    contractType: "SERVICE_AGREEMENT",
    servicesDescribed: "Nursing shifts — rostered",
    abn: true,
    exclusiveEngagement: false,
    labourComponent: "[engine-estimated-percentage]"
  },
  gateOnAdoption: "H3"
  // Worker's label as "contractor" is NOT determinative
});

My take: Payroll tax grouping is one of the most commonly misunderstood areas in Australian tax compliance. I have seen professional service firms operate multiple entities for legitimate reasons, with no intent to split wages, and find themselves exposed because they did not run a grouping analysis before each state's threshold application. The payroll-orchestrator should run a grouping check at the beginning of each financial year and whenever an entity in the group is added, restructured, or disposed of. This is not a once-and-done analysis.

audit-risk-agent — Risk Scoring and Gap Flagging

The audit-risk-agent is not a reactive tool. Its primary function is proactive: to run a risk assessment before any regulator makes contact, surface the gaps the employer needs to address, and flag the gates that must engage before any remediation action is taken. The payroll-orchestrator schedules the audit-risk-agent to run at the end of each quarter, after STP lodgements are complete and after super contributions are remitted.

The risk scoring model uses three tiers: GREEN, AMBER, and RED. Each tier has defined criteria and defined outcomes. GREEN means no action required. AMBER means review recommended — a practitioner should assess the flagged item before the next lodgement. RED means gate fires now — no further payroll processing on the affected item until the gate clears.

Risk Tier Description Trigger Examples Gate
GREEN No material risk identified. Reconciliation clean. Records complete. STP matches fund data; contributions on time; all records retrievable No gate — process continues
AMBER Potential gap identified. Requires practitioner review before next lodgement. Not yet a confirmed liability. Allowance OTE classification ambiguous; contractor engagement that may meet deemed wages test; payroll tax grouping entity added H3 — practitioner review before proceeding
RED Confirmed or probable gap. Liability likely. Formal action required. SG shortfall confirmed by reconciliation; STP/fund match gap exceeds materiality threshold; formal audit notice received H2 — registered tax agent must act; H1 for STP amendments

The risk score output from the audit-risk-agent includes a structured report for each employer. The report is segmented by regulator — ATO SG, ATO STP, Fair Work, and state payroll tax — and within each regulator, by employee and by period. Each flagged item includes the basis for the flag, the data that generated it, and the recommended gate.

// Risk score output format — audit-risk-agent
{
  "employerId": "WESTFIELD-LOGISTICS-001",
  "scoreDate": "2026-06-30",
  "overallRisk": "AMBER",
  "segments": {
    "atoSG": {
      "riskTier": "GREEN",
      "flags": [],
      "lastReconciliation": "2026-06-28"
    },
    "atoSTP": {
      "riskTier": "AMBER",
      "flags": [
        {
          "flagId": "STP-AMBER-0042",
          "employee": "EMP-0088",
          "period": "2025-Q4",
          "issue": "Travel allowance OTE classification — AWARD-GOVERNS: verify Road Transport award",
          "gateRequired": "H3",
          "status": "OPEN"
        }
      ]
    },
    "fairWork": {
      "riskTier": "GREEN",
      "flags": []
    },
    "statePT": {
      "riskTier": "AMBER",
      "flags": [
        {
          "flagId": "SPT-AMBER-0011",
          "issue": "New entity added to group — grouping analysis required",
          "jurisdiction": "QLD",
          "gateRequired": "H3",
          "status": "OPEN"
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}

The audit-risk-agent feeds its output to the payroll-orchestrator. The orchestrator holds any payroll processing that would affect a RED-flagged item until the gate clears. It does not silently continue. This is the determinism-first principle applied to audit risk: a system that processes payroll despite a known RED flag generates liability rather than compliance.

// payroll-orchestrator: gate enforcement on RED risk
const orchestratorDecision = await payrollOrchestrator.checkGates({
  employerId: "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
  processingRun: "PAY-RUN-2026-06-20",
  auditRiskReport: reconciliation.gapReport
});

if (orchestratorDecision.hasRedFlags) {
  // Processing halts. Gate fires. Human must clear.
  await payrollOrchestrator.haltProcessing({
    reason: "RED_AUDIT_FLAG_UNCLEARED",
    flagIds: orchestratorDecision.redFlagIds,
    notifyPractitioner: true,
    gateLevel: orchestratorDecision.requiredGate
  });
}
// The figure is the engine's to produce. Agent owns the gate.

The audit-trail-agent records every risk score run. It timestamps the run, records the inputs, records the output tier and flags, and records whether any gate fired. If a gate fired and was subsequently cleared, the clearance — including who cleared it, when, and on what basis — is recorded in the trail. This creates an auditable evidence chain that can be produced to any regulator on demand.

Worked Examples

These are assumed-fact illustrations. They do not represent actual entities or individuals. All figures are engine-computed and omitted here; no actual amounts appear in these examples.

Example 1 — Robert at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd

Robert is the payroll manager at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd, a mid-sized engineering firm with 47 employees including several site workers receiving a site allowance. The payroll system codes the site allowance as non-OTE on the basis that it is a reimbursement-style allowance. The audit-risk-agent runs its quarterly reconciliation for Q3 2025-26.

The reconciliation compares Harbour Engineering's STP lodgements against contribution data reported by the employees' nominated super funds. For 12 of the 47 employees, the audit-risk-agent identifies a systematic gap: the implied SG contribution based on STP ordinary time earnings (including the site allowance) is higher than the contributions the funds received. The engine computes the implied shortfall for each affected employee for each quarter going back to Q1 2024-25 — the start of the current payroll system configuration.

The audit-risk-agent scores the STP/fund match segment RED. The basis: the site allowance classification as non-OTE is at odds with the character of the allowance under the relevant modern award, which describes it as compensation for working at a specific site rather than a genuine reimbursement of additional expenditure. AWARD-GOVERNS: verify the applicable modern award before adopting any classification.

// ATO data match flag output — Harbour Engineering
{
  "employerId": "HARBOUR-ENG-001",
  "flagType": "STP_FUND_MISMATCH",
  "affectedEmployees": 12,
  "periodFrom": "2024-Q1",
  "periodTo": "2025-Q3",
  "rootCauseHypothesis": "Site allowance coded non-OTE — possible misclassification",
  "impliedShortfall": "[engine-computed — verify against ATO SGC tables]",
  "riskTier": "RED",
  "gateRequired": "H2",
  "awardGovernanceNote": "AWARD-GOVERNS: confirm OTE status under applicable award before proceeding"
}

The payroll-orchestrator halts further processing on the site allowance component for the affected employees. The H2 gate fires: a registered tax agent must review the SGC position, confirm whether an SGC liability exists, prepare any required SGC statement, and determine whether voluntary disclosure is the correct course. Robert does not have authority to clear the H2 gate himself. The control sits with the practitioner's review.

What this illustrates: ATO data-matching flags run on the gap between STP and fund data, not on the employer's internal classification. An allowance that the employer considers non-OTE can still generate a RED flag if the contribution quantum is inconsistent with the STP earnings reported. The H2 gate exists precisely for this scenario — where a potential SG liability requires a qualified professional to assess and act.

Example 2 — Angela at Adelaide Retail Services

Angela is the HR manager at Adelaide Retail Services, a multi-site retail employer with approximately 80 casual and part-time employees. A Fair Work inspector arrives at one of the stores with an inspection notice. The inspector requests pay records, time and wages records, and leave records for all employees at that site for the past three years.

Angela requests the inspection bundle from the audit-trail-agent. The agent retrieves records for the 23 employees at that site. For 18 of the 23, the records are complete and immediately producible. For 5 employees, the audit-trail-agent flags a gap: the time and wages records for a period between October 2023 and March 2024 are missing. This corresponds to a period when the site was using a paper-based timesheet system that was not digitised or uploaded to the payroll system's record-keeping module.

// audit-trail-agent: gap identification for Fair Work inspection
const inspectionBundle = await auditTrailAgent.retrieve({
  employerId: "ADELAIDE-RETAIL-001",
  siteId: "SITE-07",
  recordTypes: [
    "PAY_RECORDS",
    "TIME_AND_WAGES",
    "LEAVE_RECORDS"
  ],
  period: {
    from: "2023-07-01",
    to: "2026-06-20"
  },
  format: "FAIR_WORK_INSPECTION_BUNDLE"
});

// Output includes gap report
{
  "completedRecords": 18,
  "incompleteRecords": 5,
  "gaps": [
    {
      "employeeIds": ["EMP-0011", "EMP-0034", "EMP-0067", "EMP-0072", "EMP-0091"],
      "gapPeriod": "2023-10-01 to 2024-03-31",
      "gapType": "TIME_AND_WAGES_MISSING",
      "cause": "Paper timesheet period — not digitised"
    }
  ]
}

The H3 gate fires. A record-keeping gap in the context of an active Fair Work inspection is a serious matter. Before Angela makes any representation to the inspector about the gap, a practitioner should advise on the employer's obligations, whether the gap constitutes a breach, and whether any voluntary disclosure or remediation statement should be made. The inspector has not yet escalated — this is the moment where how Angela responds determines whether the inspection ends at a caution or escalates to infringement notices.

What this illustrates: Record-keeping obligations are satisfied only by having records that can be produced on demand, not by having them somewhere. The audit-trail-agent's gap report surfaces the missing records before the inspector does. The H3 gate ensures a practitioner frames the employer's response, rather than an HR manager under inspection pressure.

Example 3 — Wei at Blue Mountain Healthcare

Wei is the CFO of Blue Mountain Healthcare, a healthcare group operating three related entities: Blue Mountain Healthcare Pty Ltd (the primary employer), BMH Staffing Pty Ltd (a labour hire entity that supplies nursing staff), and BMH Services Trust (a corporate trustee entity with minimal employees). The group has been lodging payroll tax returns in NSW, each entity separately, each claiming the full NSW payroll tax threshold.

The audit-risk-agent runs a payroll tax grouping check at the start of the 2025-26 financial year. The check applies the NSW payroll tax grouping provisions. The result: all three entities form a group. The common ownership test is satisfied — the same natural persons control all three entities. The group's combined wages exceed the NSW threshold materially. Only one threshold is available to the group. The prior years' returns have been lodged on the wrong basis.

// Payroll tax grouping check result — Blue Mountain Healthcare
{
  "employerId": "BLUE-MTN-HEALTHCARE-001",
  "jurisdiction": "NSW",
  "groupingResult": "GROUP_CONFIRMED",
  "groupMembers": [
    "BLUE-MTN-HEALTHCARE-PTY",
    "BMH-STAFFING-PTY",
    "BMH-SERVICES-TRUST"
  ],
  "groupingBasis": "COMMON_OWNERSHIP — s72 Payroll Tax Act 2007 (NSW) — verify",
  "thresholdImplication": "ONE_THRESHOLD_FOR_GROUP",
  "priorYearExposure": "[engine-computed — do not use without H1 review]",
  "riskTier": "RED",
  "gateRequired": "H1"
}

The H1 gate fires. A registered tax agent or BAS agent must handle the NSW Revenue correspondence, the amended returns, and any voluntary disclosure. Wei does not lodge amended payroll tax returns himself. The practitioner's scope includes quantifying the back-liability (engine computes the figures), assessing the interest and penalty position, and engaging with Revenue NSW on the terms of any amended assessment. VERIFY: state payroll tax interest and penalty rates vary by jurisdiction and are subject to change — confirm current rates with Revenue NSW before any estimate is presented.

What this illustrates: Payroll tax grouping exposure can accumulate silently over multiple years if no grouping analysis is run. The audit-risk-agent running a grouping check at the start of each year catches this before Revenue NSW does. The H1 gate ensures the qualified professional, not the CFO, handles the formal engagement with the state revenue authority.

Example 4 — Priya at Metro Digital Solutions

Priya is the finance director at Metro Digital Solutions, a technology services firm that engages 14 workers under services agreements, all of whom hold ABNs and invoice the firm for their services. Metro Digital has classified all 14 as independent contractors for payroll purposes — no SG, no STP reporting, no payroll tax inclusion. This classification has not been reviewed since the engagements commenced two years ago.

Priya runs a proactive audit-risk-agent assessment, prompted by press coverage of the ATO's worker classification compliance campaign in the technology sector. The audit-risk-agent applies the multi-factor assessment framework to each of the 14 engagements, looking past the label "contractor" to how the work is actually performed.

// audit-risk-agent: proactive contractor classification review
const classificationReview = await auditRiskAgent.assessWorkerPopulation({
  employerId: "METRO-DIGITAL-001",
  workerType: "CONTRACTORS",
  workerIds: [
    "WKR-001", "WKR-002", /* ... */ "WKR-014"
  ],
  assessmentFramework: "MULTI_FACTOR",
  // Worker's label as "contractor" is NOT determinative
  labelNotDeterminative: true,
  factors: [
    "controlOverWork",
    "integrationIntoBusiness",
    "exclusivity",
    "abilityToSubcontract",
    "provisionOfEquipment",
    "abn",
    "invoicingArrangement",
    "financialRisk"
  ]
});

The audit-risk-agent scores three of the 14 engagements AMBER. All three involve workers who perform services exclusively for Metro Digital, attend the Metro Digital office daily, work under the direction of Metro Digital's project managers, and have been engaged continuously for more than 18 months. The audit-risk-agent flags these engagements as potentially meeting the characteristics of employment notwithstanding the services agreement structure. The "contractor" label is not determinative.

For the remaining 11, the score is GREEN — those workers have demonstrable multi-client engagements, provide their own equipment, and have genuine autonomy over how they deliver services.

H3 gate fires for the three AMBER workers before any payroll or SG change is made. A practitioner must assess whether these workers are employees at law — a decision with consequences for PAYG withholding, SG, payroll tax, and Fair Work Act entitlements. Priya does not reclassify them herself. The practitioner review determines the correct position. If reclassification follows, the tax consequences are material: VERIFY ETP caps, redundancy tax-free amounts, and any back-pay withholding treatment against ATO published rates, as these are indexed annually.

// Audit response preparation — H2 gate if ATO investigation follows
const auditResponsePrep = await auditRiskAgent.prepareAuditResponse({
  employerId: "METRO-DIGITAL-001",
  regulatorType: "ATO",
  auditType: "WORKER_CLASSIFICATION",
  affectedWorkers: ["WKR-003", "WKR-009", "WKR-012"],
  documentationBundle: [
    "servicesAgreements",
    "invoiceHistory",
    "workPatternEvidence",
    "multiFactorAssessmentReport"
  ],
  // H2 gate: registered tax agent must sign off before any formal ATO response
  gateRequired: "H2",
  instructionsForPractitioner: "Multi-factor assessment attached. Three workers scored AMBER. Practitioner to determine correct employment classification and advise on SG, PAYG, and payroll tax consequences before any response to ATO."
});

What this illustrates: Proactive use of the audit-risk-agent surfaces contractor classification risk before any regulator makes contact. Running the assessment when a compliance campaign is announced — not after an audit notice arrives — gives the employer time to assess, remediate voluntarily if needed, and document the decision-making process. The H3 gate protects the employer from acting on an incorrect classification without qualified oversight. The H2 gate protects the employer from making representations to the ATO without a registered tax agent's involvement.

My take: Audit risk management is the area where the determinism-first principle has its most direct commercial value. An audit that surfaces a six-year SG shortfall is catastrophic because the SGC administration fees, the nominal interest, and the non-deductibility of the SGC charge can multiply the original shortfall dramatically, even when the underlying liability is modest. The payroll engine computing every figure correctly from the outset, the audit-risk-agent running quarterly reconciliations, and the gate model ensuring qualified practitioners touch every formal regulatory interaction — these three things together are the difference between an audit that ends in a clean letter and one that ends in amended assessments, penalties, and reputational damage. Build the system to surface the risk. Let the gates handle what the agents cannot.

Next: Chapter 12 covers special employment populations — casual employees, part-time workers, commission workers, and how the special-populations-agent routes each to the correct calculation.

12 Special Populations — Casuals, Part-Time, and Commission Workers

Casual entitlements, part-time roster management, commission structures, and the special-populations-agent

Most payroll engines are built around a full-time permanent employee. The moment you introduce casual loading, pro-rata entitlements, variable commission, or piece-rate arrangements, the calculation complexity multiplies. The special-populations-agent exists to classify employment type on each pay event and route it to the correct calculation path — because applying a full-time permanent calculation to a casual is a Fair Work compliance risk.

Casual employees — the 25% loading and conversion rights

A casual employee is engaged on an irregular, as-needed basis. There is no firm advance commitment to ongoing work. In exchange for that uncertainty, the law and most modern awards provide a casual loading — a percentage uplift on the base rate of pay intended to compensate for the absence of entitlements that permanent employees receive, including paid leave.

The commonly cited casual loading rate is 25% — the standard casual loading under most modern awards. However, 25% is not universal. Some awards prescribe higher rates. Some enterprise agreements modify the position. Before any payroll engine applies a casual loading to a pay event, the award-agent must confirm the applicable rate for the specific award and classification. Applying 25% when the award requires 27.5% is underpayment. Applying it when the enterprise agreement has a different structure creates a different problem entirely.

My take: The 25% casual loading figure is so widely cited that practitioners treat it as a universal truth. It is not. I have seen payroll systems hardcode 25% across an entire workforce without a single award check. That exposes every casual employee in every non-standard award to systematic underpayment. The award-agent exists precisely to prevent this. Do not let the engine guess.

Verify the applicable casual loading rate against the specific modern award and any applicable enterprise agreement as at June 2026. Flag for verification at each award review date, because awards are updated by Fair Work Commission determinations.

Casual conversion is now the Employee Choice Pathway under the Fair Work Act, inserted by the Fair Work Legislation Amendment (Closing Loopholes No. 2) Act 2024 and in force from 26 February 2025 (26 August 2025 for small business employers). An eligible casual may notify the employer in writing that they believe they no longer meet the casual definition, after 6 months' service — 12 months for a small business employer. The employer must respond within 21 days and may refuse only on defined grounds; the old employer-offer framework no longer applies. The special-populations-agent flags pathway eligibility for review — this is an employment law matter, not a tax matter. The H3 gate — practitioner review before proceeding — applies when the employer receives an employee choice notification. The payroll engine has no role in the conversion decision itself, but it must update the employment type classification if the change takes effect, because that changes every downstream calculation immediately.

The payroll-orchestrator must receive an updated employment-type signal before the next pay run. A casual who converts to permanent part-time on a Monday and is paid as a casual on the following Thursday has been overpaid the casual loading and may have been underpaid leave accruals from the conversion date forward.

// special-populations-agent: classify casual, check loading rate
const classifyEvent = async (payEvent) => {
  const { employeeId, payPeriodStart, hoursWorked, awardCode } = payEvent;

  const employmentRecord = await db.employees.get(employeeId);

  if (employmentRecord.type !== "casual") {
    return { path: "standard", loading: 0 };
  }

  // Award-agent call — never hardcode 25%
  const awardCheck = await awardAgent.getCasualLoading({
    awardCode,
    classification: employmentRecord.classification,
    effectiveDate: payPeriodStart,
  });

  if (awardCheck.status === "AMBER") {
    // Cannot proceed — award classification unclear
    return {
      path: "hold",
      gate: "H3",
      reason: awardCheck.reason,
      awardCode,
    };
  }

  return {
    path: "casual",
    loading: awardCheck.loadingRate,   // e.g. 0.25
    awardCode,
    verifiedAt: awardCheck.checkedAt,
  };
};

Every figure that flows from this classification is calculated by the engine, never by agent code. Once the award-agent returns a confirmed loading rate, the engine applies it and records the computation with the verified rate, the award reference, and the effective date. That audit trail is what you produce when Fair Work or a union representative asks how the loading was calculated.

Casual leave entitlements — verify current Fair Work position

The relationship between casual employment and leave entitlements is not as simple as "casuals get no leave." The Fair Work Act and associated instruments have evolved, and the current position as at June 2026 requires verification against the Fair Work Commission's current materials.

Casual employees generally do not accumulate annual leave or personal/carer's leave in the same way that permanent employees do — the casual loading is intended to compensate for the absence of those entitlements. However, the position on specific leave types is more nuanced. Casuals may have entitlements to unpaid parental leave after meeting service thresholds. Long service leave entitlements for casuals vary by state legislation. Some enterprise agreements grant casuals access to leave entitlements that would not otherwise apply.

My take: Never have the special-populations-agent output "no leave entitlements" as a flat conclusion for casual employees. The correct output is a leave entitlement profile based on applicable instruments and a flag for any entitlement that requires verification against current Fair Work materials. The payroll engine cannot determine state long service leave eligibility without knowing the employee's state of engagement and continuous service history — both of which must be in the employee record.

The payroll-orchestrator receives the leave entitlement profile from the special-populations-agent and routes unpaid parental leave requests and long service leave inquiries to H3 review before any payment is processed. This is the correct architecture. Leave payment errors for casuals can result in either underpayment (if the employee was entitled and not paid) or overpayment (if the casual loading already compensated for that entitlement and leave was also paid). Both create compliance exposure.

// award-agent: confirm casual loading rate for a specific award
const getCasualLoading = async ({ awardCode, classification, effectiveDate }) => {
  const awardVersion = await awardDb.getVersion(awardCode, effectiveDate);

  if (!awardVersion) {
    return {
      status: "AMBER",
      reason: `Award ${awardCode} not found for date ${effectiveDate}`,
      loadingRate: null,
    };
  }

  const classEntry = awardVersion.classifications.find(
    (c) => c.code === classification
  );

  if (!classEntry) {
    return {
      status: "AMBER",
      reason: `Classification ${classification} not in award ${awardCode}`,
      loadingRate: null,
    };
  }

  if (!classEntry.casualLoadingRate) {
    return {
      status: "AMBER",
      reason: "No casual loading rate defined — verify award instrument",
      loadingRate: null,
    };
  }

  return {
    status: "GREEN",
    loadingRate: classEntry.casualLoadingRate,
    awardCode,
    awardVersion: awardVersion.version,
    checkedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
  };
};

The AMBER status from the award-agent is a hard stop. The payroll engine does not proceed on a casual pay event when the loading rate is unconfirmed. The job sits in a work queue for H3 practitioner review. The alternative — proceeding with an assumed rate — is the kind of assumption that compounds across hundreds of pay runs before anyone notices the gap.

Part-time employees — pro-rata entitlements and roster agreements

A part-time employee works regular, agreed hours that are less than full-time. Unlike a casual, a part-time employee has certainty about their hours. That certainty is the foundation of every pro-rata entitlement calculation: annual leave, personal leave, and long service leave all accrue proportionally to the agreed ordinary hours.

The agreed ordinary hours must be in writing. This is not a best-practice recommendation — it is a requirement under the NES and under most modern awards for part-time employees. The roster agreement specifies the days worked, the hours per day, and in many award contexts the start and finish times. A part-time employee who consistently works hours beyond their agreed roster may be entitled to overtime, or may be an employee whose agreement needs to be updated. Either way, the payroll engine cannot make that determination — the H3 gate applies when actual hours systematically exceed agreed hours.

Pro-rata annual leave for a part-time employee accrues at 4 weeks per year of the agreed ordinary hours, not 4 weeks per year of a full-time equivalent. A part-time employee on 20 hours per week accrues 80 hours of annual leave per year (20 hours x 4 weeks). A full-time employee on 38 hours per week accrues 152 hours per year (38 hours x 4 weeks). The engine, not the agent, performs this calculation, and the result is deterministic once the agreed ordinary hours are correctly recorded in the employee master.

// payroll-engine: pro-rata leave entitlement calculation
const computeLeaveAccrual = (params) => {
  const {
    employmentType,
    agreedOrdinaryHoursPerWeek,
    weeksInPeriod,
    leaveType,
    award,
  } = params;

  if (employmentType === "casual") {
    // Casual loading compensates — no standard annual/personal leave accrual
    // Verify current Fair Work position before returning zero
    return {
      accrualHours: 0,
      note: "Casual — verify entitlement against current Fair Work position",
      requiresVerification: true,
    };
  }

  const weeklyAccrualRate = {
    annual: 4 / 52,         // 4 weeks per year
    personal: 10 / 52,      // 10 days per year at agreed hours/day
  };

  if (!weeklyAccrualRate[leaveType]) {
    throw new Error(`Unknown leave type: ${leaveType}`);
  }

  const rawAccrual =
    agreedOrdinaryHoursPerWeek * weeklyAccrualRate[leaveType] * weeksInPeriod;

  return {
    accrualHours: parseFloat(rawAccrual.toFixed(4)),
    basis: `${agreedOrdinaryHoursPerWeek}h/wk x ${weeklyAccrualRate[leaveType].toFixed(6)} x ${weeksInPeriod} weeks`,
    employmentType,
    leaveType,
  };
};

Award-governs applies here. Many modern awards have specific part-time provisions that affect how overtime is defined, what penalty rates apply to specific days or times, and how the roster agreement must be structured. The award-agent must confirm the applicable award and the part-time provisions within it. The payroll engine executes these rules once the award-agent has confirmed the governing instrument; it never originates them.

Where a part-time employee's hours vary from week to week by mutual agreement, the employer must have a clear record of that variation. Ad hoc additional hours that are not recorded against a variation to the roster agreement create a paper trail problem — and where those hours attract penalty rates, underpayment is the likely consequence of poor record-keeping rather than malice.

Commission workers — SG on commissions, PAYG on variable pay

Commission income is ordinary time earnings (OTE) for superannuation guarantee purposes. This is one of the more commonly misunderstood SG rules in practice. An employer who pays a salesperson a base salary plus monthly commission and calculates SG only on the base salary is underpaying SG. The commission is OTE and attracts SG at the current rate.

The SG rate as at 1 July 2025 is 12%. Verify against the ATO for any period prior to 1 July 2025 and for any future legislative change. The payroll engine applies 12% to the full OTE figure, which includes commission. The sg-agent routes the input; the deterministic layer does the arithmetic.

PAYG withholding on commission income is more complex. Commission is not a regular, predictable amount. It varies month to month, sometimes dramatically. The ATO provides averaging methods for calculating PAYG withholding on variable commission — these are designed to smooth the withholding across the year so the employee does not face large tax debts or excessive over-withholding. Direct employees and their employers to ATO tax tables and ATO guidance on withholding variation for commission arrangements. Do not calculate a specific withholding amount here — the ATO tables and the employee's current withholding declaration govern.

My take: The commission SG error is systemic across Australian businesses. I regularly see SG calculated on base salary alone, with commissions treated as "non-OTE bonuses." They are not. The ATO's position on OTE is clear: commissions paid in respect of ordinary hours of work are OTE. The sg-agent must receive the full OTE figure, including commission, on every pay event. Every pay run that excludes commission from SG base is accumulating a liability.

For STP2 reporting purposes, commission is disaggregated from base salary. The STP2 field for bonuses and commissions is separate from the gross earnings field. The payroll-orchestrator must ensure the disaggregation is correct before each STP submission — base pay to gross, commission to the bonuses-and-commissions field. Conflating them in the gross field does not cause an ATO rejection but it does corrupt the disaggregated data the ATO uses for pre-fill and compliance work.

// sg-agent: compute SG on OTE including commission
const computeSG = async (params) => {
  const {
    employeeId,
    payPeriodStart,
    baseSalaryGross,
    commissionAmount,
    allowancesOTE,
    otherOTE,
  } = params;

  // SG rate from July 2025 — verify for periods outside this date
  const SG_RATE = 0.12;

  const totalOTE =
    baseSalaryGross + commissionAmount + allowancesOTE + otherOTE;

  // Check MSCB — maximum super contribution base
  // MSCB is indexed annually — verify against ATO for current quarter
  const mscbRecord = await sgRules.getMSCB(payPeriodStart);

  if (!mscbRecord) {
    return {
      status: "AMBER",
      reason: "MSCB not found for period — verify against ATO",
      totalOTE,
    };
  }

  const cappedOTE = Math.min(totalOTE, mscbRecord.quarterlyMSCB);

  const sgAmount = parseFloat((cappedOTE * SG_RATE).toFixed(2));

  return {
    status: "GREEN",
    totalOTE,
    cappedOTE,
    sgAmount,
    sgRate: SG_RATE,
    mscbApplied: totalOTE > mscbRecord.quarterlyMSCB,
    basis: `OTE ${totalOTE} capped at MSCB ${mscbRecord.quarterlyMSCB} x ${SG_RATE}`,
    verifyNote: "Verify MSCB and SG rate against ATO — indexed annually",
  };
};

The MSCB (maximum super contributions base) caps the OTE figure to which SG applies. Above the MSCB, the employer has no mandatory SG obligation. The MSCB is indexed annually — verify the current quarterly figure against the ATO. Do not hardcode the MSCB in the payroll engine. The sg-agent must retrieve it from a reference data store that is updated at each ATO indexation announcement.

Piece-rate workers and labour hire

Piece-rate workers are paid per unit of output rather than per hour worked. Piece-rate arrangements are common in horticulture, food processing, manufacturing, and certain construction trades. They are legal in Australia under the Fair Work Act, but the arrangement must satisfy the requirement that the effective hourly rate is not less than the minimum rate under the applicable award for the hours worked.

This creates a two-step calculation. The payroll engine first computes total earnings for the period (units completed x piece rate). It then computes effective hourly rate (total earnings divided by hours worked). If the effective hourly rate falls below the award minimum for the relevant classification, the employee must be topped up to the award minimum. The award-agent must confirm the minimum rate before any piece-rate calculation is finalised.

Labour hire workers add a further complication. A labour hire arrangement places a worker at a host employer's site, but the legal employment relationship is with the labour hire company. The host employer does not pay the worker directly — the labour hire company does. However, the award that governs the worker's pay is typically determined by the type of work performed at the host site and the applicable modern award for that industry. The labour hire company must apply the correct award for the work performed, not the award (if any) under which it operates as a business.

When a labour hire company engages the payroll-orchestrator, the award-agent must classify the worker against the host industry award. Where that classification is uncertain — where the work could fall under multiple awards, or where the classification level is ambiguous — the H3 gate fires and a practitioner must resolve the award question before pay is processed.

// special-populations-agent: piece-rate worker classification check
const classifyPieceRateWorker = async (params) => {
  const {
    employeeId,
    unitsCompleted,
    pieceRatePerUnit,
    hoursWorked,
    awardCode,
    classification,
    payPeriodStart,
  } = params;

  const totalEarnings = unitsCompleted * pieceRatePerUnit;
  const effectiveHourlyRate = hoursWorked > 0
    ? totalEarnings / hoursWorked
    : 0;

  const awardMin = await awardAgent.getMinimumRate({
    awardCode,
    classification,
    effectiveDate: payPeriodStart,
  });

  if (awardMin.status === "AMBER") {
    return {
      path: "hold",
      gate: "H3",
      reason: `Award minimum rate unconfirmed: ${awardMin.reason}`,
    };
  }

  const shortfall =
    effectiveHourlyRate < awardMin.hourlyRate
      ? (awardMin.hourlyRate - effectiveHourlyRate) * hoursWorked
      : 0;

  return {
    path: "piece-rate",
    totalEarnings,
    effectiveHourlyRate: parseFloat(effectiveHourlyRate.toFixed(4)),
    awardMinimum: awardMin.hourlyRate,
    topUpRequired: shortfall > 0,
    topUpAmount: parseFloat(shortfall.toFixed(2)),
    finalPayable: parseFloat((totalEarnings + shortfall).toFixed(2)),
  };
};
// award-agent: labour hire worker award check
const getLabourHireAward = async ({ workType, hostIndustry, effectiveDate }) => {
  const candidates = await awardDb.findByIndustryAndWork({
    industry: hostIndustry,
    workType,
    effectiveDate,
  });

  if (candidates.length === 0) {
    return {
      status: "AMBER",
      reason: `No award found for ${workType} in ${hostIndustry}`,
      awardCode: null,
    };
  }

  if (candidates.length > 1) {
    return {
      status: "AMBER",
      reason: `Multiple awards possible: ${candidates.map((c) => c.code).join(", ")} — H3 required`,
      awardCode: null,
      candidates,
    };
  }

  return {
    status: "GREEN",
