    awardCode: candidates[0].code,
    awardName: candidates[0].name,
    verifyNote: "AWARD-GOVERNS — verify applicable award before each pay run",
  };
};

Payroll tax does not exist at the federal level. Each state and territory has its own payroll tax legislation, threshold, and rate. Labour hire companies operating across multiple states face multistate payroll tax obligations. The payroll-orchestrator does not compute payroll tax — that sits outside the per-employee pay event model and requires a separate compliance process at the entity level. Flag this for the employer's tax adviser.

special-populations-agent — classification and routing

The special-populations-agent is the intake layer for every non-standard employment type. Its job is to classify the pay event, confirm the classification is supportable against the employee record and applicable award, and route the event to the correct calculation path. It classifies and routes; computing pay and SG belongs to the engine and the sg-agent.

The payroll-orchestrator calls the special-populations-agent at the start of every pay event processing cycle, before the payroll engine is invoked. The classification output determines which agents the payroll-orchestrator calls next and in what order.

// payroll-orchestrator: routing based on employment type
const processPayEvent = async (payEvent) => {
  const classification = await specialPopulationsAgent.classify(payEvent);

  if (classification.path === "hold") {
    await workQueue.addHoldItem({
      payEvent,
      gate: classification.gate,
      reason: classification.reason,
      assignedTo: "practitioner-review",
    });
    return { status: "HELD", gate: classification.gate };
  }

  switch (classification.path) {
    case "standard":
      return await processPermanentFullTime(payEvent, classification);

    case "casual":
      return await processCasual(payEvent, classification);

    case "part-time":
      return await processPartTime(payEvent, classification);

    case "commission":
      return await processCommission(payEvent, classification);

    case "piece-rate":
      return await processPieceRate(payEvent, classification);

    case "labour-hire":
      return await processLabourHire(payEvent, classification);

    default:
      // Unknown path — escalate to H3, do not process
      await workQueue.addHoldItem({
        payEvent,
        gate: "H3",
        reason: `Unknown employment path: ${classification.path}`,
      });
      return { status: "HELD", gate: "H3" };
  }
};

The classification is deterministic given the employee record and award data. If either is missing or ambiguous, the path is "hold" and the gate fires. The payroll-orchestrator never proceeds on ambiguity. This is the determinism-first principle applied to employment type: the correct classification must be confirmed before any calculation proceeds.

The special-populations-agent also monitors for classification drift. An employee who has been classified as casual for 6 months of regular, systematic work (12 months for a small business employer) may be eligible to notify the employer under the Employee Choice Pathway. The agent flags this for H3 review. The payroll engine can continue paying the casual loading; the flag exists so the employer can assess whether the pathway is available to the employee and whether the employee has been correctly informed of their rights.

// special-populations-agent: check Employee Choice Pathway eligibility flag
const checkCasualConversionEligibility = async (employeeId) => {
  const history = await db.payEvents.getCasualHistory(employeeId);

  const monthsOfService = history.continuousMonths;
  const isRegularSystematic = history.regularityScore > 0.75;

  // Employee Choice Pathway (Closing Loopholes No. 2 Act 2024) —
  // 6 months' service, or 12 months for small business employers
  // Threshold parameterised by employer size — verify current Fair Work Act provisions
  const employer = await db.employers.get(history.employerId);
  const CONVERSION_THRESHOLD_MONTHS = employer.isSmallBusiness ? 12 : 6;

  if (
    monthsOfService >= CONVERSION_THRESHOLD_MONTHS &&
    isRegularSystematic
  ) {
    return {
      flag: "EMPLOYEE_CHOICE_PATHWAY_ELIGIBLE",
      gate: "H3",
      reason: `${monthsOfService} months regular casual service (threshold ${CONVERSION_THRESHOLD_MONTHS} months for this employer) — employee may notify under the Employee Choice Pathway; employer must respond within 21 days and may refuse only on defined grounds`,
      verifyNote: "Employment law matter — verify current Fair Work Act Employee Choice Pathway provisions (in force 26 Feb 2025; 26 Aug 2025 for small business)",
      employeeId,
    };
  }

  return { flag: "NO_ACTION", employeeId };
};

The routing table below summarises how the special-populations-agent maps employment type to agent chain and gate conditions.

Employment type Classification trigger Agent chain Gate condition
Casual Employment record type = "casual" award-agent (loading rate) → payroll-engine → sg-agent H3 if award loading rate unconfirmed or Employee Choice Pathway notification received; H1 for STP finalisation
Part-time permanent Employment record type = "part-time" award-agent (pro-rata check) → payroll-engine → sg-agent H3 if award unclear or actual hours exceed agreed roster
Commission worker Pay components include commission flag payroll-engine (commission OTE) → sg-agent (full OTE) → STP2 disaggregate H3 if OTE classification disputed; H1 for STP finalisation
Piece-rate Pay type = "piece-rate" award-agent (minimum rate) → payroll-engine (top-up check) → sg-agent H3 if minimum rate unconfirmed or top-up calculation disputed
Labour hire Engagement type = "labour-hire" award-agent (host industry award) → payroll-engine → sg-agent H3 if host award ambiguous or multiple awards possible
Unknown / unclassified Employment type field empty or unrecognised Hold queue H3 — mandatory practitioner review before any processing

The STP2 disaggregation for commission workers warrants specific attention. The payroll-orchestrator must separate base salary from commission before submitting the STP event. Both components are taxable but they occupy different STP2 fields and have different averaging implications for PAYG withholding.

// payroll-orchestrator: STP2 disaggregation for commission vs base pay
const disaggregateSTP2 = (payComponents) => {
  const {
    baseSalary,
    casualLoading,
    commissionAmount,
    allowances,
    overtimePay,
    leavePayments,
    bonusAmount,
  } = payComponents;

  return {
    gross: baseSalary + casualLoading + overtimePay,
    bonusesAndCommissions: commissionAmount + bonusAmount,
    allowances: allowances.map((a) => ({
      type: a.atoCode,
      amount: a.amount,
    })),
    leavePayments: leavePayments.map((l) => ({
      type: l.leaveType,
      amount: l.amount,
    })),
    note: "Verify STP2 field mapping against ATO STP2 employer reporting guide",
  };
};

Worked example 1 — Nathan at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd

Assumed-fact illustration. Nathan is a casual boilermaker engaged by Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd. He works variable hours from week to week. In the fortnight ending 30 June 2026, Nathan works 76 ordinary hours. The relevant modern award is assumed to apply. The award-agent confirms the casual loading rate for Nathan's classification and award as at June 2026.

The special-populations-agent classifies Nathan as casual. It calls the award-agent, which returns GREEN with a loading rate. The payroll engine computes Nathan's ordinary time earnings: 76 hours multiplied by the award base rate, plus 25% casual loading (the confirmed award rate — verify award-specific rate for the applicable instrument). The sg-agent then receives the full OTE figure — ordinary hours earnings including casual loading, since casual loading forms part of OTE — and computes SG at 12%. The MSCB check confirms Nathan is below the quarterly cap. Every dollar amount above is engine-computed; the agents only classified and routed.

Nathan has no annual leave balance. The payroll engine does not accrue annual leave for a casual employee. The casual loading compensates for that absence. If Nathan believes he has entitlements arising from the regularity of his engagement, that is an H3 matter. The payroll engine applies the confirmed classification; resolving employment law disputes sits with the practitioner.

The STP2 event reports Nathan's earnings in the gross field. No commission. No leave accrual balance. The PAYG withholding is calculated using the ATO's tax tables for casual employment — direct to ATO tax tables for the applicable withholding rate.

What this illustrates: The award-agent intercept before any loading is applied is non-negotiable. The 25% rate must be confirmed for the specific award and classification on every pay event, not assumed. The sg-agent receives OTE including loading because casual loading forms part of ordinary time earnings for SG purposes.

Worked example 2 — Chloe at Adelaide Retail Services

Assumed-fact illustration. Chloe is a permanent part-time retail employee at Adelaide Retail Services. Her roster agreement, signed at commencement, specifies 24 ordinary hours per week across four days. The applicable modern award is the General Retail Industry Award — verify the applicable award for this employer and classification as at June 2026.

In the current pay period, Chloe's manager has rostered her for 30 hours across five days without a written variation to her roster agreement. The special-populations-agent detects actual hours (30) exceeding agreed hours (24) without a recorded roster variation. This triggers an AMBER flag. The payroll-orchestrator routes the pay event to the H3 hold queue — practitioner review before proceeding.

Without H3 review, the payroll engine cannot determine whether the additional six hours are ordinary time (if the roster agreement should have been updated), overtime (if the award overtime provisions apply), or rostered days off in lieu. The answer changes the dollar amount of Chloe's pay, her leave accruals for the period, and her SG base.

Once H3 review confirms the treatment — in this illustration, the additional hours are ordinary time under a verbal agreement that must now be formalised — the payroll engine computes pro-rata annual leave based on the corrected hours for the period: 30 hours worked at the ordinary rate, with leave accruing at 4/52 of 24 agreed hours per week (the formal agreement). The practitioner's determination of which hours were ordinary time is the input; the arithmetic is the engine's output.

What this illustrates: Roster agreements must be in writing for part-time employees. Actual hours exceeding agreed hours without a written variation is an automatic H3 trigger. The payroll engine cannot resolve that question — it can only detect the discrepancy and hold the event. The pro-rata leave calculation is deterministic once the agreed ordinary hours are confirmed.

Worked example 3 — Sasha at Metro Digital Solutions

Assumed-fact illustration. Sasha is a permanent full-time sales manager at Metro Digital Solutions. Her package is a base salary of $90,000 per year plus commission. In the quarter ending 30 June 2026, Sasha earns $22,500 in commission payments on top of her pro-rata base salary for the quarter.

The special-populations-agent classifies Sasha as a commission worker (employment type: permanent full-time with commission component). The payroll-orchestrator routes the pay event through the commission path. The payroll engine assembles the OTE figure: quarterly base salary plus commission. The sg-agent receives the full OTE and applies the 12% SG rate. The MSCB check is run against the quarterly cap — verify the current quarterly MSCB against the ATO, as it is indexed annually.

PAYG withholding on Sasha's commission is calculated using the ATO's averaging method for commission income. The payroll system implements the averaging method as described in ATO guidance — direct to ATO guidance on PAYG withholding for commission arrangements for the specific calculation. The payroll engine executes the method; it does not invent the rate.

The STP2 event disaggregates Sasha's earnings: base salary to the gross field, commission to the bonuses-and-commissions field. The payroll-orchestrator validates this disaggregation before STP submission. Where Sasha has made salary sacrifice contributions, those are disaggregated separately — salary sacrifice super to its own STP2 field, reducing reportable gross accordingly.

What this illustrates: Commission is OTE. There is no calculation path that legitimately excludes commission from the SG base. The sg-agent receives total OTE on every pay event. The STP2 disaggregation separates commission from base salary but both are taxable — the disaggregation is for ATO reporting accuracy, not for tax preferencing.

Worked example 4 — Marcus at Westfield Logistics

Assumed-fact illustration. Marcus is engaged by Westfield Logistics as a parcel sorter. The engagement letter describes him as a "piece-rate contractor." The special-populations-agent receives his first pay event and runs the worker classification check before the piece-rate path is invoked.

The contractor label in the engagement letter is not determinative. The special-populations-agent calls the award-agent to assess the work type and industry. The award-agent searches for the applicable modern award for parcel sorting and transport logistics work. It returns an AMBER status: two awards are potentially applicable — the Road Transport and Distribution Award and the Storage Services and Wholesale Award. The agent cannot resolve the ambiguity without further information about the precise nature of the work, whether Marcus uses his own vehicle, and other classification factors.

The payroll-orchestrator routes Marcus's pay event to the H3 hold queue — practitioner review before proceeding. This is the correct outcome. Proceeding on an uncertain award classification for a piece-rate worker creates multiple simultaneous risks: incorrect minimum rate comparison, incorrect SG base, and potentially incorrect employment classification (employee vs contractor) that the multi-factor test would need to resolve.

The H3 practitioner reviews the engagement, determines the applicable award, and confirms Marcus's employment classification. In this illustration, the practitioner determines Marcus is an employee under the applicable award, not an independent contractor. The payroll engine is then configured with the correct award, classification level, minimum rate, and piece-rate structure. Only then does the payroll engine process Marcus's pay events going forward.

What this illustrates: The "contractor" label on an engagement document does not determine the correct payroll treatment. The special-populations-agent detects award ambiguity and fires the H3 gate before any payment is processed. Proceeding without award confirmation for a piece-rate worker accumulates an underpayment liability with every pay run.

Employment type Casual loading Annual leave SG base STP2 income type
Full-time permanent Nil 4 weeks per year (full hours) OTE (all ordinary time earnings) SAW (salary and wages)
Part-time permanent Nil 4 weeks per year (pro-rata agreed hours) OTE (ordinary time at agreed hours) SAW (salary and wages)
Casual Award rate (commonly 25% — verify award) Verify current Fair Work position — loading compensates for standard entitlements OTE including casual loading — verify ATO position SAW (salary and wages)
Piece-rate Nil (separate minimum rate check applies) Pro-rata if employee; verify award OTE based on earnings (subject to MSCB) SAW (salary and wages) — verify if labour hire applies
Payment type OTE? SG applies? STP2 field Verify caveat
Base salary / ordinary wages Yes Yes — at 12% from 1 July 2025 Gross Verify SG rate for periods prior to 1 July 2025
Commission (ordinary hours) Yes Yes — commission is OTE Bonuses and commissions Verify OTE classification with ATO if arrangement is unusual
Performance bonus Generally yes if for ordinary hours Yes if OTE Bonuses and commissions Verify — some bonuses may not be OTE; ATO guidance governs
Overtime payments No — excluded from OTE No SG on pure overtime Gross (overtime component) Verify if loading vs overtime rate — mixed arrangements require care
Allowances (work-related) Varies by allowance type Only if OTE Allowances (with ATO code) Verify each allowance type against ATO OTE definition
Casual loading Yes — forms part of OTE Yes Gross Verify ATO position — confirm loading is OTE for the specific arrangement 
// special-populations-agent: output routing table
const getRoutingTable = () => [
  {
    employmentType: "casual",
    trigger: "employment_record.type === 'casual'",
    agentChain: ["award-agent", "payroll-engine", "sg-agent"],
    gate: "H3 if award loading unconfirmed; H1 for STP finalisation",
  },
  {
    employmentType: "part-time",
    trigger: "employment_record.type === 'part-time'",
    agentChain: ["award-agent", "payroll-engine", "sg-agent"],
    gate: "H3 if award unclear or hours exceed roster",
  },
  {
    employmentType: "commission",
    trigger: "pay_components.includes('commission')",
    agentChain: ["payroll-engine", "sg-agent", "stp2-disaggregator"],
    gate: "H3 if OTE classification disputed; H1 for STP finalisation",
  },
  {
    employmentType: "piece-rate",
    trigger: "pay_type === 'piece-rate'",
    agentChain: ["award-agent", "payroll-engine", "sg-agent"],
    gate: "H3 if minimum rate unconfirmed or top-up disputed",
  },
  {
    employmentType: "labour-hire",
    trigger: "engagement_type === 'labour-hire'",
    agentChain: ["award-agent (host industry)", "payroll-engine", "sg-agent"],
    gate: "H3 if host award ambiguous",
  },
  {
    employmentType: "unknown",
    trigger: "employment_record.type not recognised",
    agentChain: ["hold-queue"],
    gate: "H3 — mandatory before any processing",
  },
];

The payroll-orchestrator consults this routing table on every pay event. The table is not a suggestion — it is the control plane. An employment type that does not match a known path results in a hold, every time. The cost of one incorrect classification is lower than the cost of systematic misclassification across dozens of pay runs.

My take: Special populations are where payroll systems fail most often and most expensively. Casuals miscounted for SG. Part-timers pro-rated against the wrong hours base. Commission workers SG-calculated on base salary alone. Each of these is a known, preventable error. The special-populations-agent exists because the payroll engine should never have to guess employment type. Classification is deterministic once the employee record and award data are correct. The agent enforces that precondition before the engine ever touches a number.

Next: Chapter 13 covers worker classification in depth — the multi-factor test, sham contracting, reclassification consequences, and the H1 gate for formal review.

13 Worker Classification — Employee vs Contractor

Common law multi-factor test, recent High Court authority, sham contracting, and the H1 gate for reclassification

No question in Australian payroll law carries higher dollar risk than worker classification. Get it wrong and you face back-pay of entitlements, SG shortfalls plus the Superannuation Guarantee Charge, payroll tax exposure, FBT reconsideration, and workers' compensation liability — often across multiple financial years. The classification-agent runs the multi-factor check, but an AMBER or RED result is not a finding you act on without the H1 and H3 gates.

The multi-factor test — the legal framework

Australian courts do not determine worker classification by looking at the label the parties chose. The word "contractor" in an agreement is a starting point, not an answer. The High Court has confirmed — in decisions you should verify before citing — that the true nature of the relationship is determined by examining the totality of the contract and the conduct of the parties.

The multi-factor test emerged from decades of common law development. It draws together a cluster of factors, each of which points toward or away from employment. No single factor is determinative. A worker who sets their own hours might still be an employee if control over their work method is tight. A worker paid by results might still be an employee if they cannot delegate and are integrated into the business.

The High Court decisions of 2022 — verify citations before use — shifted the analysis. Where earlier case law placed weight on the totality of the relationship as it played out in practice, the High Court emphasised that the starting point is the written contract, provided the contract is not a sham and its terms have not been varied by conduct. This has practical consequences. Where a written contract clearly sets out terms consistent with independent contracting, courts are less willing to look behind it. But "clearly set out" does the heavy lifting. Many so-called contractor agreements are poorly drafted and do not withstand scrutiny.

The Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) introduced its own definition of employee for the purposes of the National Employment Standards and modern awards, effective 26 August 2024. The statutory definition uses "ordinary meaning" of employee at common law. This brings the High Court multi-factor analysis back into play for Fair Work Act purposes. Payroll practitioners must understand both the common law test and the statutory context.

My take: The 2022 High Court shift matters in practice. If your engagement is governed by a written contract, the classification-agent's output will be more reliable when you feed it the actual contract terms rather than a summary. Garbage in, garbage out. An AMBER result from a vague input is not the same as an AMBER from a detailed contract analysis. Both require the H3 gate.

The classification-agent applies the multi-factor test by accepting a structured worker profile. The profile should capture the terms of engagement, payment structure, equipment provision, right to delegate, integration into the business, and whether the worker operates a separate business. Each factor is scored, and the agent returns a confidence tier — GREEN, AMBER, or RED — alongside a factor-by-factor analysis.

// classification-agent invocation — assumed-fact illustration
{
  "agent": "classification-agent",
  "action": "run_multi_factor_check",
  "worker_profile": {
    "worker_name": "James Whitfield",
    "employer": "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
    "engagement_type": "written_services_agreement",
    "engagement_date": "2023-03-01",
    "payment_structure": "daily_rate",
    "hours_set_by": "client",
    "work_location": "client_premises",
    "equipment_owner": "client",
    "right_to_delegate": false,
    "performs_work_for_others": false,
    "own_abn": true,
    "invoices_submitted": true,
    "integration_assessment": "works_alongside_employees_on_same_tasks"
  }
}

The agent processes each factor against the common law multi-factor framework. Control, integration, delegation, equipment, and results-based payment are all examined. The output returns a structured assessment with a confidence tier.

Confidence Tier What it means Gate that fires Action required
GREEN Factors strongly consistent with independent contractor; low reclassification risk None — but document the assessment Retain workpaper; review annually or if engagement terms change
AMBER Mixed factors; outcome turns on weight given to contested factors H3 gate fires immediately Practitioner review before any payroll or compliance action
RED Factors strongly consistent with employment; high reclassification risk H3 gate fires; H1 gate fires if reclassification proceeds Employment lawyer advice recommended; registered tax agent for tax consequences

The H3 gate is mandatory. When the classification-agent returns AMBER or RED, the payroll-orchestrator routes the result to a practitioner queue before any downstream computation runs. The engine withholds SG shortfall, back-pay, and payroll tax exposure until the H3 gate is cleared.

// classification-agent output — multi-factor check result (AMBER)
{
  "classification_result": {
    "worker": "James Whitfield",
    "employer": "Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd",
    "confidence_tier": "AMBER",
    "overall_risk": "moderate_to_high",
    "factor_scores": {
      "control_over_method": { "score": -2, "indicates": "employee", "note": "client sets hours and supervises daily" },
      "integration_into_business": { "score": -2, "indicates": "employee", "note": "works alongside permanent staff on same tasks" },
      "right_to_delegate": { "score": -1, "indicates": "employee", "note": "no delegation right in practice" },
      "equipment_provision": { "score": -1, "indicates": "employee", "note": "client provides all tools and PPE" },
      "payment_structure": { "score": 0, "indicates": "neutral", "note": "daily rate — neither piece-work nor salary" },
      "abn_and_invoicing": { "score": +1, "indicates": "contractor", "note": "ABN held; invoices submitted" },
      "works_for_others": { "score": -1, "indicates": "employee", "note": "no evidence of other clients" }
    },
    "net_score": -6,
    "gate": "H3",
    "recommended_action": "route_to_practitioner_review",
    "note": "Contractor label not determinative. Written agreement must be assessed by employment law practitioner."
  }
}

Control, integration, and results-based payment

Three factors carry the most weight in Australian classification disputes: control over the method of work, integration into the business, and whether payment is for results or for time.

Control does not mean direction over every task. It means the right to control how work is done, beyond what work is done. An employer who says "build this component, here is the spec, here is the timeline, work from our site, use our tools, attend our briefings" is exercising control over method — even if the worker has technical autonomy. The classification-agent scores control by examining where hours are set, whether the worker attends the client's premises, whether daily supervision occurs, and whether the client can redirect the worker to different tasks.

Integration is the question of whether the worker's services are an integral part of the business or an accessory to it. A plumber who comes once to fix a pipe is an accessory. A plumber who works full-time, attends team meetings, uses a company email address, and is listed on the company website is integrated. The classification-agent checks: work location, whether the worker holds a company email or access credentials, whether they appear in the company org chart or directory, and whether they perform tasks indistinguishable from those of employees.

Results-based payment is a contractor indicator, but it is easily faked. If a daily rate is paid regardless of the actual output — if turning up is enough to earn the rate — then it is not truly results-based. The classification-agent distinguishes between payment contingent on a deliverable and payment for attendance.

My take: I see a pattern in AMBER results. The employer thinks "we pay by invoice, they have an ABN, they're a contractor." The agent surfaces the control factors and integration factors the employer had not considered. The invoice and ABN are real, but they do not outweigh four factors pointing the other way. This is exactly why the H3 gate fires — the practitioner needs to see the full factor-by-factor analysis, well beyond the headline tier.

The payroll-orchestrator handles routing when the classification-agent returns AMBER. It does not proceed to payroll computation. It creates a practitioner review task and parks the worker's classification file.

// payroll-orchestrator routing on AMBER classification result
{
  "orchestrator_action": "route_on_amber_classification",
  "worker": "James Whitfield",
  "classification_tier": "AMBER",
  "routing": {
    "destination": "practitioner_review_queue",
    "priority": "high",
    "gate": "H3",
    "blocked_downstream_actions": [
      "sg_shortfall_calculation",
      "back_pay_computation",
      "payroll_tax_exposure_estimate",
      "stp_amendment_filing"
    ],
    "required_inputs": [
      "written_contract_review_by_employment_law_practitioner",
      "registered_tax_agent_assessment_if_reclassification_proceeds"
    ],
    "escalation_note": "Do not act on classification result without H3 gate clearance. H1 gate required for tax consequences."
  }
}

Right to delegate and equipment provision

The right to delegate is one of the clearest contractor indicators — when it genuinely exists. A true contractor can send a substitute to perform the work. The client engages the result, not the person. If the contract says the worker can delegate but in practice the client would not accept a substitute, the right is illusory. The classification-agent checks both the contract terms and the practical reality by asking whether substitutes have ever been used and whether the client would accept one.

Equipment provision is a supporting factor, not a standalone test. A worker who provides their own tools, vehicle, equipment, and materials is running a business. A worker who uses the client's tools, the client's vehicle, the client's safety equipment, and the client's materials is operating inside the client's business. The classification-agent inventories the equipment question across five sub-factors: tools of trade, computer and software, vehicle, consumables, and safety equipment. Each sub-factor adds to the overall score.

The combination of no delegation right and full equipment provision by the client is a strong employment signal. The classification-agent weights these together. A worker who cannot delegate and uses the client's tools is unlikely to score GREEN.

For Mei at Adelaide Retail Services — an IT contractor engaged to build internal dashboards — the classification-agent's check on delegation and equipment produced a RED result. Mei worked entirely on Adelaide Retail Services' systems, used their developer licences, attended their daily standups, and had never sent a substitute in eighteen months of engagement. The right to delegate in her agreement was nominal.

// classification-agent — right-to-delegate and equipment check (Mei, Adelaide Retail Services)
{
  "agent": "classification-agent",
  "action": "run_multi_factor_check",
  "worker_profile": {
    "worker_name": "Mei Chen",
    "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
    "engagement_type": "written_services_agreement",
    "payment_structure": "monthly_retainer",
    "hours_set_by": "client",
    "work_location": "client_premises_and_client_remote_systems",
    "equipment_owner": "client",
    "software_licences": "client_provided",
    "right_to_delegate": {
      "contractual": true,
      "practical": false,
      "substitute_ever_used": false,
      "client_would_accept_substitute": "unknown"
    },
    "works_for_others": false,
    "duration_of_engagement_months": 18,
    "integration_assessment": "attends_daily_standups_uses_company_systems_listed_in_team_directory"
  }
}
// classification-agent output — RED result (Mei, Adelaide Retail Services)
{
  "classification_result": {
    "worker": "Mei Chen",
    "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
    "confidence_tier": "RED",
    "overall_risk": "high",
    "factor_scores": {
      "control_over_method": { "score": -2, "indicates": "employee" },
      "integration_into_business": { "score": -2, "indicates": "employee" },
      "right_to_delegate": { "score": -2, "indicates": "employee", "note": "contractual right illusory in practice" },
      "equipment_provision": { "score": -2, "indicates": "employee", "note": "all equipment client-provided" },
      "payment_structure": { "score": -1, "indicates": "employee", "note": "monthly retainer regardless of output" },
      "duration": { "score": -1, "indicates": "employee", "note": "18-month engagement, no end-date in sight" },
      "abn_and_invoicing": { "score": +1, "indicates": "contractor" }
    },
    "net_score": -9,
    "gate": "H3_and_H1",
    "recommended_action": "route_to_practitioner_review_then_registered_tax_agent",
    "sham_contracting_flag": false,
    "sg_shortfall_risk": "high — compute only after H3 clearance",
    "payroll_tax_risk": "state_specific — verify applicable state threshold"
  }
}

This is assumed-fact illustration only. What this illustrates: A contractual delegation right that has never been exercised and would not practically be accepted by the client carries little weight. The engine does not compute the SG shortfall until the H3 gate is cleared by a practitioner. When reclassification is confirmed, the H1 gate fires for the tax consequences.

Sham contracting — Fair Work Act penalties

Sham contracting is a deliberate misrepresentation, distinct from an honest classification error. Section 357 of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth) makes it unlawful for an employer to misrepresent an employment relationship as an independent contracting arrangement. The provision imposes civil penalties. Ignorance of the law is not a defence, but genuine belief that the arrangement was lawful is a defence to the s357 prohibition — it is not a defence to the classification itself.

Three sham contracting prohibitions exist under the Fair Work Act (ss357-359 — verify citations before use):

  • Misrepresenting employment as independent contracting (s357)
  • Dismissing an employee and re-engaging as a contractor (s358)
  • Making a false statement to induce an employee to become a contractor (s359)

The civil penalties are significant and indexed. Verify current penalty unit values against the Fair Work Act and the Crimes Act 1914 (Cth) as at June 2026. Penalties apply per contravention, not per engagement. An employer with multiple misrepresented contractors faces multiple contraventions.

The classification-agent surfaces a sham contracting risk flag when the engagement profile shows indicators of deliberate misrepresentation: a worker recently dismissed and re-engaged as a contractor for the same role, a contract that mirrors an employment agreement in substance but is labelled as a services agreement, or a documented business decision to convert employees to contractors without genuine change in working arrangements.

// classification-agent — sham contracting risk assessment
{
  "agent": "classification-agent",
  "action": "run_sham_contracting_assessment",
  "worker_profile": {
    "worker_name": "Priya Sharma",
    "employer": "Metro Digital Solutions",
    "engagement_type": "services_agreement",
    "prior_employment_history": {
      "was_previously_employed_by_same_entity": true,
      "termination_date": "2023-06-30",
      "re_engagement_date": "2023-07-01",
      "role_change": false,
      "duties_changed": false,
      "payment_rate_change": "reduced_slightly",
      "reason_given_for_restructure": "business_efficiency"
    },
    "current_working_arrangements": "identical_to_prior_employment",
    "abn_obtained": "after_re_engagement",
    "worker_requested_change": false
  }
}
// classification-agent — sham contracting risk output (Priya, Metro Digital Solutions)
{
  "sham_contracting_assessment": {
    "worker": "Priya Sharma",
    "employer": "Metro Digital Solutions",
    "sham_contracting_risk": "HIGH",
    "triggered_provisions": ["FW Act s357", "FW Act s358"],
    "indicators": [
      "dismissed and immediately re-engaged in same role",
      "no change in duties or working arrangements",
      "ABN obtained after re-engagement at employer's suggestion",
      "worker did not initiate change"
    ],
    "gate": "H2",
    "H2_reason": "Formal ATO and Fair Work compliance response requires registered tax agent and employment lawyer",
    "recommended_actions": [
      "cease processing as contractor immediately pending legal review",
      "engage registered tax agent for SG shortfall and payroll tax assessment",
      "engage employment lawyer for Fair Work Act exposure",
      "do not file STP amendments without H1 clearance"
    ],
    "note": "Civil penalty exposure is per contravention. Verify current penalty unit values as at June 2026."
  }
}

This is assumed-fact illustration only. What this illustrates: The classification-agent scores the current engagement and examines its history. Dismiss-and-re-engage on identical terms is the canonical sham contracting pattern. The H2 gate fires because the employer needs a registered tax agent for the tax consequences and an employment lawyer for the Fair Work Act exposure. These are not the same person.

My take: Sham contracting is the scenario that most frequently produces multi-agency liability. The ATO is interested in SG and PAYG withholding. Fair Work is interested in the civil penalty. The state revenue office is interested in payroll tax. Workers' compensation is a fourth exposure. One engagement, four regulators. The H2 gate exists precisely for this scenario — the formal compliance response has to be coordinated, not handled piecemeal.

Reclassification consequences — the full exposure matrix

When a worker is reclassified from contractor to employee, the consequences reach backwards as well as forwards. They run back to the date the relationship began — or to the earliest open limitation period, which can extend several years. The exposure matrix covers five areas: back-pay of entitlements, SG shortfalls plus SGC, payroll tax, FBT reconsideration, and workers' compensation.

Exposure type Trigger Calculation owner Gate Who reviews
Back-pay of entitlements (annual leave, personal leave, redundancy) Reclassification as employee; applicable award or enterprise agreement entitlements apply from commencement date Payroll engine — AWARD-GOVERNS flag applies; verify applicable award before engine runs H3, then H1 Employment lawyer; registered tax agent for PAYG on back-pay
SG shortfall plus SGC SG not paid during period of misclassification; SG rate 12% from 1 July 2025 — verify as at June 2026 Engine-computed, not agent-computed — SGC components include SG shortfall, interest (10% p.a.), and administration fee; verify against ATO SGC statement H1 Registered tax agent; ATO voluntary disclosure recommended
PAYG withholding shortfall Payments made without withholding during misclassification period Payroll engine calculates gross-up or net-of-tax figure; direct to ATO tax tables — engine does not produce specific PAYG amounts for user consumption H1 Registered tax agent; STP amendment required
Payroll tax exposure Wages paid to misclassified worker may be wages for state payroll tax purposes; no federal payroll tax — each state has its own threshold and rate State-specific calculation; engine flags state of engagement and relevant threshold — verify with state revenue office H1 Registered tax agent or tax adviser with state payroll tax expertise
FBT reconsideration Benefits provided during engagement may be fringe benefits if worker was an employee; cars, parking, laptops re-assessed FBT computation is payroll engine territory only if benefit values are known; employer must identify and value benefits first H1 Registered tax agent
Workers' compensation exposure Misclassified employee may have been uninsured for work injuries during engagement period Workers' compensation is state-specific; engine flags the risk and state but does not compute premium or penalty H3 Employment lawyer; notify state workers' compensation authority

The reclassification exposure calculator is invoked by the payroll-orchestrator after the H3 gate is cleared. It does not run automatically. The engine computes every regulated amount — SG shortfall, SGC components, PAYG gross-ups — but not until the practitioner has confirmed the reclassification decision.

// reclassification exposure calculator — invocation after H3 clearance
{
  "agent": "payroll-orchestrator",
  "action": "run_reclassification_exposure_calculator",
  "worker": "Mei Chen",
  "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
  "h3_gate_cleared": true,
  "h3_clearing_practitioner": "employment_law_practitioner",
  "reclassification_confirmed": true,
  "employment_commencement_date": "2022-10-01",
  "reclassification_effective_date": "2022-10-01",
  "applicable_award": {
    "status": "AWARD-GOVERNS",
    "award_name": "PENDING — verify applicable award before engine runs",
    "award_agent_check_required": true
  },
  "periods": [
    { "fy": "2022-23", "gross_payments": 110000, "sg_rate": 0.105, "sg_not_paid": true },
    { "fy": "2023-24", "gross_payments": 132000, "sg_rate": 0.11, "sg_not_paid": true },
    { "fy": "2024-25", "gross_payments": 138000, "sg_rate": 0.115, "sg_not_paid": true },
    { "fy": "2025-26", "gross_payments": 72000, "sg_rate": 0.12, "sg_not_paid": true }
  ],
  "state_of_engagement": "SA",
  "payroll_tax_check_required": true
}
// SG shortfall exposure workpaper structure — engine output
{
  "sg_shortfall_workpaper": {
    "worker": "Mei Chen",
    "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
    "note": "All amounts are calculated by the engine, never by agent code, and must be verified by a registered tax agent before any payment or disclosure to the ATO.",
    "sg_rate_note": "SG rate 12% applies from 1 July 2025. Earlier periods use rates applicable to those years. Verify all rates as at June 2026.",
    "periods": [
      {
        "fy": "2022-23",
        "ordinary_time_earnings": "COMPUTE — engine runs award-agent check first",
        "sg_rate": "10.5% — verify",
        "sg_shortfall": "COMPUTED BY ENGINE",
        "sgc_interest": "10% p.a. on shortfall — computed by engine",
        "sgc_admin_fee": "$20 per quarter per employee — verify current amount with ATO"
      },
      {
        "fy": "2023-24",
        "sg_rate": "11% — verify",
        "sg_shortfall": "COMPUTED BY ENGINE"
      },
      {
        "fy": "2024-25",
        "sg_rate": "11.5% — verify",
        "sg_shortfall": "COMPUTED BY ENGINE"
      },
      {
        "fy": "2025-26",
        "sg_rate": "12% from 1 July 2025 — verify as at June 2026",
        "sg_shortfall": "COMPUTED BY ENGINE"
      }
    ],
    "total_sgc_exposure": "COMPUTED BY ENGINE",
    "gate": "H1",
    "voluntary_disclosure_recommended": true,
    "note": "SGC is not deductible. PAYG withholding gross-up is a separate calculation — refer to ATO tax tables. Engine does not produce specific withholding amounts for user consumption."
  }
}

This is assumed-fact illustration only. What this illustrates: The engine computes every regulated figure once the H3 gate clears and the award-agent has confirmed the applicable award. The SG shortfall is not simply the SG rate multiplied by payments — ordinary time earnings (OTE) must be determined, and OTE depends on award classification. The SGC adds interest at 10% per annum and an administration fee. Total exposure compounds quickly across multiple financial years.

Award-agent must run before the exposure calculator can produce OTE-based figures. The AWARD-GOVERNS flag is a hard dependency.

// award-agent check on reclassified worker — Mei Chen
{
  "agent": "award-agent",
  "action": "identify_applicable_award",
  "worker": "Mei Chen",
  "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
  "role": "IT developer — dashboard and reporting systems",
  "state": "SA",
  "employment_status": "employee_from_reclassification",
  "note": "AWARD-GOVERNS. Award-agent output required before payroll engine can compute OTE for SG shortfall. Verify award and classification level with HR and payroll practitioner."
}

H1 gate — registered tax agent and employment law review

The H1 gate is the final control before any reclassification consequence is acted on. It requires a registered tax agent. The word "registered" is load-bearing — it refers to a tax agent registered under the Tax Agent Services Act 2009 (Cth) with the Tax Practitioners Board. Not a bookkeeper. Not a payroll manager. Not an unregistered adviser.

For worker classification matters with reclassification consequences, I recommend engaging an employment lawyer alongside the registered tax agent. The employment lawyer addresses the Fair Work Act exposure, the back-pay of entitlements, and the characterisation of the relationship. The registered tax agent addresses SG shortfalls, SGC, PAYG withholding corrections, STP amendments, and payroll tax referrals. These are different disciplines. A registered tax agent is not qualified to give employment law advice. An employment lawyer is not qualified to give tax advice.

The H1 gate fires automatically in the payroll-orchestrator when any of the following conditions are met:

  • Classification-agent returns RED
  • Reclassification is confirmed after H3 review
  • Sham contracting risk is flagged at HIGH
  • SG shortfall exposure exceeds zero for any period
  • PAYG withholding shortfall is identified
  • Payroll tax exposure is flagged in any state
// H1 gate fire — route to registered tax agent
{
  "orchestrator_action": "fire_h1_gate",
  "worker": "Mei Chen",
  "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
  "trigger": "reclassification_confirmed_sg_shortfall_identified",
  "h1_requirements": {
    "registered_tax_agent": {
      "required": true,
      "reason": "SG shortfall, SGC, PAYG withholding correction, STP amendment, payroll tax referral",
      "action": "do_not_file_stp_amendments_without_h1_clearance"
    },
    "employment_lawyer": {
      "recommended": true,
      "reason": "Back-pay entitlements, Fair Work Act exposure, characterisation of relationship"
    }
  },
  "blocked_actions": [
    "stp_amendment_filing",
    "ato_voluntary_disclosure",
    "payroll_tax_amendment",
    "workers_compensation_notification"
  ],
  "engine_status": "figures_computed_held_pending_h1_review",
  "note": "Each amount is produced by the engine, not the agent. Figures are computed and held. They are not acted on until H1 gate is cleared by a registered tax agent."
}

The audit-risk-agent runs in parallel with the H1 gate fire. Its job is to score the regulatory audit risk created by the reclassification and to flag priority areas for the registered tax agent's attention.

// audit-risk-agent scoring on reclassified worker
{
  "agent": "audit-risk-agent",
  "action": "score_reclassification_audit_risk",
  "worker": "Mei Chen",
  "employer": "Adelaide Retail Services",
  "inputs": {
    "reclassification_confirmed": true,
    "periods_at_risk": 4,
    "sg_shortfall_risk": "high",
    "sham_contracting_flag": false,
    "payroll_tax_state": "SA",
    "fbt_benefits_identified": false,
    "workers_comp_uninsured": true,
    "voluntary_disclosure_filed": false
  },
  "risk_score": {
    "ato_audit_risk": "HIGH",
    "fair_work_audit_risk": "MODERATE",
    "state_revenue_audit_risk": "MODERATE",
    "workers_comp_risk": "MODERATE",
    "overall": "HIGH"
  },
  "priority_actions_for_h1_practitioner": [
    "ATO voluntary disclosure — reduces SGC interest and penalties",
    "STP amendment for all affected periods",
    "SA payroll tax threshold check — verify gross wages against SA threshold as at June 2026",
    "Workers compensation — notify authority of uninsured period"
  ]
}

This is assumed-fact illustration only. What this illustrates: The audit-risk-agent gives the registered tax agent a prioritised action list. Voluntary disclosure to the ATO is almost always the right first step for SG shortfalls — it reduces the interest and penalty load. The engine has computed the figures. The H1 practitioner decides whether and how to file.

Worked examples

The following four examples are assumed-fact illustrations. All names, employers, amounts, and outcomes are constructed to demonstrate how the agents and gates interact. They are not legal advice. They do not substitute for practitioner review. All rates and thresholds should be verified as at June 2026.

Example 1 — James at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd

James Whitfield has worked at Harbour Engineering Pty Ltd for two years under a services agreement. He holds an ABN and submits weekly invoices. He works on-site, uses Harbour Engineering's tools and PPE, attends the site manager's daily briefings, and has never worked for another client during the engagement. The payroll team initiates a classification review when the financial controller raises a concern about SG exposure.

The classification-agent is invoked with James's worker profile. It scores the control factor as strongly indicative of employment — Harbour Engineering sets his hours, his location, and his daily tasks. Integration is high — James works alongside Harbour Engineering's permanent tradespeople on identical work. The right to delegate is not present in practice. Equipment is entirely provided by the client. The ABN and invoicing are genuine, but they represent one positive contractor factor against five negative indicators.

The agent returns AMBER. The payroll-orchestrator fires the H3 gate immediately. All downstream computation is blocked. A practitioner review task is created.

The employment law practitioner reviews the written services agreement and the actual working arrangements. The practitioner concludes that James is, on balance, an employee. The H3 gate is cleared with a confirmed reclassification finding. The H1 gate fires. The engagement of a registered tax agent is required before any back-pay or SG shortfall is computed for filing purposes.

The payroll engine runs the back-pay computation using the award-agent output for the applicable award. The SG shortfall is computed across both financial years. The engine holds all figures pending H1 review.

What this illustrates: The AMBER result from the classification-agent is the right outcome here — a gate trigger rather than a definitive finding. The H3 gate forces practitioner review. The H1 gate protects the employer from acting on computed figures without registered tax agent oversight. The contractor label in the agreement was not determinative, and the engine did not assume it was.

Example 2 — Mei at Adelaide Retail Services

This example is worked in full in sections 13.3 and 13.5 above. The classification-agent returned RED on the combination of illusory delegation right, full client-provided equipment, eighteen months of continuous engagement, and integration into the development team. The H1 gate fired. The reclassification exposure calculator ran across four financial years. The SG shortfall was computed by the payroll engine and held pending H1 clearance. The registered tax agent was engaged to manage the ATO voluntary disclosure and STP amendments. The audit-risk-agent scored overall risk as HIGH and prioritised the voluntary disclosure.

What this illustrates: A RED result on a long-duration engagement produces multi-year SG shortfall exposure. The SGC adds interest at 10% per annum, compounding the cost of delayed identification. The SG rate for periods before 1 July 2025 differs from the current 12% — the engine applies the correct rate for each period. All rates must be verified as at June 2026.

Example 3 — Angela at Blue Mountain Healthcare

Angela Reeves is a registered nurse who works at Blue Mountain Healthcare through a labour hire agency. She has worked at the same Blue Mountain Healthcare facility three days per week for fourteen months. Blue Mountain Healthcare directs her work, sets her roster, and supervises her patient care. The agency pays her and handles her superannuation contributions. The classification question is whether the direct engagement elements between Angela and Blue Mountain Healthcare create any employment relationship obligations for Blue Mountain Healthcare, separate from the agency arrangement.

This is a triangular employment scenario. The classification-agent identifies the direct engagement elements: Blue Mountain Healthcare exercises control over Angela's daily work, she is integrated into the facility's nursing roster, and the duration of the engagement is substantial. The agent flags that labour hire arrangements with strong integration and direction factors can give rise to obligations under the applicable state industrial legislation or the Fair Work Act, depending on the structure.

The classification-agent does not have authority to resolve this question. The labour hire licensing regime, the applicable modern award (verify applicable award — AWARD-GOVERNS), and the state-specific workers' compensation framework all interact. The H3 gate fires.

// classification-agent — labour hire triangular engagement flag (Angela, Blue Mountain Healthcare)
{
  "classification_result": {
    "worker": "Angela Reeves",
    "host_employer": "Blue Mountain Healthcare",
    "labour_hire_agency": "[agency name]",
    "confidence_tier": "AMBER",
    "triangular_employment_flag": true,
    "direct_engagement_indicators": [
      "host employer directs daily work and patient assignments",
      "worker integrated into facility roster for 14 months",
      "host employer supervises clinical practice"
    ],
    "gate": "H3",
    "h3_reason": "Labour hire triangular arrangements require employment lawyer review. Agency obligations and host obligations may differ. State labour hire licensing must be verified.",
    "employment_lawyer_required": true,
    "award_agent_check_required": true,
    "note": "Contractor label not determinative. AWARD-GOVERNS. Verify applicable nursing award and state labour hire licence status of agency."
  }
}

The H3 gate fires. An employment lawyer with healthcare sector experience is required. The lawyer must assess the labour hire licence status of the agency in the relevant state, Blue Mountain Healthcare's obligations as a host employer, and whether Angela's working arrangements give rise to any direct employment obligations.

What this illustrates: Labour hire arrangements are not automatically safe from classification risk on the host employer's side. The classification-agent identifies the direct engagement elements and fires the H3 gate. It does not purport to resolve the legal question — that requires an employment lawyer. The payroll engine holds all computation until the H3 gate is cleared and the applicable award is confirmed.

Example 4 — Priya at Metro Digital Solutions

Priya Sharma was a full-time employee of Metro Digital Solutions until 30 June 2023. On 1 July 2023, she was re-engaged as a sole trader contractor under a services agreement. Her duties did not change. Her working arrangements did not change. She obtained an ABN after Metro Digital Solutions' HR team sent her a template ABN registration guide. Metro Digital Solutions reduced her rate slightly to reflect the "contractor premium" for superannuation they would no longer pay.

The classification-agent's sham contracting assessment returns HIGH risk on section 357 and section 358 of the Fair Work Act. The indicators are unambiguous: dismiss-and-re-engage with no change in duties, employer-initiated ABN registration, and reduction in rate framed around the removal of SG payments.

The H2 gate fires. This is a potential civil penalty matter requiring a coordinated compliance response, well beyond an AMBER classification review. The registered tax agent is needed for the SG shortfall and PAYG withholding correction. An employment lawyer is needed for the Fair Work Act exposure. The two must coordinate to avoid any disclosure or filing that worsens the legal position.

// H2 gate fire — Priya, Metro Digital Solutions
{
  "orchestrator_action": "fire_h2_gate",
  "worker": "Priya Sharma",
  "employer": "Metro Digital Solutions",
  "trigger": "sham_contracting_risk_HIGH_ss357_358",
  "h2_requirements": {
    "registered_tax_agent": {
      "required": true,
      "scope": "ATO audit response, SG shortfall voluntary disclosure, PAYG withholding correction, STP amendments, payroll tax assessment"
    },
    "employment_lawyer": {
      "required": true,
      "scope": "Fair Work Act ss357-358 exposure, back-pay of NES entitlements from July 2023, workers compensation, strategy for voluntary rectification"
    },
    "coordination_required": true,
    "note": "Do not file any ATO voluntary disclosure or approach Fair Work Commission without coordination between registered tax agent and employment lawyer."
  },
  "blocked_actions": [
    "ato_voluntary_disclosure",
    "stp_amendment",
    "fair_work_complaint_response",
    "payroll_tax_amendment",
    "any_communication_with_priya_about_her_status"
  ],
  "engine_status": "figures_computed_held_pending_h2_review"
}

This is assumed-fact illustration only. What this illustrates: Sham contracting is a distinct category from honest classification error. The Fair Work Act penalties are civil, not criminal, but they are substantial. The H2 gate requires a registered tax agent for the formal ATO response and an employment lawyer for the Fair Work Act exposure. The payroll engine has computed the SG shortfall and back-pay figures — they are held. Nothing is filed until the H2 practitioners have coordinated a response strategy. The SG rate for the period from 1 July 2025 is 12% — verify as at June 2026. The period from 1 July 2023 uses the rate applicable to that year — verify against ATO.

My take: Worker classification is the highest-stakes judgment call in Australian payroll. The classification-agent makes the analysis systematic and reproducible. It does not make it automatic. Every AMBER fires H3. Every RED fires H3 and H1. Every sham contracting flag fires H2. The label in the contract is where the analysis starts, never where it ends. Every regulated amount is the engine's to compute. The practitioners own every compliance decision.

Next: Chapter 14 is the master reference — annual checklist, prompt library, red-flag escalation matrix, and series navigation.

14 Annual Checklist, Prompt Pack, and Series Close

Master payroll year-end checklist, 12+ prompt library, red-flag escalation matrix, and series navigation

This chapter is a reference you return to every financial year. The checklist covers every major payroll obligation from 1 July to 30 June. The prompt library shows how to invoke each agent in the agentic stack. The escalation matrix maps common payroll scenarios to the correct gate. Keep it open in a tab when you run your first payroll of the new financial year.

Master payroll year-end checklist

Year-end payroll runs as a sequence that begins in April and closes in October. Miss one step and the downstream cascade is almost always more expensive than the missed step itself. The checklist below maps every obligation to the responsible party, the agent that handles the computation, the timing, and the verification source. Run it top to bottom. Do not skip rows on the assumption that "it was fine last year".

The SG reconciliation for Q4 (April–June) is the highest-stakes item. The SG charge calculation, once triggered, is non-deductible and includes interest plus an administration component. Q4 super must be received by the fund by 28 July. STP finalisation is the next cliff: late or incorrect finalisation delays employee tax return pre-fill, generating ATO contact and sometimes amended assessments.

STP finalisation — H1 gate. STP finalisation is a mandatory H1 gate. No agent may submit a finalisation event without an authorised officer review and explicit approval. The review must confirm: all pay events reconcile to payroll system totals; reportable fringe benefits amounts are included where applicable; reportable employer super contributions are correctly coded; terminated employees are marked correctly. As at June 2026 — verify current ATO guidance on finalisation requirements at ato.gov.au/stp.

Task Responsible Agent Due date / timing Gate Verify source
Superannuation guarantee reconciliation — Q4 (April–June) Payroll manager sg-agent SG must be received by fund by 28 July (Q4 2025-26 — the last quarterly due date; from 1 July 2026 SG is due per payday under Payday Super) H1 — employer sign-off before payment ATO SG employer obligations; ato.gov.au/sg — verify rate (12% from 1 July 2025); from 1 July 2026 verify Payday Super per-payday obligation (fund receipt within 7 business days of each payday)
PAYG withholding reconciliation — full year Payroll manager / finance payg-agent Complete before STP finalisation H2 — human review of variance report ATO PAYG withholding schedule; W1/W2 BAS figures vs payroll register
STP Phase 2 finalisation event Authorised officer stp-finalisation-agent 14 July following the end of the financial year (standard deadline — verify current ATO guidance); closely held payees 30 September where applicable H1 — mandatory authorised officer declaration ato.gov.au/stp; employer finalisation guide as at June 2026
Payroll tax annual reconciliation — each relevant state Finance / payroll manager payroll-tax-agent July (most states) — verify per state revenue office H1 — authorised officer lodgement Relevant state revenue office (Revenue NSW, SRO Victoria, etc.); threshold and rate vary by state
Workers' compensation renewal — premium declaration HR / finance audit-risk-agent (data compilation only) Per insurer policy renewal date H2 — finance review before declaration State-based workers' compensation authority; premium is based on wages declared
Modern Award review — minimum rates update HR / payroll manager award-agent 1 July each year (Fair Work Commission annual wage review takes effect) H2 — confirm updated rates applied to payroll system before first July pay run Fair Work Commission annual wage review decision; fairwork.gov.au — as at June 2026
Contractor classification review — full population HR / legal classification-agent Before 30 June; update for any new engagements during the year H1 — legal sign-off on reclassification decisions ATO employee/contractor decision tool; Fair Work Act; High Court decisions as at June 2026
ETP cap verification — all termination payments Payroll manager termination-agent At time of each termination; verify ETP cap annually H1 — sign-off on ETP classification and tax treatment ATO — ETP cap is indexed annually; verify against current ATO ETP cap schedule at ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes
Redundancy tax-free amount verification Payroll manager termination-agent At time of each redundancy payment H1 — sign-off on tax-free component calculation ATO — redundancy tax-free amounts are indexed annually; verify against ATO table as at June 2026
New financial year SG rate confirmation Payroll manager sg-agent Before first pay run of new financial year (1 July) H2 — confirm system rate updated before payroll run ATO SG rate schedule; ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes — SG is 12% from 1 July 2025 (legislated ceiling reached); from 1 July 2026 the obligation moves to per-payday payment (fund receipt within 7 business days) under the Treasury Laws Amendment (Payday Superannuation) Act 2025 — a system cadence change, not a rate change; verify against ATO
ATO tax tables — new financial year update Payroll manager / system admin payg-agent Before first pay run of new financial year H2 — confirm updated tables loaded in payroll system ATO tax withheld calculator; ATO NAT 1008 (tax table schedule) — updated annually
Maximum super contribution base (MSCB) update Payroll manager sg-agent Before first pay run of new financial year H2 — confirm MSCB cap applied to quarterly calculation ATO — MSCB is indexed annually; verify against ATO as at June 2026 at ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes
National Minimum Wage — confirm updated rate applied Payroll manager award-agent 1 July (or Fair Work Commission effective date) H2 — confirm NMW update loaded before first July pay run Fair Work Commission; fairwork.gov.au/pay-and-wages/minimum-wages
Salary packaging arrangements — annual review HR / payroll manager salary-packaging-agent Before 1 April (FBT year end) and before 1 July (payroll year start) H1 — sign-off on FBT implications before new-year arrangements take effect ATO FBT guide; employer's salary packaging policy; PAYG withholding variation letter if applicable
Audit risk score — pre-new-year assessment Payroll manager / finance audit-risk-agent Before 30 June H2 — review score and resolve high-risk items before year closes Internal payroll data; ATO data matching program guidance; this checklist

Two items on this checklist are perennial sources of underpayment exposure: award rate updates and SG rate updates. The Fair Work Commission wage review decision takes effect 1 July each year. The SG rate schedule has stepped annually since 2021. Both require an actual system update before the first pay run of the new financial year — confirmed with a test pay calculation, not logged as a memo or a note-to-self. The award-agent and sg-agent can both run pre-flight checks on a test employee record. Run them on 30 June and confirm output before the 1 July payroll opens.

Prompt library — 12+ agent invocations

The table below maps each agent to its purpose and the chapter where its logic is explained. The code blocks that follow show realistic invocations. Arguments are representative — replace with your actual employee IDs, pay period dates, and state codes. Every prompt includes the gate parameter, which is mandatory. Gates are enforced by the orchestrator; omitting the gate flag does not bypass the gate, it causes the orchestrator to apply the most restrictive gate by default.

Agent Purpose Gate Chapter
payroll-orchestrator Runs the full payroll cycle for a pay period; routes to sub-agents H1 2
sg-agent Computes superannuation guarantee for an employee or cohort H1 3
payg-agent Checks and applies PAYG withholding for a payment type H2 4
award-agent Identifies applicable modern award and confirms minimum rates H2 5
termination-agent Computes all termination pay components including ETP H1 6
stp-agent Submits STP event for a pay period to the ATO H1 1
stp-finalisation-agent Runs year-end STP finalisation across all employees H1 10
salary-packaging-agent Models salary sacrifice arrangements and FBT/PAYG impact H1 7
payroll-tax-agent Calculates payroll tax liability for a state jurisdiction H2 8
classification-agent Runs multi-factor worker classification check H1 9, 13
audit-risk-agent Scores employer payroll audit risk across all major risk areas H2 11
special-populations-agent Routes edge-case workers (casual, commission, labour-hire) to correct logic H2 12
audit-trail-agent Generates immutable audit trail for a payroll event or decision No gate — records only; never modifies data 1, 2, 11

1. payroll-orchestrator — run full payroll cycle

payroll-orchestrator run \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --pay-period-start "2025-07-01" \
  --pay-period-end "2025-07-14" \
  --pay-date "2025-07-18" \
  --pay-frequency "fortnightly" \
  --employee-cohort "all-active" \
  --sg-rate "0.12" \
  --sg-rate-verify "ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes" \
  --tax-table-fy "2025-26" \
  --gate "H1" \
  --require-human-approval-before "stp-submission" \
  --audit-trail true \
  --dry-run false

2. sg-agent — compute SG for a specific employee and quarter

sg-agent compute \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --employee-id "EMP-W-0187" \
  --quarter "Q1-FY2026" \
  --quarter-start "2025-07-01" \
  --quarter-end "2025-09-30" \
  --sg-rate "0.12" \
  --include-ote-check true \
  --include-mscb-cap-check true \
  --mscb-verify "ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes" \
  --salary-sacrifice-offset true \
  --employee-choice-fund "REST-SUPER-0042187" \
  --gate "H1" \
  --output-format "json" \
  --output-path "./sg-output/Q1-FY2026/EMP-W-0187.json"

3. payg-agent — check withholding for a commission payment

payg-agent check \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --employee-id "EMP-S-0094" \
  --payment-type "commission" \
  --payment-amount "18500.00" \
  --payment-date "2025-08-15" \
  --ytd-gross "62400.00" \
  --tax-file-number-declared true \
  --tax-offset-claimed "standard" \
  --medicare-levy-exemption false \
  --scale "2" \
  --withholding-method "annualisation" \
  --gate "H2" \
  --require-human-review-if-variance-exceeds "500.00" \
  --output-format "json"

4. award-agent — check award and minimum rate for a role

award-agent check \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --employee-id "EMP-C-0221" \
  --industry "retail-trade" \
  --role-description "casual sales assistant, part-time hours, no supervisory duties" \
  --employment-basis "casual" \
  --award-check-date "2025-07-01" \
  --include-casual-loading-check true \
  --casual-loading-rate "0.25" \
  --minimum-rate-source "fairwork.gov.au" \
  --gate "H2" \
  --flag-if-rate-below-award true \
  --output-includes "award-name,classification-level,minimum-hourly-rate,casual-loading-component"

5. termination-agent — compute termination pay for redundancy

termination-agent compute \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --employee-id "EMP-P-0056" \
  --termination-type "genuine-redundancy" \
  --termination-date "2025-09-30" \
  --employment-start-date "2011-03-14" \
  --age-at-termination "51" \
  --ordinary-time-earnings-weekly "2180.00" \
  --unused-annual-leave-hours "142.5" \
  --unused-long-service-leave-hours "360.0" \
  --include-etp-calculation true \
  --etp-type "genuine-redundancy" \
  --etp-cap-verify "ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes" \
  --redundancy-tax-free-verify "ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes" \
  --gate "H1" \
  --require-legal-review true \
  --output-format "pdf" \
  --output-path "./terminations/EMP-P-0056-termination-pack.pdf"

6. stp-agent — submit STP event for a pay period

stp-agent submit \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --abn "51 824 753 556" \
  --pay-event-type "regular" \
  --pay-period-start "2025-07-01" \
  --pay-period-end "2025-07-14" \
  --pay-date "2025-07-18" \
  --employee-count 47 \
  --payroll-register-path "./payroll/FY2026/period-01/register.json" \
  --stp2-schema-version "2.0.0" \
  --include-salary-sacrifice true \
  --include-reportable-fringe-benefits false \
  --disaggregation-check true \
  --gate "H1" \
  --authorised-officer-id "AO-0003" \
  --authorised-officer-declaration-timestamp "2025-07-18T08:42:00+10:00" \
  --submission-channel "sbr2" \
  --audit-trail true

7. stp-finalisation-agent — year-end STP finalisation

stp-finalisation-agent run \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --abn "51 824 753 556" \
  --financial-year "FY2026" \
  --year-end "2026-06-30" \
  --employee-scope "all" \
  --include-terminated-employees true \
  --include-reportable-employer-super-contributions true \
  --include-reportable-fringe-benefits true \
  --rfba-source "fbt-return-FY2026" \
  --reconcile-against-payroll-register true \
  --reconcile-against-bas-w1-w2 true \
  --variance-tolerance "0.01" \
  --flag-employees-with-no-payg-events true \
  --gate "H1" \
  --authorised-officer-id "AO-0003" \
  --require-explicit-approval true \
  --dry-run-first true \
  --submission-deadline "2026-07-14" \
  --ato-guidance-verify "ato.gov.au/stp"

8. salary-packaging-agent — compute salary sacrifice novated lease impact

salary-packaging-agent model \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --employee-id "EMP-M-0033" \
  --arrangement-type "novated-lease" \
  --vehicle-value "68500.00" \
  --annual-kilometres 22000 \
  --lease-term-years 3 \
  --pre-tax-component "12400.00" \
  --post-tax-component "3200.00" \
  --gross-salary "145000.00" \
  --fbt-year-end "2026-03-31" \
  --ecm-method "employee-contribution" \
  --reportable-benefit-check true \
  --payg-withholding-variation-check true \
  --gate "H1" \
  --include-comparison "pre-packaging-vs-post-packaging" \
  --require-employee-sign-off true \
  --output-format "pdf"

9. payroll-tax-agent — check payroll tax liability for a state

payroll-tax-agent calculate \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --state "NSW" \
  --assessment-period "monthly" \
  --period-start "2025-07-01" \
  --period-end "2025-07-31" \
  --taxable-wages "387200.00" \
  --include-contractor-wages true \
  --contractor-wages "42500.00" \
  --apply-grouping-rules true \
  --related-entities "EMP-0043,EMP-0044" \
  --threshold-verify "revenue.nsw.gov.au" \
  --rate-verify "revenue.nsw.gov.au" \
  --note "No federal payroll tax applies; state/territory rules govern" \
  --gate "H2" \
  --annual-reconciliation-flag false \
  --output-format "json"

10. classification-agent — multi-factor worker classification check

classification-agent run \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --worker-id "WKR-C-0089" \
  --engagement-type "written-contract-for-services" \
  --contract-path "./contracts/WKR-C-0089-services-agreement.pdf" \
  --factors-check "control,integration,tools-and-equipment,hours,leave-entitlement,abn-held,results-basis,exclusive-service" \
  --ato-decision-tool-version "2026" \
  --fair-work-employee-test true \
  --include-sham-contracting-risk-score true \
  --prior-classification "contractor" \
  --note "Prior classification label is NOT determinative; multi-factor test governs" \
  --gate "H1" \
  --require-legal-review-if-ambiguous true \
  --output-format "json" \
  --output-path "./classifications/WKR-C-0089-assessment.json"

11. audit-risk-agent — run audit risk score for employer

audit-risk-agent score \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --assessment-date "2026-06-30" \
  --include-sg-risk true \
  --include-payg-withholding-risk true \
  --include-stp-compliance-risk true \
  --include-contractor-classification-risk true \
  --include-award-compliance-risk true \
  --include-payroll-tax-risk true \
  --include-etp-risk true \
  --data-sources "payroll-register,stp-events,sg-payment-receipts,bas-lodgements,worker-register" \
  --ato-data-match-flag true \
  --gate "H2" \
  --escalate-to-H1-if-score-above "75" \
  --output-format "executive-summary" \
  --output-path "./audit/FY2026/risk-score-30-june-2026.pdf"

12. special-populations-agent — classify and route a casual commission worker

special-populations-agent route \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --worker-id "EMP-CC-0312" \
  --employment-basis "casual" \
  --earnings-type "commission-only" \
  --award-coverage-check true \
  --industry "financial-services" \
  --ote-classification-check true \
  --sg-obligation-check true \
  --casual-loading-check true \
  --casual-loading-rate "0.25" \
  --long-service-leave-state "NSW" \
  --payroll-tax-inclusion-check true \
  --gate "H2" \
  --output-includes "ote-status,sg-obligation,award-rate,casual-loading-confirmed,payroll-tax-inclusion" \
  --flag-for-human-review-if-classification-uncertain true

13. audit-trail-agent — generate immutable audit trail

audit-trail-agent generate \
  --employer-id "EMP-0042" \
  --event-type "stp-finalisation" \
  --event-id "STP-FIN-FY2026-001" \
  --event-date "2026-07-15" \
  --authorised-officer-id "AO-0003" \
  --include-decision-log true \
  --include-agent-invocation-log true \
  --include-human-review-timestamps true \
  --include-gate-outcomes true \
  --include-data-sources true \
  --hash-algorithm "SHA-256" \
  --immutable-store true \
  --output-format "signed-json" \
  --output-path "./audit-trails/FY2026/STP-FIN-FY2026-001-trail.json"

My take: The prompt library is a living document. Add your employer-specific defaults (employer-id, ABN, standard gate levels) as environment variables or a config file that each agent loads on startup. This cuts invocation length in half and removes the risk of passing stale employer details to the wrong agent. Version-control the config. Treat changes to gate defaults as code changes — review them before merging.

Red-flag escalation matrix

The escalation matrix is the decision tree for your payroll desk. When something triggers a flag, look up the scenario, confirm the agent and gate, and take the required action. "Confidence" here refers to the agent's computed confidence in its own output — a low-confidence output always escalates regardless of gate level. High-confidence outputs still respect the gate model; H1 always requires a human.

Print this matrix. Stick it near the payroll workstation. The scenarios are ordered roughly by frequency of occurrence — SG shortfalls and TFN issues are common; sham contracting risk and ATO data match discrepancies are less frequent but higher severity when they arise.

Scenario Trigger Agent Confidence Gate Action required
SG shortfall detected sg-agent returns underpayment vs OTE for any employee in any quarter sg-agent High — computation is deterministic H1 Stop. Do not process next pay run. Payroll manager reviews calculation. If confirmed, engage SGC lodgement process with ATO. SGC is non-deductible and includes interest; early voluntary disclosure reduces penalty. As at June 2026 — verify ATO SGC process at ato.gov.au/business/super-for-employers/
No TFN declaration received payg-agent detects employee without lodged TFN declaration on file payg-agent High H2 Apply highest marginal withholding rate (no tax offset) until TFN declaration received. Do not delay payment. Record reason. Payroll manager to follow up with employee within 28 days. As at June 2026 — verify ATO guidance at ato.gov.au (TFN declaration guidance)
Casual loading dispute award-agent flags a casual employee record where casual loading is not separately identified in pay slip award-agent Medium — depends on award coverage assessment H1 Halt casual loading set-off claim if applicable. HR review. Check employment contract, pay slip history. If casual loading was embedded in a flat rate, obtain legal advice on exposure. Fair Work compliance team may need to be notified. As at June 2026 — verify Rossato/WorkPac case developments at fairwork.gov.au
Contractor classification ambiguous classification-agent returns confidence below 70% or factors split evenly classification-agent Low — ambiguous multi-factor result H1 Do not pay as contractor until resolved. Escalate to legal. Obtain written legal advice on classification. If employment relationship exists, engage as employee immediately. Do not continue engagement as contractor pending advice. The label "contractor" in the contract is NOT determinative.
STP submission failed stp-agent returns error response from ATO (non-200 status or error payload) stp-agent High — deterministic error from ATO gateway H1 Do not re-submit without diagnosing error. Review ATO error code. Common causes: ABN mismatch, schema validation failure, duplicate pay event ID. Fix root cause. Re-submit same pay event (do not create a new event). Record submission attempt and error in audit trail. As at June 2026 — verify ATO STP error codes at ato.gov.au/stp
Fair Work underpayment allegation payroll-orchestrator or award-agent receives external notice (Fair Work inspector, employee complaint) award-agent + audit-trail-agent N/A — triggered externally H1 Immediately preserve all payroll records. Instruct payroll team to make no amendments. Engage legal counsel. Audit trail agent should snapshot current state. Prepare seven-year payroll record set. Cooperate with Fair Work investigation. Voluntary back-payment before investigation conclusion is treated more favourably.
Payroll tax grouping query payroll-tax-agent flags potential grouping with related entities based on common ownership or control payroll-tax-agent Medium — grouping rules are fact-specific H1 Obtain state revenue office advice on grouping status. Do not assume each entity is standalone. Threshold is assessed on grouped wages across all grouped entities. Engage tax adviser. As at June 2026 — verify grouping rules with your state revenue office; rules differ by jurisdiction.
