ETP classification uncertain termination-agent cannot determine whether payment is a life benefit ETP or another category termination-agent Low — classification requires legal and factual analysis H1 Do not process ETP withholding until classified. Engage tax adviser or ATO private ruling if necessary. Incorrect ETP classification produces incorrect withholding and incorrect STP reporting. ETP cap is indexed annually — verify current cap at ato.gov.au/rates-and-codes. Legal advice required before payment.
Sham contracting risk detected classification-agent flags that a contractor engagement has characteristics of employment; sham contracting risk score is high classification-agent Medium — risk score, not determination H1 Cease new engagements with that worker until reviewed. Engage legal counsel immediately. Sham contracting carries civil penalties under the Fair Work Act. Do not restructure the arrangement to avoid the classification — that itself may increase exposure. Document all steps taken.
ATO data match discrepancy ATO correspondence indicates STP-reported amounts do not match tax return or third-party data audit-risk-agent + stp-agent N/A — triggered by ATO H1 Reconcile STP events against payroll register for the relevant period. Identify source of discrepancy (timing difference, coding error, or genuine underpayment/overreporting). Engage tax adviser. Respond to ATO within stated timeframe. Amend STP events if required. Document all steps in audit trail.
PAYG withholding reconciliation gap payg-agent year-end reconciliation shows material variance between total PAYG withheld (payroll system) and BAS W1/W2 amounts lodged payg-agent High — deterministic calculation H1 Do not finalise STP until gap is resolved. Trace every pay event. Common causes: BAS corrections not reflected in payroll, ad hoc payments missed, termination payments coded incorrectly. If BAS was incorrect, lodge amended BAS. Record resolution in audit trail. Do not close financial year with unresolved variance.
Award rate increase not applied award-agent detects that one or more employees are being paid below the new minimum rate effective 1 July award-agent High — minimum rate is deterministic once award is confirmed H1 Stop next pay run if within first pay cycle of the new financial year. Calculate back-pay from 1 July. Back-pay immediately. Correct payroll system rate. STP correct event may be required if prior pay events are already submitted. Engage Fair Work compliance adviser. The employer has no discretion on minimum rates — they are statutory floors.
STP finalisation overdue stp-finalisation-agent or payroll calendar flags that finalisation has not been submitted by the ATO deadline stp-finalisation-agent High — date is deterministic H1 Submit as soon as possible. Do not wait for year-end audit completion if data is materially correct. An amended finalisation can be submitted later. ATO may apply penalties for late finalisation. Contact ATO if genuinely unable to finalise (e.g. pending dispute) — request deferral before deadline. As at June 2026 — verify current deadline at ato.gov.au/stp
Worker reclassification required classification-agent confirms worker previously treated as contractor should be classified as employee classification-agent + sg-agent + payg-agent High — determination made by legal/agent H1 Engage employee immediately from reclassification date. Back-calculate SG entitlement for all prior quarters as employee. Determine PAYG withholding that should have been withheld. Engage ATO voluntary disclosure process. Notify state payroll tax authority. This is a multi-agent, multi-authority resolution — do not attempt it without a coordinated plan.
State payroll tax audit commenced State revenue office issues audit commencement notice payroll-tax-agent + audit-trail-agent N/A — triggered by authority H1 Preserve all payroll records, contractor registers, and wage calculations for the audit period. Engage tax adviser with state payroll tax experience. Do not amend records after audit commencement without legal advice. Respond to information requests within deadlines. Voluntary disclosure before formal audit finding is generally treated more favourably.

Low confidence always escalates. The confidence column in this matrix describes what the agent outputs, not what you should act on alone. Any low-confidence result from any agent escalates to H1 regardless of the scenario's default gate. An agent that is uncertain is telling you it cannot resolve the case deterministically. That is valuable information. Do not override it.

The agentic stack in one view

This table is the map of every agent in the book. Use it to navigate between chapters and to understand where each agent sits in the determinism/gate hierarchy. Agents toward the top of the stack are orchestrators — they route and coordinate. Agents in the middle are domain specialists — they compute and verify. The audit-trail-agent sits apart: it never modifies data, never routes work, and never produces a result that feeds into payroll calculations. Its only job is to record.

My take: The agents in this stack are only as good as the data they receive. The payroll-orchestrator is the entry point, but the quality of its output depends entirely on the payroll register, the employee master, and the award classification data flowing into it. Garbage in produces confident garbage out — a high-confidence result from an agent with bad input data is more dangerous than a low-confidence result from an agent with good input data. The gate model catches the second case. Nothing catches the first case except human data quality review.

Agent name Role Determinism note Gate Chapter
payroll-orchestrator Coordinates the full payroll cycle; routes to domain agents; enforces gate model Routing logic is deterministic; sub-agent outputs vary by domain H1 2
payroll-engine Core gross-to-net computation; applies tax tables, deductions, and salary sacrifice High — statutory inputs (tax tables, SG rate) are fixed per period H1 2
sg-agent Computes superannuation guarantee; checks OTE basis; applies MSCB cap High — SG rate and MSCB are fixed once confirmed for the year H1 3
payg-agent Applies PAYG withholding schedule; handles irregular payments and annualisation High for standard payments; medium for irregular payments (annualisation method) H2 4
award-agent Identifies modern award coverage; confirms minimum classification and rate Medium — award coverage can be ambiguous at the industry boundary H2 5
termination-agent Computes all termination components: unused leave, ETP, redundancy tax-free amount Medium-high — statutory components are deterministic; ETP classification requires legal input H1 6
stp-agent Constructs and submits STP pay events to ATO via SBR2 High — schema validation is deterministic; submission outcome is ATO-determined H1 1
stp-finalisation-agent Runs year-end finalisation; reconciles all pay events; submits finalisation declaration High for reconciliation; H1 gate enforces human declaration requirement H1 10
salary-packaging-agent Models salary sacrifice arrangements; computes FBT and PAYG withholding impact Medium — FBT valuation rules require current-year ATO guidance; ECM method adds complexity H1 7
payroll-tax-agent Calculates state payroll tax liability; checks grouping; generates reconciliation Medium — statutory rate and threshold are fixed; grouping determination is fact-specific H2 8
classification-agent Runs multi-factor worker classification test; produces risk-scored determination Low to medium — multi-factor test is inherently fact-specific and legal-question-adjacent H1 9, 13
audit-risk-agent Scores employer payroll audit risk across all major risk domains Medium — risk score is a model output; not a legal determination H2 (escalates to H1 if score above threshold) 11
special-populations-agent Routes edge-case workers to correct logic path; handles casual, commission, labour-hire, working holidaymakers Medium — routing is deterministic once classification is confirmed; classification itself may be uncertain H2 12
audit-trail-agent Generates immutable, timestamped, hashed audit trail for every payroll event and gate decision High — records only; never modifies data; hash is deterministic No gate — records only; never modifies data 1, 2, 11

Agentic Accounting series navigation

This book is the seventh in the Agentic Accounting series. Each book covers a distinct domain of Australian accounting and tax practice. The series is designed so that each book stands alone — you do not need to read books in order — but the determinism-first principle and H1/H2/H3 gate model are consistent across all seven books. An agent you understand from this payroll book behaves the same way architecturally in the tax agent book or the SMSF book.

Book Title What it covers
1 The Agentic Tax Agent Individual income tax returns; deduction classification; PAYG income summaries; pre-fill data reconciliation; amendment workflow; ATO correspondence agents. Covers the full individual tax lifecycle from data collection to lodgement.
2 The Agentic BAS Agent GST classification; BAS lodgement via SBR2; fuel tax credit calculation and reconciliation; instalment method vs actual GST; BAS revision and amendment workflow; ATO audit response. Covers quarterly and monthly BAS obligations for businesses of all sizes.
3 The Agentic SMSF Agent SMSF administration; annual return lodgement; investment strategy review; contribution classification (concessional vs non-concessional); pension commencement; actuarial certificate triggers; SMSF audit preparation. Covers the full SMSF compliance cycle.
4 The Agentic Trust Agent Discretionary and unit trust distributions; beneficiary entitlement calculation; section 100A risk assessment; streaming of capital gains and franked dividends; trust tax return; trustee resolution workflow. Covers distribution planning, compliance, and ATO risk management.
5 The Agentic CGT Agent Capital gains calculation across all CGT assets; discount method and indexation method; small business CGT concessions (15-year, 50% reduction, retirement, rollover); market value substitution; CGT event classification. Covers individual, trust, company, and SMSF CGT scenarios.
6 The Agentic FBT Agent Fringe benefit classification across all 13 FBT categories; taxable value calculation (statutory vs operating cost for cars); employee contribution method; exempt benefits; FBT return lodgement; reportable fringe benefits amounts for STP. Covers the full FBT year cycle from 1 April to 31 March.
7 The Agentic Payroll Agent STP Phase 2 disaggregated reporting; superannuation guarantee (12% from 1 July 2025); PAYG withholding; modern award compliance; terminations and ETPs; salary packaging; state payroll tax; worker classification; audit risk scoring. This book. As at June 2026.

My take: The direction of agentic accounting practice is determinism under audit, not automation of judgement. Every book in this series arrives at the same conclusion from a different angle. The CGT concession rules are fact-specific. The trust distribution rules require legal intent. The SMSF investment strategy requires trustee deliberation. And the payroll rules — as this book has shown — sit at the intersection of statute, award, contract, and state jurisdiction. The agent's job is to surface the relevant facts, apply the rules mechanically where the rules permit it, and stop where human judgement is required. The practitioners who get this right will not be replaced by agents. They will be the ones who know when to trust the agent and when to override it.

Close

The determinism-first principle is not a constraint on what the agentic stack can do. It is the condition that makes the stack trustworthy. In payroll, creative interpretation serves no one — employer, employee, and ATO are all worse off for it. The SG rate is what it is. The award rate is what it is. The withholding schedule is what it is. The agent's job is to apply these rules without drift, without optimism, and without the kind of rounding that feels harmless until it compounds across 200 employees over four quarters. The gate model — H1: registered tax agent or BAS agent (non-delegable); H2: registered tax agent only; H3: practitioner review before proceeding — exists because statutory compliance requires a human in the loop at precisely the moments when an agent is most likely to be confidently wrong.

Run the audit-risk-agent before the new financial year opens. Not after the first pay run, not after the STP finalisation — before. The risk score gives you a map of where the year-end left unresolved exposure. High-risk items do not resolve themselves between 30 June and 1 July. They carry forward, accumulate interest in the case of SG, and become the foundation for the next year's audit risk. The cost of a pre-year-end review is an hour of agent time and a human review session. The cost of finding the same issues in an ATO audit is measured in different units.

Every payroll cycle is a commitment to accuracy under law. The agentic stack described in this book keeps that commitment intact and gives you better tools to honour it.

Applied AI Field Guides

Disclaimer
General educational information for payroll officers, bookkeepers, accountants, and HR staff, current as at June 2026. Not tax, legal, or employment law advice. AI output must be verified against the current PAYG withholding tax tables, ATO guidance, and applicable industrial instruments before relying on it. Rates and thresholds change — verify before acting.

The Agentic Payroll Agent
